content: Preface

In Nepal, democracy emerged in 2007 BS (1951 AD) following the end of the autocratic Rana regime. Between the establishment of democracy and 2046 BS (1990 AD), 22 governments were formed. Following the restoration of democracy in 1990, the Maoist armed insurgency in 1996, and the Comprehensive People's Movement of 2006/2007, the 238-year-old monarchy ended, and the country adopted a Federal Democratic Republican system. In the 35 years since 1990, 30 governments have been formed. While economic development gained some momentum during this period, expected success remained elusive. Repeated presence of the same faces in power, stagnation in long-term development planning, the daily spread of widespread corruption, and inequality in resource distribution led to a lack of domestic employment and an increase in youth migration. The focus seemed to be more on power struggles and changing governments than on development and good governance. The reappearance of the same individuals in elections has proven the adage: 'A true leader worries about the country's future, while a cunning politician worries about the next election.'

During this period, Nepal remained categorized as an underdeveloped country on the world map. Citizens grew angry and exhausted by political interference in all sectors, ineffective public service delivery, rampant corruption, rising educated unemployment, the wide gap between the 'Haves and Have-nots,' and the extravagant lifestyles of a limited few. Recently, while the younger generation has been using technology and social media platforms as a means of employment and communication, the Nepal Government's decision on 2082/05/19 to ban social mediacombined with the aforementioned systemic issuesprompted the Gen-Z generation to call for a protest on Bhadra 23, 2082.

The protest began on Bhadra 23 at Maitighar, Kathmandu. After breaking through a police barricade in front of the Everest Hotel, the crowd was met with water cannons before reaching the Parliament building in Baneshwor. The crowd became agitated and began throwing stones at the police. When some protesters tried to enter the Parliament premises, police used force, resulting in 17 deaths that day and 12 more succumbing to injuries later. A large number of protesters and police were injured. The government faced natural and widespread criticism for the incident. On Bhadra 24, the protests turned violent. Simultaneously, at the Parliament building, Singha Durbar, the Supreme Court, Shital Niwas (President's Residence), Baluwatar (Prime Minister's Residence), and various locations across the Kathmandu Valley and the country, there were incidents of arson, vandalism, theft, and looting involving government offices, public bodies, municipalities, police stations, private property, political party offices, and commercial establishments.

These events resulted not only in the dissolution of the government but also the dissolution of the House of Representatives, creating an extraordinary situation. To investigate the facts of the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 and submit a report, an Inquiry Commission was formed by a government decision on 2082/06/05 and began work on 2082/06/09. Initially given a three-month term, the commission received three extensions due to workload and time constraints: one month on 2082/09/03, twenty days on 2082/10/08, and twenty-five days on 2082/10/26.

Following its formation, the Commission established its office at the former Office of the Prime Minister and Council of Ministers. To uncover the truth, the Commission issued a public appeal for details of the damage and requested information from both public and private agencies via formal correspondence.

Investigations were launched to gather data on the human and physical losses of those two days. The Chairperson and members led field visits and interaction programs in 13 heavily affected districts. Incident reports were requested from the Chief District Officers of 18 districts. Statements were taken from the injured and relatives of the deceased, discussions were held with government and court officials, and statements were collected from individuals involved in the incidents. Consultations regarding future investment incentives were held with private enterprises like Airtel and Lara, and testimonies/inquiries were conducted with officials who were in charge at the time.

Analysis of data and discussions with all parties revealed that the immediate cause of the Bhadra 23 protest was the social media ban, while the root causes were deep-seated corruption and poor governance. While Gen-Z did not participate in the violent activities of the 24th, other age groups joined the youth spontaneously in protest of the previous day's events. However, criminal elements and looters took advantage of the security vacuum, engaging in looting, arson, vandalism, and the assault and murder of police officers, leading to anarchy. Based on collected data, 76 people died during the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 (including 42 from security force fire, 3 police officers from protester attacks, and the rest during looting). A total of 2,522 people (including security personnel) were injured, and physical damage is estimated at approximately 85 billion NPR.

The Commission has reached solid conclusions regarding the Bhadra 23 incidents and recommended necessary actions. Since the Bhadra 24 events affected the entire country and were widespread, the Commission's limitations meant not every area could be fully investigated. However, research materials such as BTS (Base Transceiver Station) reports have been included in the report to help bring those involved in the violence to justice.

The Commission has presented recommendations for sectoral improvements to prevent future occurrences, along with an implementation action plan. This report was prepared impartially, adhering to the Inquiry Commission Act, 2026, prevailing laws, and ethical standards.

The Commission expresses gratitude to the public bodies, organizations, Nepal Television, private television stations, and individuals who provided footage and digital materials. We are also grateful for the suggestions provided by civil servants and experts. Finally, the Commission thanks the staff of the Secretariat who worked tirelessly from the office's establishment through to the report's completion.
Part 1: Preliminary
Chapter 1: Introduction
1.1 Background of the Commission's Formation

Distrust and anger toward the government and administrative mechanisms had been spreading due to the repetition of the same political leadership, arrogance, activities that ignored public concerns, corruption, and poor governance. Simultaneously, public support was growing for social activists campaigning against the government and the current system.

On Bhadra 19, 2082, the government decided to ban certain social media platforms that were not officially registered, citing the need to control undesirable activities and bring them into the tax net. Because of the growing attraction of these platforms among youthserving as both social hubs and sources of incomethe younger generation announced a protest for Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, linking the ban to issues of corruption and mismanagement. The peaceful march starting from Maitighar on Bhadra 23 turned destructive upon reaching the Parliament building in Baneshwor, resulting in 19 deaths (17 in Kathmandu Valley and 2 outside). The resulting anger led protesters to defy curfew orders on Bhadra 24, leading to unprecedented arson, vandalism, and looting of government property, political offices, and private assets.

Consequently, the Inquiry Commission was formed on 2082/06/05 under Section 3(2) of the Inquiry Commission Act, 2026, to investigate the physical and human losses during the Gen-Z protests and submit a factual report.
1.2 Evolution of the Gen-Z Movement and its Impact in Nepal

A citizen's rights are guaranteed by a country's constitution and governance. In Nepal, various movements have occurred to establish social equality and economic development. This began with the 1951 revolution against the Rana regime. Later, in 2017 BS (1960 AD), King Mahendra implemented a partyless system. Following subsequent rebellions, multi-party democracy was restored in 1990. During its infancy, the ten-year Maoist insurgency began in 1996, severely impacting democratic practice. In 2005, the constitutional monarch took absolute power, leading to the 2006/07 People's Movement, which established a full republican system.

The current constitution, drafted by a Constituent Assembly, adopted a Federal Democratic Republic aimed at sustainable peace, good governance, and prosperity. It guarantees sovereign rights, autonomy, and self-governance. Article 18 ensures the right to equality, Article 27 the right to information, and Article 33 the right to employment, among 31 fundamental rights. Part 4 outlines the directive principles of the state, and Article 51(4) mandates a clean, competent, impartial, transparent, and corruption-free public administration.

To address past dissatisfaction with centralized power, the country now operates through three levels of government: Federal, 7 Provinces, and 753 Local Governments. While the constitution is inclusive, the proportional representation system makes it difficult for any single party to obtain a full majority, making political coalitions mandatory. This often leads to power-sharing in constitutional appointments and state resources rather than focusing on public welfare. Current leaders, many of whom were champions of past movements, failed to learn from history or improve governance.

Information technology has turned the world into a 'global village.' Social media facilitates the rapid exchange of information, making today's youth highly aware of global trends and state activities. They are vigilant regarding the responsibilities of public officials, expect immediate results, and are unwilling to tolerate dissatisfaction. The protests of Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, are a direct result of this generational anger toward the state machinery.
What is Generation Z (Gen-Z)?

As society evolves, each generation leaves a profound impact on culture, economy, and technology. Gen-Z is one of the fastest-growing and most tech-savvy demographics in history. Literally, Gen-Z refers to the generation born roughly between 1997 and 2012. They are defined as the first 'Digital Natives' who grew up with the internet and smartphones. They follow the Millennials and precede Generation Alpha.
Gen-Z Movements in Various Countries:
Sri Lanka

The 2022 'Aragalaya' (Struggle) was a youth-led rebellion against economic hardship and corruption. Years of debt for 'white elephant' infrastructure and tax cuts in 2019 led to a $1.4 billion revenue loss and a 69% inflation rate by 2022. Protesters used TikTok and Instagram to organize and demand the resignation of the Rajapaksa government, providing a powerful example of how Gen-Z can challenge established structures.
Bangladesh

In July-August 2024, student activists led massive protests. Despite being a fast-growing economy, inflation reached 9%, unemployment rose, and corruption exploded. After the army withdrew support from Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina, she was forced into exile. A caretaker government led by Muhammad Yunus was formed to implement reforms and prevent further authoritarianism.
Kenya

The Kenya finance bill protests, widely known as #RejectFinanceBill2024, involved decentralized mass protests against proposed tax hikes. Protesters used X, TikTok, Instagram, ChatGPT SMS, and WhatsApp to mobilize. Following clashes where at least 22 died, President Ruto rejected the bill on June 28 and eventually dismissed almost his entire cabinet.
Indonesia

In late August 2025, Gen-Z protests in Indonesia were driven by frustration over economic inequality, corruption, and government inaction. Key grievances included high parliamentary allowances and the rising cost of living compared to politicians' lifestyles. Similar to youth in Kenya, they were outraged by elite privileges amidst widespread unemployment and poverty.

These young activists used digital platforms like Facebook, X, and TikTok to organize. They frequently posted about elite privilege and government failures, leading to clashes with police. These confrontations resulted in at least ten deaths and thousands of detentions. As a direct result of this pressure, President Prabowo Subianto's administration cut politicians' privileges and reshuffled the cabinet, including the Finance Minister.
Morocco

A series of youth-led protests in Morocco began on September 27, 2025. Known as the Gen-Z 212 movement (referencing Morocco’s international dialing code), it targeted issues like inadequate healthcare, education, youth unemployment, and systemic corruption. The movement was sparked by a tragic series of maternal deaths in Agadir, highlighting severe flaws in the health system. Protests spread nationwide, questioning government spending prioritiesspecifically large investments for co-hosting the 2030 FIFA World Cup over public services. Organization happened largely via Discord servers. Following massive peaceful demonstrations in major cities, the government introduced reforms to encourage political participation for citizens under 35 and proposed a $15 billion allocation for health and educationa 16% increase from the previous year.
Peru

On October 10, 2025, President Dina Boluarte was removed from office, and Congressman José Jerí was sworn in as Peru's eighth president in ten years. This triggered the largest protests the country had seen in five years. Beyond the political transition, Peruvians were reacting to an unprecedented 6-fold increase in organized crime and extortion between 2019 and 2024. The movement was a battle for the basic rule of law against a backdrop of corruption and sexual misconduct allegations against the new president. Clashes with police resulted in at least one death and dozens of injuries.
Madagascar

Despite being one of the world's poorest nations, Madagascar's elite, including President Andry Rajoelina, faced intense scrutiny over visible wealth and privileges. Protests erupted in September 2024 due to frequent water and electricity shortages, targeting the residences of high-ranking politicians. The 'General Z Madagascar' Facebook page became the primary organizing hub. Violence and looting led to 22 deaths. In response, Rajoelina dismissed his Energy Minister and later dissolved Prime Minister Christian Ntsay’s government. On October 11, 2025, units of the Madagascar Armed Forces staged a military coup in support of the protests.
Mexico

On November 15, 2025, thousands marched under the 'Generación Z' banner across Mexico following the public assassination of anti-crime Mayor Carlos Manzo. In Mexico City, protesters clashed with riot police near the National Palace, resulting in injuries to 100 officers and 20 civilians. While a group called 'Generación Z México' issued a manifesto representing youth tired of violence and power abuse, President Claudia Sheinbaum alleged the movement was a paid digital campaign backed by political opponents.
Philippines

The Gen-Z movement in the Philippines was sparked by a multi-billion dollar corruption scandal involving fake flood-relief projects. Activists used Reddit, Threads, and Discord to demand transparency in public funds and an end to nepotism and the high cost of living. While the movement highlighted deep-seated anger over economic inequality, its long-term impact on policy reform remains to be seen.
Hong Kong

Youth in Hong Kong were motivated by a proposed extradition bill that threatened to subject residents to China's opaque legal system. Demands grew to include independent investigations into police conduct and direct elections for the Chief Executive. What began as peaceful marches turned violent due to government refusal to negotiate and police crackdowns. The movement utilized extensive online coordination but was eventually suppressed following the passage of the 2020 National Security Law.

The conclusion drawn from these global movements is that the root causes of unrest are political malpractice, administrative delays, corruption, inflation, and the monopoly of power by a limited elite. Regardless of the system's name, its success depends on the competence and accountability of its leaders. Global history shows that when power remains concentrated in the same hands for too long, public dissatisfaction becomes inevitable.
Gen-Z Movement in Nepal

In Nepal, youth dissatisfied with the 'expired' generation’s political monopoly and the failure to transfer leadership have adopted alternative ways of thinking. This generation is not just critical of the state but is solution-oriented and innovative. They have emerged as active members of Nepal's social, cultural, and political life, driven by new technology and a consciousness for change.

content: Rapid expansion of IT and easy access to mobile internet has connected Nepalese Gen-Z with the global community. Consequently, they demand free thought, expression, and equal opportunity. They challenge old political structures with a focus on transparency and accountability. Campaigns like 'Nepo Kids,' 'Enough is Enough,' 'No Not Again,' and 'Anti-corruption' gained momentum on social media long before the physical protests.

content: The rise of Gen-Z in Nepal represents a renaissance of values and technology. While the protests of Bhadra 23 and 24 were directed at general governance, the government's decision to ban social media served as the primary spark that energized and mobilized the youth.

content: Events Following the Supreme Court Order on Social Media Bans

The recent unrest was seeded by the government's decision on 2082/05/09 to ban 26 social media platforms that were not registered in Nepal. This caused significant outrage among the Gen-Z population. The platforms included Facebook, Messenger, Instagram, WhatsApp, YouTube, X, LinkedIn, Snapchat, Reddit, Discord, Pinterest, Signal, Threads, WeChat, Quora, Tumblr, Clubhouse, Mastodon, Rumble, MeWe, VK, Line, IMO, Zalo, Soul, and the local app Hamro Patro.

Minister for Communication and Information Technology, Prithvi Subba Gurung, justified the ban using three reasons: the 'Social Media Regulation Directive 2080,' the cabinet decision of 2082/05/19, and a Supreme Court ruling from 2081/06/13. The Ministry claimed the Supreme Court's full bench had ordered the mandatory registration of online platforms to monitor unwanted content, a ruling linked to the 'Dark File Report' contempt case involving Sidhakura.com.
The court's decision primarily highlighted the following three points:

content.content: Both domestic and foreign online and social media platforms must register with relevant authorities before operating in the country.
The government should enact laws to create a regulatory body, similar to Press Council Nepal, to inspect and regulate these platforms.
New regulations must include mechanisms for evaluating and monitoring undesirable content on social media.

content: Following the cabinet decision on Bhadra 9 and the Communications Ministerial decision on Bhadra 19, the Telecommunications Authority directed mobile and internet service providers to deactivate unregistered social media platforms. By Bhadra 20, 26 social media platforms were rendered inactive.

The government misapplied the Supreme Court's interpretation to justify a complete ban on social media. In response to this decision, writ petitions were filed by Advocate Dr. Sunil Ranjan Singh and others. Initially, the Supreme Court administration refused to register these petitions, effectively supporting the government's stance. However, following an appeal against Registrar Man Bahadur Karki's refusal, the court ordered the registration of the petitions on Bhadra 24, the second day of the protests.

While the Gen-Z movement of Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, ostensibly began as a protest against the social media ban, its root cause was a rebellion against political anomalies. Factors included repeated interference by the same political groups, questions over administrative integrity, declining trust in the judiciary, an opaque private sector, the stagnation of civil society, exploitation of public resources by a limited elite, and sections of the media spreading negativity.

Successful nations rely on an honest political system, an effective independent administration, an accountable private sector, and a neutral civil society. Information technology is essential to making traditional workflows efficient and cost-effective; for today's youth, digital platforms are the foundation of their professionalism and serve as 'Digital Job Creators.' There is a significant gap between the lifestyle of past generations and the current one due to this rapid technological evolution.

In accordance with its mandate, this Commission has prepared this report on the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 based on the principles of impartiality, accountability, transparency, reliability, and ethics.
Chapter 2: Formation, Functions, Duties, Powers, and Procedures of the Inquiry Commission
2.1. Formation of the Inquiry Commission:

The Government of Nepal formed this Commission under Section 3(2) of the Inquiry Commission Act, 2026, to investigate the physical and human losses during the Gen-Z protests for national interest, good governance, and anti-corruption. The formation was formally notified in the Nepal Gazette (Part 5, Vol 75, Extra Issue 28) on 2082/06/05.
2.2. Composition of the Inquiry Commission:

content.content: Mr. Gauri Bahadur Karki, Former Chairperson of the Special Court - Chairperson.
Mr. Bigyan Raj Sharma, Former Additional Inspector General of Police - Member.
Mr. Bishweshwar Prasad Bhandari, Advocate and International Crime Investigation Expert - Member.

content: 2.3. Jurisdiction and Responsibilities of the Commission:

content.content: To investigate the actual physical and human losses during the protests in Kathmandu and other parts of the country on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082.
To inquire into the causes of the incidents and submit a report with definitive opinions and recommendations.
To receive and analyze information or petitions related to physical and human losses and provide necessary opinions regarding action.
To present measures and suggestions to prevent such incidents from recurring in the future.
To present a clear action plan for the implementation of the recommendations mentioned in the report.

content: 2.4. Working Mechanism of the Inquiry Commission:

content.content: The Commission shall perform its duties in accordance with the Inquiry Commission Act, 2026.
The Commission will operate independently and impartially, free from any outside pressure or influence.

content: 2.5. Duration and Human Resources of the Inquiry Commission:

content.content: The Commission shall submit its report along with recommendations to the Government of Nepal within 3 months from the date of commencement.

The Ministry of Home Affairs shall provide the necessary staff for the Commission's office, including: 1 Joint Secretary, 1 Under Secretary, 1 Section Officer, 1 Nayab Subba, 1 Computer Operator, 1 Light Vehicle Driver, and 1 Office Assistant.
Following the Council of Ministers' decision on 2082/07/25, additional staff were approved; currently, 15 personnel from the civil service and Nepal Police are working at the Commission.

Initially set for three months, the Commission's term was extended due to workload and time constraints: first for one month on 2082/09/03, then for twenty days on 2082/10/08, and finally for twenty-five days on 2082/10/26.

content: 2.6. Commission Procedures:

As per Section 8 of the Inquiry Commission Act, 2026, which allows the Commission to determine its own procedures subject to the Act and its rules, the Commission made necessary internal decisions to facilitate its operations and report preparation.
Chapter 3: Methods Used for Fact and Data Collection

To fulfill the designated mandate, the Commission leadership conducted interactions in the Kathmandu Valley and heavily damaged districts including Kaski, Chitwan, Kailali, Kanchanpur, Surkhet, Jhapa, Bara, Parsa, Sunsari, and Rupandehi. Discussions were held with human rights experts, National Human Rights Commission members, civil society representatives, media personnel, and legal scholars regarding human rights violations by both the state and protesters. Political parties and individuals who suffered private property damage also provided information. Within the legal limits of the Inquiry Commission Act, records from various public agencies were analyzed. Following the principles of natural justice, statements were taken from accountable government officials and relevant individuals. The specific methods used for data collection are detailed below.
3.1. On-Site Observation and Inspection Method:

The Commission leadership personally visited districts most affected by the protests of Bhadra 23 and 24, including Makwanpur, Bara, Parsa, Surkhet, Kailali, Kanchanpur, Kaski, Chitwan, Rupandehi, Banke, Jhapa, Morang, and Sunsari. This method was crucial for collecting primary data on the causes of incidents and the extent of the damage, supplemented by inquiries with local residents.
3.2. Questionnaire Method (Statements, Inquiries, and Incident Records):

Data was collected from the families of the deceased using standardized forms. Semi-structured questions were used for the injured to gather objective information. Responsible officials were summoned to the Commission office for structured and semi-structured questioning. Cross-questions and follow-up questions were utilized to ensure accuracy. Due to the high number of incidents, a Sampling Method was used to select a 'Responsible Person List,' while Snowball Sampling was employed to identify further individuals for questioning based on initial statements.
3.3. Discussion Method (Consultation with Stakeholders and Experts):

content.content: The Commission interacted with various stakeholders and target groups in different locations to gather specific data regarding the incidents.
To enhance the report's effectiveness, opinions and data were sought from specialists in security, public administration, law, and media.

content: 3.4. Interview Method (Statements and Incident Details):

Both formal and informal interviews were conducted with individuals and institutional representatives directly or indirectly linked to the events, carried out either at the Commission’s office or the respective institutions.
3.5. Evidence Collection (Audio, Video, CCTV, CDR, and BTS):

The Commission collected audio and video materials through correspondence with various agencies and public notices. These materials, along with content broadcasted in newspapers and media, were subjected to technical study and analysis to determine the reality of the events. This data served as secondary evidence for the investigation.
3.6. Testing and Evaluation of Collected Evidence:
3.7. Public Call for Information Collection:

Public notices and correspondence were issued by the Commission, the Ministry of Home Affairs, and other agencies to collect damage details and suggestions. The aim was to obtain factual data and actual loss details as primary data, which was then analyzed to prepare objective information for this report.
3.8. Data Collection Through Correspondence:

Following its formation, the Commission requested details regarding the damage and causes of the protest from various governmental and non-governmental bodies. The responses received have been utilized as factual statistics in this report.
3.9. Interactions (Eyewitnesses, Victims, and Relatives of the Deceased):

To understand the events, interactions and discussions were held with the injured, relatives of the deceased, and protest leaders at hospitals, their residences, and the Commission's office. This information was analyzed to form the basis of the report.
3.10. Limitations of the Commission:
The Commission commenced its work on 2082/06/09. During the investigation, the following limitations were encountered:

content.content: It was not feasible to conduct on-site visits to every district to collect details of physical damage across the country, compounded by a lack of technical manpower. Consequently, the detailed study conducted by the National Planning Commission has been accepted as the definitive record of damages.
Due to the widespread nature of the incidents on Bhadra 24, there was insufficient time, manpower, and resources to conduct a microscopic and comprehensive analysis of every event nationwide.
While various digital materials were obtained (CCTV, civilian videos, social media downloads) via USB drives, email, and messaging apps, their utility was limited by technical constraints.
Primary data could not always be retrieved as storage devices were often missing. The Commission had to rely heavily on secondary and open-source data.
Files received via social media and email often lacked original metadata, leading to a potential loss of crucial contextual information.
The Commission lacked the technical capability to verify with absolute certainty whether videos from social media were altered using AI tools, Deepfakes, or other editing processes.

content: Furthermore, details regarding the international standards of the Berkeley Protocol on digital evidence and the Commission's specific limitations in this regard are included in Annex 9.

content: 4.1.2 Details of Persons Injured During the Demonstrations of Bhadra 23 and 24

Details of a total of 2,522 persons injured during the demonstrations on Bhadra 23 and 24 had been received as of 2082/10/29 (Source: Ministry of Home Affairs). The detailed information of injured persons has been attached in Annex 4.
4.2. Physical Damage

One of the main mandates of this Commission was to ascertain and analyze the details of damage caused during the movement by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 and 24. Accordingly, a public notice was issued specifying a format for requesting damage details from across the country. Interaction programs were conducted under the leadership of the Commission's chairperson and members in the most affected districts, and damage details were also collected. In addition, separate correspondence was made with various agencies to obtain accurate and factual damage details.
According to the details received by this Commission, the total physical damage amounts to approximately NPR 41 billion 89 lakh 18 thousand 709 only.

Regarding the verification of physical damage caused during the demonstrations on Bhadra 23 and 24, a committee was formed under the convenorship of the Secretary of the National Planning Commission as per the Government of Nepal's decision dated 2082/06/04 to prepare a damage assessment and public infrastructure reconstruction plan. That committee conducted on-site inspections, observations, analyses and damage verification in all districts and affected locations, and submitted its report to the Government of Nepal on 2082/08/24. The estimated damage amount therein stands at NPR 84 billion 45 crore 77 lakh.

Due to the nature of the Commission's mandate and working modality, it was not possible to physically visit all districts across the country to collect damage data. Due to shortage of personnel and time, as well as the failure of contacted agencies to provide information in the prescribed format within the stipulated deadline, a complete damage assessment could not be made. Therefore, the details mentioned in this report regarding damage caused during the demonstrations, as well as the National Planning Commission's report, must both be taken as reference. The detailed breakdown of the said damage has been attached in Annex 4.
Chapter - 5: Statements and Incident Record Documents Received from Stakeholders
Recommendations:
5.1 Statement and Incident Record Documents from Officials in Public Responsibility:
5.1.1 Political Figures
1 (a). Statement of Dr. Deepak Karki, Secretary of the President's Office, on behalf of Honorable President Ram Chandra Paudel:

The statement indicates that had the issues arising in the areas of state administration and management regarding the demonstrations and damage that occurred on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082 been addressed in a timely and appropriate manner, the damage might perhaps have been less. Information was being received that establishments including Singha Durbar, the Supreme Court, Parliament House, as well as Nepal Police units were being targeted. The Nepal Army had briefed the President on security matters. A letter regarding the then Prime Minister KP Sharma Oli's resignation arrived at approximately 2:45 PM for approval, and after the Prime Minister's resignation was accepted at around 2:50 PM, at approximately 3:00 PM we moved from the President's residence to a secure location provided by the Nepal Army. Subsequently, information arrived that arson and vandalism had occurred at the physical assets, government records, and premises of the President's Office.

Regarding the question of what your view is on the questions raised by the Gen Z generation about the increase in corruption and financial irregularities in the country after Nepal's new constitution and federalism, the statement indicates that Nepal's constitution and the governance arrangements therein provide strong constitutional provisions for Nepal's independence, sovereignty, territorial integrity, national unity, self-reliance, self-respect, democratic value systems, socio-cultural solidarity, proportional participatory arrangements, protection of human rights, and democratic governance  therefore, the country's problems are not caused by the constitution. It may be that the responsible and authorized individuals and institutions failed to timely manage the questions raised about officials in executive roles of the state and to promptly correct the sectoral shortcomings visible in the country.

According to Nepal's constitution, the President serves as the Head of State, promoter of national unity, and guardian and protector of the constitution. Functions performed by the President are governed by Nepal's constitution and federal law. As per constitutional provisions, the President regularly receives information from the Prime Minister regarding current affairs in the country, matters of foreign relations, decisions of the Government of Nepal, and bills presented in the Federal Parliament. Except for informal discussions on specific matters, all functions performed by the President are carried out in accordance with constitutional and legal provisions.

Regarding the events of Bhadra 23, the statement says that continuously receiving information about what was happening, and drawing the attention of responsible government officials to take initiatives for the protection of life and immediate normalization of the situation, was carried out in accordance with constitutional responsibility. The events of Bhadra 24  including casualties, vandalism, looting, and arson  are unfortunate. One can imagine that perhaps less damage would have occurred that day had a political solution been reached in time; however, as the circumstances were highly complex, abnormal, and extraordinary in nature, the difficulties in immediate management by the security forces and other responsible state institutions were clearly evident. As it was necessary to immediately address the extreme and extraordinary situation that had arisen in the country in accordance with the spirit and intent of constitutional provisions, and to normalize the severely strained security situation, efforts were made toward that end. Given the extremely complex and unfortunate circumstances, continuous consultation and efforts were made throughout that day to normalize the security situation.
1. Statement of Former Prime Minister KP Sharma Oli:

After the Tinkune incident in Kathmandu in Chaitra 2082, instructions were given to all security agencies to minimize casualties and prevent infiltration by unwanted elements in the context of such incidents. Since the Home Minister serves as the chairperson of the Central Security Committee, and security sensitivities and related information are with the Home Minister, the then Home Minister was asked to appear before the Commission for answers. Many matters must have come to light through the Home Minister's responses. In fact, the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 were inconceivable and barbaric  the likes of which had never occurred in the country before.

Regarding immediate and rapidly unfolding events during the demonstrations by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23, these are not matters to be directly reported to the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister does not issue orders to the police  the Ministry of Home Affairs serves as the relevant line ministry for that. Logically, the Prime Minister is not accountable for this matter. The unruly acts of attacking security personnel themselves, snatching uniforms and guns from security personnel and taking selfies  these were certainly not carried out by the Gen Z members who had declared the movement, but were the acts of infiltrator groups taking advantage of the movement. The Gen Z members themselves have stated that their demands were hijacked, that their intent was not this, and that it was a peaceful movement.

The attempt to set Parliament on fire occurred on the 23rd itself. On that day, groups using youth as a vanguard to achieve their own objectives deliberately created agitation among the youth, leading to a situation where lives were lost. The arson and attacks on all three branches of government were premeditated events. The Parliament House  the seat of representation of the sovereign people  the Singha Durbar  the administrative center of the country  and the Supreme Court  the seat of justice  were all deliberately, selectively targeted, with files examined and data searched before being burned. Videos of those involved have been made public. Have their statements been taken by the Commission?

Is burning industries not burning the country's economy? There were attacks on the social fabric, attacks on the economic order, and actions aimed at destroying our culture and reducing the country to rubble. Can investment be promoted this way? The Prime Minister does not have the authority to order police to open fire during events like those of Bhadra 23. Nor should that be said. Technical questions like these must have been answered by the concerned individuals. Following the events of Bhadra 23, a cabinet meeting was held that evening and decisions were made: to form a judicial commission for impartial investigation of the events, to provide effective treatment to the injured and relief to the families of the deceased, and to lift the ban on social media imposed as per the demands of the Gen Z generation. On Bhadra 24, I submitted my resignation. If that movement occurred because I was an obstacle, then everything should have been fine after my resignation  but it wasn't, was it?

Regarding the regulation of social media  the government made a decision, and social media regulation is necessary! Should one not be accountable? Of course one should! That is precisely why the decision was made. The state officials in positions of responsibility at that time have already given their statements before the Commission on the matter, and I have repeatedly shared my views publicly on this matter.
2. Statement of Former Prime Minister Sher Bahadur Deuba:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was at my private residence located at Basukinath Marg, Ward No. 2, Budhanilkantha. That evening, I was also present at Baluwatar and had requested the then Prime Minister to lift the ban on social media, which he did  however, I did not participate in any formal meeting. On the morning of Bhadra 24, at around 10 AM, demonstrators gathered at my private residence and were chanting slogans. Despite security personnel trying to stop them, we were physically assaulted, and some Gen Z youth came to our rescue. We reached a safe location at around 10:00 PM.

The panchanama conducted by Nepal Police in the presence of the Ward Chairperson mentions cash found at my residence  the amount was not large. The social media details must be AI-generated. No report had been filed at any police office in this regard. This is a complete conspiracy, but it is not known who did it. Nepal Police has recorded the panchanama and other details regarding the vandalism, arson, and looting at my residence, and I have no information about any action taken. I have not made any political interference or shown bias, and I do not agree that there was political interference in the police's failure to assess the situation during the events of the 23rd and 24th. Trade union rights are fundamental rights in democracy and should be able to be exercised for practical rights and interests. Student organizations should also be active for broader interests. Non-political institutions or mechanisms in the country must not be politicized. Anyone involved in vandalism, looting, or arson must be held accountable. Policy, legal, and institutional reforms must be made incorporating the sentiments of the youth.
3. Statement of Former Prime Minister Pushpa Kamal Dahal 'Prachanda':

On Bhadra 23, I publicly condemned the crackdown, made a public appeal to declare the fallen demonstrators as martyrs and to restore calm, and called for an all-party meeting the following day. On Bhadra 24, I participated in the all-party meeting at Singha Durbar in the morning, and as the situation became increasingly complex, I remained under the security forces' protection in the afternoon. In my understanding, the backdrop to this movement was the growing public dissatisfaction and anger toward the government's working style. The ban imposed on social media appears to have been the immediate trigger. On Bhadra 23, the youth's anger was expressed in the form of protests, while on the 24th, I have reached the conclusion that the nature of the movement changed and was exploited by wrong elements.

On Bhadra 24, the rented residence where I had been staying in Kathmandu and my house in Chitwan were vandalized, set on fire, and looted. I do not have my own house in Kathmandu. The claim that illegal cash was found at my daughter Ganga Dahal's house is baseless. No such cash was found, nor have we earned income from any illegal source. In my view, the vandalism, arson, and looting at Singha Durbar, Shital Niwas, Parliament House, the Supreme Court, and various government and private buildings on Bhadra 24 were not merely an expression of anger against the government, but a premeditated attempt to strike at the system itself. While my party supported the spirit of the Gen Z movement, it has no involvement in violent activities. If any person affiliated with our party is proven to have been involved in activities contrary to the law, our clear position is that they must face legal consequences. The possibility of both internal and external infiltration is visible in the events of Bhadra 24. The youth themselves had, on Bhadra 23, urged people to return home citing infiltration of the movement. The subsequent chain of events indicates that wrong elements exploited the sentiments of the youth. During my tenure as Prime Minister, I made efforts to prioritize meritocracy in appointment, transfer, and promotion processes. The practices adopted in institutions such as Tribhuvan University have been exemplary. Political interference has been negatively impacting the professionalism and service delivery of the police, civil service, judicial service, and constitutional bodies  though this is not the only cause: the political system, economic structure, and geopolitical positioning are also responsible factors. Various efforts and programs for good governance, social justice, and economic reform were advanced under my leadership. I believe that an objective evaluation of those policies, programs, and budgets will enable understanding of the reasons for the government's fall and the reality of the public anger that arose thereafter. Trade union rights are constitutionally guaranteed rights, but the state machinery must not be used as a political platform. Appointments and promotions in the civil service and other agencies must be based on merit, competence, and experience. Student organizations must play a role in educational reform  not in disrupting education or engaging in indecent activities. To prevent such events from recurring in the future, people-centered programs for economic, social, cultural, and physical development must be implemented. In particular, policies ensuring youth-centered opportunities, employment, and participation must be adopted. I expect the Commission to conduct its evaluation objectively without any preconceptions.
4. Statement of Speaker of the Federal Parliament House of Representatives, Dev Raj Ghimire:

I have been serving as Speaker of the Federal Parliament House of Representatives since Magh 5, 2079. My primary duties, as stipulated by the Federal Parliament Secretariat Act, 2064 and the House of Representatives Rules, 2079, include conducting parliamentary sessions, determining the agenda, authenticating bills, and providing guidance to the Parliament Secretariat.

On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was busy at the Federal Parliament Secretariat, Singha Durbar, with the oath-taking of the chairperson of the Parliamentary Hearing Committee and the authentication of bills. On Bhadra 24, I was at my residence in Baluwatar. The security of the Federal Parliament building is the responsibility of the security forces under the Ministry of Home Affairs  the Speaker has no legal authority in this matter. Although general discussions take place before the commencement of a session, no one had anticipated that the Gen Z movement would pose a risk of this magnitude, and I had received no special information either. Therefore, no Contingency Plan, Evacuation Protocol, or written emergency plan had been prepared for such a situation.

In my understanding, the reasons why some demonstrators managed to enter the Parliament building premises on Bhadra 23 were: the protesters turned out in far greater numbers and in a different form than security personnel had anticipated, security preparations were insufficient, and there was no effective coordination among the security agencies. The structural weaknesses in the Parliament building's walls, gates, and infrastructure also appear to have compounded the security challenges. However, since the responsibility for such structural improvements and security infrastructure falls under the executive and the Ministry of Home Affairs, no special initiative had previously been taken by me in my capacity as Speaker. I received information in the afternoon of Bhadra 23 that gunshots had been fired within the Parliament building premises. Thereafter, I directed the Secretary-General of the Parliament Secretariat to coordinate with the security agencies as necessary. Regarding the vandalism and arson at the Parliament building on Bhadra 24, there are no reports of major casualties  only some minor injuries.

Apart from questions raised regarding the principles and proportionality of the use of force, my assessment is that the security personnel made every possible effort to protect state property, the Parliament building, and the lives and property of those present there, even under dangerous circumstances. The principles of use of force, the necessity and justification of firing, and similar matters are issues that must be evaluated by the relevant experts and agencies based on security policy, law, and the circumstances prevailing at the time  in my view. These matters do not fall within the direct jurisdiction of the Speaker. There is no legal provision enabling the Speaker to directly order the Prime Minister, Home Minister, or security agencies to halt the use of firearms. To prevent such events from recurring in the future, my suggestion is that a system based on a Single Command structure should be established for the security of the Federal Parliament building, with clear protocols based on periodic Threat Analysis and deployment of the required number of security personnel.
5. Statement of Narayan Dahal, Chairperson of the National Assembly of the Federal Parliament:

I have been serving in the capacity of Chairperson of the National Assembly since Falgun 29, 2080. Under the Constitution and the National Assembly Rules, 2075 (with amendments), my responsibilities include conducting assembly sessions, ensuring the sessions are dignified and orderly, and systematically advancing the agenda and procedures within the assembly. The overall security of the Federal Parliament building and secretariat is a responsibility of the Government of Nepal. On Bhadra 23, 2082, in the afternoon, I was in my own office chamber in Building No. 2 of the Federal Parliament Secretariat at Singha Durbar, preparing for a National Assembly session called for 1:15 PM that day. As the situation was not conducive, I issued a notice through the Secretary-General of the Federal Parliament and the Secretary of the National Assembly to convene the session at 3:15 PM, and as circumstances became increasingly complex, I directed that the public be informed that the session was postponed until further notice. On Bhadra 24, I remained at my residence until approximately 1:30 PM, after which I went toward the Police Headquarters at Naxal at around 2:30 PM. Previously, there was no practice of formal prior discussions or directives regarding demonstrations centered around the Federal Parliament building. The details of which security agencies were deployed, under whose command, and in what numbers during the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 fall within the government's area of responsibility, so I did not have detailed information on that. Since the Parliament building is a sensitive area declared a prohibited zone by the Government of Nepal, such a situation had not even been imagined. Overall security management falls within the policy and implementation responsibilities of the government. I was under the belief that coordination was ongoing, as the Parliament Security Force is a unit under the Ministry of Home Affairs. My understanding is that the security personnel must have followed directives received through their chain of command. Learning from the events of the 23rd and 24th, I have felt the need for a separate legal arrangement for the security of the Federal Parliament. On Bhadra 23, I was not at the Parliament building in Baneswhar but rather in my own office chamber at Singha Durbar. On Bhadra 24, I received information from the security agencies that demonstrators had entered the Parliament building and engaged in vandalism and arson. After adverse circumstances developed on Bhadra 24, I remained within the security cordon along with family members. Although no vandalism occurred at my official or private residence in Kathmandu, my private residence in Chitwan was set on fire. Maintaining peace and public order falls within the jurisdiction of the executive. Legal and structural reforms are necessary to prevent such events from recurring in the future.
6. Statement of Then Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak:

On Bhadra 23 and 24, regarding the difficult security situation that emerged in various parts of the country  particularly the Kathmandu Valley  I, in my capacity as Home Minister, bore the constitutional and legal responsibility to maintain overall peace and security. Before the events began, I had been receiving information through security agencies about growing dissatisfaction and anger on social media and through other channels, and on that basis, the Ministry of Home Affairs was continuously coordinating with Nepal Police, the Armed Police Force, and the National Investigation Department to monitor the situation. I clarify that I did not issue direct commands in the field, but rather discharged my responsibility of providing policy directives, coordination, and necessary facilitation at the political level. The Government of Nepal's Work Division Rules assign the Ministry of Home Affairs the tasks of policy, standards, law, plan implementation, and regulation related to internal security and law and order, and the execution thereof has been done through agencies under the ministry in accordance with relevant laws. The reporting and directives between the Home Minister, the ministry's administrative body, and the police administration also operated as per the relevant laws, with direct contact occasionally occurring as necessary. Since the proposal to take a decision regarding social media at the Council of Ministers did not come from the Ministry of Home Affairs, there was no separate process of seeking the security agencies' opinions on the matter. On the evening of Bhadra 22, a meeting of the National Security Council was held at Baluwatar, where information was provided by all security agencies  including the National Investigation Department and Nepal Police  that Gen Z youth would demonstrate peacefully on Bhadra 23, and no concrete intelligence of potential violence had been received. In my capacity as Home Minister, in the context of the Bhadra 23 demonstrations, I had issued directives to the Home Secretary and the heads of security agencies to ensure no casualties occurred during the use of force, to ensure no one died, to make appropriate coordinated peace and security arrangements, and to remain alert to the risk that the movement could be leaderless since it was not led by a political group, and to be vigilant about potential infiltration.

On Bhadra 23, as clashes between demonstrators and police were occurring inside and outside the Parliament building premises, my position was that the Federal Parliament building had to be secured at all costs  but I had not issued any order for the use of lethal force, i.e., to open fire. The use of force is a matter to be decided by deployed officers based on ground-level conditions, and there is no practice of the Home Minister issuing direct orders on such matters. I have made clear in my statement that there is no legal provision or practical precedent for the Inspector General of Police to seek written or oral authorization from the Home Minister for the use of lethal force, and therefore this was not done. Upon receiving information that the demonstration was intensifying and turning violent toward Baneswhar, I informed the Honorable Prime Minister, the Honorable Defense Minister, and the Chief of Army Staff. Upon receiving news of casualties from gunfire, I immediately consulted with Ministry of Home Affairs officials and security agencies to arrange for citizen safety and to stop casualties, and directed administration to facilitate treatment for the injured. Due to the tragic and unimaginable events of Bhadra 23, I was deeply grieved and distressed, and out of the immense anguish I felt, I immediately took moral responsibility and resigned from the position of Home Minister. I have publicly stated that the demands raised by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 were fundamentally correct  but I firmly believe that the tragic events that occurred after infiltration of that noble spirit, and the attacks on the state's historic and invaluable structures, cannot be limited to being described merely as the result of emotions. The statements by Gen Z leaders saying 'our movement has been successful, it is being hijacked and turned violent, friends please return home' confirmed for me the fact that there had been infiltration of the demonstrations. I am firm in my position that the events of Bhadra 24 must be viewed separately from the demands of the youth, and that anyone involved in criminal activities must face legal consequences. Demonstrators entered my private homes in Naikap, Kathmandu, and Mahendranagar, Kanchanpur, and vandalized, burned, and looted them. The new Police Bill concerning Nepal Police and the Armed Police Force had been registered in Parliament and was under discussion in the State Affairs Committee. I have acknowledged with self-criticism aspects such as the weaknesses visible in the state's security system, the lack of intelligence collection and analysis, the weakness of the state's dialogue with the younger generation, and insufficient preparedness. I have pointed out the need to clarify legal reforms, capacity enhancement of security agencies, human rights-friendly crowd management, inter-agency coordination, use of technology, and political-administrative accountability for long-term peace and security assurance.
7. Statement of Then Communications Minister Prithivi Subba Gurung:

While serving as Minister of Communication and Information Technology, I considered the matter of social media to not merely be a technical question for the government, but a serious matter connected to legal, policy, and public interest concerns. The Government of Nepal had repeatedly published public notices requesting social media platforms to register with the ministry, designate contact points and contact persons within Nepal, and come under the government's regulatory framework. In the meantime, the Honorable Supreme Court had already issued a mandamus to regulate social media, and the 'Directive on Regulating the Use of Social Media, 2080' had also made registration and contact point designation mandatory. Therefore, the then government had proceeded with the social media regulation process to implement these legal and policy provisions, and accordingly, necessary steps were taken as per the Cabinet decision of 2082/05/09.

On Bhadra 23, I was in my ministerial office chamber at Singha Durbar and later participated in a meeting with the Honorable Prime Minister. The social media regulation bill had already been prepared before I became minister; it was sent to Parliament in my tenure after necessary revisions. Through that bill, provisions were sought to bring social media platforms under a licensing system, ensure registration, contact points, grievance hearings, content regulation, user protection, and legal accountability. Its primary objective was to regulate social media to be organized and accountable while controlling digital crime, personal defamation, misinformation, and misleading information. In my capacity as minister, I consulted with the ministry's secretary, joint secretary, expert leadership of the regulatory body, and security mechanisms within my contacts, and ultimately the Cabinet's decision was implemented. The decision to restrict social media was not taken out of personal impulse but was taken as a duty to comply with court orders, directive provisions, and Cabinet decisions. Platforms like Reddit and Discord were not separately identified and prioritized as high-risk. After the events of Bhadra 23, I participated in continuous discussions and consultations with the Home Minister and others while remaining at the Prime Minister's Office. After analyzing the chain of events, the process to reopen social media was initiated that very night as per the decision, and this involved the collective decision of the Prime Minister, Chief Secretary, and others.
8. Statement of Rabi Lamichhane:

Until Bhadra 23, 2082, conditions in the prison were normal. However, on Bhadra 24, at around 12:30 PM, prison wardens summoned me to the office, and representatives of the security agencies informed me that an abnormal situation had developed outside the prison, that a large crowd had gathered, and that it had become necessary to hand me over to family and party and send me outside. I refused to leave the prison without a court order. Nevertheless, after the prison administration repeatedly requested that I personally be able to calm the crowd, I agreed to address the crowd while remaining within the prison premises and asked for a microphone to be arranged. As the police were in a panicked state, the crowd pushed me and took me outside. Thereafter, I climbed to the balcony of a restaurant near the prison and addressed the crowd, appealing for peace, restraint, and protection of public property. The prison administration had apparently been preparing to give me a formal release letter, but before the letter was ready, the crowd took me out of the prison. I later received information that a letter had come from the prison, but I had no participation in signing it or in the writing process, so the prison administration itself must be accountable for that letter. In reality, I was handed over to the crowd under the direction of the prison administration itself and was forcibly sent outside even though I did not want to leave.

Clarifying that neither I nor my party had any involvement in the events of Bhadra 24, I and the Rastriya Swatantra Party have stated that we played no direct or indirect role in any violent activities, and a commitment has been expressed that if anyone affiliated with us is found to have been involved, they will face strict legal consequences. After leaving the prison, with the help of well-wishers, I reached a safe location via artist Ramkrishna Dhakal's vehicle, and immediately informed the chiefs of Nepal Police, the Armed Police Force, and the Nepal Army about my whereabouts. That same evening, I also informed the public through a video message that 'I am safe and will appear in court again.' On Bhadra 28, after receiving assurances of security, I returned to Nakkhu Prison. Learning from these events, I suggest adopting an open prison policy for prison reform, strengthening mental health and legal counseling systems for prisoners, and establishing a prisoner review mechanism.
9. Statement of Then Mayor of Kathmandu Metropolitan City, Balendra Shah 'Balen':

On Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, I was at the official residence in Lainchaur. I supported the peaceful rally on the 23rd but was not part of the movement. The Metropolitan Police Force distributed water at the request of businesses and restaurants. I learned through media in the evening that the situation was becoming tense on the 23rd. My expressions on social media helped calm the uncontrolled movement. I appealed through social media for restraint for the protection of lives and property. After the incidents, in coordination with the Metropolitan Police Force and other departments, immediate necessary assistance was provided to families of the injured and deceased. The events of the 24th were unfortunate and may be the result of excessive use of force and a terrorist-style approach by the government on the 23rd. On the 24th, KMC had dispatched fire trucks in a timely manner and the metropolitan's own fire trucks controlled structures within the metropolis. At the request of the Honorable President and the Army Chief, I played a role in calming the movement. The then government engaged in criminal and terrorist activities and the then government and participating parties must take responsibility for all the damage. The use of the word 'burn it down' was not used in a Facebook expression I gave as a metaphor in protest after the garbage truck of Kathmandu Metropolitan was unnecessarily stopped on its way to Sisdol.
10. Incident Account of Former Prime Minister Jhala Nath Khanal:

After the People's Movement of 062-063 in our country, the fact that we built a constitution through a Constituent Assembly is the greatest achievement in history  something that has happened in only 40-42 countries in the world so far. After the constitution was made, the first election was held, and approximately two parties obtained a two-thirds majority; the government they formed was unable to work for the transformation of the country and for the proper conduct of international relations. The land reform necessary for the country did not happen. Agriculture in this country was not modernized. Similarly, there was a need to take the country toward industrialization  nothing was done. Even the industries that existed  some of which had been established in Nepal with grants and assistance from socialist China and the formerly socialist Soviet Union as gifts  were sold off for almost nothing and consumed by certain elements, while new industries could not come to the country, resulting in employment failing to develop anywhere in the country and the economy becoming paralyzed, creating a situation where only middlemen held the economy's reins. Corruption spread throughout society, laws and regulations to control it could not be enacted, no improvement was made in education or health, and large numbers of the country's youth were forced to go abroad. After this kind of situation began to emerge, widespread dissatisfaction, protest, and anger among the people was natural. Contradictions were growing and governments were unable to address the discontent by taking it seriously. Exploiting this with great subtlety, some foreign powers worked on how to take control of the country and made preparations over a very long time  in the course of making those preparations, many institutions are involved. In America, a magazine called 'Grey Zone' has been published, which explains this chain of events in Nepal in detail and has leaked a secret report of all the events that occurred in Nepal. What kinds of institutions were behind the destruction of the 24th can also be learned from that. And the events of the 23rd and 24th are a living, vivid example of how foreign powers can exploit internally developing dissatisfaction among the Nepali people. I heard that on the 23rd, the Gen Z brothers and sisters were demonstrating and had placed three demands  end to corruption, establishment of good governance, and the opening of 26 social media platforms.

Watching on TV, they had initially gathered peacefully. But I was surprised to see boys and girls in uniforms also coming to the movement  though they were doing it peacefully. As the peaceful, growing movement proceeded, the moment students reached in front of Everest Hotel, those bearing 'TOB' inscriptions entered there and it became chaotic. After that, at Baneswhar Chowk, people gathered from all sides, and ruffians had taken over the crowd. Then they began advancing toward the Parliament building and entering it, started vandalism, and clashes erupted. After that, water cannons began firing, tear gas was released, and simultaneously gunfire began. Those were possibly rubber bullets  there was chaotic fleeing to dodge the bullets. The procession is in front, the police are also in front, something like clashes are occurring in between. But in the middle of a dense crowd, if someone is hit by a bullet in the middle  you can't see who fired  but after someone falls, they must have fallen because of a bullet. How did bullets fly there? But 6-7 days later, I got to read an interview with a Gen Z brother who said that right in front of him, a bullet hit one brother who fell and was taken away by friends, and then another was fired  that bullet, he says, was fired from a window of a distant tower. In that interview, he says the window was opened and bullets were fired and then the window was closed  which could be important evidence.

Even after hearing that police were burning leaders' houses on the 24th, we did not go anywhere. At around 11:30 AM, while we were sitting on the third floor of our house, a military van came downstairs saying they had come to rescue us. After being told we might be attacked and should leave, I said my son and wife were also there, but they pulled me away saying there was no time. Within 45 minutes, my house and car were burned; my family members went even above the third floor and cried and screamed but there was nobody to care. In the meantime, when I called my son Nirbikal, he said 'Father, they're burning us' and the phone went dead. My wife had lost consciousness. Her windpipe was burned all the way down. For two and a half months she had no voice. Since she had fallen face-down when she lost consciousness, the upper part did not burn. My wife's left hand was burned to the fourth degree; she was not even counted as injured by the so-called current government. Police brothers connected a ladder from the neighboring roof to the third floor of my house and, entering through it, after rescuing my son and one police brother, on the fifth attempt the police below finally found my wife lying there and carried her out. From there, she was placed on a ladder, pulled, and taken to another house. Even when trying to take her to the hospital in a neighbor's car, murderers who wouldn't even let her be saved  shouldn't this government arrest and take action against them? What reason was there to kill my wife? Having seen many difficulties during her treatment, we took her to Apollo for further treatment. She no longer has her hand  the left hand had to be amputated. The right hand, even without amputation, is not in a state to function. For forty years she lectured on physics. During all this time, what corruption, what crime, what did she do for any foreigner, and why was she subjected to this kind of attack?

The events of the 24th are the greatest catastrophe to have occurred in this country. Since 1950, for 104 years, the Ranas looted the entire population and built palaces  and the revolutionaries of 1950 did not throw even a single stone at those palaces. In 1990, a movement occurred  for 30 years the Panchayat rulers who looted the country and governed it as tyrants built palaces  not a single stone was thrown at them. Then in 2006-2007, a major movement occurred, and the Maoist struggle that began in 1996 continued for 10 years. During that time, there was vandalism and so on, but the movement of 2062-63  which we call a revolution  brought three important changes to the country: first, the end of feudalism in the economic sphere, second, the abolition of the monarchy in the political sphere and the establishment of a republic, and third, the making of the Nepali state into a democratic state. This was accomplished by political parties that had worked for a long time among the people for national transformation, and even as those changes came, not a single stone was thrown at anyone. On the 24th, Singha Durbar was destroyed, the Supreme Court was destroyed, important documents were selectively burned. Public institutions were burned, public buildings were burned, over 500 police buildings were burned, over 90,000 bullets were seized from the police themselves, thousands of SLR rifles were seized. And over 10,000 prisoners  among whom were those convicted of serious crimes  were released in broad daylight. They have not yet come into government hands and a large number are still outside. But during this destruction, why and how was it that not a single Royalist house, not a single house of Rastriya Prajatantra Party members, was touched  how saintly they must be. And nothing happened to anyone from the Rastriya Swatantra Party  they too must be very saintly.

And buildings of municipalities in all other areas were burned, but while the house of the Deputy Mayor of Kathmandu Metropolitan City was vandalized, the Mayor's house remained untouched  how fortunate a Mayor! How were specific selected ones protected? What was the reason the statue and library at Champadevi, built in the name of Comrade Pushpalal, had to be burned? Some individual leaders whose houses were also burned  why was that done? Alongside this, the application called Discord  built around 2014-15 for gaming  was first used for political purposes in Ukraine. Then it was used in the so-called 'color revolutions' in Arab countries during the Arab Spring, and then in various third-world countries for regime change. Some time ago it was done in Sri Lanka, then in Bangladesh, and finally in our Nepal. That app used children of specific private schools  not government ones. The Gen Z movement is not something for Nepalis to take pride in. Nepalis were misused and destroyed, but we cannot blame technology  it depends on how it is used.

The criminals must be arrested with evidence and facts, brought before the bar of justice, and given necessary punishment  only then will the Nepali people find peace. Otherwise, the series of such events that has begun in this country is the beginning of a terrible calamity. To stop it, everything must be traced to its roots and countermeasures must be adopted against it. If a comprehensive package cannot be built in every sector, this cannot be countered. Therefore, a team of scholars must be formed and we must build a counter package. The attacks on Sher Bahadur Deuba and Arju Deuba are products of that bad culture. If Sher Bahadur had made some mistakes in the course of his duties, I am not aware of them  but if he had, the law should give the punishment for that, not deliver it in a forcible and inhuman manner. Is the Gen Z movement about delivering punishment in a violent and inhumane way? This is the extreme of inhumanity.
11. Account of Rajya Laxmi Chitrakar (Wife of Former Prime Minister Jhala Nath Khanal):

On Bhadra 24, while stone-pelting was occurring at the house, we did not know who had come  we were sitting in a place where stones could not reach us. They must have come to kill us; I lost consciousness because I could not even scream. The moment the fire touched me, my hand burned immediately and I lost consciousness unable to breathe. It must have taken the police brothers about an hour to find me. According to eyewitnesses, a Nepal Police Sub-Inspector Santosh Rupakheti, Saroj Khadka, and Armed Police Force's Bimal Pant, along with other police personnel, searched for and found me. Even now my body burns and I cannot do my own work. My right hand does not move properly. How could someone, without the involvement of a person who knows where important documents and treaties are kept in a place like Singha Durbar, without knowing what is on a computer, smash that very computer, take the hard disk, and commit arson? How can the government hold elections and decide who wins while keeping criminals free? The government must be vigilant in bringing criminals to account. While the masterminds of the crime walk around freely, why has the government been unable to arrest them? Why were only his house and municipality spared? Everything else burns  why not his  once he becomes Prime Minister, everything will burn.
12. Incident Account of Former Member of Parliament Ram Narayan Bidari:

I am a legal professional and have been serving as a Senior Advocate for the past 43 years. On Bhadra 23, 2082, I had gone to a para-clinic in New Baneswhar for a blood test. I support the demands of the Gen Z movement but was not involved in it. After the blood test, I went directly to my private residence at Dhungead. Being aware of the events that had occurred throughout the day, I expressed my views through a Facebook status about the merciless killing of young students. I had written calling for respect for those who had become martyrs, compensation, formation of a judicial commission to investigate, and action against the guilty. Thereafter, exhausted after the day, I rested in my own room. On Bhadra 24, 2082, I watched the news early in the morning and was very worried. I discussed with my neighbors that the country was in trouble because the country's politics was not in a proper state. At approximately 10:30 AM, I ate food at home with all household members. Having decided to stay home since curfew had been imposed inside the Ring Road, while I was sitting in my room, voices came from the main gate of my house, and I saw some people had gathered below  they were chanting slogans. I went downstairs intending to have a dialogue with them, but family members stopped me from going among those people. The group of people continued chanting slogans nonstop. The content of the slogans was: 'KP, thief, leave the country; Home Minister, down with you; Prachanda's broker, down with you.' After that, I stayed inside the house without coming out. One person entered my house and, taking me by the hand, said 'No one will do anything to you, please go outside' and brought me out, while some other people headed toward rooms inside the house. The person who brought me out is a person with the surname Thapa who lives in Bal in the same locality. After I had come outside, other family members were also brought out and we were forced to go elsewhere for security reasons. Thereafter, my mobile phone, laptop, my wife's mobile phone, my son and daughter-in-law's laptop, my daughter's laptop, jewelry and cash in drawers, gold, silver, and other jewelry were looted and the house was also set on fire. My two-and-a-half-story house in my name and my wife Nanimaya Bidari's three-room house  both houses were set on fire and destroyed, leaving only ashes. Now I have no home anywhere in the country. In the compound of that house: 1 motorcycle, 1 electric scooter, 1 scooter with a government number plate, 1 Toyota Jeep in the name of former civil servant Rupchandra Bista, and 1 BYD car  totaling 5 vehicles  were all burned by those criminal individuals. This was not done by the Gen Z youth  the evidence for this can be clearly seen on social media including YouTube and TikTok. I have sent all those photos and videos by email and WhatsApp to the Thankot Police and to this Commission as well. I have also submitted a petition of this account to this Commission and to Thankot Police Circle, in which the faces of the criminals are clearly visible. I am deeply troubled by the fact that people came to my home in broad daylight to set it on fire, loot it, and stayed at the same location all day until the house was completely destroyed. Those criminals were confident that none of the state's agencies would arrive there while they happily chatted, laughed, and joked while setting a citizen's house on fire. This is the important matter that requires this Commission's attention. Property worth approximately NPR 50 million belonging to me was looted and burned  the details of which I have also mentioned in my petitions. I, an innocent citizen, have not worked in any branch of the state for four years and have never served in an executive position throughout my life. Through 42 years of legal practice, 50 years of hard work, my wife's profession, ancestral property, life insurance, Citizens Investment Trust  why and for what reasons was the property I and my family earned destroyed? This is a matter for state investigation. It is the state's duty and obligation to protect my property. If it could not do so, full compensation must be provided. I drafted the current constitution and the current government took oath and operates under this very constitution  this means the problem was not a mistake I made in drafting the constitution. I have only been critical of corrupt party leaders, corrupt individuals, fraudsters, and criminal leaders in the country. Was this crime committed against me because of these things I said? Otherwise I have no fault, nor does any member of my family. My sons and daughters have their own separate professions and businesses  why was such a great crime committed against them? Must the state not pay attention to this? May the inquiry commission's attention go to this matter.
