Destroying all three branches  the executive, judiciary, and legislature  along with the Head of State, and trying to destroy and kill even Nepal Police, is something that innocent young children (Gen Z) would never do. Patriots also do not engage in such activities. Therefore, it is the state's duty to keep the morale of security personnel high and to honor and compensate the police who became martyrs. No national servant would commit the merciless killing of innocent young children on Bhadra 23  may the Commission also pay attention to who did this and for what purpose. It is clear as daylight whose hand it was that some political parties operating in Nepal were not touched from top to bottom in any way, while other parties  including leaders and workers  were attacked, and the homes of ordinary citizens, politicians, or national servants who criticized like me were deliberately and selectively destroyed. Therefore, may the Commission's attention also go to this matter. Big leaders ruined the country and made the people miserable. The people were angry at them. Ordinary citizens like me were MPs four years ago and hold no position beyond that. I have always advocated for people-centered issues and opposed wrong things by those in power. From the age of 14 to this day, never having tolerated the wrong deeds of those in power, this has proved to be my misfortune today.
5.1.2. Statements and Incident Account Documents of Civil Servants:
13. Then Chief Secretary Ek Narayan Aryal:

At the informal meeting of Bhadra 22, 2082, conducting a security analysis of the demonstration called by the Gen Z generation for the following day, a plan was made to coordinate security arrangements so that no loss of lives or property would occur. On that same day while the demonstration was taking place, I was in my own office chamber at Singha Durbar. That night at around 10 PM, the 39th meeting of the National Security Council was held at Baluwatar under the chairmanship of the Prime Minister, where the heads of security agencies made oral presentations. The meeting received directives from the Prime Minister to coordinate security arrangements through the security agencies, and that political coordination would be done at the government level. We discussed the matter of effectively mobilizing the entire security apparatus through the Ministry of Home Affairs to minimize the impact of the events and ensure minimum loss of lives and property. At that meeting, no concrete intelligence information was presented indicating large-scale violence, arson, or major vandalism from social media platforms such as Discord and Reddit. Although I had personally heard that activity on social media had increased, a clear assessment that such a large-scale violent situation would arise was not received from the security agencies. Although it was estimated that a procession would go from Maitighar to Baneswhar, there had been no assessment that it would cause such enormous damage.

On the morning of Bhadra 24, at around 10 AM before the Prime Minister's resignation, the Home Secretary, Chief of Army Staff, and heads of all security agencies met at Baluwatar and informed him of the ground situation. After reports came that the situation was becoming increasingly serious following the Prime Minister's resignation and that the situation was beginning to go out of control, toward evening I, the Home Secretary, the Chief of Army Staff, the Inspector General of Police, the Inspector General of the Armed Police Force, and the Director General of the National Investigation Department collectively participated in a public appeal with the aim of making citizens feel the government's presence. The heads of security agencies also informally attempted to speak with some leaders of the agitators to convey the message that we can only exist if the nation exists. I, in coordination with the Chief of Army Staff, requested the Honorable President to quickly begin the government formation process. Prior to that, security arrangements had been made to prevent damage to sensitive agencies such as the Kathmandu District Police Office, foreign embassies, telecommunications, Nepal Electricity Authority, and electricity and water offices. Security arrangements were made for senior leaders and high-ranking individuals including serving ministers, and Pashupatinath Temple was saved from vandalism. Since the constitutional provision stipulates that the Honorable Prime Minister as chairperson of the Security Council must make the decision to impose a state of emergency and deploy the army, and since I was only an ex-officio member, I was not in a position to call a council meeting.

After the events of Bhadra 23 and 24, a virtual meeting was held on Bhadra 25 to coordinate the resumption of security agencies and government offices across the country including Singha Durbar. At that meeting, directives and information were provided regarding daily operations, security arrangements, lifeline services, and basic service management. I also directed the Home Secretary to hold a virtual meeting encompassing the Chief District Officers and District Security Chiefs of all 77 districts. On-site monitoring was also carried out at agencies that had been set on fire, including Singha Durbar, the Honorable Supreme Court, and Parliament House. Even in a situation where the formal presence of political parties and the government was weak, it was not easy to continue the airport operations, other basic services, and daily tasks  but all these were accomplished in coordination with the security agencies. Later, after Sushila Karki was appointed as Honorable Prime Minister and assumed office, work was also done on security meetings to manage peace and security, initial collection of damage data, and facilitation of the operations the government needed to undertake in the coming days.

The social media claims that I played a role in giving a letter from Nakkhu Prison and safely releasing Rabi Lamichhane, Chairperson of the Rastriya Swatantra Party, have no truth whatsoever. Such work does not fall within the responsibilities of the Chief Secretary  this is what I believe to be a conspiracy to personally defame me. The lack of coordination visible among security agencies cannot be blamed on any single institution alone. Even though the army should have been at the frontline after the curfew was imposed, the visible shortage of adequate equipment, barricades, and morale in the police is an indication of the weakness of overall security management. The state will not be strong and citizens will not feel security unless the security agencies are strong and motivated. My final conclusion is that administrative and policy reforms are indispensable to prevent such events from recurring. The need to keep civil servant and security personnel morale high, end political interference, ensure career development, provide adequate training, equipment, and social security guarantees, and emphasize the maintenance of good governance must be stressed. My understanding is that the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 occurred due to increasing public anger caused by factors such as weak service delivery, lack of public trust regarding corruption, politicization of the bureaucracy, and inadequate use of technology. Therefore, my firm conclusion is that if future governments adopt a transparent, accountable, and reform-oriented working style, only then will the foundation be laid to prevent such crises from recurring.
14. Pancha Prasad Pandey, Secretary-General of the Federal Parliament Secretariat:

I have been serving as Secretary of the House of Representatives since 2080 BS and as Secretary-General of the Federal Parliament since Magh of that same year. My main responsibilities as Secretary-General, in the capacity of administrative head of the secretariat, are to conduct structural and managerial work and to provide necessary directives, coordination, and monitoring to keep the proceedings of both houses and their committees running smoothly. Regarding security arrangements, I have not been given direct legal authority, but in my capacity as office chief, I have been playing a facilitative role through continuous coordination as needed with the Chief of Marshals Security, the Baneswhar Police Circle Chief, and the Inspector General of Police, along with relevant security agencies.

On Bhadra 23, since a National Assembly session had been called, I arrived at the Parliament building in New Baneswhar at 8 AM. On that day, assessing the then security situation due to the movement by the Gen Z generation, and after discussions with the National Assembly Chairperson and Secretary, a decision was made to postpone the session. In the afternoon, demonstrations outside the Parliament building became more aggressive, with stone-pelting and obstruction. The Parliament Secretariat was unable to go to Singha Durbar, and since there was no clear plan for what to do if demonstrators entered the Parliament building, I remained there until evening in an uncertain state where security personnel would stop them if they could and if they couldn't, whatever the protesters would do, they would do. It was only after the situation somewhat normalized at around 7:00 PM in the evening that I returned home.

On Bhadra 23, the situation around the Parliament building became progressively tense. Demonstrators broke through walls and barriers and entered inside, and security personnel immediately removed them. A shooting incident also occurred outside. Injured police and demonstrators received treatment inside the Parliament building from Dr. Pushpa Rijal, and some injured persons were sent to the hospital by ambulance. But after an ambulance was burned outside, the avenue for sending additional injured persons to the hospital was also virtually blocked. My feeling was that had the security personnel not tightened security arrangements outside, the Parliament building could have been burned on Bhadra 23 itself.

On Bhadra 24, since curfew had been imposed in Kathmandu and Lalitpur, I stayed home and was in continuous contact with Police Superintendent Bimal Basnet, Police Deputy Superintendent Hari Khatiwada, and secretariat staff. However, on that day, I felt that the security personnel appeared somewhat discouraged. I acknowledge the fact that there was no pre-planned Contingency Plan for minimizing damage to the Parliament building. The possibility that the Parliament building would fall into a 'high risk' category had not been spontaneously imagined beforehand, and since no serious Threat Label or advance warning came from security agencies, initiatives to strengthen infrastructure such as high walls, sturdy gates, boom barriers, and electric fences were also not undertaken. Additionally, the absence of formally organized structures such as ROE (Rules of Engagement) within the security agencies was also one aspect of the problem.

Although continuous coordination between security personnel and the Parliament Secretariat was occurring internally, the failure to identify the level of risk beforehand, the weakness visible in security on Bhadra 24, and the failure of access control within the parliamentary premises became the main causes of the events. The arson and vandalism of that day caused enormous damage to the Parliament Secretariat, parliamentary party offices, and the physical structures of the secretariat. 18 government and private vehicles were completely burned and destroyed, 7 sustained partial damage, 37 motorcycles/scooters were completely burned, furniture, office supplies, computers, audio-video equipment, ACs, CCTV cameras, and other materials were destroyed, and the parliamentary party offices of various parties were also damaged. However, the fact that there was no loss of human life  no deaths  in this incident can be taken as a positive aspect.

The primary responsibility for preventing the enormous damage caused by these days' demonstrations rests with the demonstrators, as the arson and vandalism were initiated by them. The second responsibility lies with the security personnel, who failed to adequately secure an extremely sensitive area. Third, the failure of civilian national servants to intervene in time by explaining the situation to the agitators is also visible as a weakness.

Since the Parliament building will be relocated inside the Singha Durbar premises in the future, my suggestion is that the government must integrate the parliament security system within the overall security plan of Singha Durbar itself. To enhance the level of security, a pre-estimation system, adequate equipment, strong infrastructure, clear Rules of Engagement, strengthening of inter-agency coordination, and regular risk assessment are essential. The events that occurred at the Parliament building have clearly shown the weaknesses of the security system, lack of preparedness, and deficiency in contingency planning. Although I played a coordinator and contact person role in the management of the incident, I accept with self-criticism that the level of risk could not be estimated beforehand. Although the physical damage was extremely extensive, the fact that there was no loss of human life can be taken as a positive aspect.
14. Then Home Secretary Gokarna Mani Duwadi:

Before the events, on the evening of Bhadra 22, the Central Security Committee meeting was held at the Ministry of Home Affairs in Singha Durbar. At that meeting, a briefing was received from the heads of security agencies and Chief District Officers that the younger generation had organized a demonstration and the organizers had stated it would be peaceful. On that occasion, although the ministry's policy was to prevent the demonstration from becoming provocative and to exercise restraint, no written decision had been made. I was not aware at that time about the digital platforms such as Reddit and Discord being used by the Gen Z youth generation. I have clarified that the management of situations that could arise from closing social media did not fall within the Home Ministry's area of operation, and that I had no information on that matter.

On the morning of Bhadra 23, I was present at a State Affairs and Good Governance Committee meeting of the House of Representatives at Singha Durbar. Reporting on movement and other activities was normally done by noting in the ministry's control, and in some situations, information also came from Chief District Officers and heads of security agencies. On that same day, at around 11:30-12:00 noon, after receiving information from the Director General of the National Investigation Department that demonstrators were breaking through the security cordon and entering the Parliament building, I left the meeting and reached the ministry. Since Section 6(a) of the Local Administration Act, 2028 grants the Chief District Officer the authority to impose curfew, I was informed by phone that the then Chief District Officer of Kathmandu had imposed curfew, and during the implementation of the curfew, directives were given to the security agencies to work in a restrained and legal manner. After the curfew order, since the Chief District Officer of Kathmandu requested the Nepal Army's assistance and sought coordination, I immediately called the Chief of Army Staff twice to request the necessary assistance and coordination. I received oral reports from the heads of security agencies that large numbers of demonstrators and security personnel had been injured during the demonstrations, and all those details were immediately communicated to the Home Minister.

In the situation where many casualties had occurred in the tragic events at the Parliament building premises, my attention and that of all security agency heads was focused on treatment and facilitation of the injured. On that day, reports were received of demonstrations across the country and incidents occurring in many places. At the security briefing held at the Prime Minister's residence at Baluwatar in the evening, information was received that the situation in the Parliament building area had come under control. I learned that tragic events had occurred during the demonstrations, but I did not have detailed information at that time about exactly who had been shot.

On the evening of Bhadra 23, a review of the events was conducted at Baluwatar. In that process, discussions were held on the Nepal Army and all security agencies effectively coordinating to address potential security challenges and make appropriate security arrangements for Vital Installations. As the Home Minister resigned from his position while the situation had not yet come under full control  in the difficult circumstances arising from the human casualties in the Bhadra 23 Gen Z movement  I began reporting security information to the Chief Secretary.

On Bhadra 24, 2082, at around 8 AM, I reached the Ministry of Home Affairs at Singha Durbar and then the Prime Minister's residence at Baluwatar. On that day, after receiving information from the heads of security agencies about arson, vandalism, and looting at private residences of party leaders, party offices, police offices, government offices, and commercial establishments in various parts of the country including the Kathmandu Valley, I reported to the Chief Secretary. The Chief Secretary said he would inform the then Prime Minister, but no concrete directives could be received on that matter.

Since the jurisdiction to issue, with a letter, the release of Rabi Lamichhane, Chairperson of the Rastriya Swatantra Party, who was in custody at Nakkhu Prison in Lalitpur, for security reasons does not rest with the Home Secretary, I have not given any order or directive to that effect. Similarly, on Bhadra 24, the orders to prepare a list of detainees from the communication sets of the Nepal Police Headquarters or the Valley Police Office, to release detainees if such a situation arose, to keep their weapons, communication sets, and vehicles safe, and to not use any force other than tear gas  I did not give such orders, and I had no knowledge of such matters. After the Prime Minister's resignation in the afternoon of Bhadra 24, since it appeared necessary for the heads of all security agencies to sit together in one place and coordinate in the difficult situation in the country for managing peace and security, we all gathered at the Army Headquarters in Bhadrakali and focused on coordination. In the same process, a public call was also made to resolve the problem through negotiation and dialogue.

Regarding the provision of relief and compensation to persons who died and were injured during the demonstrations of Bhadra 23 and 24, while I was still in office, a decision was made by the Council of Ministers on a proposal from the Ministry of Home Affairs. According to that decision, provisions were made to declare those who died during the demonstrations as martyrs, to provide NPR 1.5 million in relief to each deceased person's family, for the Ministry of Health to arrange free treatment for the injured, and for the Ministry of Urban Development to construct a memorial at an appropriate location. On those days, the Ministry of Home Affairs effectively coordinated and facilitated the management of the bodies of the deceased and the treatment of the injured. On the afternoon of that same day, while I was engaged in managing peace and security, I later learned that an unknown group had entered my private residence at Ward No. 2, Nagarjun Municipality, Kathmandu District, and vandalized, set fire to, and looted it.

Although the main causes of the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 included the government's control of social media and the demand to control corruption and maintain good governance, my view is that this must be seen in connection with the country's overall governance system, economic and social conditions, and the effectiveness of service delivery. To prevent such events from recurring in the future, I have emphasized: making public service delivery technology-friendly, quick, effective, and quality-oriented; controlling delays, hassles, and middlemen visible in service delivery; ensuring political appointments are based on merit, competence, and capability; creating an environment for employment and investment; making government performance transparent and accountable; completing development and construction works on time; and ensuring equal access to state resources and equitable distribution for all.

It appears necessary to equip agencies under the Ministry of Home Affairs with physical infrastructure, system development, and training to make them efficient and citizen-friendly. Since the District Administration Office is the leading and coordinating agency of the entire district, it must be developed as a model office. My understanding is that since the Chief District Officer must also perform quasi-judicial work and peace and security matters are serious and sensitive, personnel management as a Ministerial Cadre at the Ministry of Home Affairs and subordinate agencies is necessary. I have also pointed to the need for legal reforms to simplify service delivery, develop and use technology, and provide additional responsibility and authority while facilitating coordination among federal, provincial, and local levels.

Since security teams deployed in the field use force based on their analysis of the situation and circumstances, issuing direct orders such as 'Cease Fire' from the Home Secretary does not fall within my legal jurisdiction. I accept that since information was received that the Gen Z movement would be peaceful, it was not possible to anticipate that such a large-scale violence, arson, and vandalism would occur. Although inter-agency coordination mechanisms such as the Central Security Committee and District Security Committee exist among security agencies and have been in operation, my view is that how effective those mechanisms were in the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 is a matter for the Commission to analyze comprehensively. Technical aspects such as Parliament building perimeter, buffer zone, escape routes, safety corridors, layered defense, and zeroing naturally fall within the operational areas of security agencies deployed in the field and the District Security Committee, so it is natural that I would not have direct knowledge of those matters. Additionally, the criteria for distinguishing between demonstrators and bystander citizens, crowd control SOPs, and special training matters also fall within the technical responsibilities of the security agencies.

Overall, I interpret my role as that of a coordinating body that operates within legal boundaries. Since the use of force, deployment, and technical decisions are directed by the field and the law, I assert that I did not give any orders exceeding my authority, and I express my confidence that the overall truth and responsibility determination of the incident will emerge from the Commission's impartial analysis.
16. Mrs. Radhika Aryal, Secretary of the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology:

I have been working in the civil service for 21 years and have been serving as Secretary of the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology for the past 17 months. I appeared before the Inquiry Commission and presented my views regarding the demonstrations by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, and the decisions related to social media regulation in connection therewith. My statement is focused on clarifying the background of the regulatory decision, its legal basis, the implementation process, and the effects arising from it.

In my understanding, the root causes of those events were demands for raising voices against corruption, demanding good governance, dissatisfaction against Nepo-Kids/Nepo-Babies, and demands related to the smooth operation of social media. I experienced that the movement, which had initially been declared peaceful, suddenly turned violent, and the circumstances created by the tragic events of the first day made the situation of the second day even more complex.

I wish to clarify regarding the general understanding that a 'ban' was imposed on social media  no direct ban was imposed by the ministry. Only a regulatory decision was made, in accordance with the Directive on Regulating the Use of Social Media, 2080, and the Supreme Court's order, to compel social media platforms to register in Nepal and establish an official contact point. The provision was made to temporarily deactivate platforms that did not register within the stipulated 7 days, and to reactivate them the moment they registered. The legal basis for this decision was the Supreme Court's contempt of court order and the Council of Ministers' decision dated 2082/05/09.

Regarding Risk Assessment, since social media is directly connected to information, education, health, business, and employment in Nepal, discussions were held among the ministry, Nepal Telecommunications Authority, and other relevant agencies about the economic, social, and diplomatic impacts that could arise from it. However, no written security risk assessment or suggestion was received from the police, Ministry of Home Affairs, or other security agencies.

Public notices were repeatedly issued through national daily newspapers, the ministry's website, and other media to inform the youth and general public. Social media was not deactivated on the grounds that it was a medium for escalating violence: the attempt at regulation was made with the objective of controlling misuse through misinformation, cybercrime, and fake identity.

The reason why alternative measures such as Specific Content Removal, Flagging, or Algorithm-based Regulation could not be immediately implemented was precisely the absence of formal presence and responsible contact points of the concerned social media platforms in Nepal. My view is that only after registration and establishment of official contact points could such measures be effectively implemented.

I reject the allegation that freedom of expression under Article 17 of the Constitution was violated. Our objective was not to control expression, but to bring social media within the legal framework and ensure responsible and accurate information flow. The National Human Rights Commission was not given prior notice. The deactivation period was limited  only about four days. All social media was not completely shut down: since platforms like Viber and TikTok continued to operate, there was no direct disruption to citizen services.

For the future, I wish to point out the need to establish a clear legal basis for social media regulation, strengthen diplomatic dialogue with multinational platforms, encourage the development of domestic social media, and quickly pass the relevant bill registered in the Federal Parliament with necessary revisions. Overall, my clear position is that the action taken regarding social media was not a 'ban' but an attempt at regulation and management.
17. Joint Secretary Ananda Kafle, Head of the Security and Coordination Division, Ministry of Home Affairs:

I have been managing the responsibilities of three branches under the Security and Coordination Division  the Peace, Security and Crime Control Branch; the Local Administration Branch; and the Border and Immigration Administration Branch. In addition, I have also been serving as the spokesperson for the Ministry of Home Affairs. In accordance with my area of work, I have been organizing security-related meetings and coordinating with security agencies, inquiring about complaints received from anywhere regarding crime-related matters, and making necessary coordination regarding security agencies related to my division as directed by the Honorable Minister and Secretary. I myself do not bear the responsibility of giving direct orders to the security force. I have been informing the Honorable Minister and Secretary about information received through Chief District Officers and coordinating as necessary.

On Bhadra 23, I was present at the ministry and had coordinated with the heads of security agencies in meetings. Information received through the Chief District Officer was shared with the Secretary and the Minister. On that same day, after receiving information through the Chief District Officer of Kathmandu that curfew had been imposed and that arrangements needed to be made to dispatch the Nepal Army quickly, I immediately relayed that information to the Minister and the Secretary.

On Bhadra 24, I went to Baluwatar and coordinated with the Chief Secretary and heads of security agencies to prepare and publish a press release. On Bhadra 24, although demonstrators entered Singha Durbar and set fire to the Ministry of Home Affairs, most documents related to my division remained safe.

The responsibility for the human casualties caused when dozens of people were randomly shot at almost simultaneously on Bhadra 23 lies with responsible individuals of the then government, from commanders deployed in the field to police officers at various command levels  and when deciding to take immediate action against them, it is also necessary to pay attention to the potential impact this may have on security in the coming days.

My assessment is that there was a lack of adequate information and analysis regarding the security weaknesses related to the incident. Regarding the matter of attempts to release Rabi Lamichhane from Nakkhu Prison, I received a phone call from the jailer and I had informed the Secretary of that matter.

I have reached the conclusion that the government must invest in information collection, analysis, and resource management for administrative reform. My view is that institutional reform is necessary to improve relations with the younger generation  providing regular dialogue, technology-friendly services, and predictable services.

Finally, I have pointed out the need for the Ministry of Home Affairs to reduce the tendency to put unnecessary pressure on security agencies in the name of Civil Supremacy, to work with the country and people at the center, and to take necessary steps to prevent such movements from recurring in the coming days.
18. Statement of Then Chief District Officer of Kathmandu District, Joint Secretary Chhabilal Rijal:

I have been serving as Chief District Officer of Kathmandu District with thirty-six years of experience in the civil service. I have previously successfully served as Chief District Officer at District Administration Offices in Surkhet, Jhapa, and Tanahun.

On 2082/5/23, three joint applications were received at the office  one in the name of Sudan Gurung of an organization called 'Hami Nepal', one in the names of Ankit Malla and Khemraj Saud, and one in the names of Sabal Gautam and Purushottam Yadav  requesting permission to hold a peaceful demonstration in the Maitighar Mandala area. During discussions with those who came to submit the applications, oral agreement was reached that demonstrations would be held only in areas other than prohibited zones and that the organizers would conduct the program peacefully.

After the Gen Z movement was announced, through a District Security Committee (DSC) meeting, I directed security arrangements to be further strengthened with the objective of preventing a repeat of the security lapse that had occurred at Tinkune on the previous Chaitra 15, and that relevant agencies coordinate with their line agencies in a timely manner for additional troop deployment if necessary.

Based on information received from the police administration, intelligence apparatus, and district security agencies, the movement was understood as a program to express dissatisfaction regarding social media-related matters, and estimating that it would be peaceful in its initial phase, possible routes, timing, estimated number of participants, and sensitive areas were identified, the 2082 Integrated Special Security Plan was approved, and security personnel were deployed accordingly.

Due to the assumption that the capital would remain peaceful on Bhadra 23, a National Assembly session was scheduled to be held in the Federal Parliament building on that same day, and the Honorable President's attendance at the National Assembly Hall program had also been confirmed.

On 2082/5/22, I and the District Police Chief attended the Central Security Committee meeting held at the Ministry of Home Affairs at around 6:00 PM in the evening and gave a briefing on the demonstration. A suggestion was given to rescind the decision to close social media platforms not registered in Nepal. When the Inspector General of Nepal Police asked for a directive on the line of action  what to do if it becomes necessary to use force in tomorrow's demonstration  the directive received was: to use minimum force sequentially in accordance with the principle of force escalation to bring the situation under control, but if even that fails to bring the situation under control and there is unruly activity such as violation of prohibited zones, rioting, vandalism, and arson, then to carefully use necessary force to bring the situation under control.

From 7:00 AM on Bhadra 23, I had set up a District Security Committee command center in my own office chamber and was sitting together with officials monitoring the situation. The situation was normal until approximately 10:15 AM. After the peaceful demonstration that had started from Maitighar broke through the police barricade in front of Everest Hotel via Bijulibazar Bridge and moved forward, seeing that the situation could go out of control, I called the Home Secretary to inform him and received a directive from him to 'use minimum force sequentially to control the situation.'

After the demonstrators advanced toward the Federal Parliament building and it appeared that peace would be disrupted, I called the Honorable Home Minister, the Chief Secretary, and the Home Secretary to inform them that curfew needed to be imposed. After receiving directives from all of them to bring the situation under control even if curfew needed to be imposed, on the basis of the District Security Committee's recommendation, a curfew order was issued to come into effect from 12:30 PM.

After learning that tension was escalating in the New Baneswhar area, at around 12:15 PM I also sent Armed Police Superintendent Jeevan K.C. there with the objective of maintaining peace. At around 12:00 noon, I received information that demonstrators from all four directions around the Parliament building were committing vandalism and arson and entering through the southeast, east, and southwest-facing gates.

As violence spread and serious risk to citizens' lives, property, and public and private assets became apparent, it was necessary to issue a curfew order as a last resort. After discussion with the District Security Committee, police, armed police, and Nepal Army, and after a serious review of the situation, a curfew order was issued in phases with limited time and area in accordance with Section 6(a) of the Local Administration Act, 2028.

Even after the curfew was issued, I continuously directed security personnel not to lose patience, not to be discouraged by saying that additional security personnel including the army are coming, to use minimum force in dispersing crowds, to be mindful of children's rights and human rights, and not to lose composure.

Although I gave a verbal order to fire rubber bullets targeting below the knees, I had not given an order for the use of lethal force  i.e., to open direct fire. However, in that very tense situation, I heard through the police radio set  first the Inspector General of Police and then the Acting Chief of the Kathmandu Valley Police Office  give an order to 'open fire to bring the situation under control as necessary.'

Due to the extremely tense situation in the field, exact details of how many demonstrators were around the Parliament building, how much force was used, and how many were injured or killed could not be obtained in a timely manner. Reports were received that attackers armed with helmets and carrying stones, bricks, hammers, mallets, rods, chisels, and slingshots were advancing on security personnel.

When demonstrators tried to break through or climb over the walls to enter the Parliament building, I had received information from the District Police Chief and heads of other security agencies that gunfire occurred only inside the Parliament building premises  for the protection of parliamentarians, staff, and structures present there  and that no gunfire occurred outside elsewhere.

The Nepal Army's assistance was requested for effective implementation of the curfew, but due to obstruction by the demonstrators, the army could not reach the Parliament building in time. At around 2:15 PM, I received information that one demonstrator had died in front of the Parliament building.

According to security personnel, the demonstrators became aggressive and engaged in arson and vandalism from all four directions and attacked police. After the situation could not be brought under control even after using the principles of force  baton charge, tear gas, aerial fire, and rubber bullets  security personnel deployed according to the Special Security Force and Integrated Special Security Plan, with attention to the security of the Parliament building, parliamentarians and staff present there, Nepal Bank Limited, Nepal Law Commission, the Investment Board office, and a diesel reserve of approximately forty thousand liters, appear to have used force to immediately face the situation.

I directed that minimum force be used with composure in accordance with the Dual Objective Doctrine of protecting the Parliament building and also protecting the lives of the youth who had come out to demonstrate, and that only as a last resort were rubber bullets to be fired targeting below the knees.

After the events of Bhadra 23, 2082, a District Security Committee meeting was held at 10:30 PM that night, and with the objective of preventing the situation from deteriorating on the 24th, a decision was made to impose curfew from midnight 12:00 AM to 5:00 AM that morning, and security agencies were directed to exercise high vigilance to prevent looting, vandalism, and arson of public and private property.

On the morning of Bhadra 24, a meeting was again held at 7:30 AM and curfew was declared from 8:30 AM until further notice. On that same day, anger was directed at me on social media  rumors were spread that I had died, and threatening phone calls came saying my life would be taken  but I remained focused on work, thinking at least I might be able to protect the Vital Installations.

During discussions at the Ministry of Home Affairs, after receiving information that even Singha Durbar was not safe, at around 1:45 PM I went and stayed at the Nepal Army's Narasinghdal Gana. After the Prime Minister's resignation, the vacuum visible in the executive leadership of the state created further confusion and anger in the public, and despite the curfew, arson, looting, and attacks on government offices and private businesses occurred in various places.

Since the crowd was extremely large and the multi-centered attacks occurred almost simultaneously, it was not possible to provide effective security at all locations at once. The fatigue of security personnel, shortage of equipment, prolonged continuous duty, and the effect on morale also added to the challenges.

On Bhadra 24, since the Nepal Army did not timely take over prison duty, Nepal Police alone could not provide security, leading to the situation where 20 prisoners escaped from Dillibazar Prison and more than 3,500 prisoners escaped from the Central Prison in Sundhara.

Since the federal capital Kathmandu has constitutional bodies, offices and residences of very senior officials, an international airport, ministries, important structures, and even the headquarters of security agencies, in certain situations senior security officers and high-ranking officials' directives can be received directly by security personnel  which shows the reality that the Chief District Officer's role alone is not decisive in maintaining peace and security in the district.

Even while discharging all responsibilities of my official capacity with good intent and competence, I am deeply grieved by the news that circumstances beyond what the state apparatus had estimated arose, leading to the deaths of youth who participated in the demonstrations and dozens being injured. I did fulfill my role as a Chief District Officer, but it would not be just to hold me solely responsible on the grounds that circumstances arose beyond what the state apparatus had estimated  circumstances that required the head of government to resign and necessitated helicopter rescue to protect the lives of the Prime Minister and ministers.

Without deep study of who caused the circumstances in which citizens had no choice but to rebel, one cannot say that such a major event occurred only due to the weakness of one or two government officials  this is the result of the distortions, anomalies, injustice, and inequality that have existed in this country for a long time. I have also heard complaints from Nepal Police officers that they did not openly receive the expected cooperation from the Armed Police Force deployed in the field and other security personnel.

Finally, the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 must be taken as an abnormal, unexpected, and serious test for the state apparatus. Based on the information I received, legal authority, and assessment of the immediate circumstances, I made decisions prioritizing public interest, peace and security, and protection of citizens' lives and property. To prevent such events from recurring in the future, I recommend strengthening the early warning system, increasing crowd management training and equipment, strengthening inter-agency coordination, and maintaining political-administrative clarity.
19. Statement of Joint Secretary Lila Prasad Sharma, Director General of the Department of Prison Management:

Until the evening of Bhadra 23, according to regular reporting received from all prisons across the country, the situation was normal. However, on the afternoon of the 24th, after receiving information from Nakkhu Prison that the crowd around the prison was growing, that agitators were approaching, and that there were signs the prison could be targeted, I became alert. I had immediately coordinated with the Ministry of Home Affairs and the Chief District Officer of Lalitpur and given directives to strengthen security. However, amidst the widespread disorganized movement across the country, the excitement spreading through social media, and the limitations of the local security mechanism, a large crowd managed to enter Nakkhu Prison within just a few hours, rendering the department's security plans ineffective and pushing the situation out of control.

The attack, vandalism, and arson that began at Nakkhu Prison spread across the country within a few hours. The situation went out of control due to both the aggressive entry of outside groups and the spontaneous impulse of prisoners inside. Although the department was maintaining continuous contact with Chief District Officers, police, armed police, and the ministry and giving priority-based alertness directives, it appears that the nature and intensity of the movement put excessive pressure on local security personnel. Meanwhile, prisoners in various prisons began vandalism and destruction of records themselves, making the situation even more complex.

Within three days of the incident, similar nature events occurred in 34 prisons across the country. From 28 prisons, 14,554 prisoners and detainees escaped  of whom 13,585 were adults and 969 were juvenile offenders. In Nauwasta, Dhading, and Ramechhap, 10 prisoners died during security operations. In prisons including Nakkhu, Dillibazar, Makwanpur, Tanahun, and Kaski, arson occurred, vehicles were destroyed, records were burned, CCTV was completely demolished, office buildings were damaged, and prisoner housing blocks were also burned. Due to the burning of prison records, office machinery, and assets, the prison system across the country came to an immediate standstill.

Regarding the Rabi Lamichhane matter, which became a subject of public concern, I wish to clarify that the information received from Nakkhu Prison on the morning of the 24th about the risk from agitators was only for coordination between the department and the ministry. No order or signal to release him from prison was given at any stage by the department or ministry. All department activities were focused entirely on security strengthening. I only learned later that he had been taken out of the prison by the crowd when the crowd demolished the prison. Regarding the release letter that became public after the movement, the department only received information that the jailer had signed it under duress  that letter was not issued in any procedural, legal, or departmental capacity whatsoever.

Regarding questions raised about my role, I wish to clearly state that the department was continuously undertaking risk mitigation, security coordination, information flow, and directive work within the limits of available resources. I do not consider myself directly responsible for the damage; however, the structural weaknesses of the prison system and disorganized security management have been longstanding problems.

After the incident, the department immediately commenced rehabilitation work. Priority has been given to issuing public notices for escaped prisoners to return, monitoring through immigration, updating CRS records, preparing damage assessment and budget estimates, conducting a structural review of the security force, and forming search task forces in each district. Many prisoners have returned to prison, some have been arrested, but 4,841 prisoners and detainees are still at large. Since record management was weak, a decision has been made to make mandatory entry of three-generation details of all prisoners and to expand the information system.

Regarding the juvenile reform home, 5 deaths occurred at Nauwasta Reform Center. Since housing juvenile offenders above 18 years of age in the same location has been the primary challenge, a transfer program has been initiated in coordination between ministries. My conclusion is that commitments to prison reform are not possible without structural transformation. New laws and regulations, a modern record system, strong infrastructure, an emergency management plan, and an integrated command system in the security mechanism are indispensable. Seeking alternative measures without reforming the overall structure is not practical.

Finally, even as the events of Bhadra 24 expanded to a state of national anarchy beyond control, the department was continuously making coordination, directive, and rehabilitation efforts. I express my expectation that the Commission will identify the actual facts and provide guidance toward the necessary policy and structural reforms to make prison administration credible and capable in the future.
20. Statement of Saroj Kumar Sharma, Member Secretary of the Social Welfare Council:

Member Secretary of the Social Welfare Council, Saroj Kumar Sharma, in his statement presented before the Inquiry Commission regarding events related to the demonstrations called by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, has detailed the institutional situation of the council, the existing structure of NGO/INGO regulation, its weaknesses, practices, and policy challenges. He noted that he has been working as Member Secretary of the Social Welfare Council since Kartik 29, 2082, and provided information that currently more than approximately 58,000 national NGOs and 134 international NGOs are registered and affiliated with the council.

In his statement, he acknowledged that the council does not yet have a fully integrated national digital database of active NGOs/INGOs. He openly acknowledged the situation that despite clear provisions under Article 51 of the Constitution requiring adoption of a single-window policy, and legal provisions under various sections of the Social Welfare Act, 2049, preventing direct entry through ministries, those provisions have not been fully implemented in practice. He stated that although the council has been continuously working on this matter, the practice of various line ministries bypassing the council and operating projects through NGOs/INGOs continues.

Regarding monitoring and evaluation, information was provided that the council conducts project-based on-site monitoring through joint teams comprising thematic experts, financial experts, representatives of relevant ministries, and the council. He also pointed out the reality that although hundreds of projects were evaluated in the last fiscal year alone, the council does not have sufficient human resources, technical capacity, budget, and infrastructure.

On the question of financial transparency of NGOs/INGOs, he stated that in the case of international NGOs, almost one hundred percent audit and progress reports are received, while in the case of national NGOs, regular reports are received only from a limited number of organizations that receive foreign assistance. He stated that complete details of foreign funds entering without the council's approval and agreement do not come to the council's access. He stated that a situation exists where funds are being spent from various sources without the council's knowledge based on audit reports, indicating a serious gap in the regulatory system.

Although monitoring of expenditure at the local level is done through social auditing, public hearings, review meetings, information boards, websites, and the system of prior consent by local governments, it was clarified that exact data of NGOs/INGOs operating services by bypassing the council is not available with the council. It was acknowledged that in such a situation, the council also does not have adequate legal basis to take action. The statement reflects that monitoring of NGOs/INGOs involved in policy formulation, advocacy, and software-oriented activities is also done based on project proposals, objectives, and indicators, but the practice of mandatory sharing of surveys, mapping, or data collection with the council does not exist, and a definitive assessment of the risks arising from that could also not be made.

Regarding the entry of foreign experts, consultants, and expatriates, it was noted that although the council plays a role in the process of recommending work agreement, labor approval, and non-tourist visa, sensitive areas such as borders, security, energy, and essential infrastructure do not fall within the council's direct area of work. The statement mentions that the practice of monitoring expenditure at religious, community, and social institutions is also done through thematic experts during the course of project-based evaluation.

He has made a clear demand that the Social Welfare Council needs additional legal powers such as licensing, suspension, financial freeze, and investigation powers for effective regulation. He has pointed out as his conclusion that the non-implementation of the Social Welfare Act, 2049, is the biggest weakness of the current system. He stated that the influence of NGOs/INGOs appears most sensitive in the social sector, and that an overall assessment of the risks related to financial, policy, security, and political influence is still in process.
Finally, he stated that no concrete information has been received by the council about misuse of budget under the NGO/INGO software heading during the Gen Z movement of Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082.
21. Statement of Prison Administrator (Jailer), Under Secretary Satyaraj Joshi, of the Prison Office Nakkhu, Lalitpur:

On Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, I was regularly present at my office. On Bhadra 23, the situation was normal. On Bhadra 24, from early morning, provocative materials began appearing on social media and unauthorized surveillance around the prison began increasing, after which I informed the Chief District Officer, Ministry of Home Affairs, the Peace and Security Division, and the Department of Prison Management, and called an emergency security meeting. At that meeting, discussions were held on crowd control strategy, alternative evacuation plans, and overall security preparedness.

At approximately 1:40 PM, thousands of demonstrators chanting 'Release Rabi Lamichhane' began attacking the main gate of the prison with stone-pelting and vandalism. Seeing that the crowd situation was going out of control, I again made written and oral requests to the Chief District Officer and Ministry of Home Affairs to deploy the army, but additional security force could not be made available immediately. At around 1:50 PM, the crowd broke through the main gate, forcibly entered, and took the prison under its control.

At that time, there were 1,281 prisoners and detainees in the prison. After the crowd began attacking targeting Rabi Lamichhane, keeping the security of other prisoners in mind, I made a decision to temporarily transfer him to the psychosocial disabled prisoner hospital inside the prison. Later, he asked for a microphone saying 'I will try to convince the crowd.' Thereafter, security personnel sent him before the crowd, but the crowd took him out of the prison. I immediately informed the Ministry of Home Affairs and the Chief District Officer about that incident.

After he was taken outside, the crowd became even more agitated. The prison administration records room and police building were set on fire, and both police and armed police were forced to retreat. I and administrative staff were hiding inside the psychosocial disabled prisoner hospital building trying to save our lives. In the course of that, the crowd subjected me to extreme threats and terrorized me with fear of life and death, forcing me to sign an unknown letter. I had no prior knowledge of the contents of the letter. Since that signature was made under coercion and in a terrorizing situation, it has no legal validity whatsoever.

Thereafter, the crowd set fire to and vandalized prisoner blocks and released all 1,280 prisoners and detainees outside. A few days after the incident, 749 prisoners and detainees returned to prison, 70 were transferred to other prisons, and 459 are still at large. On that day, in terms of human casualties, only some police officers sustained minor injuries.

My assessment is that the prison was saved from a major massacre precisely because the security personnel working at the prison demonstrated high restraint, wisdom, and leadership. In my view, the events of Bhadra 24 were not administrative negligence but were an inevitable circumstance beyond control.
For long-term improvement of prison security, I have presented the following recommendations:

content.content: Immediately implement a Contingency Plan and Emergency Response Protocol.
Construct a High-Security Zone or Isolated Unit for sensitive detainees.
Establish a Real-time Coordination System among Nepal Police, Armed Police Force, Prison Administration, and Local Administration.
Improve the prison's physical structure, surveillance system, and emergency exit routes, and provide crisis management training to staff.
Update the Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for prison administration and implement it in all prisons.

content: 22. Account of Assistant Chief District Officer Muktiram Rijal, District Administration Office Kathmandu:

I recall that on Bhadra 22, 2082, Sudan Gurung, Purushottam Yadav, and Ankit Malla submitted applications at the District Administration Office Kathmandu requesting permission to hold a peaceful demonstration against the Government of Nepal's ban on certain social media platforms and related matters, and the applicants who came with these applications were informed by the office to demonstrate peacefully and that certain sensitive locations in Kathmandu had been designated as prohibited zones.

After receiving the said applications, a District Security Committee meeting was held on 2082/5/22 with detailed discussions on making the demonstration peaceful and protecting demonstrators and public property, and a decision was made for all security agencies to coordinately arrange flawless security to ensure the demonstration remained peaceful, and the 2082 Integrated Special Security Plan was also approved.

As directed by the Chief District Officer to report to the office at 7 AM the following day  Bhadra 23  I arrived at the office at 7 AM, a District Security Committee meeting was held reviewing the situation, and a decision was made to exercise full caution when using force for crowd control since students and children were expected to have significant participation in the demonstration.

Thereafter, District Security Committee officials remained at the District Administration Office and a Command Post was also established, and in the command center, another District Security Committee meeting decided to strengthen security management and to strengthen coordination among security agencies to prevent any kind of undesirable activity and arrange flawless security.

From around 10 AM, demonstrators began gathering in the Maitighar Mandala area. Although those demonstrators initially gathered in the Maitighar Mandala area, when numbers grew I saw all demonstrators advancing toward Baneswhar on the CCTV display connected to the Command Post. Since the daily office services were also continuing on that day, I was also engaged in secretarial work in addition to being a member of the District Security Committee and was not able to be at the Command Post to observe all scenes.

I saw on the CCTV display that the crowd advancing toward Baneswhar broke through the barricade placed in front of Everest Hotel at around 11 AM and was moving forward, and since the District Chief was present at the Command Post from school/college-age young women at the front of the crowd, I also heard directives being given to security officials at the Command Post to exercise restraint.

As demonstrators who had broken through the police barricade reached the Parliament building in New Baneswhar and began attacking, vandalizing, and setting fire to security personnel on duty and public property, an emergency District Security Committee meeting was held at the Command Post at 12:00 PM, issuing a curfew order to come into effect from 12:30 PM in the Baneswhar area, and directing Narasinghdal Gana at Singha Durbar, Bhadrakali, the District Police Office, Armed Police Force Nepal No. 20 Gana (Rescue and Disaster) at Sinamangal, and Armed Police Force Nepal No. 18 Gana at Nilbharhhari for implementation of that order. At that time, journalists from various media were repeatedly contacting me about the events. I had informed all media that the curfew decision had been made from 12:30 PM. As soon as the curfew decision was made, security personnel were directed to announce the curfew by microphone in the New Baneswhar area, and to make it more effective, the Armed Police Superintendent Jeevan K.C., who was at the Command Post, was also sent to Baneswhar by the District Chief. Additional assistance was requested from the Nepal Army since security personnel were insufficient to control the demonstrations. After the curfew was imposed, although it was expected that demonstrators would disperse, disregarding that, demonstrators blocked the roads among trucks carrying Nepal Army security personnel going from Singha Durbar toward Baneswhar to implement the curfew, and additional assistance could not reach the International Convention Center area.

Even after the curfew was imposed, demonstrators continued to vandalize around the Parliament building. Thereafter, the Chief District Officer directed that force be gradually applied in accordance with the principles of force use to protect the Parliament building, and in my capacity as spokesperson I informed the media of that content. I informed the media that the directive from the District Chief was not to fire metal bullets but to use rubber bullets if necessary.

The District Security Committee had given clear directives to security personnel to bring the situation under control using only minimum force, keeping in mind the participation of students and children as well. Although the number of demonstrators participating in protests organized by political parties can generally be estimated, the number of demonstrators who could participate in the demonstration called by the Gen Z generation on that day was far larger than what the state apparatus had estimated. Contrary to the assessment that the demonstration would occur in a civilized and decent manner, the sudden destructive activities made it very difficult for security personnel to bring the situation under control.

On 2082/5/24, with the intention of preventing further damage, a curfew order was issued for the Kathmandu Metropolitan area to come into effect from 8:30 AM until further notice  but since demonstrators did not comply with that order, the situation went out of control and very large damage occurred.

As vandalism incidents were occurring in various parts of the district and it appeared that security personnel were unable to bring the situation under control, at around 10:00 AM I, along with officials present at the District Administration Command Post, also went to the Ministry of Home Affairs. After approximately 15 minutes at the Ministry of Home Affairs, as directed by the Chief District Officer, everyone went to the Nepal Army's Nilbharahi Gana at Singha Durbar and stayed in that Gana throughout the rest of the day.
As the situation gradually normalized, I have been regularly re-engaged in office services from Bhadra 26 onward.
23. Prison Administrator Baburam Aryal, Prison Office Dillibazar:

I have been serving as office chief at the Prison Office Dillibazar since Baisakh 2082. On 2082/05/23 and 24, while the Gen Z generation was demonstrating in Kathmandu and various parts of the country demanding national interest, good governance, and corruption control, I was working at that same office. Prior to the movement, there was no estimate of the risk of the office being burned. No Early Warning regarding Unrest or Breakout Risk at the prison had come from security and intelligence agencies. Prior to the movement, the prison had CCTV, Watch Tower, and Parameter Fencing in good condition but no Alarm System. On Bhadra 23, 2082, the prison situation was normal. I was not even aware of the country-wide movement that occurred on the 24th and the attack that also took place at the prison. In connection with that, since the security threat at the prison where I was working also increased, I had written letters and repeatedly sent SMS messages to the Chief District Officer to send additional security personnel and made reports. I had also made reports to the Department of Prison Management about the ground situation at that time. I was continuously in discussion with the Nepal Police Commander  a Police Inspector  deployed for prison security. The situation was normal throughout the day. At approximately 17:00, around 400-500 demonstrators arrived in front of the prison's main gate and became active in demonstrations, slogan chanting, and vandalism; while security personnel were managing that demonstration, at approximately 18:00, demonstrators brought a bus (later learned to be belonging to the Commission for the Investigation of Abuse of Authority) and rammed the main gate with it to break in, then set fire to the same bus; thereafter they also set fire to the nearby police barracks, CCTV operation room, garage, and other facilities, causing complete damage to everything. Later, demonstrators fled due to aerial fire action by security personnel. On that day, there was no significant reaction from prisoners inside the prison. The following morning, from early Bhadra 25, prisoners began chanting various slogans targeting the jailer and security personnel, saying that prisoners from other prisons had been released outside and only they had not been released from our prison. They demanded that they too be released with a document. Despite repeated attempts to explain by police and our administration, prisoners did not comply, did not stay in their rooms, came outside, started slogan marches, and broke the channel gate inside the prison and began coming outside; police tried to control the situation with aerial fire. During that time, after I requested additional assistance from the Nepal Army, one unit of the army arrived at the prison. Prisoners attempted to break through 2 security gates and come out. They also set fire to where they lived. During that process, 20 prisoners escaped by climbing over the perimeter wall and through the main gate. Other prisoners were successfully kept under control. On that day, there were a total of 669 prisoners and detainees at the prison. Later, 457 prisoners and detainees remaining in the prison were transferred to the Central Prison Office at Jagannath Dewal on that same day; the remainder stayed in that prison. Of those who escaped, 9 have since returned to prison, and 11 remain at large. The security commander and I were in continuous contact on that day. There is no Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for prison management but there is an operating procedure. Internal and external administration has been operating according to that procedure. On that day, as the movement occurred across the country and prisoners escaped from prisons and a situation was created where security could not be provided, prisoners escaped. During the movement, there was satisfactory cooperation from the internal administration including wardens and chief wardens. My recommendations for securing the prison are: increase security force establishment immediately; construct prison buildings that are physically strong and robust in the medium term; and construct prisons classified according to type of crime for the long term, keeping prisoners separated. Prisoners must be housed according to prison capacity.
24. Account of Acting Prison Administrator Baikuntha Raj Regmi, Central Prison Jagannath Dewal:

I am a branch officer on the establishment of the Central Prison Office Jagannath Dewal. On Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, during the Gen Z movement, I was the acting prison administrator of the Prison Office Jagannath Dewal, Kathmandu. There was no estimate that demonstrators participating in the Gen Z movement would engage in activities such as attacking, vandalizing, and arson at the prison; no Early Warning regarding Unrest or Breakout Risk at the prison had come from security and intelligence agencies. The prison has a CCTV Watch System and Tower Parameter Fencing but no Alarm System. On Bhadra 24, 2082, it was heard that demonstrators were committing vandalism and arson at various government buildings including the Federal Parliament building; in connection with that, at around 19:30 in the evening, a large group of demonstrators arrived and began stone-pelting and attempting to vandalize all three gates of the detention facility  in the process, three windows of the prison office were broken and the main gate CCTV was vandalized. After that, prisoners broke all three doors of the detention facility, came outside, and advanced toward the main gate; Nepal Police on prison security duty at the main gate tried to stop them  pressure from demonstrators from outside and prisoners from inside meant police were also unable to stop them, resulting in prisoners successfully escaping. However, since the Nepal Army's National Service Corps office was adjacent to the prison, the movement of soldiers prevented arson and vandalism of vehicles at the prison and security side. The internal security situation of the prison was good; I asked everyone not to panic, not to engage in unruly activities such as vandalism and arson, and to stay peacefully inside the reform home, and also gave the assurance of working toward the services and facilities that detainees and prisoners are entitled to by law. Since vandalism and arson were occurring on all sides, there was no possibility of arranging additional security personnel. There is no Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for prison management  there is a prison operation procedure. As acting prison administrator, responsibility for the escape of prisoners remained with me, but on that day I kept trying until the end to the best of my positional capacity and ability with the assistance of available security personnel  because of which, of the total 3,880 prisoners and detainees, I was successful in keeping 363 in prison. The remaining 3,517 escaped; of those who escaped, we have been successful in bringing back 2,317; the remaining 1,200 prisoners and detainees are still at large, and the search operation continues. Guards, AR, head wardens, and sub-wardens of the internal administration provided satisfactory cooperation. For securing the prison  security establishment should be increased immediately; structurally strong prisons should be built in the medium term; and in the long term, prisons must be built according to crime classification. Prisoners must be housed according to prison capacity.
5.1.3 Statement of the Nepal Army:
25. Statement of Chief of Army Staff General Ashok Raj Sigdel:

I joined the Nepal Army on Falgun 3, 2043, and have been serving as Chief of Army Staff since Bhadra 24, 2081. In my capacity as a member of the National Security Council, I have discharged my responsibilities in accordance with the spirit of the constitution and law during the complex and difficult circumstances that arose during the demonstrations by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082. At the Central Security Committee meeting held on Bhadra 22, information had been received from the National Investigation Department and other security agencies that the movement could be extensive. However, since a formal National Security Council meeting had not yet been held by that date, a special strategic discussion could not take place. On Bhadra 23, as the security situation in the Parliament building area became increasingly complex, I had directed that necessary security preparations be made in coordination with other security agencies as per previous decisions, and had put subordinate units on alert to be ready for curfew implementation upon the request of local administration. On that day, the Nepal Army was not deployed and security arrangements were implemented under the leadership of the Ministry of Home Affairs.

At approximately 20:00 on Bhadra 23, the 39th meeting of the National Security Council was held at the Prime Minister's residence, Baluwatar. The Nepal Army is not an institution that automatically takes to the streets  the constitutional arrangement is that it can only be deployed after constitutional and legal procedures are completed, through the recommendation of the Government of Nepal Council of Ministers and a decision by the President. By the time the meeting was held, the Home Minister had already resigned, the ban on closed social media had already been lifted, and it was assessed that the agitators' demands would be addressed  in that situation, no concrete estimate had been made that difficult circumstances would arise on the 24th, so there was no decision or discussion regarding army deployment.

In the morning of Bhadra 24, during discussions at the Prime Minister's residence, the Prime Minister asked me for my suggestions. I presented two options: first, since it was understood that some senior officials of his own political party were advising him to resign, resignation might be appropriate to go toward a political solution to prevent further human casualties; second, if constitutionally recommended, the Nepal Army was fully prepared to control the situation. However, after the Prime Minister resigned, the security situation became even more anarchic, and the Nepal Army progressively issued press releases and appeals, and from 22:00 that night was fully deployed and actively played a role in restoring peace and order.

I reject the allegation that there was a lack of coordination among security agencies. The heads of all three security agencies were in continuous contact and joint discussion, and even in dangerous situations, the Nepal Army protected and rescued members of other security agencies. The Nepal Army played a leading role in protecting Singha Durbar, the President's residence, the Prime Minister's residence, Tribhuvan International Airport, fuel depots, prisons, and other sensitive structures, where extreme restraint was exercised to minimize human casualties. It is necessary for everyone to be clear that the Nepal Army does not deploy without a clear mandate and constitutional provisions. The constitutional arrangement is that the army can only be deployed in accordance with Articles 266(1), 267(6), and 273(1) of Nepal's constitution.

Although complaints were heard that requests for the Nepal Army's assistance on Bhadra 24 were not addressed in time, that is not the reality. The Nepal Army used fire trucks to control the fire that had broken out at the National Data Center at Singha Durbar and the Ministry of Finance's Data Center. Furthermore, the main structures of the President's residence and Prime Minister's residence, Tribhuvan International Airport and airports throughout the country, fuel depots, prisons, and other Vital Installations were protected at the Nepal Army's initiative. Agitated crowds that had gathered outside the Singha Durbar and President's Office perimeter were sent away through verbal warnings, more than approximately 46 rounds of aerial fire, and by repeatedly physically pushing them away from the main gate and persuading them. Although the Nepal Army suggested imposing curfew from 05:00 AM on Bhadra 24, curfew in Kathmandu was only announced from 08:30 AM  by which time there was already a significant presence of demonstrators, so that curfew could not be effective.

I prioritized human security over physical structures. My view is that criticism regarding army deployment must be evaluated in the context of the then constitutional constraints and circumstances. Joint operations were conducted to recover looted weapons and bring back escaped prisoners, public appeals were made to return weapons, and the coordinated security mechanism was kept active.

Finally, my conclusion is that to prevent such events from recurring in the future, the state's administrative and security apparatus must be freed from political interference and strengthened and made effective on a legal basis, the National Security Council must be made more active and responsible, and deep coordination among security agencies and long-term structural reform are necessary.
26. Assistant General Manoj Baidwar, Security Commander of the President's Office, Shital Niwas:

I was assigned to the President's Office from 2081/04/18 and was at Shital Niwas on Bhadra 23. Although demonstrations by the Gen Z generation took place at various locations on that day, no abnormal security problem arose in the Shital Niwas area.

On Bhadra 24, at around 11:00 AM, information was received that demonstrators were gathering in various locations. Keeping in mind the possibility that these groups could target any sensitive location, troops were reorganized giving highest priority to the security of Shital Niwas, which was under my responsibility. Duties were deployed in blocking position on the southern side of Gate No. 2 of Shital Niwas and on the road near Maharajgunj Kanti Children's Hospital. The remaining troops were kept in post, standby, and stop-and-go positions around the Shital Niwas premises. Gates No. 1, No. 2, and the western gates were held as Strong Holds to strengthen the security perimeter. I was continuously verifying the security of Shital Niwas and the Honorable President through on-site inspection and radio set.

At around 12:30 PM, the Honorable President called me along with all advisors and discussed the overall situation. I presented information that the situation was still under control at that time and that the deployed troops were stopping demonstrators gathered on the street through persuasion. Since information regarding demonstrators' activities was continuously being received through various channels, alertness was maintained accordingly. At around 14:00, the Honorable President again called me to get information about the situation. He expressed his wish to remain safely at Shital Niwas until the last moment. I also clarified that Shital Niwas was safe until then and that preparations were ready to take him to an alternative safe location only in the final situation.

At around 15:00, after receiving information that the number of demonstrators was increasing significantly and that a large group was advancing toward Shital Niwas from the direction of the former Baluwatar, it became clear that Shital Niwas could be the potential target of all demonstrators. Keeping that situation in mind, since the first priority was the Honorable President's security, I presented the suggestion that he needed to be immediately moved to a safe location. With the assistance of the ADC team and the Special Security Directorate, at around 15:15 the Honorable President and family were successfully transported to a safe location by vehicle.

Even after this, I continued to be in continuous contact through radio set and mobile with the commander of troops deployed at Shital Niwas, military officers of the Presidential Secretariat, and the Special Security Directorate commander to understand the situation, and was continuously directing them to prevent damage to Shital Niwas through persuasion and use of minimum force as much as possible. At around 15:20, a large number of demonstrators attempted to enter through Gates No. 1 and No. 2. Although the troops stopped the entry attempt from Gate No. 2, a large number of demonstrators entered Shital Niwas through the low old wall on the right and left of Gate No. 1.

During that process, deployed troops continuously tried to persuade people not to enter, but since firing on the extremely agitated crowd could cause large human casualties, fire was not opened with weapons. The troops fell back somewhat and focused on protecting the southern office area and western residential area of Shital Niwas. The agitated crowd began vandalism and arson around the area, and at around 16:00 the fire became extensive. Nevertheless, since the troops were physically present until the last moment, continuing to persuade and preventing damage from spreading to other areas, we were successful in keeping all the southern office buildings, vehicles in the parking area, and the western President's residential building completely safe. Due to the old structure, carpets, curtains, and furniture, the fire spread quickly, causing damage to the eastern and northern parts of Shital Niwas and the prefab houses.

Inside Shital Niwas, only a small unit of 9 Nepal Police personnel was deployed for coordination purposes, and the Armed Police Force was not inside the inner premises. From the Nepal Army's side, 224 troops were deployed.
