Neither aerial fire nor tear gas was used against the agitated crowd of demonstrators, because in the situation where residential and commercial buildings were surrounding the area, there was a visible risk of causing serious human casualties.

In my understanding, the main reasons why partial damage occurred at Shital Niwas were: giving highest priority to ensuring no human casualties and using minimum force, and placing the security of the Honorable President and family in first priority in accordance with the Distinguished Person Security Procedure 2081. No weapons were lost. Throughout the entire chain of events, I coordinated with the Combat Operations Department following the chain of command and adhered to the security procedure as per received directives.

Finally, based on the experience of this incident  where it became evident that large damage can occur even from unarmed demonstrators in such situations  my conclusion is that police deployment must be gradual and planned, Nepal Police and Armed Police Force must play a primary role in accordance with the concept of Graduated Response with the Nepal Army deployed only as a last resort, and the walls, wire fencing, and multi-layered security structure of VVIP/VIP residential and office areas must be strengthened.
27. Statement of Lieutenant Colonel Diwakar Khadka, Security Commander, Prime Minister's Residence Baluwatar:
On Bhadra 23, I was present at the Prime Minister's residence and no abnormal problem arose in the security situation at that location on that day.

Until the morning of Bhadra 24, the situation in the Prime Minister's residence area was normal. However, reports were being received that uncontrolled demonstrations were occurring in outer areas. At around 14:00, information was received that demonstrators were advancing toward Baluwatar. Until approximately 14:30, Nepal Police and Armed Police Force deployed in the outer perimeter of the Prime Minister's residence were trying to stop the agitators, but information was received that the situation was becoming increasingly sensitive as the size and anger of the crowd grew.

Meanwhile, assessing the security risk through the chain of command, at 14:55 the decision to give highest priority to the Prime Minister's life and security and to rescue him to a safe location using Nepal Army aerial assets was implemented. This decision was made completely in accordance with SOP, security procedures, and in coordination with higher authorities.

Nepal Police, Armed Police Force, and Nepal Army had been continuously coordinating and cooperating according to their respective procedures for the security of the Prime Minister's residence. On the 24th as well, all security agencies were managing duties at the main gate of the Prime Minister's residence, surrounding roads, and security perimeter according to their responsibilities. As long as the Prime Minister was at the Prime Minister's residence, the concerned agencies were trying to disperse the crowd and keep the situation under control.

Neither tear gas nor aerial fire was used by the Nepal Army in the Prime Minister's residence area. The Army's role primarily focused on persuasion, restraint, and protection of the inner perimeter. In the situation where Nepal Police and Armed Police Force deployed in the outer perimeter were forced to fall back somewhat due to the extremely uncontrolled crowd, some damage occurred to part of the outer perimeter of the Prime Minister's residence. However, the main structure of the Prime Minister's residence, the residential building, the new secretariat building, vehicles, and other important physical structures were all completely safe.

Although Nepal Police and Armed Police Force appeared to have retreated from the outer perimeter at around 14:45, all Nepal Army teams except those involved in the Prime Minister's rescue remained deployed at their respective workstations and no one left their duty to go elsewhere. There was no indiscipline or dereliction of duty of any kind in this matter.

Even after the Prime Minister's rescue, the Nepal Army continuously maintained protection of VIP persons, buildings, vehicles, and other physical assets within the inner perimeter. All operations were completed by coordinating with the Combat Operations Department following the chain of command, and in accordance with received directives and SOP.

Regarding the allegation that there was no complete coordination among the three security agencies during the events of Bhadra 24, my understanding differs  coordination among security agencies had been ongoing in normal circumstances, but after an extremely large and uncontrolled crowd entered from the outer perimeter, the situation became suddenly complex, which the Nepal Army team controlled within the inner perimeter.

Finally, my conclusion is that to make the security of an extremely sensitive VVIP area like the Prime Minister's residence more effective in the future, a clear arrangement giving overall responsibility to a single security agency would be appropriate. Based on the experience that practical difficulties arise when operating jointly due to different SOPs, weapons, equipment, and chains of command of various security agencies, I have expressed my conviction that a single command system would make security arrangements more robust and effective.
28. Statement of Major Ganesh Khadka (Member of the District Security Committee Kathmandu):

On Bhadra 23, Sudan Gurung, Ankit Malla, Sabal Gautam, Purushottam Yadav, and others  leaders of an organization called 'Hami Nepal'  had sent a letter to the District Administration Office with information about holding a peaceful movement at Maitighar Mandala, and information came to the District Security Committee that the movement would be to put pressure on the then government and would be completely peaceful. An estimate of up to approximately 8,000 participants was presented to the District Security Committee. My understanding is that the initial estimate could not match reality because the movement's leadership was not clearly visible, communications were happening only through social media like Discord, and the relevant agencies could not effectively analyze that information in a timely manner.

Based on received information, the District Security Committee had decided to formulate and implement a security plan, exercise special vigilance keeping in mind the possibility of children being used, and arrange security in a coordinated manner. On Bhadra 23, after curfew was imposed, the District Security Committee made a decision to request Nepal Army's assistance in accordance with the Local Administration Act, 2028, and the Chief District Officer made that decision at approximately 12:30 PM. Immediately thereafter, I deployed troops from the battalion under the leadership of Major Santosh Dhungel.

The announcement and effective time of the curfew occurred simultaneously, and thousands of agitated demonstrators had already gathered by then. Difficulties arose in troop movement due to curfew notice not reaching people beforehand and demonstrators blocking most routes toward Baneswhar. However, continuously announcing by microphone, clearing the road, and persuading demonstrators, the troops successfully entered the Federal Parliament building compound and were deployed at approximately 14:50. The claim that troops turned back is not true  it simply took longer than expected while clearing the way and advancing.

On Bhadra 23, after receiving information that shots had been fired around the Parliament building and demonstrators had died, a joint security command post was established at the District Administration Office. Thereafter, a District Security Committee meeting was held and a decision was made to recommend to the Chief District Officer to impose curfew in the Baneswhar area until 10 PM that night and to request the Nepal Army for necessary assistance.

Under the leadership of Major Santosh Dhungel, troops were moved from the battalion at Singha Durbar toward the Parliament building at approximately 12:45 PM. As demonstrators engaged in activities such as lying under the truck wheels and blocking vehicles when advancing toward BICC, troops were held up for approximately two hours. Continuously announcing by microphone, keeping vehicles safe, and persuading demonstrators, the Nepal Army entered the Federal Parliament building compound at approximately 14:50. The claim that troops turned back in this process is not true  it simply took longer while clearing the way and moving forward.

I do not view the arson, looting, and vandalism at the Parliament building, Singha Durbar, Supreme Court, President's building, private homes, and commercial structures on Bhadra 24 as merely the weakness of any one party. The deep dissatisfaction prevalent among the general public at that time and the restrained working style adopted by security agencies with the objective of minimizing human casualties appear to be the primary causes of the situation that was created.

Finally, I consider it necessary to form a security committee with a separate structure that takes into account the special security sensitivity of the Kathmandu Valley to prevent such events from recurring in the future. Additionally, based on the experience that it is difficult to bring the situation under control when curfew is imposed without adequate advance notice and preparation after a large crowd has already gathered, my conclusion is that time, information, and coordination in such decisions must be further strengthened.
28(a). Major Ganesh Khadka (As Battalion Commander of Narasinghdal Battalion, Singha Durbar):

During the events that occurred in Kathmandu on Bhadra 23 and 24, I was serving in the responsibility of Battalion Commander at Narasinghdal Battalion, Singha Durbar Barracks, and was present at the District Command Post of the District Administration Office Kathmandu as well as at the Singha Durbar premises as needed. On Bhadra 23, when Kathmandu curfew order was issued and Nepal Army's assistance was requested, demonstrators seriously affected troop movement by engaging in activities such as lying under truck wheels when troops were being moved toward the Federal Parliament building.

On Bhadra 24, after the District Administration Office issued a curfew order and requested Nepal Army's assistance, Nepal Army troops were deployed at all six gates of the Singha Durbar Secretariat  Maitighar Mandala, Panchdewal Chowk, Hanumansthan, and other entry points. From early morning, demonstrators began gathering at major intersections burning tires, and by around 12:45 PM crowds of thousands had reached the western, southern, and eastern gates. They were pelting stones and attempting to set fire to the gates.

At around 13:25, as Nepal Police and Armed Police Force teams fell back from in front of the western gate due to the excessive crowd and entered the gate, a large number of demonstrators also entered inside. Thereafter, police used baton charges and tear gas while the Nepal Army used aerial fire to push the crowd out and the gate was closed again. Warnings were also given to the crowd not to gather in a sensitive area like Singha Durbar. At 13:42, after arson occurred at the western gate, Nepal Army's fire truck was used to immediately control the fire.

At 13:53, thousands of demonstrators again entered through the western gate with women, children, and persons carrying stretchers placed at the front. In the process, the Nepal Army used aerial fire and police used tear gas to push the crowd out again. A large number of demonstrators also entered through the southern gate via the National Investigation Headquarters gate, which made the situation extremely sensitive. After the crowd entered by breaking the main gate and iron chains on both sides at approximately 15:03, a chaotic and anarchic situation was created. In such a situation, seeing that direct gunfire could cause large human casualties, restraint was exercised and crowd control was attempted through aerial fire.

After the crowd that had entered suddenly began arson, the fire truck was obstructed repeatedly when it tried to advance. After receiving information that arson had begun near the explosive storage area (bunker) near the Ministry of Defense, assessing the risk that if fire spread there, serious human and physical casualties could occur within approximately a one-kilometer radius, a fire truck was immediately sent and the fire was controlled. At approximately 16:20, after additional troops arrived, all demonstrators were pushed out of the Singha Durbar area and fire control work was intensified through fire trucks. Refilling water from Army Headquarters, Nepal Army fire trucks along with Kathmandu Metropolitan City fire trucks were used to bring the fire under control.

On Bhadra 24, Nepal Police and Armed Police Force attempted to keep the situation under control using microphone announcements, persuasion, tear gas, and aerial fire. Coordination and cooperation among all security agencies was done as per the Integrated Security Plan approved by the District Security Committee. On that day, there was a limited number of police and no permanent presence of armed police at the Singha Durbar Secretariat, but Nepal Army, Nepal Police, and Armed Police Force jointly made repeated attempts to push the crowd out.

Even while working with good intent and restraint in accordance with security procedures, the situation became abnormal after an agitated crowd entered through all gates simultaneously. In that situation, in my capacity as Battalion Commander, I gathered troops deployed inside and outside and prioritized protecting important structures including Singha Durbar's Data Centre, the newly built Federal Parliament building, Ministry of Defense, Ministry of Industry and Commerce, Radio Nepal, and Nepal Television. I was continuously reporting the entire chain of events to higher authorities following the Nepal Army's chain of command, from where directives were received to exercise restraint to minimize human casualties.

In such difficult circumstances, a clear legal provision for a single command system appears necessary for effectively deploying joint security forces. My conclusion is that making the Singha Durbar Security and Coordination Committee more active in the future, conducting joint security exercises, and having a clear command structure determined during a crisis would make security management more effective in practice.
29. Major Santosh Dhungel (Team Commander Deployed for Additional Security of Parliament Building):

On Bhadra 23, while I was participating in the Tundikhel parade, at approximately 12:15 PM I received an order from Battalion Commander Major Ganesh Khadka of Narasinghdal Battalion, Singha Durbar, to come to the battalion headquarters. After reaching the battalion headquarters with the parade team at approximately 12:35, at 12:45 we departed toward BICC with two vehicles. While proceeding via Singha Durbar-Hanumansthan-Thapagaon toward BICC, demonstrators blocked the vehicles causing approximately 45 minutes of delay. Later, even after linking up with additional troops, demonstrators engaged in activities such as lying under truck wheels, seriously affecting troop movement for approximately two hours. I had informed the chain of command about this.

At approximately 14:40, using microphone announcements and persuasion with the assistance of local police, dismounting from vehicles and providing security to troops, we entered the Federal Parliament building compound at 14:50. During that process, my team was deployed at the front line in crowd control. At approximately 17:05, fully entering through the western gate, barricades were placed at the broken wall on the southwest side and the area was secured. Thereafter, sectors were divided and duties were assigned, and night patrol was also conducted from 20:00 that night to 06:00 the following morning. On Bhadra 23, no aerial fire or gunfire was made by my team.

On Bhadra 24, from around 07:00 AM, demonstrators began gathering at the south main gate of BICC. Initially adding barricades, dividing into different groups, at 08:35 a security and situation briefing was given to the entire team. At approximately 11:30, a crowd that had reached approximately ten thousand used stones, bricks, glass bottles, and Molotov Cocktails to attack the south and west perimeter. Throughout the day, the crowd attempted to enter the Parliament building approximately four times, which police tried to push back using tear gas.

At approximately 13:57, a large mob of demonstrators broke through the Nepal Police and Armed Police Force cordon and attacked toward the Parliament building. In the process, the Nepal Army announced by microphone requesting not to advance, not to damage national property, not to commit arson, and to exercise restraint  but disregarding the warnings, the extremely agitated crowd entered the Parliament building and began stone-pelting, using rods, and Molotov Cocktails. Chanting slogans like 'blood for blood,' fire was set to Army and police vehicles. When large-scale arson occurred in the Parliament building's Lhotse Hall from Molotov Cocktail attacks, security personnel deployed outside were also put at risk.

To prevent human casualties, Nepal Police at 14:00 left the entrance and formed a safe cordon toward the western sector. Thereafter, at 14:30, a Safe Passage was made in the northern sector and police were rescued. I again patrolled inside the building, rescued trapped police and demonstrators, and continuously appealed through microphone to stop further damage and arson. On that day, Nepal Police and Armed Police Force were deployed with barricades at the north and west gates making crowd control attempts using tear gas.

On that day, I had a total Nepal Army strength of 140 persons under my command. Exact strength of district police and armed police was not known. After the uncontrolled crowd broke through the gate and entered, police at the initial cordon used tear gas and baton charges while my team primarily only attempted persuasion and pushing back.

My conclusion is that the main reasons the Parliament building could not be fully protected were the lack of necessary non-lethal equipment for crowd control and the absence of adequate joint exercises among security agencies for such circumstances. By approximately 16:00 on Bhadra 24, my team remained in the Parliament building premises and thereafter returned to the battalion at Singha Durbar. All troops and weapons were safe.

Throughout the entire chain of events, I had coordinated with the Battalion Commander and Brigade Commander following the chain of command. Directives were received from higher authorities to exercise restraint, not to agitate the crowd, and to make appeals through microphone to prevent human casualties. My view is that to make the security of such important locations more effective in the future, the army must maintain a deterrence posture rather than crowd control; all security agencies must be deployed in adequate numbers to make access control strict; regular joint simulation exercises must be conducted; and information collection means and resources must be strengthened.
5.1.4 Statement and Incident Account Documents of Nepal Police:
30. Statement of Then Inspector General of Police Chandra Kuber Khapung:

The reporting system established at Police Headquarters Naxal serves as the main basis for obtaining information about the actual situation regarding any movement or crowd control. Reporting from the relevant area and province comes through Police Headquarters Operations, and information exchange and directive flow operate in accordance with Chain of Command with officers including AIGP, DIGP, SSP, and SP. In special circumstances, the practice of receiving general information directly through relevant department or office chiefs also exists as needed.

On Bhadra 23, the initial information was that the demonstration organized by the Gen Z generation had been held with approval as a peaceful program. It was estimated that some unruly individuals could infiltrate and the situation could become provocative, but an analysis had not initially been made at the level of vandalism and arson occurring at the Federal Parliament building. Information was received from the Police Headquarters Operations Department and Cyber Bureau about discussions and preparations occurring on platforms like Discord, and this matter was presented at the meeting of security agency heads. Before the Government of Nepal decided to close social media, formal consultation had not been made with Nepal Police about the possible security challenges that could arise from it  but after it was closed, alertness was exercised regarding possible challenges.

That same day, after receiving a report that the procession was breaking through the prohibited zone and advancing toward the Federal Parliament building, I coordinated with the Chief District Officer and directed that necessary management be made. I had said to ventilate the procession and keep the situation under control. After receiving information that demonstrators had broken through the security cordon and entered the Parliament premises, I gave directives to deploy police with restraint in accordance with the Local Administration Act. I wish to note the fact that after the District Administration Office Kathmandu issued the curfew order, since there is its own method and process for curfew implementation, the Inspector General of Police has no direct role therein.

Police personnel deployed in the field have the authority to fire in accordance with the law if the situation demands it, and there is no legal obligation to obtain prior approval from the center in each situation. It can be speculated that a different outcome might have come if central approval had been made mandatory before all types of fire use, but practically and legally it is not possible to control each situation that way. The Inspector General of Police does not have the authority to completely prohibit live rounds or give a special order for cease fire  I gave policy-level directives to use minimum force. There is a provision for live ammunition use within the scope of the Local Administration Act according to field conditions in the context of protection of important establishments, self-defense, and curfew implementation. When the Parliament building's compound wall was demolished, arson occurred at western and southern gates, entry was made into the premises by climbing walls from various directions, attempts were made to seize weapons from personnel on security posts, arson occurred at posts, and stone-pelting occurred on security personnel  at that time, I believe field officers must have felt the real risk of losing security control.

I have received information that approximately 21 persons died in the Bhadra 23 incident. Details of injured demonstrators and police personnel were received through regular reporting. Live round use is not a normal situation  in complex circumstances with crowd rushing, thousands of rounds fired, and hundreds of injured, unfortunately some demonstrators may have died from bullets hitting sensitive body parts such as the chest, head, face, throat, and abdomen. Although the situation came under immediate control after lethal force was used inside the Parliament premises, information came the next day that it had become even more aggressive. My belief is that shots were fired only after the curfew order. When the Parliament building's compound wall had been demolished, gates and security posts had been set on fire, and vandalism had begun inside the premises, I believe additional damage to the Parliament building would have been certain if lethal force had not been used.

I do not believe the human casualties were merely the result of one person's one-day decision. How the Gen Z youth's peaceful demonstration reached a violent level, what elements infiltrated, and what factors increased the anger can only become clear through in-depth investigation. My view is that security personnel deployed immediately in the field were merely made targets of anger toward other agencies. State agencies involved in information collection, analysis, and communication appear to be to some extent responsible.

Since the event was extremely tragic and it was estimated that its impact could spread across the country, all provincial police chiefs were briefed in the evening and directed to manage security with restraint. Local security assessment was directed, estimating that there could be threats of possible attacks on public property and political leadership. On the morning of Bhadra 24, a briefing was given to the Prime Minister along with security chiefs at Baluwatar, where the formation of an investigation committee was discussed while expressing grief over the large human casualties.

I had no information about how Rabi Lamichhane, Chairperson of the Rastriya Swatantra Party, who was at Nakkhu Prison, came out  contact was made through D.P. Aryal's WhatsApp number after he had already come out.

I reject the allegation that results were directly determined by orders I gave or did not give. The role of circumstances is decisive in law enforcement. I have information that bullets recovered from the bodies of the injured and deceased were sent for testing  I did not have clear information about shell casing collection. Until my retirement, identification of 12 persons was pending; DNA testing was attempted but reports were received that it was not possible in all cases.

According to the Police Act, 2012, surrendering weapons or government property to attackers is a serious crime  therefore, this must be evaluated keeping in mind the field risk and preservation of police lives. I have not intentionally given any illegal order. Since I do not have clear knowledge of Nepal Army deployment policy and procedures, I do not consider it appropriate to offer views on it. Inter-agency coordination was active from the District Security Committee to the Central Security Committee, though effectiveness varied according to circumstances.

On Bhadra 24, after attacks, vandalism, arson, and even killing of police began at most police offices, since it was not possible for only the manpower present at offices to control the situation, since attacks occurred at all offices making it impossible to immediately mobilize additional assistance, and since it seemed that if personnel present there started firing it would lead to huge human casualties, and since it appeared that keeping detainees locked up during arson could create a gravely irreversible situation, only an order for minimum force use was given. If they had been incited to open fire, an unimaginable massacre would certainly have occurred. That order was appropriate in the then circumstances.

The families of the 3 immortal police martyrs who attained martyrdom in the course of duty were provided with NPR 1 million each, 2 police personnel were promoted one rank, and others were given cash rewards. A departmental investigation committee has been formed for personnel who appeared to have evaded responsibility, and action will be taken as per that committee's recommendation.

I had expressed concern about the effect on morale when police personnel were lined up and interviewed on television  that was not contrary to the Inquiry Commission's intent. The intent was that police should not become the center of anger toward other agencies.

I have pointed out the need to make risk assessment, information exchange, training, and inter-agency coordination more effective to prevent such situations from recurring in the future. Overall, that chain of events was not the result of individual decisions but the product of an extremely complex, intense, and multi-dimensional security situation, where decisions had to be made while balancing legal limits, field realities, and human sensitivity.
31. Statement of Inspector General of Police (Then Chief of Valley Police Office) Dan Bahadur Karki:

In my capacity as a national service officer of Nepal Police, I wish to clarify the facts I observed and understood during the abnormal and extremely sensitive circumstances that arose during the Gen Z youth movement on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082. Reflecting on the duty to respect the government's decisions and assist in their implementation as a loyal national servant, and also considering this an opportunity to clarify to the extent of my knowledge some suspicions raised about the police's role, I am present to give my statement with the objective of revealing the truth.

The Operations Department at Police Headquarters plans and monitors peace and security matters across Nepal, receives information about the actual situation, reports to the Inspector General of Police about incidents, and makes reports as per suggestions and directives from the Inspector General of Police in accordance with the Chain of Command  this is the provision for obtaining information for any demonstration, sit-in, protest, or movement.

On Bhadra 23, I was working in the Human Resource Development Department at Nepal Police Headquarters. Since the movement was pre-announced, the District Security Committee had already made a threat assessment and approved a security plan beforehand, and thousands of police personnel were deployed accordingly. The Kathmandu Valley Police Office leadership had been vacant since Bhadra 18, and after some colleague DIG officers evaded responsibility  some unwillingly and some under the pretext of being unwell  I internalized the principle that in sensitive circumstances, a national servant must fulfill their role without reservation, and at approximately 09:00 AM on Bhadra 24, 2082, I arrived to take responsibility as Office Chief of the Valley Police Office as per verbal order (a letter was given to me on Bhadra 28).

Within moments of assuming responsibility, after being summoned by the ministry, I gave brief directives to senior officers and reached the Ministry of Home Affairs. Thereafter, continuously participating in various high-level security meetings including the Prime Minister's residence and Army Headquarters, I remained actively engaged in reporting, coordination, and directive roles. Decisions had to be made under time pressure and extreme pressure as violence, arson, vandalism, and attacks spread simultaneously across the Valley.

Since the Bhadra 23/24 Gen Z movement was a pre-announced program, monitoring the information of social media platforms including Reddit, Discord, and X used by the Gen Z members, a security plan was approved through the District Security Committee meeting and 4,396 Nepal Police personnel had been deployed.

Since my responsibility was at the Human Resource Development Department at Police Headquarters, it was not my work nature to analyze events  but by analyzing national/international events, as soon as I learned the news of Nepal's decision to close social media during the Gen Z movement, I called then Information and Communication Minister Prithivi Subba Gurung and suggested that since closing social media had affected online communication, business, and studies  leaving citizens frustrated  it would be better to apologize to the public and open it. After the Bhadra 23 incident, Minister Prithivi Subba Gurung also asked me at approximately 15:00 about what to do regarding the human casualties  I had expressed my views suggesting that resignation and similar actions would be appropriate reasons for the irreversible human casualties and to prevent further possible casualties.

After the tragic human casualties that occurred on Bhadra 23, the risk of the country heading toward another major conflict was clearly visible. Therefore, on Bhadra 24, the supreme security priority was set as protection of human lives. Against the backdrop of widespread condemnation of the Bhadra 23 events, with people calling it murder, massacre, and crimes against humanity (and the Home Minister having already resigned), in a situation where security agencies had to prioritize between human security and physical security for maintaining public peace and security, even though damage to structures like the Parliament building was unfortunate, since using force could potentially take the lives of thousands of innocent citizens, exercising restraint was unavoidable. In my view, the principle of 'Life over Property' was applied in this context.

The claims on social media and complaints received at this Commission that I made various attempts in my capacity as Valley Chief to bring Rabi Lamichhane, Chairperson of the Rastriya Swatantra Party who was in custody at Nakkhu Prison Lalitpur, outside are false, fictional, baseless, and come from malicious intent. Evidence is increasingly showing that one of the competitors engaged in unhealthy competition to become Inspector General of Police at that time deliberately planted that complaint. Since no law grants the Valley Police Chief the authority to release prisoners and detainees, no attempt was made outside the law. Court decisions and orders are also implemented only through the relevant agencies via the district court.

When some police offices caught fire during violent attacks and lives of detainees in custody were immediately endangered, directives to release detainees under the condition that they would appear when searched  while maintaining minimum standards of human rights and keeping records  were given only in limited and special circumstances. This was not any collective or indiscriminate order. Additionally, the allegation of only using tear gas is factually incorrect  rubber bullets, dummy rounds, and aerial fire were also used as necessary.

In that difficult situation, with limited manpower remaining at police offices and simultaneous attacks occurring, a large quantity of weapons, ammunition, and property was looted. However, through extensive searches and legal action, a large number of weapons and bullets have since been recovered, and perpetrators are being brought within the scope of legal action. My assessment is that because police personnel discharged their duty to protect citizens without caring for their own lives, much larger damage was prevented.

The fact that 3 police personnel died, and thousands of police and demonstrators were injured in these events is extremely painful. I express deep grief over all those events. The organization has been sensitive toward police personnel who worked bravely in the course of duty and toward martyr families, and appropriate honor and assistance has been provided.

Understanding the sentiments expressed in the movement, as both the then Valley Police Chief and now the head of the Nepal Police organization, I am confident that enforcing the law establishes the rule of law, the rule of law maintains good governance, and good governance establishes prosperity and civilization  and all my good efforts will be directed toward that establishment, and my efforts will not allow a conflict situation to develop again in the country. As a responsible professional police officer, many looted weapons and escaped prisoners have already been searched and brought within the legal framework. I am firm and committed to ending the atmosphere of fear and insecurity created among ordinary Nepali citizens, national servants, and justice workers by adopting public security strategies, motivating police personnel to remain loyal to the country and professionally fulfill duties according to public expectations, and maintaining peace, security, and law and order.

For the brave and honest performance of duty during the demonstrations on Bhadra 23 and 24, we immediately provided NPR 1 million each to the families of the 3 immortal police martyr families, gave one-rank promotion to 2 police personnel, and a large number have been rewarded with cash rewards recorded in official records. I consider it necessary to be vigilant about the negative impact on the force when nothing happens to irresponsible officers who fled and abandoned duty in difficult violent protest circumstances, while national servants who worked without caring for their lives for the country and its interests are questioned.

Due to the Bhadra 23 and 24 events, 1,618 police personnel working in various offices in the Kathmandu Valley were injured and 3 police personnel died. Of the injured, 1,617 have been discharged after treatment, while 1 police sub-inspector (Nagendra Prasad Patel) is still receiving treatment at the National Trauma Center.

Due to security personnel's gunfire or other reasons, 1,084 persons were injured in the Kathmandu Valley (1,323 injured across the country), and have been discharged after treatment  but 7 persons including Prakash Bahadur Bohora, Abishek Shrestha, and Mukesh Awasthi are receiving treatment at the National Trauma Center; Kamal Ghimire and Daulat Thapa at 612 Sinamangal; and Laxman Nepali and Santosh Bohora at T.U. Teaching Hospital Maharajgunj. 37 demonstrators died and 883 demonstrators were injured. Additionally, 7 persons died from burns at Chuchepati Bhatbhateni, 1 at Koteshwor Bhatbhateni, 1 at Ganga Dahal's house in Lalitpur, 1 at Udyan warehouse at Chandragiri 5 Haibision, and 1 at Hyatt Hotel  totaling 11 deaths by burning. Similarly, 25 persons died outside the Valley  (Bhadra 23: 2 by gunshot in Sunsari; Bhadra 24: 3 in Jhapa by gunshot totaling 5; Bhadra 24: 1 in Morang, 4 at Sunsari Bhatbhateni, 3 in Kaski, 1 in Parsa, and 1 in Chitwan totaling 10 deaths by burning; and 2 in Dhading, 3 in Ramechhap, and 5 in Banke totaling 10 prisoner and juvenile detainees). There are 12 bodies found in burned condition that have not yet been identified.

For internal investigation of events related to the Gen Z movement on Bhadra 23 and 24, the following committees have been formed from the Valley Police Office: a Crime Investigation, Analysis, and Scene Panchanama Committee under the chairmanship of Police SP Prabin Ghital; a Photo and Video Footage Collection and Analysis Committee under the chairmanship of Police DSP Anshu Kumar Singh; a Property Assessment and Inspection Committee under the chairmanship of Police SP Prabin Dhital; and a committee for searching and recovering looted weapons, ammunition, and other materials under the chairmanship of Police DSP Shaligram Sharma. Regarding the events of Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, a committee has been formed from Nepal Police Headquarters on Kartik 11, 2082 under the convenorship of a DIG.

Overall, this movement is a matter of serious self-reflection for the state, society, and security mechanism alike. The decisions made in the then circumstances were not easy, but my intent was centered on minimizing human casualties, maintaining public peace, and not letting the country go toward conflict again. In my capacity as head of the Nepal Police organization, I am fully committed to ending the fear and insecurity created among citizens by taking legal action on remaining weapons, escaped prisoners, and perpetrators.
32. Statement of Additional Inspector General of Police Siddhi Bikram Shah (Then Operations Department Chief):

I wish to clarify my role and understanding regarding the management of the demonstration called by the Generation-Z group in the capital on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, and the situation that developed during it. After seeing information about the demonstration spreading intensively through social media platforms like TikTok, Facebook Reels, and Discord a few days before it, I had informed the then Inspector General of Police in a timely manner and discussed necessary monitoring and preparedness. Although the received information increasingly indicated that the demonstration could be large, my understanding remained that the initial security preparedness could not be strengthened to the expected level because the government did not take the possible risk seriously enough. It had been estimated that demonstrations could occur in the prohibited zone and there could be clashes with police, but no assessment was made that the pressure created by advancing through the prohibited zone could reach a level that could not be stopped.

Initial security arrangements were primarily advanced according to the plan of the District Police Office Kathmandu and the Valley Police Office. I myself went to the field early in the morning and observed the security situation from Maitighar Mandala to New Baneswhar area and made arrangements to send additional police to locations with fewer personnel. Although I remained in continuous contact with Valley security chiefs, district police chiefs, and other senior officers, since many decisions went directly from the Inspector General of Police to field commanders, my role was mostly limited to coordination.

On Bhadra 23, after the demonstration started, the situation became progressively tense. I received information through media and communication sets that the crowd was creating excessive pressure toward the prohibited zone, risk had increased in the Parliament building area, and arson and vandalism had begun toward the south gate. After arson at Parliament's Gate No. 1, stone-pelting at police, and attempts to enter the premises by breaking through the compound wall occurred, a report was received that shots had been fired. The estimate was that there was a mixed crowd of aggressive demonstrators in front carrying stones, sticks, khukuris, rods, and other materials, and unarmed general public behind  information came that their number was approximately 25-30 thousand. Since the legal provision for the decision to fire is made by local administration and the relevant command post, I had no direct role in it. On that day, reports of force use came from Kaski, Jhapa, Sunsari, Lalitpur, Parsa, and Rupandehi in addition to Kathmandu. According to initial details, information came of 17 deaths, 327 injured, 211 police with minor injuries, 33 Armed Police Force with injuries, and 1 other  totaling 572 injured.

Since signs indicated the situation would not calm down after the incident and the movement could spread across the country the next day, I took initiatives to summon additional personnel from various districts to the Kathmandu Valley that very night. As the situation became increasingly complex on Bhadra 24, I coordinated with the Nepal Army Headquarters throughout the night about sector allocation and joint deployment of army teams. However, from early morning, as crowds began forcibly entering various police offices, party offices, and government structures, security personnel were under pressure. After the order not to fire lethal bullets from the Valley Police Office went through communication sets and demonstrators learned that police had been directed to keep themselves safe, their activities became more violent in my assessment.

On Bhadra 24, after the Police Headquarters itself began becoming a target of vandalism, I remained at the frontline to keep the morale of police inside the office high, to encourage the small teams formed at each gate for resistance, and to give directives to use tear gas and aerial fire as necessary to protect the office. Even though some senior officers had slipped away in civilian clothes, I stayed at the frontline and consider myself successful in keeping the office safe. In this context, at the Bhadra 26 meeting, the Inspector General of Police praised my role.

On Bhadra 23 and 24, Nepal Police across the country had fired 2,776 rounds of rubber bullets from shotguns, 2,920 blank rounds, 4,023 rounds from SLR, INSAS, and .303, and 8,654 tear gas shells  such statistics were received. On Bhadra 24, a report came that 1,281 weapons, 98,432 bullets, 1,315 rubber bullets, 2,220 blank rounds, 2,842 tear gas shells, 1,687 magazines, and 5,834 communication equipment were looted or lost to arson across the country. On that same day, details were received of 48 civilians (including those injured the previous day) and 3 police personnel dying, and 883 civilians and 1,804 police personnel being injured.

After orders to prepare a list of detainees and release them, not to fire lethal bullets, and to direct police to remain safe went through Victor-1's communication set, since police did not resist, demonstrators' morale rose and they engaged in vandalism, arson, and looting.

Even though the Generation-Z group called through social media to withdraw the demonstration, since persons of other age groups were seen to be involved in intensifying and advancing the violence, infiltration can be estimated. Overall, I have reached the conclusion that the main causes of the Bhadra 23 events were: the Gen Z matter not being addressed timely, the demonstration being taken as routine, intelligence about infiltration not being taken seriously, and security preparation being inadequate.
33. Statement of Deputy Inspector General of Police Om Bahadur Rana (Acting Chief of Valley Police Office on Bhadra 23):

I was serving in the responsibility of Acting Office Chief of the Kathmandu Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari, from Bhadra 19 to Bhadra 23, 2082. On Bhadra 24, after handing over the Office Chief responsibility to then DIG Dan Bahadur Karki, I was serving in the role of 2-IC (Second-in-Command) at that same office.

Since the program had been approved by the District Administration Office, organizers had given written commitments, and available information did not indicate any hostile activity, the security plan for the Bhadra 23 program was focused on normal crowd management with confidence that it would initially be peaceful. However, anticipating possible challenges, preparations were made for additional police deployment including police from Kathmandu, Lalitpur, and Bhaktapur. On that day, initially 3,441 police were deployed across the Valley and an additional 505 police were dispatched as the situation became abnormal.

The actual situation of the program developed in a different direction than expected. Although the crowd gathered in the Maitighar area initially appeared peaceful, it progressively became leaderless, impulsive, and aggressive. The environment suddenly deteriorated due to the mixture of outsider groups, unknown provocateurs, and purposeless violent tendencies along with children and adolescents in school uniforms. The crowd violated the prohibited zone and advanced toward the Parliament building and attacked public structures and police deployment. I experienced the situation of that day as violent energy that was unpredicted and suddenly transformed.

As demonstrators using stones, bricks, sticks, shovels, and hammers continued pushing toward the Parliament building and continuously striking security personnel, a real situation was created where security personnel's lives were endangered and security control could be lost. During this time, I received information through communication sets about the curfew order issued by Kathmandu administration. After the curfew order, I directed subordinate commanders to follow orders flawlessly, not to show excessive agitation, to minimize possible casualties, and to control only persons involved in anarchic activities. However, by that time the crowd had become completely uncontrolled and had spread in thousands.

According to prevailing law regarding gunfire, the order for lethal force use (firearm use) is made by the Chief District Officer. The decision to fire did not come from Nepal Police Headquarters, nor was it my personal order. I have mentioned in my statement that commanders in the field assessed the then circumstances and implemented necessary decisions. According to the report, on that day within the Valley 52 rounds of metal bullets, 417 rounds of rubber bullets, 117 blank rounds, and 1,017 rounds of tear gas shells were fired. Non-lethal options such as microphone announcements, water cannons, tear gas, and rubber bullets were actively used. But due to factors such as the severity of the crowd, lack of leadership, targeted attacks on the Parliament building from all sides, and the necessity of preserving security personnel's lives, a compulsory situation was created, and lethal force was used only after those options proved ineffective.

I have expressed deep grief over the deaths and injuries that occurred during the demonstrations. My clear view is that no security personnel desires human casualties. But the situation that determined the then decisions included clear risk of arson at the Parliament building, attempts to surround and disarm security personnel, and circumstances suggesting an objective to capture the state apparatus.

Widespread panic, fear, or morale decline was not visible among security personnel under my command. On the contrary, my conclusion is that they demonstrated the ability to maintain restraint and continue action even in difficult circumstances.

Regarding looting, arson, loss of weapons and ammunition, and destruction of vehicles and communication equipment at police offices across the Valley, I have mentioned two main causes. First, the available number of police was insufficient to face the enormous crowd size, use of improvised weapons, and anarchic violence. Second, since using excessive force could have increased the number of deaths and injuries on both sides, security personnel chose restraint while making sensitive decisions between self-defense and collective damage.

Due to the arson and vandalism of Bhadra 23 and 24, 219 police units within the Valley  including the Valley Police Office at Ranipokhari  were damaged, with estimated damage of approximately NPR 1,595,630,337. Details have been received of various types of rifles, SLRs, shotguns, multi-gas guns, gas guns, hornets, .22 magnums, INSAS rifles, revolvers, pistols, SMGs, and Non-Lethal Multi Weapons being lost, looted, or destroyed by fire. Similarly, statistics have been received of thousands of live and blank rounds being lost or destroyed, heavy and light vehicles and motorcycles being burned, and repeaters, base sets, mobile sets, handsets, and other communication equipment being destroyed or looted. 1,616 police personnel were injured due to stone-pelting and use of improvised weapons.
To develop Nepal Police as a strong, capable, and professional organization, I have presented the following recommendations:

content.content: Additional management of necessary resources and means,
Availability of modern technology for crowd control,
Arrangement of timely training for producing skilled manpower,
Special reform in recruitment, selection, and career development,
Transparency in transfer, promotion, service, and facilities,
Amending laws and regulations in line with the times to make the institution fully empowered,

Establish a resource-equipped special crowd control battalion with manpower of up to 2,500 in the Valley and 1,000 in each Provincial Police Office, which would not be deployed elsewhere except in emergency situations.

content: 34. Statement of Then Office Chief of District Police Office Kathmandu, Deputy Inspector General of Police Bishwa Adhikari:

At that time I was serving as the Chief of District Police Office Kathmandu (Senior Superintendent of Police) and am currently serving as Deputy Inspector General of Police in the Administration Department at Police Headquarters. My statement before the Commission is a detailed account of the extremely sensitive security situation that arose around the Parliament building and New Baneswhar area during the Gen Z generation's demonstration on Bhadra 23 and 24.

Although it was estimated that protest activities could occur in the situation where the then Government of Nepal had closed social media, no clear advance warning of any unexpected activity or serious security challenge had been received. Based on the conclusions of the District Security Committee and Central Security Committee meetings on Bhadra 22, a written self-declaration had been made that 3,000-5,000 persons would participate, the demonstration would be peaceful, a rally would proceed from Maitighar to the edge of the prohibited zone in Baneswhar and a corner meeting would be held back at Maitighar, there would be no entry into the prohibited zone, and no violent activities would be engaged in. Based on that estimate, a program of the Honorable President at the National Assembly Hall and a National Assembly session were also scheduled for Bhadra 23.

In coordination with the main parties submitting applications for the demonstration  Sudan Gurung, Anil Baniya, K.P. Khanal, Deepak Bohra, Victor Paudel of the 'Hami Nepal' group, and the current Honorable Minister Gupta and others  the 'Integrated Special Security Plan, 2082' was approved with confidence that the program would be peaceful. A Command Post was established at the District Administration Office in Babarmahel under the main command of the Chief District Officer for duty deployment. For security management, 1,965 from Nepal Police, 631 from Armed Police Force, and 230 from the National Investigation Department were deployed for intelligence collection. A permanent team of the Nepal Police Special Task Force was deployed for security of the extremely sensitive structure like the International Convention Center (Parliament building).

No advance warning regarding discussions or possible violent plans on digital platforms including Discord, Reddit, and X (Twitter) was available to the District Security Committee. At the Central Security Committee meeting held at the Ministry of Home Affairs on the evening of Bhadra 22, the Chief District Officer and I participated and estimated the demonstration would be peaceful. Since youth and students would be participating, directives were received to exercise restraint, use force as little as possible, and not to do 'heavy deployment' by keeping manpower close. A policy was established to use force progressively only if there was vandalism or arson in the prohibited zone.

The demonstration was scheduled to start at Maitighar according to the pre-approved program and hold a corner meeting at the same location. But as one section advanced toward the prohibited zone and became agitated and began stone-pelting, and after breaking through the barricade at approximately 11:45, water cannons were used to maintain peace. With attention to the risk of approximately 600 staff, Members of Parliament inside the Parliament building and the diesel storage tank, a Layered Security Plan was implemented  iron barricades, human barricades, reserve force, and special task force were deployed. However, I experienced the situation beginning to go out of control after continuous attacks from all four directions.

Demonstrators used stone-pelting, petrol bombs, Molotov cocktails, and improvised sharp weapons. Attempts to demolish walls, arson at gates, security personnel being injured, screams of 'they're trying to kill us' on communication sets, and arson of the Armed Police ambulance made the situation extremely difficult. A curfew order was issued in accordance with the Local Administration Act, 2028, with the consent of the Ministry of Home Affairs. My view is that gunfire was used only as a Last Resort under the Doctrine of Necessity, and waiting for a separate written order in the then circumstances was not practical.

'Total Withdrawal' was not possible, because doing so could have caused widespread casualties and irreparable damage to state structures. Therefore, field commanders were directed to use non-lethal measures  water cannons, baton charges, tear gas, etc. After exercising restraint for approximately two hours and violent attacks continued, additional force was used only in extremely difficult circumstances.

On the evening of Bhadra 23, at the Prime Minister's residence Baluwatar, a meeting was held with the Prime Minister, Home Minister, Defense Minister, Foreign Minister, Nepali Congress President, Home Secretary, chiefs of all four security agencies, the Chief District Officer, and me to review the events. Discussions were held about deploying the Nepal Army at Vital Installations and deploying other security personnel in the field with the assessment that the situation could become even more complex on the 24th.

In social media analysis, the fact that attack location mapping was being done through platforms like netakhor.vercel.app and preparations were being made to make the crowd violent by spreading 'fake news' came to light later. Information was received on the 24th about plans to defy curfew, attack senior leaders' residences, and display bodies.

From the morning of Bhadra 24, simultaneous planned attacks began at Kalanki, Sankhu, Gongabu, Balaju, Balkhu, Koteshwor, Jadibuti, Boudha, Chabahil, Naikap, Kirtipur, Budhanilkantha, and Parliament building surroundings. Local governments, police offices, and government establishments began experiencing arson and vandalism. After the attack on Harisiddhi Police Office, attacks spread to other units. After small units became unsafe, manpower was concentrated at circles and divisions. Keeping in mind the risk of arson at offices and the possibility of detainees in custody suffering collective casualties, following directives received from Victor Control (Kathmandu Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari), after risk assessment, 206 detainees were released at various times.

There was sometimes a time gap in Tactical Movement between Armed Police and Nepal Police, and coordination challenges arose due to SOP and command clearance procedures. My view is that if the Nepal Army had taken over static duty, Nepal Police could have been fully focused on crowd control, but the practical complexity of deployment modality and risk of lethal weapon use may have limited the expected assistance.

At that time, reorganizing security personnel back at barracks, lifting body documentation panchanama, or securing the scene and collecting bullet casings was extremely challenging, as the rioting crowd was surrounding and attacking from all sides.

I have acknowledged the weakness in infiltration and advance warning analysis. My view is that if clear information had been received in time, the outcome could have been less horrific. Security clearance, CCTV surveillance, and continuous communication coordination of tall buildings around the Parliament building were ongoing.

Finally, I have analyzed the event as an organized violent attack on extremely sensitive state structures. I have reached the conclusion that decisions had to be made within legal bounds, the Doctrine of Necessity, and the principle of collective life preservation. Pointing out the need to strengthen information collection, risk analysis, special training, and inter-agency coordination to prevent such events from recurring, I have stated my readiness to provide necessary assistance to the Commission.
35. Statement of Then Office Chief of District Police Office Lalitpur, Senior Superintendent of Police Shyam Krishna Adhikari:

Senior Superintendent of Police Shyamkrishna Adhikari has presented in detail before the Commission the situations he saw, experienced, and had to face managerially regarding events that occurred in the Lalitpur area during the Gen Z movement on Bhadra 23 and 24. On Bhadra 23, since youth demonstrations were occurring throughout the capital, road security duty was also deployed from the Lalitpur Office. On the 23rd, the situation in Lalitpur area remained normal  no unpleasant incident occurred. Although no tension was visible from the events of that day, estimating that the possible risk would increase on the following day the 24th due to the Kathmandu area events of the 23rd, he had placed all subordinates on Standby from 6 AM. He notes that he gave the team the directive to 'remain calm, move in groups, and take action only after receiving orders.'

From early morning on the 24th, the atmosphere became abnormal. From approximately 11:30, simultaneous anarchic attacks began at ministers' and chief ministers' residences, Nepali Congress and NCP-UML party offices, police divisions and circles, and even Nakkhu Prison. At all police offices in Lalitpur except those in Chapagaon, Godawari, Harisiddhi, Badegaon, Bhaisepati, Imadol, Satdobato, Lubhu, Balkumari, and surrounding areas  as well as at Vilence School, private buildings including Prachanda's residence, Bishnu Paudel, Prithivi Subba Gurung, Pampha Bhusal, Hitendra Dev Shakya, businessman Hira Maharjan's building, and Gyanendra Karki's houses  arson and vandalism occurred. On that day, the crowds were extremely large; the crowd had blocked roads making it impossible to send additional forces, and since expected assistance from the Armed Police Force and Nepal Army did not come, police alone could not contain the crowd and lost control. He experienced the most difficult situation of his career that day.

After the anarchic crowd had already entered the office premises, he and his team made every effort to move weapons, communication sets, and other sensitive materials to safe locations. But 19 .303 rifles, 2 Hornet rifles, 4 Magnums, 2 gas guns, 3 pistols and other weapons were destroyed or lost to arson and looting. After the security of detainees became seriously at risk, detainees from Chapagaon and Satdobato circles had their lists updated and were released to prevent casualties. While some released detainees appeared again, some are still at large, which makes the tension and severity of insecurity of the situation even clearer.

He provided a detailed depiction of the extremely sensitive and dangerous situation that arose at Nakkhu Prison on the afternoon of that same day. After an anarchic crowd of approximately 500-600 persons surrounded the prison chanting 'Release Rabi Lamichhane,' he immediately requested assistance from the CDO, Army, Armed Police Force Chief, and others  but no positive security assistance could be obtained. He notes that after the crowd broke through the prison's main gate and entered, seeing that further damage could occur and that the small number of police could not control the situation, keeping Rabi Lamichhane in the prison became impossible. Regarding the promotion that prison administration 'released him with a letter,' he stated that he had no immediate information about it and only learned about it later through social media.

After the events, complaints were registered from victims  ministers, leaders, businesspeople, and local residents  and investigation and action are ongoing, he stated. Of the initial 22 suspects, 20 had been released on attendance bail while 2 were in custody.

Finally, he recalls Bhadra 24 as the most difficult, challenging, and mentally pressured day of his entire police career. Police could not walk in uniform, could not use vehicles, carrying weapons increased risk instead, and with no arrangements even for eating and sleeping, police had to spend the night in forests and temple premises. Overall, according to him, the intense severity of circumstances, lack of coordination among security agencies, uncontrolled size of the crowd, and insufficient forces pushed the events out of control.
36. Statement of Senior Superintendent of Police Somendra Singh Rathore, Working at Tribhuvan International Airport, Deployed as Additional Assistance at Parliament Building on Bhadra 23:

On Bhadra 23, while working at his own office, at approximately 12:00 noon he was deployed as additional assistance of 65 persons toward the Parliament building under phone orders from then Inspector General of Police Chandra Kuber Khapung; his team had SLR, .303, shotgun, gas gun, pistols, and necessary rounds in armed readiness, which were returned to the office unused; walking on foot from Tilganga to the Parliament building, it was already approximately 15:00 when they arrived; there were thousands of demonstrators outside while many police were resting inside having been injured; after consulting with DSP Deepa Samsher Rana (Victor-4) who had been deployed there before him, all Nepal Police and Armed Police Force teams inside were pushed out through the western gate at 15:30 toward Baneswhar Chowk, where a crowd of approximately 1,500-2,000 was contained; the pressure from the agitated crowd was increasing; but no gunfire or gas was needed from his side except baton charges. He clarified that during his deployment at the Parliament building from 15:00 to 18:15, demonstrators were not successful in entering the Parliament building, and he himself did not see any gate being broken or motorcycles entering. During the return, when stone-pelting occurred from the crowd, his team's personnel Kamlesh Luhar was injured and was taken to KMC Hospital.

Regarding who fired at demonstrators inside the Parliament premises, why heads and chests were hit, and how many were injured and killed  since firing had already occurred by the time he arrived, he had no information about it. As the crowd was becoming increasingly agitated toward an 'out of control situation,' commanders above him  Victor-4, Kilo-4, Kilo-1, Victor-2, and SSHO  were continuously exchanging information and directives through radio sets.

On the 24th, after rumors spread on social media that top leaders would flee abroad from the airport, a team under the command of a Police Sub-Inspector was deployed at the Golden Gate for security. After the Civil Aviation Authority issued a notice that visibility had deteriorated due to the arson in the city, international flights were suspended, which helped ease the situation. On the 24th, since no demonstrators caused any damage at his office, his team did not need to fire either.
The arson throughout the capital on the 24th, attacks at police offices, killings of police, looting, scenes of burning vehicles, and the crowd's behavior toward police have lowered police morale.

He noted that on Bhadra 23, the Armed Police Force did not play an effective role at the north and south gates of the Parliament building, and even after the curfew order was issued at 12:30, although the army was visible at the south gate of Parliament, no active defense-response was seen.

His conclusion is that the crisis of the 23rd and 24th deepened due to: the strategic weakness of repeating the mistake made in the previous Durga Prasai-led movement (directly using gunfire without baton charges), a very weak cordon in the Baneswhar area, the absence of 5-6 layers of security perimeter near the Parliament building, lack of coordination among police-APF-special task force, failure to detect attack plans repeatedly spread on social media (Discord, Reddit), weak information analysis mechanism, and lack of a separate special unit for crowd control.

Finally, he gave concrete recommendations for the state and security agencies for reform: the authority and responsibility regarding riot control between police and armed police must be clarified, responsibility and legal structure must be clear, the information mechanism must be strengthened, a coordinated cordon system must be built for critical zones like Parliament and Singha Durbar, and long-term preparation is necessary to prevent such movements from turning violent in the future.
37. Statement of Senior Superintendent of Police Deep Samsher J.B.R., Working at Kathmandu Valley Police Office, Deployed as Additional Assistance at Parliament Building on Bhadra 23:

He stated that on Bhadra 23, 2082, during the Gen Z generation's demonstration, since his office was not of a nature that directly goes to the field, but coordinates peace and security of all three Valley districts and monitors and supervises security arrangements, on that day too, with the objective of monitoring, he left the office at approximately 10:00 AM and reached the Parliament building in Baneswhar via Maitighar Mandala and Bijulibazar Bridge.

Initial information estimated the demonstration would be peaceful; although the demonstration initially remained so, after the procession from Maitighar reached Everest Hotel in Baneswhar and turned into a crowd, demonstrators broke the metal and human barricades placed in the prohibited zone in Baneswhar, surrounded the police, and advanced; reports were received that demonstrators coming by motorcycles from both sides of the service track further agitated the crowd. Within some time, the report came that surrounding the Parliament building from all four sides with the objective of unauthorized entry into the Parliament building in New Baneswhar, demonstrators were demolishing walls from outside, breaking railings, and had surrounded the Parliament building from all four sides and started vandalism and arson. Since he was also at that location, he was repeatedly telling field commanders through communication sets to use force progressively according to the principle of force use and work wisely; he directed all teams to contain the Parliament building from all four sides and to use water cannons along with gas shells, aerial fire to push the crowd outside the prohibited zone and contain it outside that area; but despite all security measures being adopted, since some demonstrators entered the Parliament building with aggressive behavior, some were brought under control and some were pushed outside the Parliament building using progressive force. After the situation remained tense, at 12:30 PM a curfew order was issued by local administration. Police made efforts to control the situation using non-lethal weapons including gas shells, dummy rounds, and rubber bullets. But the situation suddenly went out of control, and after certain deployed police teams were trapped and attacked with improvised weapons including petrol bombs and fire was set with the intent to kill, police used progressive force including aerial fire for self-defense. The report came that 19 demonstrators died and many police personnel on duty were also injured in that day's demonstration.

The following day, Bhadra 24, in light of the chain of events that developed after the 23rd events, district police offices were directed to be vigilant about what kinds of events might occur on the 24th, collect information, and deploy police without error. Approximately 85% of the Kathmandu Valley territory was under the influence of aggressive crowds  from Sankhu in the east to Nagdhunga in the west, from Tokha in the north to Harisiddhi and Lubhu in the south. Reports were continuously coming in that the crowd was extremely agitated, aggressive, and leaderless, and attacks, arson, and vandalism were occurring at small and large establishments, private property, and police offices.

Since most were deployed in the field with only 5-7% manpower remaining at the office, since using lethal force would cause large civilian casualties, and since it was impossible to control with normal force while aggressive demonstrators took advantage of the directive not to use lethal force, aggressors set fire to the Kathmandu Valley Police Office itself and burned weapons, ammunition, vehicles, and destroyed them. On that day, reports came that almost all police offices in all three districts of the Valley, residences of VIPs, important establishments, private commercial establishments, the Supreme Court, Parliament building, Singha Durbar, President's residence, and others were uncontrollably attacked with looting and arson. The crowd was also reported as extremely agitated, aggressive, and leaderless.

Treating police restraint as weakness, demonstrators killed 3 police in a horrific manner. Many police remain in various hospitals for treatment with fractured backs, broken heads, and permanently disabling injuries. On that day, attacks were specifically targeted at police. At Maharajgunj Police Circle, when demonstrators locked police personnel inside and set fire to the building, 24-25 police personnel who were trapped in the office were rescued from an agonizing death situation, saving their lives. Orders came to prepare a list of detainees and release them if such a situation arose, to keep their weapons, communication sets, vehicles, and themselves safe, and not to use any force other than tear gas  and this order appears to have come from a correct evaluation of the time.

The crowd developed in an extremely disorganized, leaderless, and frenzied manner, and there is strong possibility that 'infiltrator groups' were actively involved therein. It was these infiltrator groups that turned the movement into a violent, destructive, and coordinated attack form.

In abnormal circumstances, structures being damaged, weapons and materials being looted, police officers and constables being injured  his conclusion is that these events occurred not because of his personal desire or any single officer's error, but due to shortage of available resources and manpower, not receiving appropriate assistance from other security forces, extreme pressure from the crowd, weakness in information and prediction, and suddenly erupting multi-dimensional attacks.

For the future, he emphasizes three things: (1) strengthening social media-based advance warning systems, (2) availability of adequate non-lethal force and modern crowd control equipment, (3) clear chain of command, joint exercises, and structural reform among security agencies.

Finally, he says police have played as appropriate a role as possible in fulfilling their state security responsibility with available means, legal structure, and in the then circumstances. Nevertheless, seriously internalizing the weaknesses shown by Bhadra 23-24, institutional reform is indispensable to prevent such extraordinary circumstances from recurring in the future.
38. Statement of Then Office Chief of Central Special Task Force (STF), Superintendent of Police Krishna Kandel:

By order of the Operations Department of Nepal Police Headquarters, a team has been deployed from the Task Force for security of the Parliament building, remaining under the Kathmandu Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari, since 2065/02/13. Although the Central Special Task Force team deployed at the Parliament building does not have a written Mandate, the primary responsibility is the security of the Parliament building, Members of Parliament, staff, and sensitive structures within the premises. In difficult circumstances, work is done according to the orders of the field command deployed at that location, and operational leadership has been through the Baneswhar Circle, District Office Kathmandu, and Valley Police Office. Teams deployed at the Parliament building are equipped with 5.56mm INSAS rifle, 5.56mm LSW, 9mm SLR, Type 54 pistol, 9mm Petro-Beretta, gas guns, and rounds used in them.

On Bhadra 23, while I was at firing training at Kopan, by verbal order of the Operations Department of Police Headquarters, I arrived at STF's Gate No. 3 (near Nepal Commerce Bank) only at approximately 1:15 PM and was deployed from that time onward in dispersing and controlling the crowd at the intersection in front of the gate. At approximately 14:00, while controlling the crowd, demonstrators were about to vandalize  an ambulance was safely rescued and secured inside the STF premises. Even as the demonstration became more aggressive, the team demonstrated high professional skill and successfully protected the water tank and Nepal Commerce Bank area without allowing any human or physical damage, returning to the office at 21:00. The order to control the crowd using minimum possible force came from Senior Superintendent of Police Deep Samsher Jabra who was present as Commander in that area, and in the process of dispersing the crowd, 90 gas shells, 22 shotgun rubber cartridges, and 19 SLR blank rounds were expended, and 6 shields and 3 helmets of the team were broken, but lethal force (live ammunition) did not need to be used.

After estimating that demonstrations could be even more aggressive on the 24th due to the events of the 23rd, the first team from his office reached the Deuba residence, the second had to stay at Budhanilkantha to save the division, and the third team was also deployed for the Deuba residence but could not reach that location due to obstruction on the way and returned to base. 73 police under my command were deployed for security of Police Headquarters, Naxal. When demonstrators took an aggressive form and surrounded the Police Headquarters from all four directions, STF and No. 1 Battalion, without using lethal force, through tear gas, baton charges, and aerial fire as necessary, succeeded in preventing the crowd from entering the Police Headquarters and provided important security preventing damage to physical structures, weapons, ammunition, communication equipment, vehicles, supplies, and the lives and property of police personnel inside the Police Headquarters premises.

On that day, when severe arson, vandalism, and looting occurred inside the Parliament building, the report received was that on the STF side, all barracks, mess, canteen, CCTV room, office buildings, Walk Through Gate, vehicles, and all personal clothing and documents of all working STF personnel were completely burned and destroyed. Additionally, weapon magazines, ammunition, gas shells, security gear, bulletproof materials, and communication sets were all completely burned and destroyed. His conclusion regarding the main reasons for being unable to protect those structures is: the extreme aggressiveness of the crowd, suspicion of large-scale infiltration of the crowd, the practice of deploying the STF in normal crowd control, inadequate communication, and the compulsion of multiple teams being divided and deployed.

He notes that although the Armed Police Force was nearby on the 23rd, they were not as active as expected; on the 24th, when attacks increased from the Inspector General of Police's gate, a Nepal Army team of 10-12 persons was forced to become neutral  this makes clear the lack of coordination among the security apparatus.

His suggestions include: when deploying the Central Police Special Task Force as additional assistance, at least one platoon team should be deployed under the task force's own commander's leadership; adequate communication sets must be provided for the task force to coordinate with Metro Control; the task force should be deployed only in special nature situations and not in normal situations; the task force must have adequate resources; and since police personnel working in the task force are very reluctant to stay in the task force, an additional allowance of at least 20% of salary should be arranged.
39. Statement of Superintendent of Police Apilraj Bohra, Working at District Police Office Kathmandu:

I have been affiliated with Nepal Police since 2054 and was serving as Second-in-Command (Kilo-2) at the District Police Office Kathmandu from Magh 15, 2081 to Asoj 4, 2082. On Bhadra 23, since the Office Chief was in the field outside the office, I was inside the office. On that day, no specific location had been assigned to me in the security plan. But after the curfew was imposed and an order came to 'immediately go to the ICC (Parliament building),' he arrived at the ICC western gate at 12:50 with 7 police in his vehicle and assumed duty. His team had gas guns, SLR, INSAS, and 9mm pistols.
