When he arrived at the ICC, force had already been used between police and demonstrators in the Baneswhar area, and although the crowd had dispersed, they were again gathering from various directions and becoming aggressive, pushing toward the ICC premises. Through radio channel-2, he heard communications including: police barricade fell at 11:47, water cannon used at 11:48, police force use and firing had occurred, some demonstrators had climbed the eastern wall of the Parliament building and entered, at the southern gate, hammer and rods were being used to try to demolish the wall, gunfire had already occurred from the security force, and orders had come from the line office to use force to prevent entry into the Parliament building.

When he arrived at the western gate, the scene was even more serious  demonstrators were throwing petrol bombs, attempting arson toward the ICC building, the large crowd from Baneswhar was shifting toward the western gate, and repeated attempts were being made to demolish the gate. Since higher officers than him (overall commander Bishwa Adhikari (Kilo-1), Deep Samsher Rana (Victor-4), and Somendra Singh Rathore (SPO)) were present there, he did not need to take separate command. APF and Nepal Army also arrived in the latter moments but his assessment is that expected professional cooperation, systematic defense, or collective coordination from both security agencies was not visible.

According to him, the crowd at the western gate was different from crowds elsewhere  extremely aggressive, anarchic, and destructive. The crowd appeared to have the objective of breaking the gate, setting fire to the structure where police were stationed, and entering the Parliament building directly. In such circumstances, with his team, using minimum and proportionate force, he continuously tried to fire tear gas and prevent the gate from being demolished. 13 rounds of tear gas were fired by their team, but those were used to stop a Royalist procession that came after the Gen Z crowd had dispersed. He stated that his team did not fire any gunfire.

Since firing had already occurred when he assumed duty, he has no information about how demonstrators who had entered inside the ICC premises died or how they were shot. But he commented that the special task force deployed for security of important structures could have fired; shots hitting heads and chests could have occurred due to wall structure and Zeroing (aim-alignment) mismatches; and since the firing power of other security agencies is also equivalent, it would be inappropriate to place all blame on the police.

At approximately 18:30 on the evening of Bhadra 23, he reached Baneswhar Chowk from the western gate, where a Royalist crowd of 1,200 persons was pushing toward the Parliament building and it was possible to control it by dispersing the crowd twice. That night, arson, vandalism, and burning of police beat at Naaya Sadak Asan area were monitored through the communication set.

On Bhadra 24, he was deployed for office security. Initially after arson occurred at Harisiddhi Police Division, attacks spread to other Vital Installations. He learned through radio sets that the ICC, Singha Durbar, Supreme Court, and Ministry of Health were on fire. At approximately 19:30 in the evening, when a looter group of agitators looted an Armed Police Force truck and, using weapons looted from security forces, a group of approximately 200 persons threatened police to surrender and tried to break the gate of the District Police Office by ramming the truck loudly  he and his team fired tear gas and opened fire, chasing the crowd away and taking control of the vehicle. Until approximately 22:30, they used drones for reconnaissance, and when repeatedly tricked with feigned attacks, and when another looted Armed Police Force truck was brought from Bhadrakali direction and attempted another attack, that truck was also chased away. Even though attack attempts from demonstrators continued for approximately 3-4 hours, as commander he did not allow any damage to the District Police Office, dispersed the anarchic crowd that came to attack, and fulfilled the commander's role. During office security on the 24th, some rounds of bullets were fired but no deaths or injuries occurred to pro-crowd persons in the premises area according to his claim. After receiving an order through radio set from the upper line office to release detainees after assessing 'extreme risk, unlimited damage, and fire possibility at buildings' at various units across Kathmandu on that day, for security reasons 205 detainees in the course of investigation in the premises were released at 17:30 after time extension.

He believes infiltration occurred in this demonstration by looters, persons with criminal backgrounds, those who had been in prison, mentally ill persons and accused, police fugitives, thugs troubling society, and persons over 40 years of age saying 'king come, save the country'  with the involvement of groups with different objectives making infiltration in the demonstration. On that day, misinformation and disinformation also played a major role in making the movement anarchic.

He stated the main causes of the event were: weakness in information analysis, delay in threat assessment, ambiguity in post-curfew directives, lack of coordination in joint security mechanism, and lack of immediate decision-making capacity at the state leadership level. For preventing such situations in the future, his suggestions include: equipping Nepal Police with resources, making firing training (Zeroing) effective, establishing clear chain of command among security agencies, strengthening fortification of police offices, and clearly stipulating the role of the Armed Police Force in crowd control.

Finally, my conclusion is that the violent attacks that spread on Bhadra 24 were not merely an emotional reaction against the state and police, but a combined result of factors such as excitement spread through social media, misinformation, infiltration by criminal groups, and the retaliatory mindset of persons affected by incarceration.
40. Statement of Police Superintendent Kuldeep Chand, working at the District Police Range, Kathmandu:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), I was busy all day at the office with the regular work of the Crime Investigation Branch, as usual; therefore, I was not personally involved in or directly informed about the details of the protest events. Since I was not deployed to the incident site on the 23rd, I cannot say anything regarding the actual crowd size, use of force, firing, or the aggressive sequence of events that day.

On the 24th, however, the situation became different. From early morning, I heard information over the radio set about increasing crowds and chanting at various intersections of Kathmandu. After that, following the instructions of the Office Head, I set out for duty monitoring towards the Budhanilkantha-Maharajgunj area. While heading toward Budhanilkantha via Hattigauda-Chapali, the crowd gathered on the road prevented the pickup van I was in from moving forward. As the situation became complicated, I attempted to take the van through alternative routes, but as one path after another was being blocked, I ultimately left the van in a secure spot and proceeded on foot with my team toward the Deuba Residence. My team joined the police units from the Budhanilkantha Police Post and Maharajgunj Police Circle stationed at Deuba Chowk. At that time, an aggressive crowd of about 300–400 people was advancing while continuously pelting the police with stones and bricks. Although the police attempted to contain the crowd through announcements, persuasion, and barriers, the situation grew tense within those four hours. As more crowds joined from various alleys and concentrated in one place, the compound wall of the Deuba Residence was breached, vandalism and arson began on small structures inside, and crowd control became nearly impossible.

After that, a team of about 30 Nepal Army personnel staying inside the Deuba Residence came out and attempted to stop the crowd. However, as the size of the crowd became uncontrollable, we consolidated our teams, took a defensive stance, and moved all personnel to a secure location at the Shivapuri National Park checkpoint. Once the road conditions became normal around 1:00 AM, I returned to the office with the team. Regarding crowd control that day, no lethal force other than tear gas canisters was used by my team. Due to the convergence of crowds from all areas that day, the inability of the existing manpower to hold the crowd, and the fact that additional security forces could not arrive for further assistance, it became difficult to protect the team, myself, equipment, and weaponry as the sequence of vandalism, arson, and stone/brick pelting from the agitated crowd turned aggressive.

He mentioned that he did not see any deployment of the Armed Police Force in the Deuba Residence area, but noted that a Nepal Army team was inside the inner compound and came out to assist in control after the situation became abnormal. In his personal experience, he stated that he felt security equipment, resources, and structural preparations were inadequate. Since he was not involved in any processfrom the command and mobilization of the movement to security meetings and information collection or analysishe stated, 'I cannot say anything based on speculation regarding whether there was infiltration in that day's protest.'
41. Statement of Chief of Jalla Police Range Bhipruka Office, Senior Superintendent of Police Manav Prasad Budhathoki:

On Bhadra 23, there were no Gen-Z youth programs anywhere in Bhaktapur district, and the district's peace and security remained normal. However, taking into account the unstable environment following the incident at the Parliament Building in Baneswhar on the same day, all police were briefed about potential risks on the 24th, alertness was increased, and a special security plan was prepared for the 24th with police deployed in various areas and important locations.

Although a curfew order was issued prohibiting any type of gathering, procession, demonstration, assembly, meeting, or encirclement at various locations in Bhaktapur district effective from 08:30 AM on Bhadra 24, 2082, until further notice, people began gathering in large numbers in defiance of the curfew from the morning at Manohara, Gatthaghar, Pepsi Cola, Pura No Thimi, Chyamasingh, Dattatraya, Sallaghari, Sudhaal, Kharibot, and many other places in Bhaktapur. What initially appeared as a slogan-chanting procession grew increasingly enraged, with the crowd hurling stones, bottles, and other objects along with abusive and insulting chants directed at police. Although police requested peaceful conduct through persuasion, the crowd was not in a state to be reasoned with. As the crowd turned aggressive, a decision was made to consolidate the smaller police units since immediate backup could not be sent. I maintained continuous communication with the supervisory offices, providing updated reporting on developments, risks, and needs.

As the crowd expanded rapidly, from 11:00 AM Bhaktapur district was fully pushed toward an abnormal state. Arson, vandalism, and attacks began simultaneously at multiple locations. From the Thimi and Jagati police circles to Ddhikot, Sano Thimi, Sudhaal, Kharibot, Katuje, Duwakot, Changu Narayan, Doleshwar, Gund, NEC College, Divyeshwari, Tathali, traffic units, tourist police units, and others  a total of more than 18 police structures, as well as 9 government offices, 5 local government bodies, and 11 private residences and commercial establishments were damaged by the demonstrators' attacks. In the course of crowd control at various locations, approximately 394 gas gun cells, 72 blank rounds from SLR, 14 live rounds, 20 rounds from shotgun, and 20/20 rounds from INSAS rifles and T-303 rifles were fired. In the Pura No Thimi area, one demonstrator (Sajan Rai) was shot and wounded and died in the course of treatment. More than 20 police personnel were injured during the demonstration, including one constable who was shot in the leg. 28 detainees from Thimi and Jagati circles were released after assessing the situation and prioritizing the preservation of life given the heightened security risk, and 268 juvenile offenders also escaped from the Juvenile Correction Home. Demonstrators looted 57 police firearms, 32 were destroyed by fire, and of the looted weapons, only 24 were recovered in subsequent police operations.

During the demonstrations on that day, the following locations were attacked: K.P. Oli residence (arson), Mahesh Basnet residence (arson), Kiran Thapa residence (arson), Gokul Bastola residence (arson), Ananda Pokhrel residence (arson), Amar Bahadur Raimajhi residence (arson), Mohan Bahadur Basnet residence (arson), Sumitra Thapa residence (arson), Rabindra Sapkota residence (arson), Agni Sapkota residence (arson), Raj Kumar Joshi residence (arson), District Administration Office Bhaktapur (arson), Road Division Office (arson), District Coordination Office Bhaktapur (arson), District Urban Development Commissioner Office (arson), Treasury and Accounts Controller Office (vandalism), Far-Eastern Law Faculty Examination Control Office (arson), Tank Prasad Acharya Multi-Technical Institute (arson), Transport Office (arson), Archaeology Office Bhaktapur (arson), Madhyapur Thimi Municipality (arson), Suryabinayak Municipality Ward No. 7 Office (arson), Suryabinayak Municipality (vandalism), Suryabinayak Municipality Ward No. 4 Office (arson), Bhaktapur Ward No. 3 Public Toilet (arson), Gund Drinking Water and Sanitation Main Consumer Committee (arson), Nepal Scout Branch Office (arson), and RadheRadhe Bhat-Bhateni (vandalism). He clearly states the reasons these incidents could not be prevented: the incidents spread simultaneously, at multiple locations, and at an extremely rapid pace; expected assistance from the Armed Police Force and the Nepali Army was not received in time; and the extremely low police manpower created virtually impossible conditions to manage.
42. Statement of Office Chief of Police Circle Thankot, Deputy Superintendent of Police Eliza Giri:

During the Gen-Z demonstration on Bhadra 23, I was deployed at Maitighar Mandala. My responsibility was to coordinate with the demonstrators, manage the crowd, guide them peacefully to the border of the restricted zone via the service lane, and provide security until the procession returned to Maitighar and was transformed into a public rally. The initial state of the demonstration was calm and orderly. The gathering, which started with 15–20 people raising slogans such as 'Together for Reform, Against Corruption,' pledging to demonstrate peacefully, cause no disturbance, remain non-unruly, and not damage plants, gradually grew in numbers. As students in school uniforms began joining the demonstration, I informed the supervisory control about the matter. As the crowd size increased rapidly, the rally advanced from Maitighar ahead of the scheduled time. As a crowd of over five thousand occupied all three lanes and moved forward, management became increasingly challenging.

Near the Telecommunications Office, the crowd began getting agitated  throwing water bottles at security personnel, tearing down hoardings and posters. I requested restraint through a public address system and instructed my team to form a cordon around the crowd. School students appeared increasingly agitated, and TG groups were seen instigating them. Around 11:25 AM, when the crowd reached the restricted zone, a warning was given that breaking the restricted zone was illegal under the Local Administration Act and that legal action would follow. The crowd was disorganized and appeared to have no clear leadership. When unruly groups coming from behind instigated the crowd and broke through the restricted zone, the control room was notified. Water cannons and tear gas were then deployed. Thereafter, upon receiving orders to be deployed for perimeter security of the Parliament Building, I along with my team was stationed at the south gate of the Parliament Building.

As the crowd repeatedly attempted to enter through the south gate, repeated persuasion was carried out. When the crowd did not comply, minimum force was used including a baton charge, but the situation grew more intense with stone throwing, chunks of cement, and iron rod attacks. The situation became extremely dangerous when petrol bombs were also used. Even in that situation, since there were orders not to allow entry into the gate under any circumstances, I instructed the personnel under me to stay safe while containing the crowd, making announcements, and dispersing them.

Demonstrators were hurling stones and petrol bombs from the rooftops of small buildings on either side of the gate, and there were also attempts to set the water cannon vehicle on fire. While my team and I stood firm at the gate preventing entry into the Parliament Building, the crowd threw tear gas shells back at us, hurled stones and iron rods. I myself was seriously injured in this attack  my nose and mouth were struck and I bled. I immediately informed Victor Control about the serious threat to life, and my team took me to a safe location inside the Parliament Building. I rested for approximately one hour and then returned to duty as ordered.

After the curfew order was issued, the crowd was informed through public address announcements. As the demonstrators continued attacking in defiance of the curfew, orders were issued to take action in accordance with the law. After receiving the order to withdraw at around 21:30, I returned to my office. On Bhadra 23, the entire team deployed from Police Circle Thankot expended 5 blank rounds, 25 gas shells, and 11 rubber pellets.

In view of the potential violent situation on Bhadra 24, the first order received from the higher authority was to protect our own office, detainees, weapons, and police personnel. When the crowd turned aggressive in the Thankot area, vandalizing the police office and throwing petrol bombs, at around 16:30 in the afternoon I made the decision to temporarily take shelter at the nearby Nepal Army training institute while securing the weapons, ammunition, and personnel. A register was prepared and detainees were released to protect their lives. Although some documents and materials were destroyed, we were largely successful in keeping most vehicles and weapons safe. The crowds committed arson and vandalism at Police Circle Thankot, Police Division Balumbu, Nagdhunga, the former Vice President's residence, former Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak's residence, CG Group, Chandragiri Hills, and other locations.

In my conclusion, the Gen-Z movement, which initially started with anti-corruption and good governance slogans, later turned destructive due to the infiltration of unruly, criminal, and unlawful groups. The emotional exploitation of school students, arson, looting, and violent activities have had a serious impact on police morale. It is my view that a thorough investigation of the groups and elements involved in such incidents is necessary. On that day, I honestly endeavored to the best of my ability to protect the police office, weapons, personnel, and detainees within my area of responsibility.
43. Statement of Office Chief of Police Circle Kalinati, Deputy Superintendent of Police Madhu Kumar Thapa:

While serving at Police Circle Kalimati, I was deployed at Maitighar Mandala on Bhadra 23 during the demonstration called by the Gen-Z group, with the role of crowd management, queue control, and support for the rally under the Integrated Security Plan. In the initial phase, the crowd appeared very orderly, with the presence of school and college students (in uniform) as well as youth. However, from around 10:30 AM, as numbers grew rapidly to approximately five thousand, the situation became progressively complicated. After activities such as tearing posters and throwing water bottles began near the Telecommunications Office, multiple attempts were made via the public address system to urge restraint and move forward peacefully, but the crowd became increasingly aggressive and advanced toward the restricted zone. Around 11:25 AM, after the crowd began raising inflammatory slogans against corruption, against politicians, and including threats to burn down the Parliament Building, it became clear that the situation was heading toward abnormality. The crowd was leaderless.

After the crowd breached the restricted zone, I notified Victor Control of the situation, from where instructions came to issue legal notice to the crowd, urge restraint, and use minimum force. Despite continuous announcements via police handheld microphones, the crowd grew large and aggressive and began pushing toward the Parliament Building. Even after the curfew order came into effect at around 12:30, the situation could not be calmed. The crowd continued attacking police with stones, bottles, and abusive language. Tear gas shells were even reversed and thrown back toward police; the sheer number of demonstrators  most of them students  turning aggressive all at once rendered ordinary force application ineffective. In the course of crowd management, I was struck twice on my right leg by iron rods from the crowd and was thus also injured.
From the team deployed under me for crowd management, 14 blank rounds from SLR, 21 gas gun shells, and 15 rubber pellets from shotgun were used.

At the time the crowd entered the Parliament Building premises, I was deployed toward the south gate. The crowd broke and vandalized the main gate and walls, and even set fire inside the chambers. Motorcycle groups wearing black jerseys and full helmets were present in an organized manner with the apparent purpose of instigating the crowd. I have no information as to how and from where the shooting began, but the orders came from Petre-q, Victor-2, and Kilo-4.

On Bhadra 24, in light of the heightened risks anticipated from the previous day's events, instructions were received to disseminate information about the curfew, station police at major intersections, and take other preparatory measures. Although I was myself still injured from the previous day, as soon as an enraged crowd began arson, stone throwing, and attacking police in the Kalankhi, Balkhu, and Kalimati areas under my ward, I arrived on duty after taking medication and injections. When a large crowd gathered at Kalankhi Police Division and began arson, I along with approximately 25–30 police personnel attempted to stop the crowd using baton charges and tear gas, but was unsuccessful. During attacks, vandalism, and arson at various checkpoints and beats in Balkhu, Kalankhi, Syuchatar Chowki, and Kalimati, I managed to secure much of the weapons, communication equipment, and police manpower, successfully merging Kalimati Circle police personnel along with their weapons into the District Police Range Kathmandu. At around 15:30, when a crowd of nearly over two thousand attacked the Kathmandu Police Range, a joint team of 153 police and municipal police including myself used baton charges, tear gas, and aerial firing to prevent arson and vandalism and successfully protect the building.

On that day, instructions from Victor Control came to police to 'prepare a list of detainees,' 'release detainees if necessary,' 'not use excessive force beyond tear gas,' and 'keep yourself and your weapons and communications secure.'

On both the 23rd and 24th, external groups must have infiltrated the peaceful demonstrations to conduct vandalism, arson, petrol bombing, stone throwing, and attempts to burn down the Parliament Building. Groups with political and criminal mindsets were aggressively involved in carrying out these activities. No complaints related to Gen-Z victims have been registered at Kalimati Circle to date.

The events of the 23rd and 24th provided deep experience in dealing with large crowds, high-risk situations, and the challenges of crowd control. Though it was painful to see police structures burned and destroyed, unity and courage within the police team was maintained, which helped prevent much greater human and material losses.
44. Statement of Operations Officer of District Police Range Kathmandu, Deputy Superintendent of Police Sundar Tiwari:

From Magh 2082 to Asoj 2082, I served as Operations Commander of District Police Range Kathmandu, responsible for maintaining peace and security. During the Gen-Z movement on Bhadra 23 and 24 of 2082, I played a direct role in coordinating the entire security apparatus, planning, and implementation.

On Bhadra 23, all security duties were deployed as per the security plan from 8:30 AM. Demonstrators who gathered at Maitighar Mandala were initially peaceful and orderly. In accordance with the decision of the District Security Committee, a sensitive, human rights-friendly, and restrained action plan was implemented jointly by Nepal Police, Armed Police Force, and Traffic Police.

The monitoring of Nepal Police duties deployed as per the security plan was also carried out by Additional Inspector General of Police Siddhi Bikram Shah, Chief of the Operations Department at Police Headquarters; Senior Superintendent of Police Deep Samsher J.B.R. of Valley Police Office Kathmandu; and District Police Range Chief Senior Superintendent of Police Bishwa Adhikari.

However, from 11:00 AM onward, the situation deteriorated progressively. As crowds grew and advanced from Maitighar Mandala toward New Baneswhar, attempting to forcibly enter the restricted zone, a crowd of over 12,000 began hurling stones, bricks, and water bottles targeting police. Police and volunteers made repeated public address announcements to persuade and warn the crowd and attempt de-escalation, but the crowd grew more aggressive.

As crowds crossed the barricades placed on the road in front of Hotel Everest to prevent them from reaching the Parliament Building and arrived at New Baneswhar Chowk, the attack on the Parliament Building began at approximately 12:00 noon. The crowd broke through the Parliament Building's walls and railings in attempts to enter, and some used slingshots, rods, Molotov cocktails, and improvised weapons. After repeated warnings from security personnel went unheeded, water cannons and tear gas were used at approximately 11:48 AM under the Local Administration Act, 2028, for crowd control. Defying repeated verbal warnings from security personnel, some demonstrators, while rioting, attacking security personnel, and engaging in confrontations, broke and climbed over the Parliament Building's walls and railings and unlawfully entered the Parliament Building premises with the intent to set it on fire. Of approximately 100–110 demonstrators who unlawfully entered the Parliament Building premises, some were taken into custody while others were chased back out. Meanwhile, as informed via radio sets, youth arriving on approximately 100 motorcycles from various areas including Kalankhi and Gaushala  some wearing black T-shirts with '108' written on them  revved their motorcycles, caused commotion, and further agitated the crowd, intensifying the attack on the Parliament Building. Demonstrators snatched shields, helmets, batons, and other equipment from security personnel. Emboldened by snatching these items, demonstrators also attempted to seize weapons from security personnel.

On that day, the Federal Parliament's National Assembly 45th meeting of its 18th session was scheduled to be held, and some Honorable Members of the National Assembly had also arrived at the Parliament Building. Taking into account the security of parliamentary secretariat staff, dignitary escorts, and other persons present for parliamentary proceedings, as well as a diesel storage facility of 40,000-liter capacity located within the Parliament Building premises and the risks it posed, the overall security of the Parliament Building and the New Baneswhar area was extremely sensitive.

As peace and security deteriorated and the number of demonstrators kept increasing, with the security risk to the Parliament Building visibly rising and the situation appearing to affect national security as well, an emergency meeting of the District Security Committee Kathmandu was convened at 12:00 noon to maintain peace and security  after demonstrators began breaking through the restricted zone and attacking, vandalizing, and setting fire to duty security personnel and public property  and a curfew order was issued for the Baneswhar area effective from 12:30 PM, with the public informed through the District Administration Office Kathmandu's website and various media outlets. Security personnel deployed at New Baneswhar also informed demonstrators on all sides via public address systems. While it was expected that demonstrators would disperse following the curfew, they continued defying the order. As they blocked two Nepali Army trucks and one Armed Police Force Nepal truck that were heading from Singha Durbar toward Baneswhar to implement the curfew  obstructing them mid-road  additional reinforcements were also unable to arrive.

While announcements were being made informing demonstrators of the curfew order and warning that force would be used if the curfew was defied, the throwing of stones at police, use of metal rods to break railings and walls of the Parliament Building, and vandalism at the Parliament Building gate structure grew increasingly unruly and anarchic. Petrol bombs were hurled at the security personnel's residential quarters at the Parliament Building's south gate, and arson was committed at approximately 12:35, turning the movement into a destructive one. When the fire engine inside the Parliament Building premises was used to control the blaze, heavy stone pelting was directed at the fire engine and police personnel, prompting the use of rubber bullets to disperse the crowd around the gate and the arson at the Parliament Building structure was brought under control.
In the meantime, Gen-Z Nepal had called on everyone to immediately return home, stating that self-interested groups had agitated the crowd.
Demonstrators' attacks using iron rods, iron pipes, stones, bricks, and slingshots resulted in 211 Nepal Police personnel and 84 Armed Police Force Nepal personnel being injured.

On that day, some groups, in the name of peaceful protest, ran politically motivated infiltration, disinformation, and incitement campaigns through social media, which turned the movement violent. As the situation appeared to be going out of control and the Parliament Building seemed on the verge of being seized, a compulsory situation arose requiring use of lethal force (firing below the knee) in accordance with legal provisions.

Upon analyzing information received from social media at a later stage, it was found that a significant number of rumors were spread on the night of the 23rd (including information received even around midnight on the night of the 24th  regarding alleged rape of female students at a hostel near Global College in Mid-Baneswhar  upon which my team was mobilized and the incident was found to be untrue), and that a very large-scale preparation had been made for destructive and violent activities. Mapping of attack locations was done on netakhor.vercel.app; information was exchanged on social media platforms such as Discord and Reddit for launching attacks with Molotov cocktails; bulk messages were sent via MALIKA_ALRT to the effect that 'Nepal can only be at peace with the blood of leaders who shed the blood of innocent children'; calls to attack Nepal Police offices and personnel were circulated on social media; disinformation was spread alleging that Nepal Police went to hostels and raped female students, and that 15–16 Gen-Z youths were killed in Tokha and other locations. Such messages were shared especially in closed groups, and as such, all of this information could not be obtained at the time.

Although I have heard on social media that shots were fired by groups other than security personnel in connection with the deaths and injuries that occurred during the Bhadra 23 demonstration, I have no objective evidence in this regard.

Although a curfew order was issued on Bhadra 24 for the Kathmandu Metropolitan City ring road area effective from 08:30 AM until further notice, even the implementation of the curfew became impossible. Agitated demonstrators descended in large numbers from all directions armed with improvised weapons, and most police offices within the Kathmandu Valley were subjected to arson and vandalism. In the immediate situation, police personnel in small units were centralized at the circle and division levels. Instructions relayed through Victor Control were to use tear gas as much as possible to disperse demonstrators, use lethal force only as a last resort and in self-defense, and  in situations where arson in the office would lead to casualties  keep a record of detainees and release them so they would not be caught in the fire. Weapons and equipment were to be secured, and responsibilities were carried out with extreme restraint in accordance with these instructions. As the curfew order could not be effectively implemented, demonstrators armed with improvised weapons and petrol bombs conducted coordinated simultaneous attacks on local government bodies, police offices, government offices, and important establishments, creating a situation that was beyond the control of security personnel at that time, and the security personnel themselves being at risk led to the damage that occurred.

In the current reality where social media has developed the world into a global village, the biggest failure was the lack of sufficient intelligence gathering about a demonstration that was taking inspiration from protests in Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Indonesia, and other countries  organized through various closed groups and hashtags on social media  and instead assessing the demonstration based on Nepal's conventional past movement experience.
Because the intelligence that formed the backbone of the security plan was itself flawed, the strategy from the District Security Committee could not be effective.

In conclusion, the events of Bhadra 23 and 24 took the form of a planned riot; security personnel acted in compliance with human rights, law, and restraint; and the minimum force used to protect the country's constitutional institutions and public property was unavoidable.
45. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police Jageshwar Bhandari, Office Head of Police Circle Lainchaur, Sorhakhutte:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), according to the integrated security plan, I was deployed for crowd management on the northern lane in front of Alpha Beta Complex, west of New Baneshwor, with call sign C-56. A team of 55 personnel was mobilized with me, consisting of 15 from the Police Circle and 40 from the reserve. The formation was prepared with a team equipped with barricades and batons in the first line, a team with shields in the second tier, and a team with weapons in the rear tier. We were equipped with weapons and gear including gas guns, tear gas cells, shotguns, rubber cartridges, SLR, blank rounds, live rounds, Chinese pistols, and megaphones.

Although the crowd initially appeared calm near the barricades, the situation began to turn unstable after people from the back became agitated and started shouting slogans about breaking the barricades. Despite our continuous efforts to persuade them through megaphones, the angry crowd engaged in a tug-of-war with the barricades, reaching a point where iron poles were being broken; therefore, water cannons were used to spray water. However, as the crowd increased from all sides and moved toward an anarchic state by pelting stones, they eventually broke the barricades and advanced toward '100' (Parliament House). Subsequently, I was instructed by 'K4 Sir' to support Gate No. 2 (West) of '100', where I took up duty. Information regarding vandalism and arson in other locations was continuously coming over the communication set, and orders were received from the control room to use force systematically to prevent vandalism and arson. After vandalism and arson began at Gate No. 2 as well, my team fired tear gas, but a situation requiring live rounds did not arise. As the situation grew critical, baton charges, AT (Anti-Riot), and finally, three rounds of rubber cartridges were fired into the air. It is unknown how the crowd entered other gates of the Parliament House. Although Armed Police Force (APF) teams were visible in the barricaded area, they did not play an active supporting role for the Nepal Police in crowd management. During the protest, individuals arriving in groups on motorcycles assisted in further inciting the demonstration.

After reaching the office at 23:00 on the night of Bhadra 23, a review of the day's activities and tomorrow's duty was conducted. I informed all police personnel to remain on standby in crowd control gear at the office the next day. From 9:30 AM on the 24th, crowds began to gather at Sorhakhutte Chowk and surrounding areas, showing signs of burning tires, chanting slogans, and riotous tendencies. Around 13:55, stone-pelting began at the police station, and two vehicles, including a mobile van parked outside the compound, were set on fire. Despite limited manpower, the protesters were chased out of the office compound with the help of local residents, preventing them from entering the office or causing further damage.

On that day, as police stations across the country were being attacked, an order came from 'Victor Control' stating: 'Do not use lethal weapons, use tear gas and non-lethal weapons as much as possible, keep weapons, communication equipment, and vehicles safe, and if a situation arises where human loss could occur in the detention cells due to arson, release the detainees after keeping a record.' Following this, we released 45 detainees after recording their details. However, we were successful in keeping case files, weapons, ammunition, and communication sets safe from the protesters' attack.

Since the crowd was leaderless and there was no uniformity in the slogans shouted by the protesters (some shouted anti-government slogans, some anti-system, while others shouted slogans regarding good governance, corruption control/investigation, and against the ban on social media), it can be inferred that there was infiltration in the incident.

From the events of Bhadra 23 and 24, I experienced challenges such as the physical structure of police offices within urban layouts, security perspectives, lack of escape routes, and the inability to keep vehicles secure.
46. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police Jayeshwar Rimal, Office Head of Police Circle Bouddha:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), according to the integrated security plan, I was deployed on duty in front of New Baneshwor-Alpha Beta Complex from 8:30 AM. My responsibility was to prevent protesters coming from Maitighar toward New Baneshwor from entering the prohibited zone. To prevent a crowd of approximately 12,000 that arrived from Maitighar around 11:00 AM from entering the prohibited area, all initial crowd management methodsincluding megaphones, barricades, and persuasionwere implemented. However, some individuals in the front row of the crowd appeared agitated from the start; the shaking of barricades, attempts at vandalism, stone and brick pelting, and indecent sloganeering increased. The crowd was leaderless. after 15–20 minutes of scuffling, the barricade on the northern lane was broken, and the crowd succeeded in entering the prohibited area. Major challenges included the Armed Police Force team moving backward at that time, weak coordination, and the crowd circling around from behind the banners.

In the main incident of that day, the situation near the main gate of '100' (Parliament House) was extremely tense. Updates were coming via radio that the crowd was breaking the Parliament House railings, climbing on top of water cannons, pelting stones, and engaging in vandalism. My team was then deployed to support the main gate of '100'. Upon reaching near Gate No. 1, located on the south side of '100', I saw that the crowd had already broken the outer railing of the gate.

About 40–50 protesters were seen on the roof of the Gate No. 1 building, and 20–25 people were seen on top of the APF water cannon at the gate, chanting slogans. We could not move toward that area as the police at the gate were being attacked with stones, bricks, rods, and iron pieces. As soon as I received information that the curfew was implemented starting 12:30 PM, I repeatedly broadcasted the information to the public using a hand-held megaphone. The crowd refused to follow the curfew order, providing defiant responses like 'Let us go inside the Parliament House for just today.' Although the police clearly adopted a policy of not using force without orders and initially used verbal warnings, barricade security, megaphones, and restraint, once the crowd began direct attacks on the police with sticks, rods, bricks, and water bottles, the crowd was dispersed using tear gas, rubber cartridges, and blank fire. No lethal weapons were used by my team that day, and not a single protester was injured by my team. However, due to the protesters' pelting of stones, slingshots, and rods, four helmets of my team were smashed, six batons were broken, and three shields were shattered during the scuffle.

On Bhadra 24, in accordance with the security plan of the higher office, I managed the deployment of police personnel and kept the rest on standby from 7:00 AM, and I was redeployed to the New Baneshwor-Bakery Cafe area from 8:30 AM. The situation this day was even more sensitive than on Bhadra 23. Continuous reports came over the communication set that crowds were simultaneously gathering in 20–25 locations in Kathmandu, including Gokarna, Jorpati, Chabahil, Bouddha, Tushal, and Battisputali, and that vandalism and arson had begun at some police posts, houses of ministers and political leaders, private property, and government buildings. Following instructions to return and protect my own area, I found that by the time I was returning to the office, some police posts, the municipality, and other government offices, as well as the houses of some political party leaders, had already been vandalized. In this situation, since police could not be secure in small booths, an order was issued via 'Victor Control' to merge into the nearest large office and to bring communication sets and weapons to the larger office for protection. When my team reached Police Circle Bouddha around 10:30 AM, information arrived that more than 600 protesters had vandalized the Thali Post, more than 1,000 had surrounded the Jorpati Post, and over 100 had pelted stones at the Sankhu Post. Around 10:45 AM, a crowd of about 3,000–4,000 surged toward Circle Bouddha simultaneously from the Stupa, Jorpati, Dakshindhoka, and Mulpani directions and began pelting the station with stones. I immediately contacted the Ward Chairperson, Mayor, and local individuals for help; for a short time, local residents joined hands and helped divert the crowd toward Tushal, but after they also joined the movement, other crowds attacked the office again. By 12:30 PM, dozens of police offices, government buildings, and houses of ministers and political leaders had been vandalized and set on fire. To minimize human loss, orders were to control the crowd using gas cells and baton charges except in essential circumstances, and to avoid lethal force as much as possible. As stone-pelting, arson attempts, preparation of petrol bombs, and direct stick-rod attacks on police were occurring, the situation went out of control, and there was no possibility of receiving help from additional security agencies. Evaluating the situation where the lives of detainees and police were at risk (and as an order to release detainees after preparing a list came from Victor Control), all detainees in custody were released after a situational assessment.

Subsequently, my team secured 86 long weapons, 18 small weapons, important vehicles, and communication equipment and reached the Airport area through a 'Tactical Withdrawal.' However, many case files and ammunition remaining at the office were destroyed by fire when the crowd set the office and those goods ablaze after we departed. The primary challenges were petrol bombs, arson attempts, the crowd size in the thousands, the siege, the crowd psychology, and the lack of assistance from the Armed Police Force; despite this, after five hours of resistance, the police under my command performed their duties under extreme physical and mental pressure.

In the events of Bhadra 23 and 24, the leaderless nature of the crowd, the activity of inciting individuals, the use of school uniforms, the activities of unidentified groups on motorcycles, and the lack of uniformity in the crowd's slogans and objectives were the main indicators of infiltration.

The experience from the widespread arson and vandalism of numerous police offices and government structures in the capital on Bhadra 24, the damage to private property, the destruction of police officers' personal property, the decline in morale, and the fact that police were put at extreme risk even while exercising extreme restraint and minimum force, was profound.
47. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police Rajbabu Maharjan, Office Head of Police Circle Kirtipur:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), during the GNZ (00-2) demonstration, I was deployed on duty near the barricade starting from approximately 08:30 AM with the objective of preventing the crowd from entering the prohibited area on the southern lane of Alpha House. Teams were deployed on the southern, middle, and northern lanes of Alpha House under the command of different Deputy Superintendents of Police with distinct call signs. The large crowd coming from Maitighar appeared to reach around 15,000 as it advanced toward the Parliament House. Once the protesters reached the barricade in an extremely agitated state while chanting slogans and marching, we repeatedly used megaphones to advise them to remain restrained and not to enter the prohibited area. However, as the crowd tried to break the barricade and advance, scuffles broke out with the Armed Police Force, and the situation gradually became abnormal.

According to the orders received from Victor Control and adopting the principle of the systematic use of force, baton charges were initially used, followed by tear gas. From around 11:00 AM, the crowd became even more aggressive, and once they reached the front of the Parliament House, stone-pelting intensified. We attempted to control the situation while protecting ourselves and the team using shields. After the curfew order was issued at 12:00 PM, information was repeatedly given via megaphones, but since the crowd did not disperse, tear gas, rubber bullets, and blank rounds were used as ordered. Continuous efforts were made to warn, deter, and push back the crowd according to the principle of minimum use of force.

After the crowd became excessively aggressive, engaging in stone-throwing, vandalism, and arson, it became necessary to enter the Parliament House. The field command belonged to Kilo-1, and senior officers were also active in the field. I saw protesters vandalizing and setting fire to vehicles near the south gate of the Parliament House, breaking the wall, and entering the building. Due to the crowd carrying stones and tools, the situation went out of control despite the police's attempts to stop them. Subsequently, according to orders, warning shots were fired into the air. The situation for the final use of force was created to prevent the aggression of the crowd, curfew violations, and widespread damage. I do not know where the first shot was fired from. I personally did not fire, and I received no information regarding any damage caused by my team's firing. During the events, I saw the injured being taken to the hospital via ambulance. The exact details of human casualties were not known that day; I learned of them later through communication media. No one on my team was seriously injured; they recovered after receiving treatment for minor injuries. It is my understanding that the systematic use of force is necessary to protect important structures and property according to police duty.

Since there was a possibility of continued protests the day after the Bhadra 23 incident, my entire team was kept on standby at the office. On the morning of the 24th, protesters gathered in the Tribhuvan University area, and coordination efforts were made. However, later, as the number of protesters increased, vandalism and arson occurred at the municipality office and the residence of the Speaker. After a crowd of about 13,000 surrounded Police Circle Kirtipur and began vandalism, some returned after we tried to persuade them using the Newari language. Later, a large crowd returned and set fire to the police office, destroying documents and looting 10 pieces of weaponry. According to orders from Victor Control, priority was given to the safety of detainees, weapons, vehicles, communication equipment, and the team, and efforts were made to save them as much as possible.

During the sequence of events, individuals wearing masks and helmets, inciting the crowd with loud motorcycle noises, and using domestic weapons were seen, leading to suspicions of infiltration in the movement.
48. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police Uddhab Singh Bhat, Office Head of Police Circle Swoyambhu:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), I was deployed for security management at a program at the National City Hall (Rastriya Sabhagriha), Pradarshani Marg, where the President was the Chief Guest. My call sign was 'Circle-46,' and I was in charge of road protection with 25 police personnel. I was not directly deployed for duty during the Gen-Z movement on that day.

On September 9, 2025 (Bhadra 24, 2082), according to the security plan of the District Police Range Kathmandu, my team was deployed near Janata Bank on the way to Shankhamul from 08:30 AM. That day, an order came from Victor Control not to use any force other than tear gas and baton charges. Between approximately 14:00 and 15:00, I received information that the crowd from the Parliament House (Minbhawan side)

had started arson and vandalism. My team was at the designated location, and I only realized there was a fire after seeing smoke rising from the Parliament premises. Although both the Nepal Army and Armed Police Force were stationed inside the Parliament House, only the Nepal Police attempted to stop the arson.

That day, an order came from Victor Control to release detainees, save weapons and communication equipment, and not to use force except for tear gas. I gave instructions to my subordinate, Police Inspector Suraj Baniya, via phone to protect the office. However, due to a shortage of police personnel and a high number of sick and female police officers, the office and vehicles could not be saved from arson during the protest; 12 police officers were injured, and communication equipment and weapons were destroyed.

In the Shankhamul area where I was deployed, warnings were given via megaphone to stop the crowd, followed by a baton charge and the firing of 18 tear gas canisters, which prevented the crowd from entering the prohibited area. Later, after the arson at the Parliament House, our team also moved toward Baneshwor. During that time, my team snatched and secured a weapon from a protester who was carrying an SLR looted from the police during attacks in other locations. I sustained injuries to my knee and shoulder when struck by a rod from the crowd; however, when I tried to fire warning shots to deter the crowd, the gun did not firethat weapon likely belongs to the 18th Battalion of the Armed Police Force. I immediately informed Victor Control about this incident via the radio set.

After learning about the arson at the office on the 24th, I stayed with my team at the Nepal Army Logistics Battalion in Kalikasthan. The next day (Bhadra 25), I returned to Police Circle Swoyambhu in a City Police vehicle and started cleaning and reconstruction work with the help of local residents. In the events of Bhadra 23 and 24, other groups infiltrated the Gen-Z movement, creating a leaderless crowd that encouraged looting, arson, and vandalism. I and the police under my command fulfilled our duties with integrity, restraint, and by taking risks.
49. Statement of Office Chief of Police Circle Maligaun, Deputy Superintendent of Police Sunil Jung Shah:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was on security duty in the Baluwatar area from 9:00 AM, and after 1:00 PM I was deployed toward the Thapagaun and Baneswhar areas, where upon seeing approximately 700–800 demonstrators surrounding two Nepali Army trucks, I fired 12 dummy aerial rounds to disperse the crowd and rescued the trucks. Apart from that, the rest of my duty remained normal.

On Bhadra 24, while deployed in the Baluwatar area from 9:00 AM, I received information at around 12:30 PM that a large crowd was committing arson and vandalism at former Minister Gagan Thapa's residence. Upon arriving with my team, I dispersed a crowd of 1,400–1,500 people toward the Gaushala area using graduated force, public address announcements, and baton charges. Meanwhile, upon receiving information that another large crowd from the Maligaun direction was heading to attack the Police Circle Maligaun office, I arrived at the office to find the crowd attempting to enter the office, seize weapons, commit arson, and destroy case files. In managing that crowd, I used 62 dummy rounds and 40 AT rounds and successfully protected the office premises, weapons, case files, vehicles, communication equipment, and detainees from arson and looting. The crowd's numerical strength was immense, and as they were attacking with improvised weapons, the situation was extremely challenging for police.

On Bhadra 24, the situation became complicated due to the crowd appearing extremely aggressive and organized, repeated attack attempts with improvised weapons, and the failure to receive expected cooperation from the Armed Police Force and other security agencies. The events of Bhadra 23–24 were suspect in terms of possible infiltration, and the challenges were compounded by lack of coordination, insufficient weapons, ammunition, and training. He stated that based on complaints registered after the movement, he arrested and prosecuted individuals suspected of vandalism and arson, and that no detainee was released on bail while he was in service. He concluded that he and his team performed their duties with dedication, restraint, and at great personal risk during the events of Bhadra 23–24. He also put forward suggestions that 'cooperation, coordination, modern equipment, training, and preparedness' are essential to prevent recurrence of such incidents.
50. Statement of Office Chief of Police Circle Gaushala, Deputy Superintendent of Police Shukdev Khanal:

On Bhadra 23, the day of the demonstration, I was deployed for security at the National Assembly Hall on Exhibition Road, where a program related to the Shahid Dharmabhakta National Transplant Center was being held with President Ramchandra Paudel as the chief guest. As such, I have no direct knowledge of the demonstration that day, the state of the crowd, use of force, activities within the Parliament premises, or the firing that occurred there.

On Bhadra 24, I handed over security responsibility of the office (Gaushala Circle) to Police Inspectors Shekhar Sah and Uddhav Pokhrel, and was deployed with a team of 13 personnel at 8:30 AM to secure the New Baneswhar water tank area as per the security plan. My assigned responsibility was to prevent the crowd from advancing toward the Convention Center, i.e., Baneswhar Chowk, and to cordon them off at that water tank road section. Initially the crowd was sparse and appeared to be chanting slogans such as 'Police and people are brothers,' but from 10:30 AM the crowd suddenly grew aggressive. Two individuals claiming to be commanders began responding in a threatening manner, and when 10–15 people from within the crowd pushed from below and simultaneously shoved the large crowd forward, the police barricade collapsed and a direct confrontation ensued. He observed that some individuals in the crowd appeared to be behaving as if under the influence of intoxicants.

After that, although I issued initial warnings and informed of the possibility of graduated force, the instruction from control was only to persuade and not to use force. As the crowd swelled to 8,000–10,000 by around noon, when additional forces were requested, a supplementary team of 40 personnel arrived from the District Police Range under a commander approximately 45 minutes later, but even those personnel were unable to hold back the crowd due to intense back-pressure. In the meantime, some aggressive individuals including one who identified himself as 'Dalit activist Khagendra Sunar' came out loudly from inside a building onto the road, and I immediately took them into custody and sent them to the compound. Meanwhile, Armed Police Force personnel were present under a commander, but although the commander was heard instructing his personnel to assemble in one place, the Armed Police Force took no decisive action in that chaotic situation. As the crowd continuously hurled tires, furniture, ropes, and stones at police, I attempted to take up positions around Gate No. 2 (the side gate of the Parliament). However, after the crowd launched a concentrated attack on Gate No. 2 from all four sides, the human barricade police had set up at that gate began to weaken.

As the crowd entered the Parliament premises through Gate No. 2, he witnessed four trucks with Nepali Army personnel inside the Parliament premises exit completely through the gate within five minutes  an event he described as extremely shocking and demoralizing. After the Army and APF withdrew from crowd control, only police remained at the gate, and the crowd successfully entered the Parliament Building. My team and I then moved toward Thapagaun junction as the risk at that location had increased significantly; from there, the Parliament Building could be seen burning with plumes of smoke rising on all sides. After receiving orders from control to proceed to the nearest safe unit, when we tried to go to our own office we learned it had already been set on fire, so we proceeded via Hanumansthan to District Police Range Bhadrakali. In the course of confronting the crowd, I sustained a bruise on my leg from a stone. At 9:00 PM that night, when agitators rammed a truck into and broke the Kathmandu Range gate and began vandalism, police fired blank rounds and successfully drove the demonstrators away from that location. That same night, after 03:30 AM, having taken shelter at the Tribhuvan Airport Army garage, he felt the full horror of the situation.

On that same day, when an enraged crowd committed extensive arson, vandalism, and looting at Gaushala Police Circle, the office building, five prefab structures, more than 20 computers, more than 370 motorcycles, 9 four-wheeled vehicles, case files (527 pending, 26 active), and many government and personal items were completely destroyed by fire. A large number of weapons were also looted, including SLRs, rifles, gas guns, shotguns, various types of pistols, and handcuffs. Subsequently, Gaushala Police recovered a total of 8 weapons  5 pistols, 1 SMG, 1 shotgun, and 1 gas gun  along with more than 200 rounds of ammunition. His assessment is that the situation could not be brought under control because effective support was not received from the Armed Police Force and Nepali Army on the 24th, neither agency cooperated in a coordinated manner, and in some situations they stepped aside even before the police. His conclusion is that since those appearing to be under the influence of intoxicants and criminal elements were seen mixed in with the crowd, the possibility of infiltration was very high  not just Gen-Z. His duty experience on the 24th was one of 'a tragic, frustrating, and demoralizing day that dealt a huge blow to government structures and police prestige,' with weak coordination among police, APF, and the Army; an excessively large crowd; inadequate resources; absence of strategic support; and the crowd's aggressiveness placing the entire state security apparatus under pressure.
51. Statement of Office Chief of Police Circle Janasewa, Deputy Superintendent of Police Krishna Kumar Chand:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, I was deployed from 8:30 AM as a Striking Team Commander with callsign C-66 and a team of 11 personnel for perimeter security of Singha Durbar. My team was equipped with SLRs, gas guns, shotguns, mini Chinese pistols, and related ammunition. The demonstration at Maitighar Mandala was initially normal  the crowd was proceeding from the Mandala toward Baneswhar carrying placards  but within a short time, individuals arriving on bullet motorcycles with scarves tied around their heads began instigating the crowd and attempting to push it toward the western and southern gates of Singha Durbar, which increased the security sensitivity. I then immediately erected barricades and established a two-tier structure to prevent crowd entry  a baton-and-shield plus public address team at the front, and an armed team at the rear. As the situation began turning forceful for a period, I notified Victor Control and requested additional support, and after the Armed Police Force and other teams arrived, the situation remained under control.

On that day, no force of any kind was required in my area, and neither my personnel nor anyone else needed to use firearms or tear gas. After the curfew order was issued, I made announcements via the public address system to inform the public, though some demonstrators disregarded the order. After the curfew was declared, as per Victor Control's orders, my team set out at approximately 13:30 via the Anamangal corridor and the steel bridge route to go to Baneswhar, but as the crowd was demonstrating at that location, it was not possible to proceed. Returning and heading to Baneswhar via Thapagaun route, as demonstrators had blocked the road at various points there as well, we proceeded on foot to New Baneswhar. Upon receiving information through Victor Control that demonstrators from Maitighar Mandala were heading via Wupareshwar through Sundhara toward the National Trauma Center, I immediately called gold and silver traders along New Road by phone to inform them to close their shops in order to prevent possible looting.

In the evening, while reviewing the day's events and making preparations with forecasts for the next day, it was learned that 8 bodies of individuals who had died in the course of treatment at the Trauma Center and approximately 85 injured persons were receiving treatment  as a result of which, relatives and well-wishers of the victims vented their anger at and attempted to create disturbances with police deployed for security; they pelted stones at the Nepal Telecom office in Sundhara, burned 2 private motorcycles at Nagasthan, and also set fire to the Sundhara Police Beat. That very night on the 23rd, since the detention facility at Police Circle Janasewa was in a dilapidated condition, detainees were transferred to the Range and the detention cell was vacated. Beats, CRV, and QRT units under my command were also merged and brought to Police Circle Janasewa for safe keeping. From early morning on the 24th, all active police personnel were placed on full-gear standby; in addition to office security, a large team was formed and deployed; security guards at banks and gold and silver shops were alerted; patrolling was conducted in nearby areas; gold and silver traders were requested not to open their shops; and a strong team was dispatched for the security of the injured at the Trauma Center  all of which were briefed and prepared accordingly.

From early morning on Bhadra 24, I was monitoring all activities in the Janasewa Police Circle area. On that day, the Armed Police Force was not present in a coordinated manner; the Army appeared to have arrived, but due to the extreme aggressiveness of the crowd, expected support was not received. After an enraged crowd set fire to the Paropakar Police Beat at around 11:00 AM, the situation rapidly deteriorated. In the New Road area, Pipalbot, Bhitta, Asan, and Lagan, aggressive crowds continued stone throwing, petrol bombing, and vandalism. Police dispersed the crowd through public address announcements, baton charges, and ultimately by using 19 tear gas shells when control could not be maintained.

Due to the aggressive crowd, the old dilapidated building of Janasewa Circle (case section and detention cell) was completely destroyed by fire. From that same crowd, 3 Chinese pistols, 4 revolvers, and 521 rounds of ammunition were looted. Through subsequent police operations, 2 Beretta pistols, 1 mini Chinese pistol, and 1 Vrint Three rifle along with 10 rounds of ammunition looted from various police units were recovered and returned, but the 3 Chinese pistols and 4 revolvers from Police Circle Janasewa remain to be returned.

On that day, I had heard Victor Control relay orders that in the event of arson or physical attack on the office, a list of detainees should be prepared, records kept, and detainees released to prevent any human casualties; to keep weapons, communication sets, and vehicles secure and oneself safe; and to use rubber bullets with minimum damage as necessary without using lethal weapons as much as possible. In accordance with the orders, detainees were removed from the risk zone and transferred to District Police Range Bhadrakali, and sensitive office materials, weapons, and communication equipment were securely placed at the Nepali Army's Shardulajang Battalion.

He expressed suspicion about the possible infiltration of various vested interest groups in the incident and noted that an impartial investigation is necessary. Throughout the crowd's demonstration, police were at great risk; the building being dilapidated and the security structure being weak made defense difficult; curfew prevented local support persons from coming out; and the inability to stop vandalism even with the Army present negatively affected police morale.

He offered the following suggestions: It is extremely necessary to make the relevant stakeholder agencies responsible for strengthening the state's intelligence apparatus. During the Gen-Z movement, thousands of prisoners escaped from jails nationwide, creating a major security challenge for the state. Therefore, to prevent recurrence of such events and to strengthen jails, even though partial responsibility has been given to the Armed Police Force Nepal, the complete security responsibility of all prison offices throughout the Kingdom of Nepal should be immediately handed over to the Armed Police Force. The conditions under which the Armed Police Force should be mobilized for crowd control in addition to Nepal Police must be clearly determined before deploying the Armed Police Force, and clearly defining and assigning the duties, responsibilities, and authority of police and the Armed Police Force when the Armed Police Force is deployed would be effective.
52. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police Gyan Bahadur Bista, Office Head of Police Circle Balaju:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), from approximately 15:00 PM, a team of 15 under my command was deployed to secure Gate No. 2 of the Office of the President. On that day, my area remained normal and my team did not have to use any force. I received information regarding the implementation of the curfew at around 12:30 PM via radio set and news. Since I was not deployed for the security of the Parliament House, I had no direct information regarding the activities there. After finishing duty and returning to the office at approximately 22:00 PM, a debriefing was held regarding the incidents, and instructions were given to all personnel to remain on standby in full gear for the following day.

On the morning of September 9 (Bhadra 24), after receiving information that a crowd was gathering in the Banasthali-Kharibot area, field monitoring was conducted. Local residents were agitated following news that one resident of Banasthali had died due to gunfire at the Parliament House on Bhadra 23. The crowd turned aggressive, reaching a state of burning tires and pelting stones. After receiving information that the crowd was moving toward the private residence of the Honorable President in Bohoratar, attempts were made to stop them by creating a cordon. Megaphones, persuasion, tear gas, and warning shots into the air were used, but the crowd continued to grow. In the meantime, after receiving information that a crowd of thousands was advancing toward Police Circle Balaju, I returned to protect the office. At Police Circle Balaju, the crowd surrounded the premises from all sides, launching stones and petrol bombs, and committing arson and vandalism. Significant damage was caused to office rooms, barracks, detention cells, vehicles, communication equipment, and case files. On that day, a total of 7 weapons were looted, including 1 Hornet, 2 shotguns, 3 Berettas, and 1 Chinese pistol; to date, 2 items have been recovered, while approximately 1,731 rounds of ammunition are missing. Additionally, 13 communication sets, 33 chargers, and 49 batteries were destroyed. Motorcycles and vehicles were burnt to ashes. As the crowd set the office on fire and smoke entered the detention cells, creating a risk of suffocation for the prisoners, approximately 74 detainees were released after their records were noted. Subsequently, after securing as many weapons as possible, we moved to the Nepal Army's No. 1 Brigade in Nepaltar at approximately 15:30 PM.

During that day's movement, arson and vandalism were carried out at Police Circle Balaju, Police Post Nepaltar, Police Stations Jitpurphedi and Dharmasthali, Police Bits Chamati, Gumba, and Sangla, Temporary Police Post Bohoratar, and Traffic Police Post Nepaltar, among others. Furthermore, within the Circle area, arson and vandalism occurred at the Bhat-Bhateni Super Market building in Machhapokhari, the Honorable President's private residence in Bohoratar, the Tarkeshwar Municipality office and ward offices, the Nepal Bureau of Standards & Metrology office, and the Central Fisheries Promotion and Conservation Center office in Machhapokhari.

To me, it appears that various interest groups had infiltrated the movement. Groups on motorcycles, anarchic elements, and external mobilization turned the protest violent. Expected assistance from the Armed Police Force did not arrive sufficiently, but assistance was received from the Nepal Army, and the crowd was dispersed.

From this incident, I have felt that improvements such as underground weapon storage, high walls, escape routes, and fortification of police offices are necessary. Currently, the reconstruction of Police Circle Balaju is underway with the help of locals."
53. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police Janardan Ghimire, Office Head of Police Circle Singhadurbar:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), I was deployed for security at Maitighar Mandala with a team of 20 personnel and call sign Circle-71. On that day, protesters began gathering in a normal but organized manner from 9:00 AM. After they advanced toward New Baneshwor around 10:30 AM while shouting anti-government slogans, I regularly updated the status to '40 Control' and, following the control's instructions, mobilized duty while remaining in the prohibited area of the Mandala. Since there was no stone-pelting, arson, or any violent activity from the protesters in the Mandala area, the need for 'minimum and proportionate use of force' did not arise. I repeatedly informed the general public about the curfew order issued at 12:30 PM via megaphone, but a large number of protesters ignored the order.
Regarding the incidents that occurred within the Parliament House premises and the use of force conducted there, I have no information about the details as I was not deployed to that location.

On the morning of the 24th at 9:00 AM, I was again deployed at Maitighar Mandala and had verbally given the responsibility of office security to '210'. Around 3:00 PM, after receiving information that vandalism had started in many places around Singhadurbar, I headed toward the south gate of Singhadurbar, during which protesters even snatched away my communication set.

At the same time, upon receiving news that a large number of protesters had entered the office and started arson and looting, the limited manpower at the office loaded weapons and ammunition onto a 1-ton truck to take them to the Nepal Army Logistics Battalion for safety. However, when the gate did not open, we transported them all the way to the Police Headquarters in Naxal. Due to the arson and vandalism by the protesters, extensive damage was caused to the office computers, case files, various technical equipment, and arson was committed on 2 private four-wheelers and 2 motorcycles. Furthermore, protesters looted 412 rounds of live cartridges for shotguns, one magazine with 7 rounds of ammunition for a 7.62 mm Type 54 Chinese pistol, one magazine for a Type 77, and one magazine for a Beretta. During police operations, 10 units of 9mm CF98 pistols, 10 extra magazines for the same, 4 units of 7.62mm Type 54 Chinese pistols, 6 extra magazines for the same, 1 unit of SMG, 1 unit of INSAS rifle, 10 units of Taser guns, 31 units of handcuffs, and 1 communication set, which were found hidden in a discarded state at the waste collection center in Bhrikutimandap, were recovered and handed over to the District Police Range, Kathmandu.

On the 24th, damage occurred due to arson and vandalism within the Singhadurbar premises at various ministries including the Prime Minister's Office, the Supreme Court, the District Government Attorney's Office, the Department of Roads, the Ministry of Health, the Office of the Attorney General, the Kantipur Publications building, 13 vehicles of K & C Automobile Pvt. Ltd., 7 vehicles of Sipradi Trading Pvt. Ltd., 7 vehicles of Honda Kathmandu Pvt. Ltd., 6 vehicles of CG Company, and 1 vehicle of Samar Jung Company, as well as their offices. In many places,
due to simultaneous attacks, the excessive number of the crowd, low manpower, and the inability of the higher office to send additional forces, we were unable to prevent the said damage.

While there was cooperation from the Armed Police Force on Bhadra 23, there was a lack of expectedly adequate coordination to prevent damage on the 24th; however, the Nepal Army assisted the police in defense while the south gate was under attack. He felt that performing duty amidst low manpower, limited resources, and an extremely aggressive crowd had a serious impact on the morale of the security personnel.

To prevent the recurrence of such incidents in the future, he suggested that the state should prioritize adequate study, strengthening of security structures, enhancement of technical resources, expansion of manpower, and boosting of morale.
54. Statement of Deputy Superintendent of Police Raj Kumar Shrestha, Office Head of Police Circle Durbarmarg:

Regarding the protest, I was deployed from 08:30 AM with call sign 0-61 to secure the area around the prohibited zone of Harit Griha (Vice President's Residence) in accordance with the security plan of the District Police Range, Kathmandu. On that day, the situation in that area remained normal.

Due to the serious situation on Bhadra 23, special alertness instructions were issued by the police command regarding the possibility of further protests at various locations on the 24th. Clear instructions were received to first use persuasion, megaphones, and all gentle measures of control if a crowd gathered; to use tear gas only if absolutely necessary; and to avoid using Lethal Weapons under any circumstances. According to the security plan received from the Police Range Kathmandu, I was deployed on duty at the south gate of the Palace Museum from 08:30 AM on Bhadra 24.

However, as the day progressed, the situation began to grow abnormal. After receiving information that a large number of protesters were heading toward the Vice President's Residence, he immediately moved to that area. On the road in front of the Dairy, the Nepal Army was tightening security by forming a cordon. While thousands of protesters were coming toward that area chanting slogans, I felt that the crowd was being stopped to some extent while I was

requesting them via megaphone to go through the north gate of the museum. But at that time, another large group arrived from the direction of the Department of Mines, and after the Nepal Army suddenly abandoned the cordon, the police team got stuck in the middle, creating an extremely risky situation. Consequently, the team was forced to retreat, and the protesters set fire to the Vice President's Residence. The same crowd then targeted the office, carrying out arson, vandalism, and looting. Regarding weaponry, there was general damage to the office (however, the Crime Investigation Branch was completely destroyed by fire); looted/destroyed weapons included 3 Beretta pistols, 1 .303 rifle, and 1 small gas gun. Regarding ammunition, 45 rounds of INSAS ammunition, 25 rounds of Type 54 pistol ammunition, 10 rounds of 5.56mm blank ammunition, and 8 rubber bullets for shotguns were lost. Regarding inventory, 15 computers, 4 laptops, 17 full gear sets, 62 transparent shields, 24 helmets, and 2 motorcycles were destroyed by fire. He stated that among the looted items, only one .303 rifle was recovered and handed over to the Police Headquarters, while the remaining weapons are still missing. From that day's demonstration, Aloft Hotel was vandalized with partial damage, the Bhat-Bhateni store was completely destroyed by fire, the Non-Resident Nepali Association (NRNA) building sustained partial damage from vandalism, and the private residences owned by Binod Chaudhary, Basanta Chaudhary, and Arun Chaudhary were set on fire.

I heard a transmission from Victor Control stating that in the event of arson or physical attack on the office, a list of detainees should be prepared and recorded, and then the detainees should be released, while ensuring that weapons, communication sets, vehicles, and personnel remain safe to avoid any human casualties. As the situation became complicated, some ammunition was sent to the Police Headquarters in Naxal, some was secured in vehicles, and 3 detainees were released after recording their details.

The reasons for being unable to stop the abnormal situation on the 24th included: thousands of aggressive protesters against an extremely low number of police, simultaneous attacks in many locations, the inability of additional police or fire engines to arrive promptly, the crowd starting to attack as soon as they saw the police, and the crowd responding to megaphone warnings by throwing petrol bombs, rods, and bricks at the police.
