His understanding is that although the Nepal Army was stationed around the Parliament building, it did not have the expected impact. While expressing regret over the heavy losses despite the efforts of security personnel to protect sensitive structures and public safety amidst dangerous conditions while performing their duties, he submitted suggestions to the Commission. To prevent such incidents in the future, he recommends addressing the shortage of non-lethal equipment, developing a clear coordination structure among security agencies, strengthening technology-based information systems, and providing additional training on crowd behavior psychology.
117. Statement of Armed Police Force (APF) Nepal No. 20 Battalion HQ (Disaster Relief) Sinamangal Commandant and District Security Committee Member, Superintendent of Police Jeevan K.C.:

I was not aware of the Gen-Z youth generation having prior permission for the protest on September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23); however, a meeting of the District Security Committee on September 7 (Bhadra 22) decided to create an integrated security plan involving all four security agencies to arrange security in a coordinated manner. According to that plan and the security plan of APF Nepal No. 9 Pashupatinath Brigade, forces were mobilized from the morning of the 23rd at Maitighar, prohibited areas, and sensitive locations using personnel from Kathmandu's No. 20 and 18 Battalions, No. 24 Company Jamal, No. 21 Battalion Lalitpur, No. 15 Battalion Kavre, No. 22 Battalion Bhaktapur, and the APF Academy Matatirth. Necessary coordination was maintained with the Nepal Police at the district and field levels.

On September 8 (Bhadra 23), I was in overall command at the Command Post located at the District Administration Office, Babarmahal, with the call sign 'Kilo Charlie-1.' There were 638 Armed Police personnel deployed under my command. Forces were stationed at a minimum platoon level at Maitighar Mandala, the Supreme Court, in front of Everest Hotel, the District Administration Office mode, Tinkune, Bhadrakali, and the Bagmati Bridge. Additionally, Field Reserve, Unit Reserve, Water Cannon, and Ambulance units were kept in a state of readiness.

Around 9:30 AM, a peaceful crowd of 300–350 people was seen, but by the time they reached the front of Everest Hotel, the number increased to over 3,000 and gradually became aggressive. Around 11:30 AM, after the crowd reached 20,000–25,000, attempts were made to break the barricades of the prohibited area. After breaking the barricades, the mob attempted to surround the Parliament building and enter with motorcycles. Despite the use of megaphones, persuasion, water cannons, baton charges, and tear gas (AT), it became difficult to bring the crowd under control. After stone-pelting, vandalism, and arson began in the Baneshwor area, tear gas and blank fires had to be used according to the principle of minimum use of force to prevent entry into the Parliament building.

According to the decision of the District Security Committee, a curfew order was issued starting at 12:30 PM. Although information about the curfew was provided via megaphones, the unruly mob continued to defy it. I personally entered the Parliament building from the Command Post for field command and control. The use of water cannons, baton charges, tear gas, and ultimately blank fires was carried out according to the principle of minimum force.

Following the incident, information arrived regarding 17 deaths, hundreds of injured protesters, and a large number of injured personnel from the Nepal Police and Armed Police. I did not have confirmed facts as to whether all deaths and injuries resulted specifically from the use of force. From the APF side, 50 rounds of blank fire from INSAS, 9 rounds from SLR, and 266 tear gas shells were used. Even though some staff became agitated when an ambulance was set on fire, I maintained command and control and instructed them to exercise restraint.

Evaluating the challenges likely to occur on September 9 (Bhadra 24), the curfew was re-issued from 08:30 AM, and approximately 958 personnel were mobilized under my overall command. Additional forces including 3 SSPs and 7 SPs were mobilized from the Head Office. Forces were stationed at more than 17 locations, and Field Reserve, Unit Reserve, Water Cannon, and Ambulance units were kept ready.

However, due to destructive groups infiltrating the crowd, it became difficult to prevent all physical damage with limited resources and manpower. Arson and vandalism occurred at various government offices, constitutional commissions, party offices, VIP residences, and security bases. The Minbhawan security base was completely damaged, and weapons and materials were looted. To take control of the situation in Kathmandu, 407 tear gas shells, 143 blank rounds, and 33 rounds of blank fire from .12 bore rifles were used.

On the 24th, a policy of minimum force was adopted with the aim of preventing further human casualties amidst the anarchy seen in the capital and across the country. However, due to the lack of non-lethal weapons, it was felt that casualties increased when lethal weapons had to be used in certain situations. It appeared that there was planned infiltration by various groups into the protest, although I did not have solid evidence of this in my hands.

In my capacity as the overall commander for the Kathmandu district of the Armed Police Force Nepal, while feeling some shortcomings in not being able to keep the entire situation under control, I see that the field commanders were mobilized according to the principle of minimum force to prevent further human loss under limited resources, manpower, and extreme circumstances. To prevent such incidents in the future, I see a need for training, mobilization policy, non-lethal weapons, logistics, morale boosting, intelligence improvement, clear demarcation with the Nepal Police, and additional Vital Installation Security Units.

I express confidence that the Commission of Inquiry will conduct a factual investigation and reach a just conclusion regarding the use of force, the role of security personnel, available resources, and the circumstances. The events of September 8 and 9 seem linked to growing frustration among the youth, corruption, and weaknesses in service delivery. My understanding is that some elements attempted to destabilize the state by capitalizing on this. I consider it necessary to strengthen the capacity, policy, logistics, and morale of security personnel in the days to come.
118. Statement of Armed Police Force (APF) Nepal No. 20 Battalion HQ (Disaster Relief) Kathmandu Chief, Deputy Superintendent of Police Suman Chalise:

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), I was deployed as a platoon commander in front of the Everest Hotel in New Baneshwor, as per the security plan prepared by the District Security Committee's decisions and operations branch. [Note: Original copy of statement Page-8, under Serial No. 117 of Schedule-5].
I have received training regarding the Use of Force. Since the initial estimation was that the protest would remain peaceful, the security plan was prepared on that basis.

When I was deployed for duty that day, I received 18 SLR units, 1,800 rounds of ammunition, 9 gas guns, and 180 tear gas shells from APF No. 20 Battalion, and I was on duty with the call sign KC-6. Following the demonstrations, 6 rounds of blank fire and 50 tear gas shells were expended, and I returned the remaining materials to the office. Although the protest was initially peaceful, the crowd grew 10–12 times larger than estimated. My team and I, positioned 100 meters behind the Nepal Police deployment, witnessed the crowd breaking through the barricades. We had estimated the crowd would only arrive from the Bijulibazar side; since there were no barricades on other routes at that location, the sudden entry from all directions made the situation complex, creating a risky environment where the police themselves were caught in the middle of the mob.

After the curfew order was issued at 12:30 PM on September 8, the Nepal Police and Armed Police provided information through joint megaphones, but the crowd did not implement the order. Later, when the mob aggressively pushed the gate at the West Gate of the Parliament building to attempt entry, we initially used megaphones, persuasion, baton charges, and tear gas. However, seeing that thousands of protesters were about to enter a sensitive structure, I took direct permission as a commander from SP Jeevan K.C., who had arrived at the location, and fired 6 blank rounds from a .12 bore rifle. We were instructed from the morning not to use final force (shooting) and to attempt containment as much as possible; therefore, no lethal fire was used except for blank fires. No shots were fired from my team toward protesters outside the Parliament building premises.

Following the use of force, I attempted to rescue and transport the injured from both the police and protester sides to the hospital. However, after the mob set fire to the APF Hospital ambulance, we had to find alternative arrangements. Out of the 103 Armed Police personnel injured that day, 27 were seriously injured, and two (ASI Gita Khatri and Constable Kumar Lama) were immediately rushed to the hospital. I only returned from duty at 11:54 PM (23:54) that night.

Regarding the 24th, as I was injured, I was not on field duty but remained on unit-standby. Regarding statements that the Armed Police were present but did not help on the 24th, we provided all possible assistance to the Nepal Police despite the nationwide tension, massive crowds, and the abnormal flow of circumstances due to sudden risks.

It can be assessed that there was clear infiltration in the protests of the 23rd and 24th. Protesters arrived carrying domestic weapons, crowbars, and pickaxes; dozens of petrol bombs were thrown to set the water cannon on fire, and even an ambulance arriving for humanitarian aid was burned, which shows that the 'peaceful movement' was pushed toward a violent turn. Although military deployment was expected, there was no concrete intervention, leaving the heavy burden of security responsibility on the Nepal Police and Armed Police.

He requested a just evaluation that does not decrease the morale of professional and dutiful security personnel deployed in risky conditions. He concluded that the root causes of the incident were a lack of information, an extreme shortage of security equipment, policy ambiguity, and a background of public dissatisfaction. I wish to advise that for the future, we must strengthen policy clarity, investment in security mechanisms, citizen-friendly decisions, timely information systems, and grievance management systems. Additionally, if police officers on duty are provided with a safe environment and morale, they can provide even more effective service in such challenging conditions.
119. Statement of Armed Police Force (APF) Deputy Superintendent Pramod Rai, Acting Commandant of APF Nepal No. 22 Battalion HQ, Guheshwori, Bhaktapur:

Although no formal program for the Gen-Z protest was scheduled in Bhaktapur district on September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), it was known that demonstrations could occur in Kathmandu. Accordingly, additional support duty was mobilized from my office: one team under Inspector Surendra Kumar Shrestha to Tinkune, Kathmandu, and another under Inspector Hare Ram Sharma Dahal to the Federal Parliament building in New Baneshwor. I was not deployed for field duty that day. From the deployed teams, 15 Armed Police personnel were injured and 73 tear gas shells were fired on that day.

Due to the events that occurred in Kathmandu on September 8 (Bhadra 23), orders were received from higher authorities and commanders regarding potential incidents on the 24th, stating that massive anti-government public demonstrations could occur in our area of responsibility, and to prepare a security plan for mobilization. Orders from the District Security Committee and the respective office specified the use of only minimum force following the sequential stages of the use of force. In accordance with those orders, six teams were formed in Bhaktapur district and mobilized to maintain peace and security across the district.

At approximately 11:35 AM, around 1,000–1,200 Gen-Z protesters arrived at the District Administration Office, Bhaktapur, and began surrounding the office and chanting slogans; from 12:00 PM, they committed vandalism and arson at the District Coordination Committee and the District Administration Office. Similarly, protesters surrounding the private residence of then-Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli in Balkot became anarchic, pelting stones and vandalizing the residence and the security personnel deployed on all sides, even attempting to set security personnel on fire and committing arson on the building. On that same day, vandalism, arson, and looting occurred at 32 locations, including the Juvenile Court, District Commissioner's Office, District Police Range, and the private residences of former ministers Mohan Basnet, Gokul Baskota, and Mahesh Basnet, former MP/UML Politburo member Ananda Prasad Pokharel, Ward Chairman Rabindra Sapkota (Suryabinayak Municipality-7), and the daughter of Suryabinayak Municipality Mayor Basudev Thapa, along with other police and government offices. Additionally, 79 juvenile delinquents escaped from the Juvenile Reformation Home in Sanothimi, Bhaktapur. On that day, even the APF Nepal No. 22 Battalion HQ, Guheshwori, Bhaktapur, where I am stationed, was vandalized by protesters, and 9 tear gas shells were fired to bring the situation under control.

On the 24th alone, 54 tear gas shells and 25 blank/blank rounds were fired by my subordinates; 39 protesters were slightly injured during crowd control, and significant damage and looting occurred, including SMG magazines, ammunition, some logistics, vehicles, and communication equipment from the office and teams on security duty. He also mentioned that several police personnel were injured and sent to the hospital through coordination with the Red Cross and Nepal Police, and assistance was received from one platoon of the Nepal Army.

As for the causes of the incident, he pointed to infiltration in the crowd, groups carrying domestic weapons, a lack of public trust, a lack of dialogue between the administration and the movement, weak information analysis, and a lack of resources in emergency situations. He stated that it would be appropriate for the Commission of Inquiry to recommend to the government a clear mobilization policy, the provision of technology-equipped non-lethal weapons and resources for security personnel, and the arrangement of technology-based intelligence for the development of a reliable information mechanism to prevent such incidents in the future.
120. Statement of Armed Police Force (APF) Deputy Superintendent Sangam Shrestha, Commander deployed for the security of Nakkhu Jail:

I served as the Commander at Nakkhu Jail from July 16, 2025 (2082/04/01) to October 19, 2025 (2082/07/02). The morning of September 9, 2025 (Bhadra 24) at Nakkhu Jail was normal, like any other day. Anticipating that a crowd might gather and head toward Nakkhu Jail due to the events of September 8, I had prepared barricades for the main gate using available concertina wires and iron poles. Around 10:00 AM, the relevant bodies of Nakkhu Jailthe jail administration, Nepal Police, and the Armed Police Forceheld intensive discussions on how to manage and control potential mob violence. The Jail Administrator reached the conclusion that additional security personnel needed to be deployed outside the jail and was making requests to the District Security Committee. Subsequently, the three blocks within the jail were locked, and the leaders (Naike/Bhai-naike) of each block were summoned to the jail administration office and instructed to remain safe. Protesters were moving outside the jail toward the Ministers' Quarters in Bhaisepati. Around 10:30/11:00 AM, ASI Ramesh Chaudhary from the North Post reported that a crowd of approximately 700–800 had gathered about 700–800 meters north of Nakkhu Jail, chanting slogans and burning tires. Similarly, information was being received via social media that buildings in Harisiddhi, Lalitpur, and other areas were burning, security personnel were casualties, and weapons were being looted. The Jail Administrator repeatedly reported to the relevant authorities for additional security. That morning, a mobile team of 10 personnel under an ASI from the APF Jail Security Base was deployed to the Ministers' Quarters. Additionally, for jail security, I deployed 15 personnel at the main gate under a Junior Police Officer, 7 personnel in each block, and a regular team of 5 at the perimeter light points.

Around 1:00 PM (13:00), the crowd began gathering at the main gate of the jail. The mob, numbering 3,000–4,000, was issuing threats of vandalism, arson, and attack, demanding that Rabi Lamichhane be released within 10 minutes.

After the Assistant Sub-Inspector (ASI) at the main gate reported this to me, I gathered the security personnel for a briefing and was instructing them to use minimum force. At that point, the Nepal Police commander and the Jail Administrator informed me of an order to bring out Rabi. (From Block 2, the Nepal Police Inspector, the Jail Administrator, and I escorted Rabi Lamichhane to the psychosocial hospital located inside the jail, under the pretext of allowing him to meet his family). Around 2:00 PM (14:00), an enraged mob broke the main gate and entered the jail premises. Security personnel managed the crowd for about an hour through persuasion and baton charges. During this time, Rabi Lamichhane came from the hospital to the main gate where the crowd was, saying he would reason with them. As his safety was our responsibility, we separated him from the crowd and took him a few meters back to the Pipalbot Chautari (resting place under a peepal tree) inside the jail. He was asking for a microphone to address the crowd and stated, 'I am legally inside the jail; I will not go out without a formal letter.' Due to the noise of the mob, the situation became nearly unmanageable for the security personnel. At approximately 3:00 PM (15:00), that same mob took Rabi Lamichhane out of the jail.

Around 3:30 PM (15:30), the crowd returned to the jail and engaged in stone-pelting, vandalism, and arson. I personally entered the field to disperse the crowd using persuasion, restraint, and minimum force, which successfully scattered them for a while. Subsequently, the Nepal Police team left the jail premises. We were then left with only 46 personnel from the APF base. The Nepal Police had a strength of 52 personnel. The mob vandalized the jail administration office, set fire to vehicles, broke the locks of all three blocks, and freed the prisoners. Some prisoners even came to the security personnel's location to seek safety, while the mob was shouting at us to throw down our weapons, take off our uniforms, and run away. The mob freed the prisoners from Nakkhu Jail; this occurred between 4:00 PM and 4:15 PM (16:00–16:15). One Nepal Police constable also came to the APF security base for safety; he had one .303 rifle. We managed to secure his weapon and gave him an APF uniform to wear.

The Armed Police Force used 21 tear gas shells, 6 blank rounds, and 5 rounds of blank fire. Our APF personnel acted with restraint and persuasion, and managed to save the psychosocial hospital inside the jail, the Vipassana Meditation Center, one Bolero pickup belonging to the jail administration, one water tanker, one psychosocial hospital vehicle, and the CRC block.

Operations were carried out with human casualties in mind, given that the mob was enraged and its numbers were so high that the only alternative would have been the use of lethal weapons. The Armed Police Force took necessary initiative and made every effort within its capacity and the given circumstances to control the chaotic situation. From the morning, the situation was handled with constant restraint, despite the possibility of fire from the crowd, especially following social media reports that security personnel's weapons had been looted.

On September 9, 2025 (Bhadra 24), there were 1,281 prisoners in the jail. The prisoners were freed from the jail between approximately 3:50 PM and 4:15 PM (15:50–16:15). The Chowkidar of the CRC block was Rabin Shahi and the Mul-Naike was Ashok Khadki; for the DRC block, the Chowkidar was Bishal Dhwoj Karki and the Mul-Naike was Ujjwal Khatri; and for the New Block, the Chowkidar was G. Saud and the Mul-Naike was Sajal Karki. Essentially, the prisoners who were given internal administrative responsibilities assisted the jail administration and security personnel at their respective levels.

Rastriya Swatantra Party Chairman Rabi Lamichhane faced security challenges inside the jail due to the enraged mob coming from outside. I had no knowledge regarding any letter given to him, as I was deployed in the field for crowd management. I was injured myself that day, and my Inspector was also injured. When Rabi Lamichhane was brought from Rupandehi Jail to Nakkhu Jail on August 16, 2025 (2082/04/31), a large number of Nepal Police were deployed for security outside Nakkhu Jail, but on September 9, 2025 (Bhadra 24), no additional security personnel arrived from anywhere. Analyzing the incident, the events of September 9 were much more violent, chaotic, and involved a larger crowd.
The APF remained deployed and did not leave the jail premises until 10:30 PM (22:30). We returned to the battalion only after the Nepal Army team arrived.

Reports of vandalism of government offices, arson, and looting of weapons had an impact on us security personnel. Furthermore, after the Nepal Police team stationed at the jail left their unit, it created an even more challenging situation to keep the Armed Police Force personnel deployed on restrained duty.

Security personnel stood their ground and remained engaged in crowd management even after the main gate was breached 2 or 3 times. The APF also worked to extinguish the fires on that day. I performed my duties professionally to the best of my intelligence and discretion. I state before the Commission that I did not perform any act to help any prisoner escape or assist in their escape.
121. Statement of Armed Police Force (APF) Sub-Inspector Subodh Kumar Budhathoki, serving at APF Nepal No. 24 Company 'A', Kathmandu:

Regarding the Gen-Z demonstrations on September 8 and 9, 2025 (Bhadra 23 and 24), I was on duty on the road in front of the Everest Hotel in Baneshwor on the 23rd. My platoon commander was Inspector Santosh Thapa. I was part of Platoon No. 1, out of the two platoons deployed from the No. 20 Battalion. I had a handheld radio with call sign 'Kilo Charlie-6' and a megaphone. Before deployment, we were briefed at the 7:30 AM assembly by DIG Suresh Kumar Shrestha and SSP Narayan Datta Paudel. We were told that this was a minor event involving children/youth lasting about two hours, that we should handle the situation normally, and that there would be no need for force. The briefing emphasized that these protesters are like our own siblings or children, and we should only focus on containment. This shaped our mindset as we deployed. On the 23rd, at exactly 9:15 AM, two platoons (35 members each) from No. 20 Battalion were deployed to Baneshwor. Each platoon was equipped with six SLR rifles, 1,800 rounds of ammunition, three gas guns, one megaphone, one TG camera, and 26 members were in full riot control gear. Around 10:15 AM, a Nepal Police DSP coordinated with our overall commander, DSP Suman Chalise, regarding personnel numbers; there were 105 APF personnel (3 platoons) and about 150–160 Nepal Police. From 10:30 AM, protesters began gathering in Baneshwor, forming a massive crowd. Initially, thinking they were ordinary people, we did not remove them from the Baneshwor police barricades, but we made general requests for them not to loiter unnecessarily. Around 11:30 AM, a huge mass gathered at the Baneshwor barricade and began chanting slogans. There were many people caught between the Police and APF lines. Suddenly, they pulled down the police barricades. The mob pushed the Nepal Police team back, and the APF was also pushed back toward Gate No. 2 of the Parliament Building. The crowd was massive and began pelting us with stones, bricks, and water bottles. Using the megaphone, I repeatedly requested them to remain calm, exercise restraint, and not engage in illegal activities. Ignoring our requests, they continuously attacked us at Gate No. 2 with stones, iron rods, and glass bottles filled with petrol. After the attacks became excessive, my commander ordered Platoon No. 1 to fire tear gas and six rounds of blank fire from SLRs. The crowd retreated slightly but surged back to the gate as we couldn't hold the forward position. They set fire to a police vehicle, climbed the gates to enter, and began dismantling the iron compound of the Parliament Building. The security teams felt surrounded and fearful. I then heard a command within the crowd to 'fire bullets' as the order had supposedly been given. I heard the sound of gunfire, but I did not see who gave the order or who fired. An ambulance that came to collect the injured was also set on fire by protesters. I was not aware of lethal firing being initiated by our side; we were repeatedly announcing via megaphone that a curfew had been imposed and people should go home safely. The protesters ignored this and continued attacking security personnel with various objects. The crowd finally left around 7:15 PM, and we returned to the office from the Parliament Building at 10:15 PM. On the 24th, I was on standby with an ambulance at the battalion premises. Regarding the 23rd, Constable Krishna Thakur from Platoon No. 1 was the one who fired six rounds of blank fire from an SLR.
122. Statement of Armed Police Force Constable Subina Lama, serving at No. 20 Battalion HQ (Disaster Relief), Sinamangal:

I was deployed under the command of a DSP during the Gen-Z movement on September 8 and 9, 2025 (Bhadra 23 and 24). On the 23rd, we were on duty from the morning in front of the Everest Hotel in Baneshwor. Our team consisted of 35 personnel equipped with gas guns, SLRs, and riot control gear, alongside 3–4 other APF platoons. We were stationed behind the barricades, behind the Nepal Police. As a constable, I had a full gear set, a baton, and a shield. The crowd of Gen-Z protesters was moving from Maitighar Mandala toward Baneshwor. Initially small, the crowd grew significantly. Being in Platoon No. 1, I was in the line alongside Nepal Police. As the crowd surged, they broke the perimeter and pushed against the police; it became difficult to control them. They pelted our APF team with stones. Following the commander's orders, we used baton charges. The mob continued to push and pelt stones, driving us back from Baneshwor to the Parliament Building. Many of my colleagues were injured by stones. One colleague collapsed after being hit in the head, and another was hit in the hand; I entered the Parliament Building premises along with my colleagues for safety.

Subsequently, the protesters vandalized the Parliament Building gate and waved the national flag from atop the gate. Later, orders were given and firing occurred; people were killed. A curfew was later imposed, and as the crowd was gradually brought under control, our team reached Baneshwor. From Baneshwor junction, my team moved forward to secure the road toward Shankhamul. We returned to the Sinamangal office around 10:00–11:00 PM for a meal rotation. On September 9 (Bhadra 24), I was again on duty from early morning at Baneshwor junction. There were 35 of us under a DSP, plus 4–5 other platoons from our battalion. Small groups began attacking us from all sides of the junction with stones and glass bottles. As we chased away smaller groups, the crowd grew larger. I was in full riot gear with a shield and baton. They kept pushing the police back. I used baton charges as ordered. Despite our efforts, the protesters became more aggressive and eventually pushed past the police to enter through Gates No. 1 and 2 of the Parliament Building. A colleague next to me was injured by a stone.

The protesters were shouting slogans against high-ranking officials and abusing the police. They pelted stones at the water cannon vehicle stationed at Gate No. 2. Our overall commander, the SP from No. 20 Battalion, led us to secure the water cannon; I was part of the team that managed the crowd to successfully bring the vehicle inside the gate. At that time, when the police fired tear gas, the protesters were picking up the shells and throwing them back at the security forces. When an APF ambulance arrived near Gate No. 2 to help injured personnel and protesters, the mob pelted it with stones, chased away the medics, and set the ambulance on fire. I witnessed the burning ambulance from the gate. I heard over the commander's radio that Gen-Z protesters were injured or killed and taken to the hospital due to tear gas, blank fire, and water cannons.

The protesters initially vandalized and set fire to the Parliament Building, a significant public structure. I was near Gate No. 1 at that time. After setting the building on fire, the protesters chased the police again with stones; I was injured by a stone strike during this time. I heard over the radio that our base in Minbhawan was also vandalized, weapons were looted, and the base was set on fire. As the mob became impossible to hold back, our team gradually retreated.

We eventually reached the rear gate of Singhadurbar. Despite being injured, I stayed with my commander and the team. We took refuge and stayed safe inside the Army barracks at Singhadurbar. I also heard reports about various government and private properties being damaged and set on fire.
5.1.6 Statements and Incident Report Details of the Armed Police Force Nepal:
123. Statement of the then Chief of the National Investigation Department (NID), Hutraj Thapa:

The National Investigation Department is the country's sole intelligence agency, and its core nature of work involves the duty and responsibility of collecting information on various subjects that impact national security. Information is gathered from various branch heads established at the central office, provincial and district heads, sources and undercover personnel deployed in various fields, as well as open-source personnel. Although the NID falls under the Prime Minister's Office, reports regarding information related to maintaining daily peace and security were regularly submitted to the Ministry of Home Affairs every day. Similarly, sealed reports regularly prepared by the Department were sent daily to the Right Honorable Prime Minister and the Chief Secretary. All activities occurring within the Valley are monitored by the Operations Division located within the Department's premises.

Since the beginning of September, videos and memes accusing the children of politicians of leading luxurious lifestyles funded by corruption were widely circulated under hashtag campaigns on social media platforms including TikTok and Reddit. In this context, on September 4, the government decided to ban 26 social media platforms, including Facebook and Instagram, citing non-compliance with registration rules. From September 6, calls were made on Reddit and TikTok under the 'Nepo Baby' and 'Nepo Kid' campaigns to take to the streets in front of the Parliament Building. It was assessed that the social media ban would directly impact digital businesses, online marketing, and digital content creation sectors, particularly affecting these groups during upcoming festivals like Dashain and Tihar. Consequently, a challenge was identified where creators and digital influencers might provoke their followers to join the movement for emotional or financial reasons.

It came to our knowledge that the Gen-Z group, identified as the organizers of the movement protesting against nepotism, corruption, and the social media ban, declared their protest would be peaceful. Three separate applications were submitted to the District Administration Office, Kathmandu: jointly by Ankit Malla and Khemraj Saud, jointly by Purushottam Yadav and Sabal Gautam, and by Sudan Gurung on behalf of 'We Nepal.' Approximately 35 individuals and groups expressed solidarity with the demonstration scheduled for September 8 (Bhadra 23) under the Gen-Z banner, and various artists, TikTokers, and YouTube content creators indicated their participation. Additionally, there was support from groups and parties eager to fulfill their own interests through the protest. While the actual number of Gen-Z participants was estimated at 3,200–5,000, it was projected that 10,000 to 15,000 people would participate due to the presence of solidarity and interest groups.

Globally, there is a rising trend of security threats originating from digital and cyber mediums; however, the Department's cyber and technical intelligence structure in Nepal has not been updated over time. For this reason, the Department failed to conduct deep tracking, source verification, and profound forecasting of signals issued via social media during the events of September 8–9 (Bhadra 23–24). He admits that the Department lacks the necessary equipment and skilled manpower to analyze videos, 'trigger' content, provocative messages, and the influence of foreign groups spread across platforms like TikTok, Discord, Reddit, and X. This is because a dedicated Cyber Unit has not even been established within the Department to date.

Prior to September 8 (Bhadra 23), the Department had prior information regarding social media posts by Gen-Z movement callers protesting corruption and the social media ban, with messages like: 'Sri Lanka did it, Indonesia did it, the Philippines did it, Bangladesh did itnow it's Nepal's turn! The time has come for us Gorkhalis to rise. The time has come to raise our voices.' This included AI-generated photos and videos showing public structures like the Parliament Building and Singhadurbar being burned or destroyed. However, following the events of September 8, there was no information regarding the mapping of top leaders' houses and plans for attacks on Netakhor.vercel.app.

Around 11:50 AM, I received information from the Valley Operations Division control room that protesters had broken the barricades in the prohibited area at New Baneshwor and moved toward the Parliament Building, with some reaching Gate No. 1 and starting slogans. Around 12:00 PM, after protesters pelted security personnel with stones, it was reported that police attempted crowd control using baton charges, tear gas, and water cannons. By 12:20 PM, information was received that police had fired several rounds of blank fire and rubber bullets as protesters attempted to enter the Parliament Building.

Following the demonstration on September 8 (Bhadra 23), a security meeting involving the heads of security agencies was held in the presence of the Right Honorable Prime Minister that evening. The Home Minister had already resigned. During the meeting, security chiefs provided detailed reports of the day's events according to their respective areas of responsibility. Information was shared that a committee had been formed to investigate the high number of casualties and their causes. Additionally, I informed the meeting about the afternoon's events and noted that because many protesters had died, there were plans to continue the movement nationwide. I reported that a 'Spot Map' had been circulated via the Reddit app showing where to protest, and appeals were made to the parents and relatives of the deceased to join. Regarding Kathmandu Valley, I informed them that the security situation would become more sensitive as protesters planned to demonstrate in their own areas from the morning before converging at the Parliament Building.

At 7:30 AM on September 9 (Bhadra 24), all heads of security agencies gathered at the Home Secretary's office. After discussing the security situation, we went to the Prime Minister's residence in Baluwatar under the leadership of the Home Secretary. While briefing the Right Honorable Prime Minister on the latest developments, the PM mentioned that certain parties had demanded his resignation, but emphasized that any resignation must be in accordance with the Constitution and law. He also informed us that an all-party meeting had been called to discuss the situation.

In Thapa's analysis of the September 8 (Bhadra 23) incident, 'underestimation' appears to be the primary problem within the security structure. He states that the first day's crowd size, initial behavior, the crowd regulation methods used by the police, the tension near the barricades, and the activities of certain provocative groups within the crowd created a massive risk for the second day.

Thapa stated that since the Parliament Building area is an extremely sensitive 'Red Zone,' the fact that the crowd was able to enter, the barricades were breached, and security personnel came under pressure represents a major weakness of the security mechanism. He notes that although the movement was leaderless, the Department had raised preliminary information regarding the potential infiltration of dissatisfied political groups, anarchist elements, and 'underground' groups, but the prevention mechanisms did not appear sufficiently active.

To prevent such incidents in the future, there is a need for adequate resources, high morale, and strengthening of all security agencies, including the country's only intelligence institution. It appears necessary to make all security agencies related to national security technology-friendly and to establish a high-level Inter-Agency Information Sharing Center under the head of the executive to ensure that information obtained is accessible to all security bodies.
124. Statement of Krishna Prasad Khanal, Chief of the Valley Operations Division of the National Investigation Department (NID):

During any movement or protest, the Department Head and Division Chief obtain real-time situational information through the Operations Division's Control Room via personnel deployed in 32 different sectors across the Valley, as well as through personnel specifically assigned by the Division using radio sets and mobile phones. Information exchange and coordination among the chiefs of all four security agencies in the Valley are conducted regularly via phone and through direct meetings as required.

Before the government's decision to ban social media, the potential security risks arising from it were not evaluated. However, reporting had been conducted regarding the widespread impact of the ban across various sectors and the resulting dissatisfaction. [Note: Original under Serial No. 123 of Schedule-5].

Posts were made from various fake accounts on social media platforms such as Discord, Telegram, Reddit, Bitchat, TikTok, Instagram, and Facebook with slogans like 'IF INDONESIA CAN, NEPAL CAN TOO!' and the 'Nepo Baby' trend, highlighting the luxurious lifestyles of high-ranking officials' children and calling for investigations into major corruption scandals. The Department lacks the technology to track such fake accounts.

The Department had information that the September 8 (Bhadra 23) demonstration would involve approximately 8,000 people, specifically adolescents, college-level students, and groups that have previously protested against the system and government in various forms. While the 'Nepo Baby' trend driven through alternative social media like Reddit, Discord, and X was a spontaneous social reaction, the analysis suggested that certain political interest groups might deliberately exploit this opportunity to fulfill their own interests.

Final force appears to have been used after protesters attempted to enter the Parliament Building by committing arson at Gate No. 1. Gunfire from nearby security personnel was first heard at 12:10 PM. While firing live ammunition may be appropriate for both the protection of sensitive installations and self-defense, it is felt that such a necessity may not have existed at that specific time.

On the morning of the 9th (Bhadra 24), during an informal meeting of Valley security chiefs at the Nepal Army Valley Pritna, discussions were held regarding the possibility of arson, vandalism, and looting at the private residences of party leaders, party offices, police stations, government offices, and commercial establishments.

Regarding former Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli's comments in the media claiming that the police did not fire but that criminal elements fired in a planned manner from the outside, I consider this an entry of baseless and irresponsible commentary from a person of that stature. Such comments from responsible individuals only increase confusion and rumors.

I believe there was infiltration not only by groups dissatisfied with the system and government but also by disgruntled factions within the major parties. No reports were received suggesting that the Armed Police Force personnel deployed on duty in the Valley on September 9 (Bhadra 24) failed to perform their tasks. These accidents occurred because the relevant agencies did not take the information provided by our Department seriously.

In the abnormal circumstances of September 9 (Bhadra 24), the NID has no direct role as it lacks access to the security arrangements of the Valley's prisons. The probability of the incident of prisoners escaping being pre-planned in connection with the protesters' activities seems low. Since there was no pressure from protesters at prisons other than Nakkhu on the 24th, the prison security administration itself appears accountable for the escape of inmates.

It is essential to develop an integrated mechanism for information exchange and analysis among high-level intelligence officers of the NID and the other three security agencies. Furthermore, the system for tracking new emerging security challenges arising via digital platforms must be strengthened. During protests or movements, management can be achieved by establishing an integrated command post consisting of high-level IT officers from all security agencies.

Regarding the events of the 8th (Bhadra 23), it is felt that due to the lack of necessary intelligence equipment and skilled manpower, the NID was unable to provide reporting to the government with the level of precise analysis that was required.

Following the events of the 8th (Bhadra 23), since the security agencies were in a defensive state, the analysis suggested that an attack on the Federal Parliament Building was inevitable. The centers of the Legislature, Executive, and Judiciary were destroyed by arson through plans woven overnight on the foundation of the anger generated by the events of the 8th.

The new generation cannot agree with an entirely traditional mindset; therefore, the state machinery must involve the new generation in every level of policy-making and state-governance opportunities. Additionally, the state must prioritize transparency and good governance. If the middleman system that has taken root in the past can be eradicated and the system itself institutionalized, it appears that relations with the youth generation can be improved alongside institutional reforms in every sector.

If the bill regarding National Intelligence operations is appropriately amended and given legal form to strengthen and professionalize the NID, the organization can become strong, professional, and competitive with organizations from other countries. For the immediate term, if legal support for the formation and operation of the intelligence mechanism, along with skilled manpower development, logistical support, and necessary budget arrangements are made, the organization would have the opportunity to prove its effectiveness. Furthermore, modern telephone tapping equipment brought at great expense remains idle due to a lack of legal framework; its operation alone would have enabled the acquisition of necessary information in the field of national security.
5.2. Statements of the Organizers and Incident Report Details:
126. Statement of Sudan Gurung:

I operate an apartment business, an IT company, and work as an Adventure Coach; I am also affiliated with the organization 'Hami Nepal.' Hami Nepal is a non-profit social organization registered at the Office of the Company Registrar, Tripureshwor (Reg No. 241179/077/078). Since its establishment, it has been involved in relief and rescue during the 2015 earthquake and subsequent landslides. We managed the entire operation of the Trauma Center hospital and coordinated with management at TU Teaching Hospital during the COVID-19 pandemic. During the second wave, we established and operated 270 beds at the COVID Unified Hospital (Bir Hospital) within three days. We distributed portable X-ray machines to the Trauma Center, saved 57 patients on high-flow oxygen in various hospitals, and sent 14 tons of relief materials to Turkey in coordination with the Nepal government during their earthquake. I have served as the Chairman of 'Hami Nepal' since its registration.

Under Section 4 of our charter, motivated by transparency and accountability in a country facing extreme corruption, we applied to the District Administration Office, Kathmandu on September 7, 2025 (Bhadra 22), for legal permission to hold a peaceful protest at Maitighar Mandala on September 8 (Bhadra 23). Our volunteers gathered at 7:00 AM with first-aid kits and banners reading 'YOUTH AGAINST CORRUPTION.' We organized protesters into groups of 20 with designated leaders to maintain discipline and prevent anarchist elements from infiltrating. On the day of the Gen-Z protest, a business named 'Chulho Daura' provided 80 cases of mineral water, which we distributed. A day prior, we received information through a separate Discord group about potential anarchist activities, including the construction of Molotov cocktails and plans for arson and looting. Anil Baniya and I personally informed the then-Police Chief of Kathmandu and the Cyber Bureau at Bhotahity about these threats. While we were at Maitighar around 9:00 AM, a group of 55 people led by Raksha Bam arrived and told us, 'This is not your protest, stay silent.' We handed over our microphone and speakers to them.

As the crowd grew, our volunteers repeatedly requested them to stay calm. To prevent escalation, we stood at the front with banners. We climbed onto a vehicle and urged protesters to remain disciplined. Raksha Bam also gave a speech from the vehicle, saying 'We won't throw stones today, we won't hit anyone.' However, the crowd soon began shouting 'We are going towards Everest Hotel' and started moving. We tried to control them using the vehicle, but the mass surged toward Baneshwor. I was in the vehicle with PK Khanal (current Minister of Youth and Sports), Raksha Bam, Purushottam Yadav, and Bablu Thapa. When protesters began tearing down photos of the then-Prime Minister from utility poles, we tried to stop them, but they continued. The crowd briefly stopped for slogans but was pushed forward by the mass behind them. Despite our volunteers' efforts to hold the line at the Everest Hotel barricade, the crowd breached it. I personally warned the youth repeatedly not to move toward the Parliament Building, which was a restricted zone. I even submitted a pen drive containing videos I made for my social media platforms advising students and youth on safety measures. Most of us were unaware of the curfew declaration because security forces had already started using tear gas, water cannons, and batons, and injuries had begun. Our team focused entirely on rescue operations, taking the injured to Civil Hospital and our medical camp at Maitighar. The noise was so great that police announcements via megaphone were inaudible to the protesters. I only learned about the deaths from gunfire once my team informed me.

While visiting the injured at Civil Hospital, I saw a police team aiming long guns at protesters inside the hospital. Dr. Toshima Karki (then MP) was also present. Together with hospital doctors and Hami Nepal volunteers, we requested the security forces to stay outside to prevent further conflict. About 15-20 of our volunteers guarded the main gate. Around 3:00 PM, I saw a 19-year-old girl in college dress who had been hit by tear gas smoke in the eyes, and another protester covered in blood from a head injury being carried by five people to Civil Hospital. Regarding the Discord platform, we had clearly announced on our official Instagram that any talk of violence or arson would result in a permanent ban and reporting to the police. Hami Nepal received no outside funding for the September 8-9 protests; I personally spent 50,000 rupees from my own funds, for which I have submitted screenshots.

On September 9 (Bhadra 24), I went to Civil Hospital at 7:00 AM to check on the injured. Later, from our office in Ranibari, I coordinated blood donations and mobilized volunteers. After the then-Prime Minister resigned, I went to the Parliament Building, but arson had already occurred. We tried to prevent further damage and then moved to Maitighar. Hearing reports of fire at Singhadurbar, we rushed there but found protesters had already entered. We then moved to a hotel near Baluwatar where two people were reportedly trapped; we helped clear furniture to create a path and urged protesters not to burn it. When I reached the Prime Minister's residence, the main gate was on fire. I then received a call stating that the Chief of Army Staff (COAS) wanted to meet at the Army HQ in Bhadrakali. I believe the nationwide chaos was a result of spontaneous citizen anger and retaliation for the killing of unarmed students and youth at the Parliament Building on the 23rd. On the evening of the 24th, while celebrating the PM's resignation near the Traffic Police Office, we urged protesters not to engage in arson. My team and I played no part in the violence; in fact, the IGP and SSP Narayan Datta Paudel called me asking to help protect the Police HQ. We mobilized volunteers via Discord to form human chains and protect both the Police and Army HQs. We collaborated with Army Officer Bhola Thapa to run a helpline and provide information to the Army throughout the night when police presence was zero.

During the second wave of COVID, Dr. Sanduk Ruit contacted me to offer support. I requested office space, and he provided rooms at the Barbara Foundation office in Baluwatar. We have no other affiliation or financial ties with the Barbara Foundation. I have submitted our audit reports and bank statements for the last three months as proof. For the rule of law to prevail, the government must ensure stability and accountability to prevent such tragedies from recurring.
127. Statement of Raksha Bam:

I am a Master's student at K&K College, New Baneshwor, and I previously worked at the Ujwal Thapa Foundation. Information about a protest on September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), demanding good governance and anti-corruption measures, was circulating widely on social media. Many groups supported the call. As a Gen-Z individual, I decided to participate. I discussed this with various Gen-Z groups, eventually meeting at Maitighar. While Miraj Dhungana's group had differing views and wanted to protest only after 6:00 PM, the rest of us agreed to gather at 9:00 AM, march to the Everest Hotel in Baneshwor as permitted by the DAO, and return to Maitighar for conclusion. On the morning of the 23rd, Sudan Gurung's group was already managing the site when I arrived. The crowd was massive. Around 10:30 AM, we began the march. We reached Baneshwor around 11:30 AM. Protesters at the front reached the barricades. I heard that scuffles broke out between protesters and security forces. Later, I heard the barricades were breached. Protesters expressed anger when security forces pointed weapons at them. While some urged restraint, the sheer volume of people broke the line. Reports came that people were trying to enter the Parliament Building. I heard gunfire. Realizing the situation was worsening, I urged students in uniform to take alternative routes home. By 1:00 or 2:00 PM, my friends and I moved to Anamnagar for safety. Baneshwor was like a battlefield. In the evening, I learned of the high number of casualties. The public was furious at the state's use of lethal force.

On September 9 (Bhadra 24), protests broke out nationwide. I was near the Parliament Building in Baneshwor. The crowd was trying to enter, and security forces fired from inside; I saw an unarmed protester killed right in front of me. This further enraged the crowd. They began vandalizing the building. While some of us tried to stop the arson, the crowd was beyond listening. Eventually, the Parliament Building was set on fire. The mob then targeted Singhadurbar, courts, and commercial establishments. This violence was a direct result of the state's failure to manage the peaceful protest on the 23rd and the subsequent killing of youth. There were also intoxicated individuals in the crowd who were aggressive. I also saw people identifying themselves as pro-monarchists. I believe all political parties had representation there. On the 24th, I even saw an intoxicated man with a gun acting recklessly. I hope this commission investigates potential infiltration. The state must provide compensation and respect to the families of the martyrs and the injured. Those responsible for the killings must be punished, as should those involved in destructive arson.
128. Statement of Khemraj Saud:

I am a student at Pashupati Multiple Campus and run a business called Badimalika Agriculture Company Pvt. Ltd. I am the founding chairman of 'Deshbhakta Yuva Nepal,' a non-profit that has campaigned for justice for Nirmala Panta, cooperative victims, and against the MCC, SPP, and the Citizenship Bill. We operate on personal contributions. I joined a Discord group on September 5, 2025 (Bhadra 20), following the corruption and 'Nepo Baby' trends. Ankit Malla and I sought written permission from the DAO to protest at Maitighar. I did not know Sudan Gurung or Purushottam Yadav before this. On the 23rd, I arrived at Maitighar at 9:00 AM. Our group of about 400-500 people wore black masks as planned. We carried banners against corruption and the social media ban. As the crowd grew, I spoke from a vehicle, emphasizing that Gen-Z is an intellectual generation and we should not destroy property or target individuals. We planned to march to the restricted zone, hold a corner meeting, and return. The road from Maitighar to Bijuli Bazar was packed. At Everest Hotel, I noticed the security presence was weakfew personnel and only a single line of barricades. The commander's address to the crowd was harsh. After some scuffling, the police retreated. We tried to hold the crowd, but police began using water cannons and tear gas, causing the crowd to surge forward. The road to the Parliament Building was suddenly empty as police retreated inside. Protesters surrounded the building. When gas was fired from inside, the crowd became enraged and started climbing the walls. I didn't notice exactly when the curfew began. I tried to call the CDO, but couldn't connect. Suddenly, bullets were fired. I was near the south gate; a protester next to me was shot. He begged me to save him. Three of us carried him to Civil Hospital, where he was declared dead. I rescued three people in total that day. At the south gate, I saw someone with hands raised being shot. Police were targeting protesters. At the hospital, police blocked relatives from entering and treated me rudely. I left for a friend's place in Bhaisepati that night.

On the 24th, I stayed at the room until 4:00 PM, watching the arson on social media. I posted appeals to not burn national heritage. Later, I met friends at Sundhara; we discussed the destruction of the judiciary, executive, and parliament. When we heard rumors of a plan to burn Pashupatinath temple, we rushed there to protect it, but found it safe and informed the Army. Later, we heard the Army wanted to talk. We met at the 'Hami Nepal' office and some went to Bhadrakali. That night, we received calls from girls' hostels about strangers at their doors; we alerted the Army. We discussed who should be the next PM. While Balen Shah was the first choice, he wasn't interested. We then approached Sushila Karki due to her legal expertise; she agreed, and the Gen-Z group recommended her name to the Army Chief. I believe there was no infiltration on the 23rdthe violence on the 24th was a reaction to the killing of 19 people. However, the specific acts of looting weapons and killing police officers likely involved elements with malicious intent.
129. Statement of Ronesh Pradhan:

I run Radhika Suppliers in Thulo Bharyang. I have long volunteered for 'Hami Nepal.' Following the social media promotion of the Gen-Z protest on September 8 (Bhadra 23), our team went to Maitighar with doctors, medicine, and an ambulance around 9:00 AM. The crowd was immense. We moved with the protesters toward Baneshwor. At the Everest Hotel barricades, the crowd tried to breach the line despite police warnings. We also urged them not to break the barricades, but they wouldn't listen and even turned on us. Intoxicated individuals were inciting the crowd to push. When the crowd pelted water bottles, police used water cannons and tear gas. The mass eventually broke through and became aggressive, throwing stones at the Parliament Building. Then, gunfire started. I saw a student in a Global College uniform get shot in the head; his brains were visible. I took him and two other injured people to the Trauma Center. The student died on the way. On the 24th, I assisted the injured at the Trauma Center. I heard about the fire at Singhadurbar but it was too late to help. At the Hotel Museum in Baluwatar, we heard people were trapped, but they had already been evacuated. Some were looting items from the hotel. I spent the rest of the day helping with evacuations at the Hilton Hotel.

On the 23rd, I saw intoxicated individuals inciting others. I believe the massive destruction of government and private property on the 24th occurred because anarchist elements took advantage of the Gen-Z protest to infiltrate and cause damage.

I operate an apartment business, an IT company, and work as an Adventure Coach; I am also affiliated with the organization 'Hami Nepal.' Since its establishment, it has been involved in relief and rescue during the 2015 earthquake and subsequent landslides. We managed the entire operation of the Trauma Center hospital and coordinated with management at TU Teaching Hospital during the COVID-19 pandemic. During the second wave, we established and operated 270 beds at the COVID Unified Hospital (Bir Hospital) within three days. 127. Statement of Raksha Bam:

I am a Master's student at K&K College, New Baneshwor, and I previously worked at the Ujwal Thapa Foundation in Panipokhari. Information about a protest on September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23), demanding good governance, national interest, and anti-corruption measures, was circulating widely on social media. Various social media groups were supporting the movement. As a Gen-Z individual, I decided to participate and contribute. Subsequently, I and other acquaintances discussed this with various Gen-Z groups. Our final meeting was held in the open space of Maitighar, Kathmandu. Among them, Miraj Dhungana's group had slightly different views and stated they would only protest at 6:00 PM (18:00) after the other Gen-Z groups had finished. During the discussions, it was decided to gather at Maitighar at 9:00 AM, march to the Everest Hotel in Baneshwor as permitted by the District Administration Office, and then return to Maitighar to conclude the program peacefully. Accordingly, I arrived at Maitighar around 5:00 AM on September 8 (Bhadra 23). Sudan Gurung's group was already there managing the site. Protesters continued to gather, and the crowd grew so large that Maitighar Mandala could no longer contain it. Around 10:30 AM, the march began from Maitighar. We reached Baneshwor around 11:30 AM. I was slightly behind, while the protesters at the front had reached the barricades placed by security forces near the Federal Parliament Building to mark the restricted zone. While the plan was to return to Maitighar from there, I heard that scuffles broke out between the front-line protesters and security forces; shortly after, I heard the barricades were breached. I later understood that when protesters reached the barricades and expressed their anger, security forces began pointing weapons, leading to a push-and-pull. In the process, the massive crowd broke the barricades, though some protesters had urged others not to push and even asked security forces to reset the barriers. Soon, news arrived that protesters had moved past the restricted zone to Baneshwor Chowk and were attempting to enter the Parliament Building. The sound of gunfire began to erupt. The situation became increasingly complex. At that time, a wounded protester came running by. Realizing the situation was deteriorating, I urged students in school uniforms to use alternative routes to go home. Some returned home, while others remained in the movement. Protesters were throwing stones, and security forces were firing indiscriminately. By around 1:00–2:00 PM, some friends and I moved to Anamnagar for safety. By evening, the Baneshwor area resembled a battlefield. Once things calmed down, I learned that a large number of protesters had been killed or injured by security fire. The public was extremely enraged by the state's lethal suppression on September 8 (Bhadra 23). I felt the people were restless, waiting for dawn. Due to the events of the 23rd, nationwide protests began on the morning of September 9 (Bhadra 24). That day, I was near the Parliament Building in Baneshwor. The crowd was massive and attempting to enter; again, security forces fired from inside, and an unarmed protester was killed right in front of me. After the killing, the protesters became beyond furious. They attempted to enter the Parliament Building by vandalizing it, and security forces used equal force. Some of us tried to advise against vandalism and arson, but the protesters were in a state where they wouldn't listen to anyone. Eventually, they vandalized and set fire to the Parliament Building. On that day, mobs also caused damage by vandalizing and setting fire to main administrative bodies at Singhadurbar, judicial bodies including courts, government offices, and residences of high-ranking political figures and commercial establishments. The arson and vandalism on the 24th were the result of the public anger caused by the state's failure to manage a peaceful protest and the killing of many youths on the 23rd. There were many intoxicated individuals in the protest who would turn on us when we tried to advise them. I also saw people identifying as pro-monarchists. I believe there was participation from all political parties. On the 24th, I even saw an intoxicated man in Baneshwor with a gun acting recklessly. I am hopeful that this commission will investigate whether any groups or individuals infiltrated to incite the protest and take legal action. The state must provide high honors and proper livelihood management for the families of the martyrs and the injured. I demand legal punishment for those responsible for the killings and for those involved in destructive acts like arson and vandalism.
128. Statement of Khemraj Saud:

I am a Bachelor's student at Pashupati Multiple Campus, Chabahil, and I also run a business called Badimalika Agriculture Company Pvt. Ltd. I have served as the founding chairman of a non-profit organization called 'Deshbhakta Yuva Nepal.' This organization has worked against social evils and served individuals and communities in need. Through this organization, we campaigned for justice for Nirmala Panta, for victims of cooperatives, and against the MCC, SPP, and the Citizenship Bill, as well as for the release of Bipin Joshi. The organization is run through the personal contributions of its members. During this time, trends regarding widespread corruption and 'Nepo Babies' were circulating on Facebook and TikTok. Expressing solidarity with these voices, I joined a Discord group on the night of September 5, 2025 (Bhadra 20). Information was circulating that a protest would be held in front of the Parliament Building at 12:00 PM on September 8 (Bhadra 23); however, as the Parliament is a restricted zone, we agreed to protest at the state-designated site, Maitighar Mandala. Ankit Malla and I sought written permission from the District Administration Office, Kathmandu. I had no prior acquaintance or consultation with Sudan Gurung or Purushottam Yadav regarding this. I reached Maitighar around 9:00 AM on September 8 (Bhadra 23). There were about 400–500 members from our 'Discord 03' group, though members do not personally know each other. We had agreed to wear black masks. We participated with banners saying 'End Corruption,' 'Social Media Cannot Be Banned,' and 'End Nepotism and Favoritism.' Other groups were also participating. As the crowd grew, I climbed onto a vehicle equipped with a megaphone and announced that 'Gen-Z is an intellectual and capable generation; as conscious citizens, we do not break trees or litter, we should not target individuals with slander, and we will protest against irregularities and corruption with restraint.' We had a general plan to march to the restricted zone, hold a corner meeting, and return to Maitighar. I was on the vehicle leading slogans. The rally filled the entire road from Maitighar to the Bijuli Bazar bridge. At Everest Hotel, police had placed barricades, but from what I saw, the security cordon was not strong. The number of personnel was low, and the barricade was only a single line. Furthermore, the police commander failed to address the crowd effectively and spoke quite harshly. Because a crowd contains people with various motives, a minor scuffle occurred, and the police retreated. We repeatedly urged the crowd not to move forward and held the line for a while. However, after the police continuously used water cannons, batons, and tear gas, the protesters ignored our pleas and moved forward. The police simultaneously retreated inside the Parliament Building. The road was completely empty; there was no obstruction from security forces up to the Parliament. Protesters surrounded the building. After tear gas was fired continuously from inside, the crowd became further enraged, and some began climbing the main gates and walls. I didn't realize when the curfew was imposed. We continued to ask the crowd not to engage in reckless activity. I tried to call the CDO to report the situation, but the call wouldn't connect. Suddenly, gunfire started. I was near the south gate of the Parliament; a protester next to me was shot and collapsed. Hearing him say 'Brother, save me' left me deeply distraught. Three of us carried him to the nearby Civil Hospital, where doctors informed us he had already passed away. While at the hospital, the influx of injured people increased. I returned to the Parliament area. I saw another protester fall from a gunshot and helped rescue him to the hospital. In total, I rescued three people. The last time I reached the south gate, someone was lying shot, and while another person was pleading with hands joined not to fire, that person was also shot. That day, the police were taking aim and shooting at protesters. After taking that person to the hospital, the police didn't even let us out. Police were deployed at the hospital gates, preventing relatives from entering. The police inside the hospital even behaved rudely toward me. Around 9:00 PM (21:00), I left the hospital and went to the Trauma Center after hearing of more casualties, but the police blocked our entry. I then went to a friend's room in Bhaisepati, Lalitpur, to sleep. There was no conversation on Discord that night. I stayed there until 4:00 PM (16:00) the next day. I learned of the arson and vandalism on the 24th through social media. I appealed via social media that national heritage like the Parliament and Singhadurbar should not be burned. After 4:00 PM, I and friends like Junal Gadal and Pawan Gautam entered the city to see the situation. Seeing Singhadurbar and the Supreme Court on fire, we gathered at Sundhara and discussed the destruction of the Judiciary, Executive, and Parliament, the PM's resignation, and how to move forward. In the evening, hearing rumors that the Pashupatinath temple was being targeted, we rushed there to protect it but found the situation normal and informed the Army. After hearing that the Army had called the protesters for talks, we gathered at the 'Hami Nepal' office in Ranibari, and some went to Bhadrakali. That night, we received calls from various girls' hostels saying unknown individuals were at their doors; we informed the Army. Discussions continued regarding the next Prime Minister. While Balen Shah was the first choice, he showed no interest. Given the critical situation, we approached Sushila Karki due to her legal expertise, and after she agreed, the Gen-Z group recommended her name to the Chief of Army Staff.

There was no infiltration in the protest on September 8 (Bhadra 23). However, the brutal suppression by the government, where 19 people were killed by gunfire within three hours during a peaceful protest, caused the public anger that brought people to the streets on the 24th. I believe that while the initial anger was spontaneous, the massive destruction of administrative bodies, the Supreme Court, private property, and police stations, as well as the killing of police and looting of weapons, involved infiltration by individuals with vested interests.
