At the South Gate of the Parliament building, I personally tried to maintain order, telling people, 'This is not our motive, please sit down,' and we began singing national songs like 'Rato Ra Chandra Surya.'

The situation escalated when a protester named Yadav and others climbed the gate. Police responded with tear gas and batons. As firing began, I saw protesters hit in the head and torso; the bloodshed was immense. We used national flags as bandages to stop the bleeding and crammed 5–10 injured people into each ambulance. My friend Sulav Raj Shrestha was shot in the head and died instantly beside me. We were chased into alleys where police continued firing. Later, I saw a person shot in the chest who died at Civil Hospital. I rescued approximately 20–22 people with horrific injuries, including missing limbs, exposed ribs, and a shot to the eye. Despite my friends Suresh Magar and Amrit Thapa trying to take me home, I stayed, inspired by our ancestors like Bhimsen Thapa. By 5:45 PM, I took a neck-wound victim to Civil Hospital on a motorcycle. My clothes were soaked in blood. The next day, I joined protests in Panauti. I believe the movement was a spontaneous outburst against corruption and 'Nepo Babies,' though I suspect the 'TOB' group and others on the 24th may have been infiltrators or anti-democratic elements.
132. Statement of Purushottam Subhaprabhat Yadav:

I am an unemployed journalism graduate. I participated in the anti-corruption movement as a concerned citizen, not as part of an organized group. Sabal Gautam and I had even requested written permission from the District Administration Office for a peaceful protest. On Bhadra 23, I reached Maitighar around 8:00 AM. The protest was peaceful, featuring slogans like 'Wake Up Nepal' and 'Say No to Corruption.' As the crowd grew, we decided to march to the Baneshwar restricted zone and return to Maitighar for a hunger strike. Security was initially thin, but as the crowd became aggressive and breached the single barricade, I realized things were getting out of control. My colleague K.P. Khanal and I sought safety inside a nearby college building. When I heard gunfire, I issued a press release via social media and newspapers, stating that the protest had been infiltrated and urging everyone to go home. We stayed hidden in the college all night for safety. I maintain that our intent was peaceful, but the event was hijacked by interest groups.
133. Statement of Bishnu Dhungel, Former FSU President, Nepal Law Campus:

I am a legal professional committed to the rule of law. I was arrested on Mangsir 10 and held for 18 days based on a 5–6 second viral video clip of me speaking about judicial corruption. My comments, which were part of my long-standing advocacy for reform based on Justice Hari Krishna Karki's 2078 report identifying 29 types of middlemen in the courts, were taken out of context and linked to the arson at the Supreme Court. I was not even present at the Supreme Court on Bhadra 24, and I do not know the individuals who set the fire. My 'attack' was a metaphorical call to fight corruption and misgovernance. My family and I have been deeply traumatized by this 'media trial' and the criminal charges against me, which are based solely on a snippet of speech without any physical evidence of my involvement in violence.
134. Statement of Tenzin Dawa Lama, Chairperson of TOB Group:

I am a tattoo artist living in Kapan. I am the founder of 'The Original Brother' (TOB), a motorcycle club. While I previously used the term 'Tibetan Original Blood' to represent my heritage, it is not a formal organization. Our club is a social service group with about 12 current members and no political manifesto. On Bhadra 23, I joined the protest after hearing about it on social media. My group of 10–12 motorcycles reached Bijulibazar around 11:40 AM. I was the person seen in viral videos wearing a jacket with 'Tibetan Original Blood' on it, but this was coincidental clothing choice. I shouted slogans like 'My Country, My Pride,' referring to a corruption-free Nepal, not any separatist agenda. During the protest, I was hit in the head by two stones while trying to tell protesters not to vandalize property. I left shortly after to seek medical treatment. I am a citizen by descent, but due to misinformation on social media, I have been evicted from my room and my shop. I am living like a refugee in my own country and request to be allowed to live a normal life as a Nepali citizen.
5.3. Statements from Physicians Involved in Treatment
135. Statement of Dr. Rupesh Yadav, National Trauma Center, Mahabouddha:

On Bhadra 23, I was on duty in the ICU. As the 'Gen-Z' protest involved students, we initially anticipated only minor security threats or light baton charges. However, after hearing media reports of gunfire, we cleared our operation theaters and wards to respond to potential casualties. Around 1:00 PM, an unexpected number of victims with serious gunshot wounds to joints, thighs, hips, and the spinal cord began arriving. We declared a health emergency and categorized patients into Red, Yellow, Green, and Black zones. By 11:00 PM, we had completed approximately seven major surgeries for life-threatening gunshot injuries. On Bhadra 24, new casualties arrived from Koteshwar and other areas, specifically those with shrapnel injuries. Some victims were bystanders hit while simply drinking coffee. Three patients died on the 23rd and one on the 24th; most were between 17 and 18 years old.
136. Statement of Dr. Binita Pradhan, K.M.C. Hospital, Sinamangal:

At KMC Hospital, we activated our Disaster Management Committee around 1:45 PM on Bhadra 23 as casualties began arriving. We converted 15 emergency beds into a 'Red Area.' Unlike typical protests involving limb injuries, these patients had gunshot wounds to the chest and head. We received 37 casualties on the 23rd, and twoSaurabh Krishna Shrestha and Subas Bohoradied that day. On the 24th, we treated 83 additional people, mostly for shrapnel and blast-related injuries. All treatment was free, and costs were submitted to the Ministry of Health. During the crisis, one of our ambulances was vandalized in Imadol, and the hospital faced threats from protesters demanding the release of a high-profile patient, former minister Mo. Aftab Alam, creating significant mental stress for the staff.
137. Statement of Dr. Ramesh Panta, Civil Hospital, New Baneshwar:

As a hub hospital, we were on alert from Bhadra 22. On Bhadra 23, casualties began arriving at 12:15 PM, quickly overwhelming our triage zones. We referred neurosurgery cases to the Trauma Center and Teaching Hospital, but ambulances faced massive blockages and vandalism. The hospital itself was affected by tear gas, and we feared arson as fires burned nearby. We treated 252 injured individuals on the 23rd, with three fatalities. On the 24th, we treated 210 people, including both protesters and police officers. To ensure safety, we removed the officers' uniforms and placed them in separate wards. Out of 462 total patients, 50 underwent surgery and 42 were referred to other hospitals.
5.4. Statements from Eyewitnesses
138. Statement of Subas Khatri:

I am a student at G.P. Koirala College and operate a snooker house in Chabahil. My friend Umesh Mahat and I were not participating in the protest on Bhadra 23. On Bhadra 24, hearing of unrest at the Gaushala Police Office, we went to observe. Around 1:00 or 2:00 PM, while we were standing opposite the station, a clash involving stones and gunfire broke out. Umesh was shot in the right chest and collapsed. We rushed him to Frontline Hospital on a scooter, but he was pronounced dead around 4:00 or 5:00 PM. I suspect the violence on the 24th was caused by outside infiltrators.
139. Statement of [Student Participant]:

I am a BBS student at Public Youth Campus. On Bhadra 23, after seeing the protest on TikTok, I went to Maitighar and followed the crowd to New Baneshwar. Protesters were throwing stones and even returning tear gas canisters thrown by the police. When the police opened fire, I heard the crowd shouting about injuries. While I was standing near the overhead bridge at the Everest Hotel, I personally saw a man, later identified as Ishwar Adhikari, get shot in the chest right in front of me. He was wearing a black helmet and jacket. I fled the scene in terror. I believe the subsequent vandalism on the 24th was the result of criminal groups infiltrating the angry crowd.
140. Statement of Kishor Ghimire, Security Guard at Thahakhabar.com, New Baneshwar:

I work as a security guard at the Thahakhabar building. On Bhadra 23, around 12:30 PM, protesters breached the restricted zone near Parliament. I personally witnessed 4–5 people fall after being shot by security forces from inside the Parliament building. Protesters searched our roof multiple times suspecting police snipers but found no one. On the 24th, a larger crowd returned. Around 1:00 PM, I saw the police surrender by raising their weapons, after which the protesters completely set the Parliament building on fire. A group of hundreds later entered our office, telling us to leave so they could burn the building because they claimed it belonged to 'Prachanda.' We turned off the power and prevented major damage, though they looted petrol from motorcycles on the premises.
141. Statement of Netra Prasad Chaudhary, Security Guard at Thahakhabar.com, New Baneshwar:

I work as a security guard for the Thahakhabar.com building in New Baneshwar through Bishwa Security Pvt. Ltd., Naxal. My regular shift is from 06:00 AM to 06:00 PM. On Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, I was on duty. On the morning of the 23rd, I received instructions from both my agency and the Thahakhabar office to be extra vigilant as a peaceful Gen-Z student protest was scheduled. Around 09:00 AM, I saw a large number of students and youths gathering and heading toward Maitighar. By 11:30 AM, thousands were returning toward New Baneshwar in protest. Around 12:30 PM, protesters breached the restricted zone and began pelting stones aggressively while advancing toward the Parliament building. By 1:00 PM, the crowd became even more aggressive as people converged from all directions. I then personally witnessed security forces firing from inside the Parliament building; I saw 4–5 people fall after being hit. Protesters rescued the injured. As the crowd grew more violent, security teams retreated inside the building. Throughout the evening, the cycle of attacks, injuries, and security forces firing followed by retreating continued. Protesters suspected shots were being fired from our office roof because tear gas canisters (shells) fired from the Parliament side were arching through the sky and landing nearby. Groups of 8–10 protesters searched our roof four times to verify this, but no security personnel were found there. During the chaos, they broke our office locks to hide inside. I saw about 15–16 people injured by evening. Protesters repeatedly threatened that our office belonged to 'Prachanda' and would be set on fire the next day. The situation lasted until 7:30 PM. On the morning of Bhadra 24, families of the deceased and protesters gathered near Gate No. 1, chanting slogans against the Prime Minister and Home Minister. Around 8:30 AM, a truck of Nepal Army personnel arrived, conducted blank fires, and dispersed the crowd toward Thapathali, Maitighar, and Tinkune.

Subsequently, APF and Nepal Police set up barricades. After the Army left, protesters gathered again around 9:30 AM from Koteshwar, Sankhamul, Thapathali, and Old Baneshwar. Thousands breached the barricades and surrounded the Parliament building. They attacked with stones, pickaxes, iron rods, and glass bottles. Around 11:00 AM, as protesters attempted to burn the building and police fired tear gas and bullets, I saw 6–7 more people get injured. An ambulance and a motorcycle were set on fire. Protesters pelted stones at our office, and tear gas entered our building. Suspecting police were on our roof, they forced their way to the 8th floor and, finding no one, discharged four of our fire extinguishers into the air. During the confrontation, a bullet pierced our gate and entered our changing room, punching a hole through a uniform hanging on the wall. Around 2:00 PM (14:00), after Nepal Police and APF personnel surrendered by raising their weapons and exiting, I saw the protesters completely set the Parliament building on fire. The Army was not present then. Until evening, I saw people looting goods and entering the premises with motorcycles. Some protesters were walking around in police uniforms carrying captured police weapons, conducting 'victory rallies.' Around 5:45 PM, hundreds arrived, told us to leave so they could 'do their work,' and set the building on fire, claiming it belonged to 'Prachanda.'
142. Statement of Mahesh Kumar Shrestha:

I have been operating 'HARDFORD EDUCATION' in New Baneshwar for 20 years, providing Japanese language training. My office is located on the northeast corner of the New Baneshwar intersection. On Bhadra 23, around 11:00 AM, the crowd grew thick and breached the barricades at Everest Hotel. Police used water cannons and batons to control them. Protesters were demolishing the Parliament wall and entering through Gate No. 1. At approximately 2:18 PM (14:18), a bullet suddenly shattered the glass window of my office. We fled to another room. Ten minutes later, I saw that the bullet had pierced the eastern window, shattered a wooden and glass cabinet, pierced an index file, and lodged into a cemented board wall on the western side. When I called an APF DSP to ask if we could leave, he told us to stay put and stay safe. we hid on the back balcony until 6:30 PM before going home. I believe the events of the 24th were triggered by the anger over the human casualties on the 23rd.
143. Statement of [Hotel Owner/Manager - Name not specified in snippet]:

On Bhadra 24, 2082, while I was at my hotel, Gen-Z protesters at Koteshwar Chowk attacked the Koteshwar Police Circle with arson and vandalism. Several police officers fled for their lives and entered my hotel through the roof. They arrived with torn clothes, injuries, and without shoes. Since protesters were chasing them with bricks, stones, and sharp weapons, I hid the officers in various rooms and locked them from the outside for their safety. When protesters entered the hotel asking for the police, I convinced them that only foreign guests were staying there and no police were present. The protesters shattered my CCTV cameras, solar panels, and windows, causing about NPR 100,000 in damage. Once things calmed down, I gave the officers my own clothes and shoes, fed them, and sent them off. Some left at 10:00 PM that night, and others the next morning. I do not recognize the individuals who broke my property. This statement is true.
144. Statement of Laxman Shrestha:

My house is located opposite the Eyeplex Mall on the road to Ratna Rajya Lakshmi College, facing the Parliament building. I run a mobile shop on the ground floor. On Bhadra 23, the situation was normal until noon. Around 1:00 PM, a crowd of students in uniforms arrived from Old Baneshwar chanting slogans. They were chaotic and pelted stones randomly. They vandalized an ambulance in front of my shop. Once they reached Gate No. 1, they committed arson. I saw one protester who had been shot being carried away on a motorcycle for treatment. The area remained tense all day, and the roads were impassable. Things only normalized around 7:00 PM. Later, I learned via social media about the multiple deaths and injuries.
145. Statement of Suraj Parajuli:

I was at my shop during the Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. Around 11:00–11:30 AM, a massive crowd including students in uniforms arrived at the Parliament building. Clashes occurred when police tried to stop them from entering. Stones were pelted at police and local houses. Protesters seemed intent on entering the building. I heard continuous gunshots. I saw one person fall near the overhead bridge at the intersection. Protesters took the injured person away in an ambulance. I went home to Minbhawan around 4:30 PM. I later learned from social media that 19 people had died and many were injured. It appears the police fired to protect a vital institution.
146. Statement of Saroj Tandon:

I have worked as a cashier at Amico Cafe for two years. On Bhadra 23, I reached work at 8:00 AM. Our cafe is located at the southwest corner of the Parliament intersection. Around 11:30 AM, I saw the streets filled with people, including students in uniforms. They were vandalizing railings, poles, and CCTV cameras. Police used water cannons and batons. Some people were using pickaxes and hammers to demolish the Parliament wall. Police announced a curfew via megaphones. Around 2:00 PM, I saw two people fall from gunshotsone in front of our cafe and one at the intersection. Protesters took them to the hospital on motorcycles. The clashes continued all day and only settled by evening.
147. Statement of [Security Guard - Name not specified in snippet]:

I work for G-Force Security at the Rastriya Banijya Bank, Baneshwar branch. On Bhadra 23, the crowd and police began clashing around 11:00 AM. Our office stayed open until 3:30 PM. Outside, there was tear gas, baton charges, and firing. The crowd shattered our north-side windows with stones. I learned of the deaths from social media. On the 24th, we were ordered to stay in civilian clothes and prioritize our own lives if things turned critical. Around 11:30 AM, protesters broke through Gate No. 3 and entered the Parliament building. They were seen carrying out tables, chairs, gas cylinders, and laptops. By 7:30 PM, protesters committed arson and broke into our office vault, looting approximately NPR 50 million in cash and 18 kg of gold and silver.
148. Statement of Advocate Parvati K.C.:

As a legal professional, I was at my Pavitra Laxmi Law Firm in Baneshwar on Bhadra 24. I learned that a curfew was imposed due to widespread arson and looting. It is my understanding that anti-state, chaotic, and violent groups infiltrated the peaceful Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, inciting violence and attacking security forces. Consequently, security personnel were forced to fire to protect the Parliament building and for self-defense. These same groups were involved in the arson, looting, and killing of security personnel. The planners and members of such groups must be identified and punished according to the law.
149. Statement of Sanunl Das:

Around 11:00 AM on Bhadra 23, the crowd at New Baneshwar became chaotic and started pelting stones at the Parliament, police, and local houses. Some were using tools to demolish the walls. Some tried to climb police posts to snatch weapons. Police announced a curfew, but the crowd ignored it. Fire broke out at the south gate. I heard about 10–15 gunshots. Around 3:30 PM, I saw two people lying on the road near the west gate as I was heading to my room in Thapathali.
150. Statement of Ram Shrestha:

I operate a mobile shop in front of the west gate of the Parliament. On Bhadra 23, protesters gathered from all sides. Security forces were initially trying to persuade them to protest peacefully. However, a group began inciting the crowd to enter the building, and some 'Bullet' motorcycles were being revved loudly to agitate the crowd. Protesters then breached barricades and pelted stones. Police used water cannons and tear gas. The protesters created a situation where police were forced to fire. On the 24th, a large number of people, including Gen-Z and others, arrived with domestic weapons, petrol bombs, and flammables. They attacked security forces, vandalized vehicles, demolished walls, and committed arson and looting inside the Parliament building and barracks.
151. Statement of Nabin Basnet:

On Bhadra 23, I was with friends at a hotel northeast of the Parliament building. I am not certain of the exact time, but hearing a commotion outside, I went to look and saw a crowd pelting stones and using catapults. Some people were demolishing the Parliament wall. The police were also firing tear gas and using batons. People wearing jackets labeled '703' were riding motorcycles unnaturally, which further incensed the crowd. Tension escalated when Members of Parliament inside attempted to leave in ambulances. During this, I saw a security official in an Armed Police Force (APF) uniform fire a shot, and someone fell in front of Gate No. 2. As soon as the person fell, people rushed them toward the hospital. The crowd had started thickening from 11:00 AM. I observed that the crowd, which mostly consisted of people from the Madhesi community, entered the Parliament building, committed arson and vandalism, and looted belongings. I saw one protester fall right in front of my house, but I didn't see who fired the shot. On the 24th, the Nepal Army was present, but arson occurred right in front of them; the Army remained silent spectators. I felt my house vibrate from the sound of gas cylinders exploding. The fire was finally extinguished by fire engines only on the 25th.
152. Statement of Biljes Karki:

Around 11:00 AM on Bhadra 23, a massive number of protesters gathered around the Parliament building and began demonstrating aggressively. Protesters of various ideologies started pelting stones indiscriminately. The police retaliated with water cannons, tear gas, and baton charges. I learned through various media outlets that a large number of protesters were killed or injured during the police action that day. The following day, the 24th, the Nepal Army, Nepal Police, and APF were deployed for Parliament security. Around 11:00 AM, protesters began breaking the Parliament wall and iron railings to enter; the Army left their positions and retreated. After entering, protesters set fire to security posts, barracks, and the Parliament building itself, as well as the APF barracks located at the nearby Department of Roads.
153. Statement of Sachin Bhandari:

On the afternoon of Bhadra 23, 2082, all shops closed after noise and chanting started at Baneshwar Chowk. At that time, many individuals in school and college uniforms were present. Protesters were pelting stones everywhere, and some were demolishing the Parliament wall and breaking the iron rods on top of it. The police used baton charges and tear gas. Shortly after, the sound of gunshots was heard. I also heard that the crowd blinded a policeman in one eye. People on motorcycles wearing jackets labeled 'TOB' and carrying black flags were provoking the crowd. On the 24th, I reached Sasto Market for regular work around 10:00 AM. By 11:00 AM, people started coming from all directions, though there wasn't much presence from the Gen-Z group that day. Around 2:30 PM, hearing that the Parliament building had been set on fire, I went there. The streets were full of people, and the building was burning fiercely. Protesters were beating and chasing the police. There was no police presence inside the building; the Army was there but acted as silent spectators. Some protesters were running away with whatever they could findflower pots, furniture, etc. Some were setting fire to parked vehicles.
154. Statement of Dalram Shrestha:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, many people in school and college uniforms were present at Baneshwar Chowk. Protesters were pelting stones and demolishing the Parliament wall. Police used baton charges and tear gas; gunshots were also heard. Individuals on motorcycles with black flags and 'TOB' jackets were inciting the crowd. The next day, Bhadra 24, people began gathering from all sides around 11:00 AM. Around 2:30 PM, they set fire to the Parliament building. There was no police presence inside, and while the Army was there, they remained silent spectators. Protesters were setting fire to vehicles and carrying away various items.
5.5. Statements from the Injured Regarding the Incident
155. Statement of Lokraj Upadhyay:

I participated in the protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. I was injured and hospitalized, so I did not participate on the 24th. I joined spontaneously on the 23rd, believing it was necessary to support national interest and good governance by controlling corruption. I reached Maitighar Mandala around 10:00 AM. Around 11:00 AM, we proceeded forward, and by 12:00 PM, I reached the south gate of the Federal Parliament in Baneshwar. When I arrived, protesters had already broken the police barricades and surrounded the building. Police were trying to disperse them. In the process, security forces used tear gas and rubber bullets, while protesters pelted stones. During this, a shot fired from the south side of the building wounded my left flank, and another shot hit slightly below my left leg, injuring me. My mobile phone, worth NPR 32,000, was also lost. Other participants helped take me to Civil Hospital. The next day, doctors operated and removed a metal bullet from my leg. I was discharged after five days and remain under follow-up. The government covered my treatment and provided NPR 20,000. Regarding the 23rd, I suspect that in the protest on the 24th by citizens angry at the state, some other group or individuals infiltrated to incite vandalism and arson. I request an investigation and legal action against the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, CDO, Police Chief, and field commanders who ordered the firing on the 23rd, as well as those involved in the destruction on the 24th.
156. Statement of Aryan Adhikari:

On Bhadra 23, the day called by the Gen-Z generation, I was in the Patan Industrial Area and was not an eyewitness. Hearing that the state had killed children by firing during the anti-corruption movement, I felt I should join and left my room around 10:30 AM on Bhadra 24. I went toward the Nakkhu Jail area hearing that Rabi Lamichhane was being released. Around 3:00 PM, when Rabi Lamichhane drove away in a vehicle, the crowd followed. The crowd and vehicle went together to Nakkhu Dobato Ring Road, after which the vehicle sped ahead, and I followed the crowd to Jawalakhel Chowk. There, I helped rescue 2-3 protesters who had collapsed from tear gas. In the evening, while moving toward Jawalakhel Chowk after having snacks, I saw protesters coming from Lagankhel who had seized an APF 'one-ton' van. They were planning to attack the Jawalakhel police. About 20-25 of us youth were trying to block the van, saying we shouldn't attack the police. Around 7:00–7:30 PM, the lights suddenly went out, and in the darkness, the police opened fire. I was shot in the right thigh and my right wrist. Sanjay Ghising, whom I know slightly, was there and dragged me to a house gate. Seeing the police following us, we tried to move again, but I fell. More shots were fired, and Sanjay was hit in the hand. We tried to reach Alka Hospital but saw another person fall from a police bullet. We hid in an alleyway in a local house for a while. Later, Sanjay's brother arrived on a motorcycle, and we reached Patan Hospital. I was hit by live rounds. I was hospitalized for eight days. The government bore all my medical expenses.
157. Statement of Smriti Rai:

I was a full participant in the Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. Around 10:30 AM, the rally was moving peacefully toward New Baneshwar, and I was at the back of the crowd. We reached the south gate of the Parliament building. We requested a designated space for a peaceful sit-in, but the administration ignored us and instead used baton charges and tear gas. The use of water cannons further angered the crowd. I saw about 10-15 protesters climb onto a small building inside the south gate. I saw one man on the roof bleeding and reached there to help lower him with the assistance of 2-3 others. He was bleeding from his head and legs, and the blood got onto my jacket. As I shouted toward the police, 'Who gave you the order to fire on the people's children?', a bullet came from in front and hit me in two places below my right knee. I screamed for help, and my friends took me to Civil Hospital in an ambulance. I was hit by rubber bullets. I was hospitalized for three days. My health is now normal, and the government covered all expenses.
158. Statement of Ayush V.K.:

I spontaneously participated in the Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. I was injured that day and did not participate on the 24th. When we reached the police barricades near the Everest Hotel, the police blocked us. I was at the front near the barricade. Protesters at the back began pushing forward, causing the front line to crash into the barricades. After some pushing, the police and APF started using water cannons and tear gas. Protesters began picking up tear gas canisters and throwing them back at the police. Eventually, the crowd broke the barricade. As the police retreated while firing, the crowd became more agitated and pelted stones. Protesters were being hit by rubber bullets. Around 12:35 PM, I was hit by a rubber bullet on the left side below my waist. Friends took me to Civil Hospital. I was told I had been hit by nine rubber bullets. Due to police intervention near Civil Hospital, my family transferred me to T.U. Teaching Hospital, Maharajgunj. Three rubber bullets are still inside my body. I am under follow-up. I believe the vandalism on the 24th was due to infiltration by other groups. I also want to report that various groups are using the names of the injured to collect funds nationally and internationally for their own benefit, and I request this be controlled.
159. Statement of Krishna Sunar:

I spontaneously participated in the Gen-Z protest on Bhadra 23, 2082. I was injured and thus not present on the 24th. I reached the south gate of the Parliament building around 12:00 PM via Ramshahpath. By the time I arrived, the protest had already turned violent. Around 1:00 or 2:00 PM, a bullet from an unknown direction hit me, and I lost consciousness. I woke up at Civil Hospital around 3:30 PM. I was shot near the shoulder joint above my left chest. I had surgery that day to remove one metal bullet, and another surgery on the 25th to remove four pellets (shrapnel). Doctors say two more pellets remain and will be removed later. Currently, my left hand does not function well. Civil Hospital referred me to T.U. Teaching Hospital because of nerve damage. The government has paid for my treatment and provided NPR 20,000, though I have had to buy some medicines personally, costing about NPR 8,000 so far. I believe the vandalism on the 24th was the work of infiltrators. I request action against the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, CDO, and field commanders who ordered the firing, as well as those involved in the arson on the 24th.
160. Statement of Manoj Bahadur Bohara:

The protest was initially peaceful but became aggressive. As far as I remember, security forces gave no prior warning or appeal for dialogue. I was shot in the limbs and head. the distance between security forces and protesters was about 10–12 meters. I don't know how I was treated as I was unconscious, but I saw no police surveillance at the hospital. I lost my mobile phone and cannot provide the video I had recorded. The state used excessive force irresponsibly. I believe there was widespread infiltration in the movement; Gen-Z would not burn down Singha Durbar or courts. Those responsible must be found and punished. Leaders should be selfless and responsible, and those who suppressed the movement and the corrupt should be held accountable. The then-government must take full responsibility.
161. Statement of Sanjit Yadav:

I spontaneously joined the protest in front of the Parliament building in Baneshwar. It was peaceful at first, but the atmosphere turned violent after the police fired tear gas. There was a significant security presence, but they gave no warning before using force. I was injured in the leg by a bullet and water pressure from about 10 meters away. My treatment was delayed. A major incident occurred due to state suppression. Such events could be avoided through corruption control and public awareness. There was infiltration on the 24th; the guilty should be identified and punished.
162. Statement of Krishna Thapa:

On Bhadra 23, I was in front of the Parliament Building in Baneshwar, and on the 24th, I was in the Dhobighat and Kalimati areas. I spontaneously joined the movement called by Balen Shah to protest against corruption and the misuse of taxes, and to advocate for good governance. The protest was unrestrained. Security personnel fired tear gas. No appeal for dialogue was made, and no information regarding restricted zones was provided. There was a heavy security presence. While attempting to assist and protect the police, I was pushed by the crowd into a police beat, where I suffered an electric shock and injured my hand. the distance between the police and security personnel was 12 meters. I am unaware of the specifics of my treatment, which took place under police surveillance. I have some photos and videos. The state committed suppression; I demand action against corrupt individuals and the provision of education, health, and employment for the victims.
163. Statement of Yubaraj Thapa:

I spontaneously joined the protest at the front lines in the Baneshwar area around 12:00 PM to protest against the looting of the people. The crowd was aggressive. Security personnel provided information about the restricted zone but did not appeal for dialogue. I was struck with batons on my buttocks and chest. I received treatment. I do not have any photos or videos. The government suppressed us. I seek compensation, policy reform, and judicial action through the Commission. All those involved in the suppression should be hanged. There was infiltration from political parties.
164. Statement of Surendra Jung Thapa:

I spontaneously participated in the protest in front of the Baneshwar Parliament Building at 12:30 PM to oppose corruption. The protest was peaceful. The police and APF presence was moderate. I was not aware of any information regarding restricted zone barriers before force was used, as those zones had already been breached. I was shot in the leg, elbow, and thigh. My treatment was obtained independently. I have photos and videos. I heard orders to 'shoot, shoot.' I do not know the ranks of those involved. The government suppressed us. I demand compensation, policy reform, judicial action, and I seek an apology. There was infiltration by political parties.
165. Statement of Dipen Gurung:

I spontaneously joined the protest in Dillibazar to demand good governance and action against corrupt individuals. I did not see the Army, and security personnel gave no warning. I was shot in the chest. I do not have photos or videos. Treatment was obtained independently. I heard orders to 'shoot, shoot,' but I do not know the ranks. The government suppressed us. I demand compensation, policy reform, judicial action, and an apology. There was infiltration by political parties.
166. Statement of Ashok Rai:

On Bhadra 24, I spontaneously joined the protest in Gaushala around 10:00 AM to protest against the shooting and killing of students. The crowd was peaceful, but security personnel gave no warning before using force. I was shot in both knees. I sought treatment independently. I have no photos or videos of the incident. I heard orders to 'shoot, shoot.' The government suppressed us. I seek compensation, policy reform, and judicial action from the Commission. There was infiltration by political parties.
167. Statement of Santosh Chaulagain:

I spontaneously participated in the protest from Maitighar to Baneshwar to demand good governance and action against the corrupt. Security personnel gave no warning. I was struck on the head by a baton and tear gas canister. I do not know their names or ranks, but I can identify them; I have photos and videos. Treatment was independent. I heard orders to 'shoot, shoot,' but do not know the ranks. There was infiltration by political parties.
168. Statement of Suryasan Prajapati:

On Bhadra 24, I spontaneously participated in a protest for good governance at the District Administration Office, Bhaktapur. On the 23rd, I was in Baneshwar. Security personnel included the Nepali Army and Police. They gave no warning. I was hit in the head by a gas canister. I have no photos or videos. Treatment was independent. I seek compensation, policy reform, and judicial action from the Commission.
169. Statement of Ramindra Paswan:

I joined the spontaneous protest against corruption at Gaushala Chowk only on the 24th, during which I was shot in the finger. The protest was unrestrained. There were many security personnel. The shots were fired from a distance of about 12 meters. There was a delay in treatment, but it was not under police surveillance. I did not recognize any security commander; I have no photos or videos. There was infiltration in the protest. I hope for improvements in laws and for the demands of Gen-Z to be met.
170. Statement of Yubaraj Upadhyay:

I spontaneously joined the protest at Baneshwar Chowk for national change and against corruption. The demonstration was peaceful. Security personnel fired shots without any prior warning or appeal for dialogue, hitting me. The distance between the security personnel and us was likely around 30 meters.

Treatment was obtained independently and on time. I can identify the face of the commander who gave the order. I have no photos or videos. I seek compensation, policy reform, and judicial action from the Commission. To prevent such incidents from recurring, corrupt individuals must be prosecuted and jailed. There was infiltration in the movement.
171. Statement of Ram Charmakar:

I spontaneously joined the protest in Kalanki for good governance and against corruption. There was a heavy security presence. The crowd was peaceful. Security personnel fired shots without warning before using force, hitting me in the eye. The distance between the security personnel and us was about 15–20 meters. There is a video of the incident. The state used excessive force. I demand compensation, judicial action, and policy reform. To prevent a recurrence, corrupt individuals must be punished. There was infiltration by political parties. The guilty must be identified and prosecuted.
172. Statement of Suman Bohara:

I spontaneously participated in the protest at Tripureshwar to advocate for the end of corruption and for good governance. The crowd was aggressive, and the presence of security forces posed a danger. Security personnel charged with batons without any warning or dialogue, hitting me in the leg. I received timely medical treatment independently. I do not possess any photos or videos. The state used excessive force. I demand compensation, judicial action, and policy reforms from the state. To prevent such incidents from recurring, corrupt individuals must be punished. There was infiltration in the movement; the culprits must be identified and prosecuted.
173. Statement of Rokaya:

I spontaneously participated in the protest at Baneshwar for good governance and an end to corruption. The crowd was aggressive and the security presence was threatening. Security personnel fired shots without warning, hitting me in the forehead. I received timely treatment independently. I do not have photos or videos. The state used extreme force. I seek compensation, judicial action, and policy reform. Corrupt officials must be punished to prevent future incidents. There was infiltration on the 24th; the guilty must be identified and penalized.
174. Statement of Vijesh Tamang:

I spontaneously participated in the protest from Maitighar to Baneshwar for good governance. The crowd was initially peaceful but became aggressive upon reaching the Parliament building. Police and APF were deployed. I am unaware of any warnings. I was shot in the leg. I received timely treatment independently. I do not have photos or videos. The state used excessive force. I demand compensation, judicial action, and policy reform. Corrupt individuals must be held accountable. There was infiltration; the guilty must be identified and punished.
175. Statement of Kumar Upadhyay:

I work as an accountant at Kumari Oil Stores. I did not participate in the protests against corruption and social media bans on Bhadra 23, nor on the 24th. While I was on the roof of my house, protesters were committing arson at the Gaushala Police Office. As police fired tear gas and bullets, a bullet struck me in the upper arm, penetrating through to my lungs and back. I am under regular medical care. The state has covered all medical expenses. I request the state to provide for my family's livelihood and take action against the guilty.
176. Statement of Badal Rai:

I participated in the protest personally. Initially, the crowd from Maitighar to Baneshwar was peaceful, but it became aggressive once the firing started. Police and APF were on duty from the beginning; the Army arrived later. Before the use of force, there was some information provided regarding the restricted zone. Efforts were made to control the crowd. I sustained a head injury from stone-pelting. The distance between me and the police was only 4–5 meters. Others took me to the hospital. I am receiving good treatment. I do not have photos or videos. The state misused its power. There was infiltration. I have given an interview to Onlinekhabar regarding the incident. Such suppression should not occur during peaceful protests; there should be change according to the spirit of the movement.
177. Statement of Ekta Shah:

I am 17 years old, daughter of Sushil Chand Shah and Bunu Shahi, living in Changunarayan-8. I recently passed Grade 12 and cleared the MBBS entrance exam through the Medical Education Commission; I am currently resting at home. On Bhadra 23, 2082, I spontaneously participated in the protest at the south gate of the Parliament building in Baneshwar. I am unaware of the leadership. During the protest, police first hit me on the right thigh with a baton. Later, I was hit by a rubber bullet in the arm. Since I was in my school uniform, I thought the police wouldn't hit me, so I didn't flee with others and continued chanting slogans. During that time, I was hit by a live round in my left knee and injured. I received free treatment at KMC Sinamangal and B&B Gwarko. I am currently undergoing physiotherapy at home. I face financial difficulties with daily treatment costs. Due to my physical condition, I am unable to travel elsewhere for studies; therefore, I request arrangements for me to study at the nearby KMC Medical College. I also request employment for my guardians.
178. Statement of Shambhu Dangal:

I am the operator and executive editor of Nayapost.com located in Jorpati, Kathmandu. On the morning of Bhadra 23, 2082, around 10:00 AM, we reached Old Baneshwar Chowk while filming a procession of students from Manaslu College and St. Lawrence College in Chabahil. When we reached the Bijulibazar bridge on the Maitighar-Baneshwar road around 11:30 AM, the procession began pushing against the barricades placed by security forces in front of the Everest Hotel. Security forces used water cannons, and the youth knocked down the barricades and rolled them toward the bridge. Around 12:00 PM, the protesters started pelting stones and security forces began firing tear gas. By 12:30 PM, the situation had escalated. The number of riot police on the road was small, while there was a heavy presence of security forces armed with sophisticated weapons inside the Parliament building. The New Baneshwar area reached a state where one could not see or breathe due to tear gas. The number of dead and injured from indiscriminate firing by security forces was rising. While I was filming these scenes around 2:30 PM near the Parliament gate, a metal bullet fired by security forces from inside the Parliament building struck my left leg, piercing through the ankle. I was admitted to the Trauma Center for 16 days. Doctors say it will take another year for my bone to fully heal. The primary responsible parties for the events of Bhadra 23 are the then-government, Prime Minister, Home Minister, and Information Minister. Although the government promised free treatment, only the medicine has been provided; costs for transportation and other essential supplies for the patient have not been covered.
179. Statement of Prakash Bahadur Bohara:

I joined the protest at Maitighar Mandala around 1:45 PM. As I approached Gate No. 1 at Baneshwar Chowk, I was suddenly shot in the thigh, above the left knee. My purpose for joining the protest was to oppose political corruption and advocate for a stable government, a strong economy, and to prevent youth from having to migrate abroad. I joined spontaneously from Maitighar. Initially, the crowd was peaceful, but it became aggressive after I was shot. I did not see security personnel anywhere in the crowd. Police fired without any warning or information about restricted areas. Friends took me to the National Trauma Center by ambulance. I did not see any security commander at the site; video of me being shot is available. I see this as a chaotic abuse of power. I do not run any NGO or INGO. There was no infiltration when the protest started, but as it turned violent, significant infiltration occurred.
180. Statement of Bhim Bahadur Damai:

On Bhadra 23, around 11:30 AM, thousands of people from the Everest Hotel side moved toward the Parliament building. Later, another group from Sankhamul joined them. After people in the crowd pelted stones, police used water cannons, batons, and tear gas. Police were announcing a curfew via megaphone. I was on a veranda; as people pushed the main gate from outside to enter, I was pushing back from the inside. At that moment, I heard a gunshot and felt a shock in my right arm; blood was flowing from my upper arm and my right rib area. The bullet had pierced the main gate and ricocheted into my arm and ribs. I stayed inside out of fear as it was impossible to leave. My landlady took me to Civil Hospital the next day. I couldn't work for 15 days due to the injury; I am now normal. I didn't pay for treatment, but I haven't received state support yet. I am in the process of getting an injury card from the DAO Kathmandu.
181. Statement of Santakumar Mehta:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, during the Gen-Z protest, I was at my Himalayan Java coffee shop in New Baneshwar. After 11:00 AM, the crowd thickened, and protesters gathered after breaking the barricade at Everest Hotel. Police were using water cannons and batons. Some people were trying to climb Gate No. 1 of the Parliament building. After tear gas was fired, people dispersed and came to my shop asking for water to clean their eyes. I saw two people fall on the road from shots fired from inside the Parliament compound, so I closed my glass door and watched from inside. Around 1:30 PM, I suddenly collapsed. I had been shot in the neck. An Everest Hospital ambulance took me to the Trauma Center, where I was in ICU/Ventilator for 8 days. The bullet had severed my jugular vein on the right and lodged in my neck muscle. While hospitalized, I learned via social media about the violence on Bhadra 24arson at ministries, the Supreme Court, and private properties, as well as the murder of police and looting of weapons. These acts on the 24th must have been triggered by the rage following the human casualties on the 23rd.
182. Statement of Gita Kumari Shah, injured by arsonists:

On Bhadra 24, 2082, I was returning from a watch shop at Koteshwar Chowk. I saw protesters surrounding Police Constable Chij Kumar Kumal, trying to kill him with sharp weapons, bricks, stones, and batons. When I tried to intervene to save him, they beat me as well. There was a girl aged about 22/23 in that group. As I pleaded with them not to hit him, Chij Kumar Kumal tried to hide behind me for safety. Protesters were shouting that he must be killed. While I was trying to save him, they threw a petrol-like liquid on both of us. Fire broke out immediately, and my body was engulfed in flames. I screamed for help and tried to remove my burning clothes. I was taken to Kantipur Hospital and then transferred to KMC, where I regained consciousness. About 40% of my body is burntmy right hand, from shoulder to foot. I am still undergoing treatment; I cannot walk or stand without assistance.
5.6. Incident Statements from Families of the Deceased:
183. Statement of Kaji Ratna Maharjan, brother of the deceased Vinod Maharjan:

My 34-year-old brother Vinod Maharjan worked in painting and handicrafts. He spontaneously joined the Gen-Z protest with friends. I don't know exactly where he was that day, but neighbors last saw him distributing water in Sankhamul around 12:00 PM. He was shot on Bhadra 23 and taken to the hospital by protesters, where he was declared dead at 1:58 PM. Based on social media videos, he likely died on the spot. Witnesses say security forces shot him twice in the face without warning in a repressive manner; the bullets exited through the back of his head. We received NPR 1.5 million from the government and NPR 500,000 from Lalitpur Metro. The Commission must take judicial action against the guilty.
184. Statement of Ambika V.K., wife of the deceased Santosh Vishwakarma:

My husband Santosh worked as a laborer after studying up to Grade 10. He spontaneously joined the Gen-Z protest personally. We last spoke at 10:30 AM on Bhadra 23. He was shot that day and brought to the hospital by fellow protesters, but died before he could be treated. I haven't received the post-mortem report. There was no negligence in treatment. Security forces fired without warning in a repressive manner. We received NPR 1.5 million in relief. I expect the Commission to ensure justice. Those responsible include the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, CDO, IGP, and commanders. To prevent a recurrence, the country needs good governance, an end to corruption, and job opportunities.
185. Statement of Anil Bhatt, brother of the deceased Vimal Babu Bhatt:

My brother Vimal was a Grade 12 student who joined the protest spontaneously. He was at Baneshwar. We last spoke on Viber. He was shot at Baneshwar Chowk and declared dead at Civil Hospital at 12:45 PM. There was definitely negligence in treatment. Neither the hospital nor police have taken a written statement from us. Security forces shot him in the chest without warning in a repressive manner. We received NPR 1.5 million. The Commission must ensure maximum judicial action, as it feels the government has done nothing for the victims. Policymakers and security leadership of that time are responsible. Future leaders must ensure corruption and mismanagement end so citizens don't have to die for raising their voices.
186. Statement of Dinesh Chaulagain, father of the deceased Abhishek Chaulagain:

My son Abhishek was a Grade 12 student. On Bhadra 24, he was shot at New Baneshwar while returning home from his office in Thamel. We last spoke on the 24th. I only learned of his death from the news at 6:30 PM on the 25th. He died on the spot. Security forces aimed and shot him in the head without warning in a very aggressive and repressive manner. We received NPR 1.5 million. I expect the Commission to ensure swift justice. The then-Prime Minister, Chief Secretary, Home Minister, CDO, IGP, and field commanders are responsible.
187. Statement of Parvati Adhikari, wife of the deceased Dev Kumar Subedi:

My husband Dev Kumar Subedi was a daily-wage guard. He spontaneously joined the Gen-Z protest. He was in Baneshwar on Bhadra 23 and in Kalimati on Bhadra 24. We last spoke on the 24th; he said he was at the protest and would be home soon. We have photos of friends helping him after he was shot. He was taken to the hospital by friends at 1:20 PM on Bhadra 24 and declared dead on the 25th. There was no negligence in treatment. Security forces fired without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million. The Commission must prosecute the guiltythe policymakers and security chiefs of that time. Strictest punishment is required to prevent a recurrence.
188. Statement of Abhilakh Bhatt, brother of the deceased Arjun Bhatt:

My brother Arjun Bhatt was preparing to go abroad for studies. He spontaneously participated in the Gen-Z movement against the then-government with his friends. He was at the Baneshwar protest on Bhadra 23 and in Kalimati on Bhadra 24; I was with him during the protest on the 24th. Police from the Kalimati Circle opened fire indiscriminately, and after he was hit, his friends rushed him to the hospital. He was declared dead at Vayodha Hospital. There was no delay or negligence in treatment because his death was instantaneous. Security personnel fired indiscriminately and without warning in a repressive manner. We received NPR 1.5 million as relief from the Government of Nepal. The Commission of Inquiry must take judicial action against the guilty. To prevent such incidents from recurring, all those responsible must face the strictest punishment.
189. Statement of Birendra Singh Saud, brother of the deceased Dipak Singh Saud:

My brother Dipak Singh Saud was a student. He participated spontaneously in the Gen-Z protest against the then-government. On Bhadra 23, he was with his college friends at the Baneshwar protest. Around 12:00–1:00 PM on Bhadra 24, he was shot at Kalimati Chowk and taken to Vayodha Hospital by ambulance, then transferred to Bir Hospital for further treatment. He was declared dead at the hospital on Ashwin 26. The post-mortem report shows one bullet wound to the head and another to the neck. There was no negligence in his treatment. Security personnel in Kalimati fired indiscriminately and without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million in relief. The Commission must provide justice by taking action against the guilty. Those responsible are the then-head of government, security personnel, and their commanders. To prevent a recurrence, the state must respect civil liberties and freedom of expression. There should be a guarantee of good governance, a corruption-free society, and employment so that fundamental rights are not violated.
190. Statement of Sunita Rai, wife of the deceased Sar Kumar Rai:

My husband, Sar Kumar Rai, studied up to Grade 8 and worked as a painter. He participated alone and spontaneously in the Gen-Z protest. He was involved on both Bhadra 23 and 24. After being shot on Bhadra 24, fellow protesters took him to the hospital, where he was declared dead. There was no delay in treatment as he had already passed away before reaching the facility. Security personnel fired aggressively without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million in relief. The Commission should take judicial action against the guilty. Those responsible include the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, Chief District Officer, and Inspector General of Police. To prevent such tragedies, the country needs good governance, a corruption-free environment, and job availability.
191. Statement of Narayan Shrestha, father of the deceased Dhiraj Shrestha:

My youngest son, Dhiraj Shrestha, had completed his undergraduate studies and was preparing to go abroad while also running a business. He spontaneously joined the Baneshwar protest on Bhadra 23. When he left home on the 24th, he told me he was going for the good of the country. He was shot that day and taken to the hospital by protesters; Tarkeshwar Hospital declared him dead. I am unaware of any negligence in treatment; I believe he died on the spot. Security forces fired without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million in relief. All high-ranking officials of that time are responsible. We must end corruption and provide jobs to ensure this never happens again. All guilty parties must be identified and punished.
192. Statement of Sujan Neupane, brother of the deceased Yogendra Neupane:

My brother Yogendra was a Master’s student who participated spontaneously in the protests. On Bhadra 23, he was at Baneshwar. The last time we spoke, he said firing had started and he was trying to return. We have video footage of him in the protest. He was shot in the head at Baneshwar Chowk and taken to Everest Hospital by Gen-Z peers, where he was declared dead. There was no delay in treatment, though the post-mortem was delayed. Security forces shot him in the head without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million in relief. Responsibility lies with the policymakers, security personnel, and commanders. We lost a family member to the government's aggressive style while he was peacefully protesting for good governance and against corruption.
193. Statement of Ram Krishna Shrestha, brother of the deceased Yog Shrestha:

My brother did not tell anyone he was joining the protest on Bhadra 23. Usually, he went to the gym and ate at 3:00 PM, but that morning at 10:00 AM, he asked his sister for food early, saying he had to go out. We have video he filmed on his own phone during the protest. He was shot in the ribs while filming at Baneshwar Chowk. Dr. Manish Bikram Shah declared him dead on Bhadra 23. I believe my brother lost his life at a young age due to delays in treatment. Security personnel were arrogant and repressive, firing without warning. We received the government relief. Those at the policy level and the commanders who ordered firing at sensitive body parts must receive the harshest punishment.
194. Statement of Mausam Kulung, brother of the deceased Pravin Kulung:

I had no prior knowledge of the protest plans on Bhadra 23. On the 24th, my brothers Anil, Pravin, and I, along with Pasang Dawa Sherpa and one other, left our room around 12:00 PM to join the thousands already on the streets. We moved toward Gaushala Chowk via Gopi Krishna bridge. At Chabahil, we lost track of Anil and Pasang. At the Gaushala intersection, the crowd was stone-peting the police station, while police fired tear gas and bullets from the roof. When an army vehicle arrived from Battisputali, we moved toward it while wiping away tear gas. My brother Pravin was walking ahead, talking on the phone, when he was suddenly hit by a bullet fired from inside the police station. I held him in my lap; the bullet had entered his chest and exited his back. I took him to Frontline Hospital on a motorcycle, where he was declared dead. We received NPR 1.5 million in compensation. Action must be taken against the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, and the specific police officers who opened fire.
195. Statement of Ganesh Prasad Chaulagain, father of the deceased Shriyam Chaulagain:

My son Shriyam was a student who joined the protest spontaneously. We hoped he was at college that day. He last spoke to his brother at 1:00 PM. He was shot in the head at Baneshwar Chowk and was brought to the hospital dead; the official declaration was at 3:15 PM. Security forces acted repressively, firing without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million. The Commission must ensure judicial action against the Prime Minister, Home Minister, CDO, IGP, and field commanders. To prevent a recurrence, Nepal must become corruption-free with ample job opportunities. Those who ruthlessly killed innocent citizens must be punished immediately.
196. Statement of Basanta Kishore Shrestha, father of the deceased Shauran Kishor Shrestha:

My son Shauran had passed Grade 12 and helped with household affairs. He joined the Gen-Z protest alone. He spoke to me and his sister that morning, saying he was going to a peaceful rally. We have photos and video of him being hit by two bullets in the right chest, along with shrapnel in his hand and neck, while he was trying to help another fallen protester. He was taken from Baneshwar Chowk to Civil Hospital and then to KMC Hospital Sinamangal, where he was declared dead at 2:53 PM. We received NPR 1.5 million. Judicial action must be taken against the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, Home Secretary, National Investigation Chief, CDO, and the IGP. We must end bad governance.
197. Statement of Ishwar Bahadur Adhikari, father of the deceased Ishwat Bahadur Adhikari:

My youngest son Ishwat was a student with a double Master's degree. He spontaneously joined the protest against the government. We last spoke at 2:12 PM on Bhadra 23. Ishwat told me he was seeing 6 or 7 dead bodies with visible brain matter in front of him. I told him to be careful. Later, when he was shot at Baneshwar Chowk and I called his number, a stranger answered and told me to come to the Bir Hospital Trauma Center immediately. Security forces shot him in the chest without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million. The Commission should ensure justice. Responsibility lies with the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, and National Investigation Chief. Corruption must end to provide jobs and good governance.
198. Statement of Ishwar Basudev Joshi, father of the deceased Gaurab Joshi:

My son Gaurab had passed Grade 12. He joined the protest after leaving his consultancy classes in Putalisadak. He last spoke to his sister on the afternoon of Bhadra 23, saying he was at the protest, there was a curfew, and he would return soon. He was shot in the head at Baneshwar and taken to the hospital by strangers. I found out he had died after checking the Trauma Center and being directed to Teaching Hospital on the morning of the 24th. Security personnel fired without warning. The Commission must ensure maximum judicial punishment. We need a corruption-free country with employment opportunities.
199. Statement of Rachana Khatiwada Khatri, mother of the deceased Rasik Khatiwada Khatri:

My only son Rasik was a Grade 12 student. He joined the protest with friends. He left home on Bhadra 23 saying he was going to the Gen-Z rally. He was shot in the chest and ribs at Baneshwar Chowk and taken to the hospital by protesters, where he was found to have died. Security forces used repressive measures without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million. Responsibility lies with the then-policymakers, the government, and the field commanders. Corruption, bad governance, and nepotism were the root causes of this event; we must change the active structures of the state to end these issues.
200. Statement of Bharat Rai, nephew of the deceased Sajan Rai (Mason):

My uncle Sajan Rai was a mason and was not formally literate. He joined the Gen-Z protest spontaneously for personal reasons. He was at Baneshwar on the day of the incident; we were staying together in a rented room in Bhaktapur. After he was shot at Baneshwar Chowk, we took him to the hospital. He was declared dead at the Korean Hospital at 7:49 PM on Bhadra 23. Security forces shot him in the chest without warning. The Commission should ensure maximum judicial punishment for the guilty.
201. Statement of Vishnu Bahadur Tamang, father of the deceased Buddhi Bahadur Tamang:

My eldest son Buddhi Bahadur had studied up to Grade 12 and was a businessman. He joined the protest alone. When he left on Bhadra 23, he said he was going for the good of the country. He was shot at Baneshwar Chowk and taken to the hospital by friends; he was declared dead at the Trauma Center on Bhadra 23. Security forces fired without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million. The Commission must ensure strict judicial action.
202. Statement of Kirti Ballabh Panta, father of the deceased Niraj Panta:

My son was a small-scale medicine businessman. He joined the Gen-Z protest spontaneously to provide social service. He was in Maharajgunj on Bhadra 24 when he was shot and taken to the hospital by protesters. He was declared dead at Teaching Hospital. Security personnel behaved poorly and fired without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million. The Commission should ensure the highest level of judicial punishment. The government and security forces must exercise restraint to prevent such incidents.
203. Statement of Loknath Gautam, father of the deceased Nikhita Gautam:

My daughter Nikhita was a student. Although she was interested in the Gen-Z movement, on Bhadra 24, she was simply sitting on the roof of our house in Gaushala Chowk with relatives. After she was shot, a stranger placed her in her mother's lap on a motorcycle and rushed her to Frontline Hospital, where she was declared dead. Security forces fired indiscriminately and without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million. Those responsiblethe then-government and its leadershipmust face the maximum penalty to ensure this never happens again.
204. Statement of Bhim Bahadur Parajuli, father of the deceased Anish Parajuli:

My only son Anish had passed Grade 10 and ran a small business. Agitated by the police brutality on Bhadra 23, he joined the protest on the 24th with friends. He was in Maharajgunj that day and was shot; protesters took him to the hospital, where he died shortly after arrival. Security forces shot him in the chest without warning. Policymakers and commanders are responsible. We need policy reforms to end nepotism, bad governance, and corruption.
205. Statement of Ajay Kumar Chaudhary, brother of the deceased Vijay Chaudhary:

My brother Vijay had completed Grade 12 and worked as a contractor. He spontaneously joined the protest on Bhadra 24. He was behind the Parliament building when he was shot. Protesters took him to the hospital on a motorcycle, where he passed away. Security forces fired without warning. To prevent such incidents, the country must ensure employment, an end to corruption, and good governance. We received NPR 1.5 million in relief.
206. Statement of Keshav Budhathoki, father of the deceased Mahesh Budhathoki:

My eldest son Mahesh was a student who spontaneously joined the protest with friends. He last spoke with his mother. He was shot at Koteshwar Chowk and was already dead by the time protesters brought him to the hospital. The post-mortem confirms he died of a gunshot wound. Eyewitnesses say security forces fired aggressively without warning. We received NPR 1.5 million. The Commission must ensure judicial action and push for policy reforms.
207. Statement of Sumitra Mahat, sister of the deceased Umesh Mahat:

My brother was an undergraduate student. He spontaneously participated in the movement organized by the Gen-Z generation against the then-government along with his friends. On Bhadra 23, Umesh participated in the Baneshwar protest, and on Bhadra 24, he was at Gaushala. The last time I spoke to him, he was at home. I don't know who took him to the hospital after he was shot while walking in Gaushala on Bhadra 24. Umesh was declared dead at Frontline Hospital that same day. Security personnel shot him in the chest in an aggressive and repressive manner. We have received NPR 1.5 million as relief from the Government of Nepal. The Commission must take judicial action against the guilty. Those responsible for the incident are all those at the policy level at that time, including the then-Prime Minister, Home Minister, Chief District Officer, and Inspector General of Police.
208. Statement of Dharmaraj Ojha, Information Officer of Global College Kathmandu:

I have been working at Kathmandu Global College since its establishment. On Bhadra 23, 2082, after the first shift students were dismissed at 11:00 AM, some studentsShriyam Chaulagain and Samrat Jung Karkiconvinced the driver of the college-rented bus, Karna Bahadur Pulami, that they would get off early. They got off near Bharosa Hospital to personally participate in the demonstration. No one in management was aware of this. We only learned about the shooting in Baneshwar around 5:00 PM that evening. Shortly after, news of student casualties began appearing in the media. Upon learning that the situation outside had deteriorated, we handed over the afternoon shift students to their parents. We confirmed that a student from our college was among the casualties only after seeing the deceased's photo in the media. Seven days later, we visited Shriyam Chaulagain’s home and learned he had informed his sister in Germany and his mother at home about his intent to join the protest. No incident occurred at our college on the 23rd, though police informed us of rumors on social media regarding the harassment of female students at a Global College hostel. We informed the police that we do not operate a hostel and no such incident occurred. On Bhadra 24, protesters entered the college, causing massive damage through vandalism and arson. We have filed applications regarding the damages with both the police and this Commission. While the building, servers, computers, and smartboards are insured with Sanima GIC Insurance, the company is hesitating to provide the full reconstruction amount, which is hindering our ability to resume regular educational programs.
209. Statement of Tulsiram Bhandari, Program Director of Orient College, Vasundhara:

On Bhadra 23, 2082, after the first shift concluded, all students were sent home in regular transport. The second shift began at 11:00 AM as usual, but after hearing news of tension in the New Baneshwar area around 1:00 PM, we closed classes at approximately 12:30 PM and sent the children home after informing their parents. We later discovered that four students from our college participated in the Gen-Z protest in their college uniforms. There was no institutional participation from the college management. It is understood that the students involved were from the morning shift of Grade 12, and they participated from their homes after being absent from college that day. No injuries or human casualties occurred among our students, nor was there any physical damage to the college.
210. Statement of Dr. Toshima Karki:

On Bhadra 23, I was attending a parliamentary committee meeting. Around 10:30 AM, after learning the protest in Baneshwar had escalated, I left by car but was stopped before reaching the Babarmahal bridge. I insisted on proceeding and took a lift on a motorcycle. When the motorcycle could no longer move forward, I walked through the back alleys of Minbhawan to reach Civil Hospital. On my way, I saw police firing in the alleys while protesters pelted stones; both sides were in a clash. I tried to reason with both the young protesters and the security personnel, but no one was in a state to listen. I reached Civil Hospital around 12:00 PM as the injured were starting to arrive. I stayed outside the hospital facilitating treatment. Protesters would often return to the movement immediately after receiving basic treatment, even when I urged them not to. I repeatedly went outside to ask security forces to stop their aggression, but they did not listen. Tear gas fired by the police even reached inside the hospital. The situation was heartbreaking. I stayed at the hospital that night assisting all the health workers who were working tirelessly.

On the morning of the 24th, the Baneshwar area remained tense. By 10:00 AM, the injured were being brought in again. I eventually entered the Parliament building premises, where I found a man lying on the ground. After checking his pulse and finding none, I had him taken to the hospital. I pleaded with protesters to stop the vandalism. I then returned to Civil Hospital to assist with treatment until late at night, coordinating the transfer of critical patients to other hospitals. I stayed at the hospital that night as well and finally left on the morning of Bhadra 25. This event was extremely tragic; I did not see the police exercise any restraint.
211. Summary of Dil Bhusan Pathak’s statement to the Commission:

During the Gen-Z protests on Bhadra 23 and 24, major landmarks including the Federal Parliament, Singha Durbar, Supreme Court, and Hilton Hotel were vandalized or set on fire, and many lives were lost. When questioned if the arson at the Hilton Hotel was primarily caused by a video report aired on his YouTube channel 'Tough Talk with Dil Bhusan Pathak,' and asked about the fact-checking process and journalistic ethics followed, he stated: 'Journalism is the backbone of democracy. In today's society, professional journalism is not just a medium for information but a moral compass for the nation. Journalism cannot remain silent against bad governance and corruption. It is the primary responsibility of investigative journalism to expose the abuse of power, scams, and irregularities. The Constitution of Nepal ensures the freedom of press and expression, providing the legal basis to speak the truth. However, freedom comes with responsibility. Maintaining truth, impartiality, and balance is the core of professionalism. My belief is in the courage to speak the truth and stand with the people. Journalism makes the government accountable. For 33 years, I have been active through journalism for the rights and interests of the country and the people, and I am committed to remaining so until my last breath.'
212. Statement of Rajendra Kumar Sharma, Former President of NRNA:

It would be a great mistake to understand the Gen-Z movement of Bhadra 23 and 24 as merely a political event. This movement is a social explosion resulting from poverty, unemployment, rising economic inequality, and the state's weak service delivery. Approximately 8 million Nepalis living abroad are worried about the country's condition. The absolute zero accountability of the state is the root cause of this movement.
213. Statement from the Federation of Nepalese Chambers of Commerce and Industry (FNCCI):

The youth movement last Bhadra clearly demanded political change along with decent employment, economic prosperity, and socio-economic development. The younger generation expects entrepreneurship and job security through technology-based business development. To realize these expectations, the strength of the private sector is essential. However, the series of attacks and arson against private property, industries, and business establishments during the movement has severely demoralized the private sector. Development is impossible without private sector participation. Therefore, to rebuild confidence and prevent such incidents in the future, it is necessary for the Government of Nepal to approve and implement a 'Strategic Plan for the Protection and Promotion of the Private Sector,' drawing from the experiences of other countries that have faced similar unrest.
Detailed statements and incident reports are included in Annex 5.

content: Chapter 6: Incident Details Received from Chief District Officers of Heavily Impacted Districts

To ascertain the situation before, during, and after the movement organized by the Gen-Z generation, the Commission requested incident reports from the then-Chief District Officers (CDOs) of the districts most affected by the protests. These reports received from the districts have been utilized as primary data for investigation and inquiry. Brief summaries of the incident reports and statements provided by the requested CDOs are presented below.
6.1. Koshi Province
6.1.1. District Administration Office, Morang

I served as the CDO at the District Administration Office, Morang, from January 07, 2025, to October 31, 2025. Subsequently, since November 03, 2025, I have been serving as the CDO of Mahottari district. Regarding the events of September 07, 2026 (Bhadra 24) involving the Gen-Z generationwho demanded national interest, good governance, and corruption controlit appears that attacks on public and private property continued until the following morning due to personal vendettas, even after the deployment of the Nepal Army on the night of the 24th. Per your request for a factual report on the major incidents, their causes, the key individuals or groups involved, and the status of security coordination in Morang, I provide the following details:
Status of Bhadra 23 (September 06):

Despite continuous heavy rainfall from 6:00 AM to 12:30 PM, representatives arrived at the office at 11:00 AM to request permission for a peaceful program. They specified a gathering of 700–1,000 people at Himalayan Road, Biratnagar, to advocate for good governance and the lifting of social media bans. Approximately 800 people participated. Although minor demonstrations targeted the Land Revenue Office and the Metropolitan Office in the afternoon, the situation remained otherwise normal. No force was used, and no injuries occurred. However, protesters became agitated following news of fatalities in Itahari (2 deaths) and Kathmandu. Coordination meetings were held with PABSON/N-PABSON to close local schools for three days. A Provincial Security Coordination Committee meeting was held at 6:00 PM for briefing; no untoward incidents occurred in Morang that day.
Status of Bhadra 24 (September 07):

A command post meeting was held at the District Administration Office at 6:00 AM to discuss preventing loss of life and property. Following verbal coordination with the Ministry of Home Affairs and the Provincial Ministry of Internal Affairs, a curfew was issued starting at 11:00 AM. Every effort was made to protect the Provincial Assembly buildinga primary targetand it remained secure until 2:00 PM. However, at that time, the Internal Revenue Office building at Traffic Chowk was set on fire, and the situation began to slip out of control. When social media and news reports claimed the Prime Minister had resigned and Singha Durbar was on fire, the morale of deployed security personnel weakened as they perceived a nationwide collapse of order. Arson then began at the Provincial Assembly building, followed by Koirala Residence, and eventually, protesters breached the District Administration Office around 3:00 PM. Attacks then spread to other government offices, private properties, and party offices. Prior to these events, a 10:00 AM meeting with local political parties had issued a public appeal for peace, and student unions were consulted to maintain order. The District Security Committee remained together on the ground until 7:00 PM, by which time 155 government offices, private establishments, homes, and political offices had sustained damage.
Causes of the Incident:
The primary cause was deep-seated anger toward the state and a desire for revenge following the deaths in Kathmandu and Itahari.
Main Individuals/Groups Involved:

The movement was fundamentally led by Gen-Z protesters. However, it was socially rumored afterward that certain 'social activists,' anti-establishment factions within political parties, and individuals posing as journalists on social media incited the crowd. I did not personally recognize these individuals.
Security Deployment and Coordination:

Because no force was used on Bhadra 23, our security analysis did not anticipate the following day's escalation. There was no lack of coordination among security agencies. After the Nepal Army was deployed at 10:00 PM on Bhadra 24, there were attempts to set fire to the Nepal Oil Corporation’s regional fuel depot and facilitate a jailbreak at the prison, but our local coordination and security deployment successfully prevented those outcomes.

Overall, while there were no physical injuries or dismemberment due to the use of force in this district, we were unable to protect the district from arson once reports of nationwide fires began to spread. No further damage to public or private property occurred after the Army took control. This statement was provided by the then-CDO of Morang, Indra Dev Yadav (Annex 6).
6.1.2. District Administration Office, Makwanpur

Per orders, I provide the following details regarding the events of September 07, 2026 (Bhadra 24) involving Gen-Z youth, including the attacks on property that occurred into the following morning and the coordination of security forces in Makwanpur district.
