Major Timeline of Events: September 06, 2026 (Bhadra 23):

At 10:00 AM, approximately 1,200–1,300 youths and students from 'Gen-Z Youth Against Corruption' gathered in front of the Hetauda Sub-Metropolitan City office in Ward No. 2. They protested against corruption and social media restrictions. The rally moved from the municipal office through Piple, Buddha Chowk, Sima Chowk, and TN Road to the Provincial Assembly office. After chanting slogans, they returned via Ram Mandir, Bypass Road, and Sima Chowk to Buddha Chowk. The group dispersed peacefully at 3:15 PM. A joint team of the Armed Police Force and Nepal Police was deployed at the site.
Major Timeline of Events: September 07, 2082 (Bhadra 24):

content.content: Around 09:00 AM: Approximately 100–200 youths associated with 'Gen-Z Youth Against Corruption' gathered at Sima Chowk, Hetauda-4. They protested against corruption and the ban on social media. The crowd grew to 1,500–2,000, advancing while vandalizing and committing arson against government offices.
10:45 AM: Gen-Z supporters (100–200) vandalized student union banners inside Makwanpur Multipurpose Campus near Manakamana Chowk, Hetauda-2.
11:30 AM: Protesters vandalized the Hetauda Sub-Metropolitan City signboard at Yuddha Chowk.
11:50 AM: Around 200–250 Gen-Z supporters in Manahari-7 broke the locks of Nepali Congress, CPN-UML, and CPN-Maoist party offices near the Area Police Office, burning furniture in the street.
12:10 PM: Protesters set fire to the CPN-UML party office at Pushpalal Park, Hetauda-4.

12:45 PM: The three-story District Coordination Committee building in Hetauda-2 was completely destroyed by arson. Two motorcycles and a Hilux vehicle (Ba 1 Jha 9430) at the Industry and Commerce Office were burned.
12:54 PM: The District Land Revenue Office in Hetauda-2 was completely destroyed by arson and vandalism.

01:30 PM: A six-story building in Hetauda-1 housing several provincial ministries (Agriculture, Infrastructure, Labor & Transport, and Water & Energy) was completely destroyed. Arson consumed 2 Scorpios, 1 car, 12 motorcycles, and 4 scooters in the parking lot.
01:40 PM: Protesters vandalized the Bagmati Provincial Ministry of Economic Affairs and Planning, burning furniture and government motorcycles.
01:55 PM: The Commission for the Investigation of Abuse of Authority (CIAA) office in Hetauda-1 was vandalized, and vehicles were set on fire.
02:00 PM: The Inland Revenue Office in Hetauda-2 was attacked. A canteen and a two-story office building were completely burned, along with 6 motorcycles and 2 cars.

02:10 PM: The Hetauda Bench of the Patan High Court was set on fire. The three-story building, furniture, and windows were destroyed. Five motorcycles and 2 taxis were burned, and 4 cars were vandalized.

02:20 PM: The High Government Attorney’s Office and District Government Attorney’s Office were attacked. Arson destroyed the first floor of the District office and the first floor of the High office, with subsequent floors vandalized. Two cars, 6 motorcycles, and 4 bicycles were incinerated.

02:35 PM: Protesters breached the Bagmati Province Assembly building, causing total destruction by fire. Two parliamentary party buildings, a canteen, and a library were destroyed. A Rastriya Banijya Bank ATM was vandalized, and 6 vehicles were burned.

02:45 PM: The four-story building housing the Ministry of Health and Ministry of Social Development was entirely destroyed. A warehouse and canteen were burned, along with 25 cars, 15 motorcycles, and 7 scooters.
02:55 PM: The Manahari Rural Municipality office (Ward No. 9) was set on fire.
03:45 PM: Protesters entered and set fire to the Area Police Office in Newarpani, Hetauda-19.
03:55 PM: A 10-room yard belonging to Baniya Construction Service in Tintapke was destroyed. Vandalism and arson affected 19 tippers, 3 cement bulkers, and a JCB.
04:10 PM: The digital board and CCTV cameras at the Ward No. 1 office in China Quarter were vandalized.
04:20 PM: One Scorpio, one pickup, and two motorcycles were burned at the Vehicle Examination Office in Thanabharyang.
04:25 PM: The Transport Management Office in Thanabharyang was completely destroyed by arson, including 3 vehicles in the parking lot.
04:30 PM: Narayani Transport Business Company Pvt. Ltd. was vandalized and set on fire.

04:35 PM: The Basamadi Police Post was completely destroyed. Personal belongings and food supplies of 8 police personnel were incinerated. Nineteen motorcycles, one scooter, and one ambulance were burned.
04:40 PM: The two-story Urban Development and Building Office in Hetauda-2 was completely destroyed by fire.

05:00 PM: Protesters breached the Makwanpur District Court. Arson destroyed the six-story building and several vehicles (1 Sumo, 1 Bolero, 1 pickup, 46 motorcycles, and 7 bicycles). The canteen was also vandalized.
05:05 PM: The Treasury and Accounts Controller Office was attacked. Windows and doors were damaged, computers broken, and files scattered. A generator, one Sumo, and three motorcycles were burned.
05:10 PM: The residence of the Bagmati Province Chief Secretary was completely destroyed by arson.
05:20 PM: The Ministry of Internal Affairs and Law was vandalized. Protesters stole a TV, a laptop, and NPR 30,000 cash. Seven vehicles were burned.
05:30 PM: The three-story Survey (Napi) Office was completely destroyed by arson, including three vehicles in the parking lot.
05:40 PM: The Public Service Commission (Lok Sewa Aayog) office was vandalized, and two vehicles were burned.
05:55 PM: The Ministry of Industry, Commerce, Land, and Administration was vandalized, and three vehicles were set on fire.
06:10 PM: Arson was committed at the Ward No. 4 office and the District Sports Development Committee office.
06:15 PM: Protesters attacked the house of Bishwakanti Kalawati Sumargi (Ajay Sumargi), burning three cars and destroying 13 rooms, including furniture and electronics.
06:20 PM: The Agriculture building of Hetauda Sub-Metropolitan City in Thanabharyang was pelted with stones, damaging all doors and windows of the three-story structure.
06:35 PM: Arson and vandalism were committed at the Provincial Emergency Operation Center (PEOC) and the District Traffic Police Office, Makwanpur.
06:35 PM: The Ministry of Cooperatives and Poverty Alleviation was attacked; the first floor was destroyed by fire while the second floor was vandalized.
06:40 PM: The Bagmati Province Police Disaster Management Gulm was attacked. Barracks, windows, and the mess area were damaged; furniture and a TV were broken, and 4 motorcycles were burned.
06:45 PM: A three-story ward office building in Hetauda-11 was vandalized and set on fire.
06:50 PM: The Madan Bhandari Academy of Health Sciences was attacked. A 12-room building was destroyed, along with 5 high-end vehicles (Prado, Scorpio, etc.).
07:00 PM: The seven-story Bhatbhateni Supermarket in Hetauda-2 was completely destroyed by vandalism, looting, and arson.
07:20 PM: The residence of Ananta Prasad Paudel (CPN-UML leader/former CA member) was attacked. Protesters stole jewelry and a projector; the house sustained partial arson damage.
07:25 PM: The two-story house of Hidam Lama (Chair of Bhimphedi Rural Municipality) was completely destroyed by arson.
07:30 PM: Three buildings of the Provident Fund (Sanchay Kosh) office were attacked, and 5 vehicles were incinerated.
07:35 PM: The residence of former Chief Minister Dor Mani Paudel was vandalized, causing external damage.
07:40 PM: The three-story residence of Chief Minister Indra Bahadur Baniya was completely destroyed by arson.

content: Major Timeline of Events: September 08, 2082 (Bhadra 25):

content.content: Around 12:55 PM: Inmates in Block 'A' of Bhimphedi Prison broke through three iron gates, launched a protest with stone-pelting, and breached the security perimeter. They converged at the main gate and committed arson/vandalism against the administration building, main gate, and APF barracks. Security forces (APF) fired 2 rounds from SLR and 6 tear gas shells. The Nepal Army (Kali Prasad Battalion) fired approximately 200 aerial rounds. Out of 554 inmates, 259 escaped via the western wall. Two inmates, Deepak Sunar and Naresh Murau, sustained gunshot wounds to their legs and were treated at Hetauda Hospital.

10:15 AM: While inmates in Block 'B' of Bhimphedi Prison were attempting a jailbreak by breaking the wall, APF forces used 5 tear gas shells to regain control. They detained 296 inmates, but 181 successfully escaped.

content: Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies

content.content: Around 12:55 PM, inmates in Block 'A' of Bhimphedi Prison, Makwanpur, broke through three iron gates, chanting slogans and pelting stones. They breached the security perimeter, gathered at the main gate, and vandalized/arsoned the prison administration building, the main gate, the APF Prison Security Base barracks, the check post, and the administration area. In response, the APF team fired 2 rounds from SLR and 6 tear gas shells. A 70-member team from the Nepal Army (Kali Prasad Battalion) fired approximately 200 aerial rounds. Out of 554 inmates in that block, 259 escaped by demolishing the western wall. The remaining 295 were brought under control and returned to their blocks by 3:30 PM. Two inmates, 25-year-old Deepak Sunar (theft case) and 33-year-old Naresh Murau (homicide case), sustained leg injuries from gunfire and were treated at Hetauda Hospital before being returned to custody.

On September 08, 2082 (Bhadra 25) at 10:15 AM, while inmates in Block 'B' of Bhimphedi Prison were attempting to escape by breaking the wall, the APF Prison Security Base team fired 5 tear gas shells to control the situation. They successfully detained 296 inmates, while 181 managed to flee.

content: Regarding the events of Bhadra 23 and 24, all heads of district security agencies met in the Chief District Officer's (CDO) office to analyze the situation. Initially, force was not used. Deployment and coordination were carried out according to the Security Committee's decisions, as detailed in the incident report by the then CDO of Makwanpur, Skim Shrestha (Annex 6).
6.1.3. District Administration Office, Sunsari:

On Bhadra 23 at approximately 10:40 AM, about 1,000 students and youths led by Gen-Z campaign coordinator Srishti Bhattarai and local leader Bikal Regmi gathered at Itahari Golchowk and began a rally. The crowd eventually swelled to an estimated 4,000–4,500 people. During the rally, infiltrators began shouting provocative anti-government slogans, and the protest went beyond the leaders' control. Protesters vandalized the digital notice board at the Itahari Sub-Metropolitan City gate and tried to break the main gate. When security forces intervened, they were attacked with stones, petrol bombs, and slingshots. Some protesters entered the office, damaging two fire engines, a water tanker, and other vehicles, while setting fire to the Mayor's office and several administrative branches. The administration had previously requested Gen-Z representatives to obtain formal permission and warned them to remain vigilant against potential infiltration.

As the crowd grew and speeches became more inflammatory, the Gen-Z team was requested to brief their group to prevent the movement from becoming leaderless. Security forces provided escorts for the students. Since the protesters lacked a sound system, the police coordinated with Durga Party Palace to facilitate its arrangement. The CDO and SP requested local political leaders to ensure their cadres did not infiltrate the Gen-Z protest. Despite repeated warnings to remain peaceful, the mob began vandalizing fire engines and setting fire to offices. The Security Committee contacted protest representatives via phone and used miking in the area, urging them to leave the Sub-Metropolitan premises to minimize damage.

Publicity regarding the prohibitory and curfew orders (issued at 3:30 PM under the Local Administration Act, 1971) was carried out via media, social networks, and miking at the protest site. Orders were repeatedly given to use minimum force only to control those on the frontline of the arson and attacks to prevent further casualties. Security forces detained 13 individuals involved in the vandalism and took them to the Area Police Office, Itahari.

Following the arson at the municipal office, the crowd moved toward the Area Police Office at 5:00 PM to demand the release of the 13 detainees. A mob of approximately 5,000 launched a targeted attack on the police office. To protect the office, weapons, documents, and other detainees, a joint force of Nepal Police and APF used tear gas, blank rounds, and live aerial firing to disperse the crowd. The dispersing mob took some injured individuals with them, while the police rescued two injured persons and sent them to BPKIHS, Dharan, for treatment.

The use of firearms was not based on a specific written order but was a spontaneous act of self-defense following the principles of graduated force. Faced with a mob of 5,000 armed with stones, rods, and petrol bombs attempting to breach the detention center and potentially kill police personnel, the forces opened fire to prevent further loss of life and secure the premises.

The mob vandalized the digital display and vehicles parked at the police office. Several police officers were injured as protesters repeatedly attempted to force entry into the detention cells to free their colleagues. Arson attempts were made against various branches, communication equipment, and the weapons storage area within the police compound.

The Security Command Post decided to request reinforcements from the Bhagawati Dal Battalion (Nepal Army) in Itahari. An email request was sent at 4:03 PM, and the Army arrived at 5:24 PM. In the interim, the self-defense firing incident occurred during the attack on the police office. The Army team returned that night and was redeployed after 10:00 PM on Bhadra 24.

During the clashes, 10 Nepal Police personnel (including DSP Lakshmi Bhandari and several Inspectors) and 4 APF personnel were injured. Fifteen protesters were also reported injured. Evidence recovered from the scene included bricks, slingshots, and beer bottles smelling of petrol with cloth wicks (molotov cocktails). The mob utilized rods and bricks salvaged from demolished structures along the Asian Highway expansion project to carry out their attacks.

Analysis of the incident suggests causes included: a lack of leadership quality among Gen-Z leaders, infiltration by interest groups, and the viral spread of fake news on social media (e.g., false reports of bodies inside the Parliament building). Public frustration with the government and a shortage of security personnel relative to the crowd size also contributed. Furthermore, a lack of 'Unity of Command' was noted between the APF and the district command post. Participants included members of major political parties, religious extremists, and other interest groups. Efforts to identify those involved through social media footage are ongoing.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

Initially, 135 personnel (80 Police, 55 APF) were deployed at Golchowk. As numbers surged and the crowd turned violent, reinforcements were added, bringing the total to 363 personnel (222 Police under SP Sumun Kumar Timsina and 141 APF under SP Nirmal Kumar Thapa). After the arson at the municipal office at 3:20 PM, minimum force was used to push the mob out. When the mob attacked the police office at 4:50 PM, security forces used tear gas and aerial firing. Unfortunately, two protesters died during the clashes. On Bhadra 24, a massive jailbreak occurred at Jhumka Prison, where 1,796 inmates were forcibly freed by a mob of 500–600. Detailed damage reports for various government and private properties will be submitted. According to the then CDO Dharmendra Kumar Mishra, maximum restraint was exercised on Bhadra 24, resulting in no further serious injuries or deaths (Annex 6).
6.1.4. District Administration Office, Jhapa:

content.content: On Bhadra 22, youth groups requested permission for a peaceful rally. On Bhadra 23 at 10:35 AM, around 200 youths in Damak protested against the government's ban on social media, carrying banners against corruption and corrupt politicians.
Despite the organizers declaring the program over, the group marched to the Damak Municipality gate, vandalized CCTV cameras, and burned vehicles. Police used minor force to prevent further damage.

In Birtamode, a spontaneous gathering of 550 grew to 5,000. They attacked the Birtamode Municipality building, breaking windows and trapping the Deputy Mayor and staff inside. Security analysis suggested these incidents were exacerbated by internal political friction within both the Birtamode and Damak municipal leaderships.

Following the events in Kathmandu and the Home Minister's resignation, communication with the Ministry became difficult. To prevent night-time violence, curfews were imposed in Birtamode (starting 8:30 PM) and Damak (starting 10:00 PM) on Bhadra 23.

On Bhadra 24, the curfew was extended. However, crowds defied the order, gathering by 10:30 AM. In Birtamode, a large mob burned records and furniture at the Traffic Police Office and vandalized the Municipality building and various political party offices.

In Damak, the mob pelted stones and committed arson at the municipal office. Violence spread to surrounding municipalities (Gauradaha, Kankai, Arjundhara, etc.), where administrative buildings, ward offices, and customs offices were targeted.

By 2:00 PM, groups on motorcycles led attacks on the private homes of political representatives. In Damak, a mob brutally attacked the Area Police Office, looting weapons, ammunition, and uniforms. Similar looting occurred in Birtamode, where protesters even used the stolen weapons to fire at security forces. Gas cylinders were detonated at the Birtamode Traffic Police Office. Due to the massive crowd size, sending reinforcements or tear gas supplies was nearly impossible.

Three individuals died in Birtamode; it remains unclear if they were hit by police fire, fire from looted weapons, or injured during the accidental explosion of gas cylinders by the protesters themselves. Attacks on police units continued until 9:00 PM in rural areas.
Total munitions used in the district over Bhadra 23 and 24 included 496 aerial rounds and 254 tear gas shells by the Nepal Police, and 12 aerial rounds and 166 tear gas shells by the APF.

Causes of violence included local leadership disputes, the social media ban, and the inflammatory effect of photos showing casualties from Kathmandu. The participation of drug users, unemployed individuals, and those previously penalized by traffic police helped turn the crowd more aggressive. As police became occupied defending their own armories after 2:00 PM on the 24th, lawlessness increased.

content: Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

content.content: Starting from Bhadra 22, formal and informal meetings and discussions were held repeatedly among the members of the Security Committee.

From 11:00 AM on Bhadra 23, the heads of the Nepal Police, Armed Police Force (APF), and National Investigation Office remained in the Chief District Officer's (CDO) chamber to collect intelligence and provide necessary instructions to subordinates. Additional personnel were also deployed. On the morning of Bhadra 24, a command post involving Security Committee officials was established at the District Administration Office to monitor the situation and boost the morale of the forces. While Nepal Police and APF units were deployed throughout Bhadra 23 and 24, the Nepal Army joined for joint patrols after 11:00 PM on the 24th. Overall, coordination among security agencies was satisfactory, as stated in the incident report by the then CDO of Jhapa, Gopal Kumar Adhikari (Annex 6).

content: 6.2. Madhesh Province
6.2.1. District Administration Office, Parsa

On Bhadra 23, the Gen-Z generation organized an awareness program in Birgunj, the headquarters of Parsa, starting at 11:00 AM. They gathered at Ghantaghar for about an hour and conducted a peaceful rally demanding national interest, good governance, and corruption control. However, following news of deaths resulting from police force against young protesters in Kathmandu, the group reassembled. Around 5:00 PM, they gathered again at Ghantaghar to protest the Kathmandu fatalities. By 6:00-7:00 PM, the crowd became agitated, vandalizing traffic dividers and starting fires. When persuasion failed to control the crowd, a curfew was issued from 9:00 PM until 9:00 AM the next day (Bhadra 24) in specified areas of Birgunj to disperse the mob.

At 8:00 AM on Bhadra 24, an all-party meeting was held at the District Administration Office with political parties and security heads to assess potential damage and discuss whether to continue the curfew. Following this, a new curfew order was issued at 11:00 AM. While Nepal Police and APF were on the field, the Nepal Armyanticipating that their immediate involvement might lead to higher casualtiescoordinated with their high command while awaiting specific orders. Meanwhile, crowds in Birgunj grew as various groups merged, primarily gathering for slogans in front of Ghantaghar.

Between 1:00 PM and 2:00 PM, social media reports and videos showed damage to key structures in Kathmandu (Singha Durbar, International Convention Center, Supreme Court, President's Office) and the private residences of political leaders. This incited the local crowd to attack a police beat near Ghantaghar, the Nepal Telecom office, and vehicles. Subsequently, vandalism, looting, and arson spread to the Birgunj Metropolitan City office and private homes. After 10:00 PM that night, following a statement and order from the Chief of Army Staff, the Nepal Army was deployed. Coordinated efforts by all three security branches prevented further damage after 10:00 PM.

On Bhadra 23, some children escaped from the Juvenile Detention Center after breaking the gate, and an attempt to break the main prison gate was thwarted. On the morning of the 25th, after protesters tried to dig a hole in the prison compound wall to free inmates, security forces used miking to warn them and eventually used tear gas and aerial firing to prevent a jailbreak. Following these incidents, instances of looting and arson decreased, and the security situation gradually stabilized. From Bhadra 23 until the end of the month, the situation was normalized through coordinated patrolling and the declaration of curfews and riot-affected zones.

The attackers targeted almost all office buildings and vehicles of the Birgunj Metropolitan City. Significant arson and vandalism also occurred at the private residences of several leaders: Pradeep Yadav (former Minister/MP), Ajay Chaurasia (former Minister/MP), Raj Kumar Gupta (former Minister/MP), and Lal Babu Raut (former Chief Minister, Madhesh Province), where two four-wheelers were burned. Three out of four houses within the compound of Anil Rungta (Constituent Assembly member) were completely destroyed, and his vehicles were vandalized. During these fires, a worker named Shila Devi Dusarin was severely burned and later died during treatment in Kathmandu. One of the two houses belonging to Rajeshman Singh, Mayor of Birgunj, was completely destroyed, while the other suffered arson on the ground and first floors and vandalism on the upper floor.

Other damages included the Nepal Telecom office at Ghantaghar, where the ground floor and upper-floor batteries were destroyed, and seven vehicles (including cars, auto-rickshaws, and motorcycles) were burned. At the Inland Revenue Office, motorcycles were burned, and ground-floor records were destroyed by fire. Vandalism occurred at the High Court (Janakpur-Birgunj Bench), the Transport Management Office, and several ward offices of Birgunj Metropolitan City (9, 10, 11, 14, 21, 26, and 31). Furthermore, buildings belonging to Area Police, police posts, and beats under the District Police Office were targeted, the Parsa prison gate was damaged, and a jailbreak occurred at the Juvenile Detention Center.

The causes of the escalation were linked to the news of Gen-Z deaths in Kathmandu on Bhadra 23. This was exacerbated on Bhadra 24 by social media broadcasts showing the burning of major government buildings and the private residences of President Ram Chandra Paudel and leaders like K.P. Sharma Oli, Sher Bahadur Deuba, and Pushpa Kamal Dahal 'Prachanda'. This created a retaliatory mindset among the public against the leadership.

While the movement was initially attributed to Gen-Z youth, it is estimated that the vandalism, looting, and arson on Bhadra 24 involved infiltrators from other age groups, including unruly individuals, drivers, laborers, and factory workers.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

There was a sound relationship among the Nepal Army, Nepal Police, APF, and National Investigation District Office throughout the events. A curfew was issued in anticipation of violence on Bhadra 24. Since maintaining peace with only the Police and APF proved nearly impossible, written and oral requests were made for Army deployment. However, per central command orders, the Army only moved into the field after 10:00 PM on the 24th. It is estimated that if joint deployment had occurred at 11:00 AM alongside the curfew, the day's destruction could have been prevented.

Since the Army's deployment at 10:00 PM on Bhadra 24, no significant incidents occurred except for minor vandalism at remote local ward offices. The then CDO of Parsa, Toyanarayan Subedi, submitted this statement (Annex 6) based purely on memory, as he is no longer in that office and could not consult current staff or records.

District Administration Office, Dhanusha: Per the Local Administration Act, 1971, the District Security Committee is chaired by the CDO and includes heads of the Army, Police, APF, and National Investigation Office, with the Assistant CDO as member-secretary. The committee analyzes security, collects intelligence, and decides on deployment plans, which were followed accordingly.
On Bhadra 23 and 24, security heads analyzed the situation in the CDO’s chamber. No force was used, and as a result, no individuals were injured in Dhanusha district.

However, private properties were attacked and houses were burned by various groups. Detailed records of these incidents are maintained at the District Police Office and can be provided to the Secretariat upon request.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies

The mutual relationship and coordination among the Chairman and members of the District Security Committee were exemplary and highly supportive. As a result, although there was damage to private and public property, no human casualties occurred. This is detailed in the incident report by the then Chief District Officer of Dhanusha, Shankarhari Acharya, in Annex 6.
6.2.3. District Administration Office, Rautahat:
The protests were driven by nationwide unrest and anger over the deaths and injuries during the Kathmandu movement on the 23rd, as well as general frustration regarding governance.

Protesters set fire to and damaged municipal buildings in Chandrapur, Phatuwa Vijaypur, Katahariya, and Garuda, which are located far from the district headquarters. Similarly, Maulapur, Bodhimai, Gaur, and Paroha municipalities were set on fire, causing partial damage to office buildings, while some vehicles in Maulapur, Chandrapur, and Garuda were burned. Regarding government offices, the Area Police Office in Chandrapur, Road Division Office, District Traffic Police Office, District Forest Division Office, and the Provincial Treasury and Accounts Controller Office were set on fire, resulting in damage to both buildings and vehicles. Minor damage occurred at some ward offices in Brindaban, Gadhimai, and Madhav Narayan municipalities. In terms of private property, the houses of Assistant CDO Kiran Nidhi Tiwari and former PM Madhav Kumar Nepal were damaged, and the house of Garuda Mayor Kanthamani was looted. On the 23rd, hundreds of protesters attempted arson and vandalism at the District Administration Office in Gaur and the District Court; however, the deployment of Armed Police Force (APF) No. 11 Battalion HQ and Nepal Police prevented major damage, resulting only in minor broken glass on buildings and vehicles.
No significant damage occurred in the district after the deployment of the Army on the night of the 24th.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies

The deployment of security agencies was excellent. Security personnel (APF and Nepal Police) were extensively deployed in a coordinated manner to maintain public safety and peace. Except for minor incidents, no major damage occurred in the headquarters, Gaur. However, despite the efforts of security forces, protesters managed to cause damage in municipalities far from the headquarters.

On the 24th, a security command post was established at the District Administration Office building to coordinate peace and security arrangements. The CDO remained at the office, establishing the command post and deploying APF and Nepal Police. Between 1:30 PM and 4:45 PM, protesters repeatedly tried to enter the District Administration Office premises to cause damage, but through the use of minimum force, no damage occurred in the headquarters. Around 10:30 PM on the 24th, some inmates escaped from the District Jail but were immediately brought under control by security forces. However, about 131 inmates (out of 291) remained at large, according to the incident report by the then CDO of Rautahat, Binod Kumar Khadka, in Annex 6.
6.3. Bagmati Province
6.3.1. District Administration Office, Kavrepalanchok:

Before the events started in Kathmandu on August 23, 2082, the district situation was normal. The District Security Committee was focused on ensuring safe travel on the B.P. Highway (damaged by previous rains) and preparing for the upcoming Dashain and Tihar festivals, including road upgrades and disaster management. Upon receiving news of the unpleasant events and curfew in Kathmandu at 1:00 PM, an emergency Security Committee meeting was called. That evening, a discussion was held regarding a request for a peaceful program in Banepa Bazaar by Ayush Tiwari on behalf of Gen-Z youth. It was decided to deploy Nepal Police and APF to Banepa and other sensitive areas. Special vigilance was maintained to prevent outside infiltration into the Gen-Z crowd. Around 50 Gen-Z youths began a peaceful program with placards, but when 200–300 others tried to join, security separated the groups. Due to provocative speeches by the infiltrating group, additional security was added, and the program concluded peacefully around 8:30 PM.

On the morning of August 24, 2082, the security command post became active. The committee directed high-alert deployment and instructed the Nepal Army’s Shri Mehar Battalion in Dhulikhel to remain on standby. Essential services were also alerted. By 9:30 AM, reports of planned protests and potential violence in Banepa and Panauti emerged, leading to an immediate analysis by the security committee.

As crowds began gathering in an unorganized manner, and considering the curfew in the Kathmandu Valley, it was decided to issue a curfew order for Banepa, Panauti, Dhulikhel, and Panchkhal starting at 12:15 PM under the Local Administration Act, 1971. The Shri Mehar Battalion was requested for deployment and immediately mobilized. The curfew was publicized via media and social networks, and joint security teams used miking to inform the public. Despite limited equipment, security forces made maximum efforts to restrain a violent crowd of 8,000–10,000 using minimum force. To protect government and private property, tear gas, lathi charges, and aerial firing were used as per the law and Ministry of Home Affairs instructions.

Around 1:00 PM, protests and rallies escalated into violent activities in Banepa and Panauti, surpassing the capacity of available security personnel. Security chiefs were instructed through the command post to maintain order within the legal framework of the Local Administration Act using minimum force. These instructions specifically emphasized human rights, the district's situation, and the previous day's incidents in Kathmandu to ensure a balanced response. Coordinated action among all security units was mandated.

As reports of the uncontrolled and destructive mob in Banepa and Panauti came in, the command post provided continuous direction. The chaotic crowd set fire to and vandalized the Banepa Municipal Office, Area Police Office Banepa, Nepali Congress and CPN-UML party offices, the District Traffic Police Office, and various police posts. Arson and vandalism also targeted the private homes of the Mayor and Deputy Mayor of Banepa. Similarly, the Panauti Municipal Office, various ward offices, and police posts in Panauti, Panchkhal, and Hinguwapati were attacked. Protesters also targeted the houses and vehicles of the Mayor of Dhulikhel, the Mayor, Deputy Mayor, and former Mayor of Panauti, Constituent Assembly member Shiva Humagai, and social worker Rameshwar Thapa. Detailed damage reports will be provided by the District Administration and Police offices.

On August 25, an attempt to instigate arson and a jailbreak at Dhulikhel Prison, led by an inmate named Sagar Mainali (alias Ram Krishna Mainali), was thwarted by deploying additional security and using minimal force. While minor fire damage occurred, the prison and inmates remained secure. Four individuals involved in the jailbreak attempt were transferred to the District Police custody and subsequently to the Central Jail in Kathmandu.

Security forces successfully protected the district headquarters and government offices by blocking the mob at Baghchok, Dhulikhel, through strategic deployment and minimum force. The restraint, patience, and courage of the personnel were exemplary. After five hours of clashes, the situation was brought under control, and the Nepal Army took over security at 10:00 PM. The curfew was extended and eventually lifted on the night of August 28.

Overall, in Kavre district, no one was killed or significantly injured during the movement. No security weapons were looted, though reports from the Area Police Office in Banepa indicated some missing ammunition.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

Due to excellent coordination and deployment, all offices in the headquarters (Dhulikhel), including the prison and court, remained safe with no human casualties despite the damage in Banepa and Panauti. Control efforts followed the principle of minimum force using tear gas, lathi charges, and aerial firing. While some property damage could not be prevented due to the influence of events in the Kathmandu Valley and a temporary dip in security morale, the adoption of a sound security strategy successfully protected all government and non-government institutions in the Dhulikhel headquarters. This summary is based on the incident report by the then CDO of Kavrepalanchok, Umesh Kumar Dhakal, in Annex 6.
6.3.2. District Administration Office, Chitwan:

The Gen-Z generation, organized through virtual platforms, prepared to take to the streets on September 08 to show a strong presence against the government. Their demands included ending corruption, maintaining good governance, lifting the ban on social media platforms not registered in Nepal, and ending delays in service delivery at government offices. To make the September 08 (Bhadra 23) uprising successful, extensive publicity was carried out through various online media, print media, and social networks (TikTok, Discord, Reddit). To ensure the uprising remained peaceful from 10:00 AM to 11:30 AM, Gen-Z leaders including Abhash Sapkota, Rabin KC, Sandip Neupane, Milan Kandel, and Amit Giri submitted a signed letter to the District Administration Office and District Police Office on September 07 (Bhadra 22).

On September 08 (Bhadra 23) at 09:55 AM, approximately 200 youths led by Abhash Sapkota, Rabin KC, Sandeep Neupane, Milan Kandel, Amit Giri, and Sagar Karki gathered in front of the District Administration Office gate. At 11:40 AM, the group submitted a memorandum to the Chief District Officer (CDO). They then took out a rally with anti-corruption slogans, passing through Bharatpur Metropolitan City-10 Chaubiskoti Chowk and the Metropolitan Gate to Pulchowk. Around 2:20 PM, they returned to the Bharatpur Metropolitan Gate, where they became aggressive and pelted stones. To control the protesters, the police had to use tear gas, lathi charges, and aerial firing. In the process, 18 protesters and 14 police personnel were injured.

Although people from both sides were injured during the anti-corruption program that day, there were no fatalities in the district. However, after 19 people died in other parts of the country (especially Kathmandu), Gen-Z and the general public became enraged and aggressive toward the then-government and senior leadership. To minimize potential damage, a meeting was held on the morning of Bhadra 24 with representatives from PABSON, HISSAN, the Bharatpur Education Branch, and major schools to emphasize student safety. At 8:00 AM, a meeting with active political parties was held to urge restraint and maintain peace. The District Security Committee also imposed a curfew starting at 12:00 PM on Bhadra 24. Despite efforts to keep the situation calm, thousands gathered on Bhadra 24, turned aggressive following the previous day's deaths, and engaged in vandalism and arson against government buildings and leaders' private homes. This included government offices, local level and ward offices (except Madi Municipality), political party offices, media houses, and the vehicles of various leaders. On Bhadra 23, Gen-Z representatives had been warned to keep the demonstration peaceful and prevent infiltration.

On Bhadra 23, the memorandum was received at 11:30 AM, and the Gen-Z group was sent off with thanks. That evening, security officials visited those injured in the demonstrations at the hospital and instructed the hospital administration to ensure proper treatment. At 7:00 PM, after reviewing the day's events, it was decided to call representatives of PABSON, HISSAN, and political parties for a discussion on the morning of the 24th.

While only minor stone-pelting occurred at the Bharatpur Metropolitan building on Bhadra 23, the situation escalated on the 24th. The agitated mob vandalized or set fire to 53 government offices, 74 municipal and ward buildings, 6 political party offices, and 51 private buildings. Additionally, 86 four-wheelers and 106 two-wheelers were damaged, and one media house and the Bhat-Bhateni Supermarket building were completely destroyed. A damage assessment committee estimated the total loss at approximately 1,618,421,840.50 NPR (excluding the value of the Bhat-Bhateni store). Two protesters, including one of Indian origin, died after entering burning buildings to retrieve items.

Regarding the causes, the protesters became further agitated by the deaths in Kathmandu on the 23rd. Although attributed to Gen-Z, the Bhadra 24 protests involved various groups, including criminal elements, daily wage laborers, and scrap collectors. Direct involvement of local leaders and cadres from the RSP, CPN-UML, Nepali Congress, Maoist Center, RPP, and Biplav-led CPN was observed. Groups like RSP and Biplav's CPN primarily targeted government offices, while UML, Maoist Center, and RPP cadres targeted the private residences of individuals against whom they held grudges. The pattern involved stone-pelting and breaking glass followed by entering buildings for arson and looting. At the District Court and Government Attorney offices, criminal individuals and their relatives burned case files. The attacks on the District Administration Office appeared to be a retaliatory response to the events of Bhadra 23. It was understood that active political parties and their sister organizations largely participated in the arson and vandalism.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

Per the decisions of the District Security Committee on Bhadra 23 and 24, all four security agencies were deployed coordinately. Nepal Police personnel were stationed at 73 units as well as major city intersections. The Armed Police Force (APF) was divided into four main sectors: the Metropolitan area, East Chitwan, and South Chitwan. Following the curfew order, the Nepal Army was also deployed.

A curfew was issued for Bharatpur's market area and government office zones starting at 12:00 PM on Bhadra 24. This was extended district-wide from 5:00 PM on Bhadra 25 to normalize the situation. On Bhadra 26, the curfew was lifted and replaced by a prohibitory order, and offices were instructed to clean their premises.

Unexpected damage occurred because demonstrations happened simultaneously in many locations, making it impossible to get reinforcements from other districts. Security personnel were occupied defending their own units against massive crowds.

After the Army deployment on the night of the 24th, the situation began to normalize. However, while focus was placed on securing banks, financial institutions, and jewelry shops, inmates at the district prison began a protest. Despite the use of force and aerial firing by the Nepal Police and APF, the situation remained uncontrolled. According to a report passed on 2082/07/11, all inmates had escaped before the Nepal Army could take over duty on the morning of the 25th. Out of 750 inmates, 614 have been brought back so far.

The situation in the capital following the Prime Minister's resignation further fueled the damage. As evening approached, the general public began returning home, but individuals with criminal backgrounds remained to vandalize and loot offices. The situation began to improve after 10:00 PM on Bhadra 24 as crowds thinned and security personnel became more vigilant. The situation normalized as the Nepal Army took over security of the District Police Office, and police/APF units cleared the streets of aggressive groups. The role played by the CDO and the Security Committee was crucial. This information is from the incident statement by the then CDO of Chitwan, Ganesh Aryal, in Annex 6.
6.4. Gandaki Province
6.4.1. District Administration Office, Tanahu:

In view of potential protests following the events in Kathmandu, the District Security Committee issued a prohibitory order for government office zones in the district headquarters starting at 10:00 AM on Bhadra 24. A security command post was established in the CDO's office with heads of the Nepal Police, APF, and National Investigation Department to ensure coordination and regulation.

Around 11:00 AM, about 40-50 protesters gathered at Ansan Chautari in Damauli for a peaceful rally. By noon, the crowd grew and began attacking the offices of the CPN-UML, Nepali Congress, and Maoist Center. Between 1:00 PM and 3:00 PM, they pelted stones at the residence of the Right Honorable President in Vyas-2 and vandalized Vyas Municipality vehicles. From 3:00 PM to 7:00 PM, arson and vandalism occurred at the Division Forest Office, Inland Revenue Office, Water Resources and Irrigation Office, Land Revenue Office, Water Supply Office, Government Attorney Office, Tanahu District Court, and the District Coordination Committee office.

The District Administration Office was pelted with stones at midday. While security forces initially dispersed the crowd, a second attack occurred at 5:00 PM, resulting in the arson of vehicles and the administrative building. The CDO's residence and the police quarters stationed for office security were looted and burned, as were other staff residences.

Around 7:00 PM, protesters moved to the Tanahu District Prison. Following stone-pelting from the outside, the inmates inside also began vandalizing and set fire to the facility. All 327 inmates escaped after breaking the prison structure.
Vandalism and arson also occurred at the Shuklagandaki Municipality and various ward offices.

Despite the deployment of the Nepal Army on the night of Bhadra 24, looting of offices and staff residences continued. Identification of the perpetrators has been difficult as CCTV cameras around the offices and residences were destroyed during the protests.

On Bhadra 25, upon receiving reports that protesters were planning to attack ward offices in Vyas, Rishing, Ghiring, and Shuklagandaki, security teams including the Nepal Army conducted patrols to manage the risks.

On Bhadra 26, the District Security Committee decided to coordinately deploy security agencies to effectively implement the prohibitory and curfew orders issued via Nepal Army communiqué No. 13 on Bhadra 25.

On Bhadra 28, an Obstacle Management Committee was formed to assess and manage the damage to vehicles, structures, and materials. This was done to secure public spaces, clear transport obstructions, and create an environment to resume public services.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

The deployment of security agencies within the district was effective. A meeting was held involving security agencies, heads of government offices, and local representatives to ensure the continuity of services in offices damaged during the demonstrations. A committee was formed under the coordination of the Assistant Chief District Officer to manage the necessary machinery, tools, and furniture, creating an environment to resume services immediately.

During the looting, several passports and National Identity Cards that had reached a private residence from the District Administration Office were safely taken back into control. This information is contained in the incident statement by the then Chief District Officer of Tanahu, Ram Krishna Adhikari, in Annex 6.
6.4.2. District Administration Office, Kaski:

On August 22, 2082, following requests for permission for protest demonstrations by the Gen-Z generation starting from August 23, correspondence was sent to the District Police Office, Kaski, and the Armed Police Force Nepal No. 24 Battalion HQ, Kalika, Kaski, for security management of the protest programs.

For the security management of the Gen-Z protests declared from August 23, 2082, the District Security Committee made decisions on August 22. District security agencies formulated security and manpower deployment plans accordingly. On August 23, after demonstrations by the Gen-Z youth in various locations, the mob concentrated on the District Administration Office, Kaski, and other government offices. Following the decision of the District Security Committee, a prohibitory order was issued for specified areas. On the same day, after protesters attempted to vandalize the Office of the Chief Minister and Council of Ministers, clashes occurred between security personnel and protesters, resulting in injuries to both parties.

A curfew order was issued from 10:00 AM on August 24, 2082, until 10:00 PM the same night. As the possibility increased that protesters would defy the curfew and engage in destructive acts such as vandalism and arson of government, public, and private property, a meeting of the District Security Committee at approximately 12:00 PM on August 24 decided to request the Nepal Army's Krishna Dal Battalion, Phulbari Barracks, to implement the curfew and maintain peace and order.

Following the violation of the curfew (10:00 AM to 10:00 PM) and an increase in destructive activities, a request was made to the Shri Krishna Dal Battalion, Phulbari Barracks, at approximately 12:00 PM on August 24, per the District Security Committee's decision, for the coordinated deployment of all security agencies to implement the curfew and maintain peace.

At 10:40 PM on August 24, the fire and looting at Srijana Mini Market in Srijanachowk were controlled, and security was provided to the nearby Blood Bank of the Nepal Red Cross Society, Kaski District Branch.
Actions were taken to control vandalism and arson at Pokhara Trade Mall, and arson at the pharmacy in front of Western Regional Hospital, the URL Building, and BG Mall.

On August 25 at 8:30 AM, at the agriculture market in Shantiwan, Pokhara-7, some unruly individuals working in the market used the name of Gen-Z to attempt arson and vandalism due to personal grudges regarding past tender processes of the Market Management Committee. Upon receiving this information, a team from the Nepal Army's Krishna Dal Battalion immediately arrived and removed the anarchists. Nabaraj Duwadi, Chairman of the Agriculture Market Management Committee, filed a report with the District Police Office, Kaski, regarding vandalism in the administration, accounting, and planning sections, the Chairman's office, and the pesticide residue analysis lab, as well as the loss of Rs. 3,60,000 (three lakh sixty thousand) collected as rent. Investigations are ongoing.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

Even after the incidents of vandalism and arson starting from August 22, officials of the District Security Committee established a command post in one location and held meetings as needed. All security agencies worked coordinately to maintain peace, security, and harmony, aiming to prevent further human or material loss.

Attached are copies of the Security Committee's decisions for deployment and coordination, attendance records, and the security and manpower plans prepared by the agencies. Following the nationwide deployment of the Nepal Army on August 24, a team led by Deputy Superintendent of Police Hari Bahadur Basnet from the District Police Office, Kaski, with the help of the Pokhara Municipality fire brigade, controlled the fires at the District Police Office and the Bhat-Bhateni Supermarket building in Pokhara.

The Nepal Army was deployed at 9:45 PM on August 24, 2082. Following the deployment, actions included broadcasting curfew information via miking, foot patrols, mobile patrols, vehicle patrolling, and checkpoints. They successfully controlled fires in various parts of Pokhara, managed theft and looting, and recovered looted weapons and equipment. This information is from the incident statement of the then CDO of Kaski, Rudra Devi Sharma, in Annex 6.

6.4.3. District Administration Office, Parbat: On August 23 and 24, 2082, the Gen-Z generation held protests in this district with demands including national interest, good governance, and corruption control. Detailed incident reports are attached. Joint security forces of the Nepal Police and Armed Police Force were coordinately deployed to maintain peace and security.

During the movement, vandalism and arson occurred at the CPN (UML) District Party Office at Maitrichowk (Kushma-6), the Nepali Congress District Party Office at Vidhimarga (Kushma-4), the Maoist Center District Party Office at Darshantol (Kushma-5), the Modi Rural Municipality Executive Office (Modi-2), Yubraj Joshi's house at Shantitol (Kushma-4), and the house of former MP Padam Giri at Phulbari (Kushma-7). Statues of martyrs Govinda Nath Sharma and Amrit Prasad Joshi at Loktantrik Chowk (Kushma-5) were also damaged.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

After the deployment of the Nepal Army on the night of the 24th, no further attacks on public or private property occurred due to personal grudges. All district security agencies were coordinately deployed to normalize the overall security situation. This information is from the incident statement of the then CDO of Parbat, Hari Prasad Ghimire, in Annex 6.
6.5. Lumbini Province
6.5.1. District Administration Office, Rupandehi

On August 23, 2082, at 11:40 AM, a group led by independent student Bibek Bhandari, consisting of approx. 50-60 motorcycles and 1,000 pedestrians, started a rally from Eye Hospital Chowk (Siddharthanagar-3) protesting against the social media ban and corruption. A team of 55 personnel led by DSP Ganesh Sapkota was deployed. At 1:50 PM, another group arriving from Butwal joined them, totaling 2,000–2,500 people in front of the District Administration Office (Siddharthanagar-5). At 2:15 PM, the rally reached the Nepali Congress party office, where stone pelting broke two window panes and damaged four cars, three scooters, and one motorcycle. Police dispersed the crowd after counseling them.

On August 23 at 5:35 PM, approx. 500–600 people from the Gen-Z group gathered at Buddhanagar (Sainamaina-5), held a corner meeting, and burned tires. A team of 17 led by Inspector Khagendra Adhikari from Saljhandi Area Police Office arrived, extinguished the tires, and normalized the situation. At 5:50 PM, approx. 300–400 Gen-Z members gathered at Saljhandi Chowk (Sainamaina-10), paraded through the market, and held another meeting. A team of 10 from Saljhandi normalized the situation.

On August 23 at 7:45 PM, approx. 500 people from the Gen-Z group gathered at Pharsatikar Chowk (Suddhondhan-4) for a protest rally and corner meeting. A team of 13 led by Inspector Shailendra Pandey was deployed; the program ended at 8:30 PM.

On August 23 at 8:30 PM, during the curfew, approx. 250–300 Gen-Z members gathered via social media at Manigram Chowk (Tilottama-5), chanting anti-government slogans and pelting stones at a private house. A team of 5 led by Inspector Sunil Thapa Nepali from Mangalapur reached the site. When they attempted to counsel the group, they were assaulted. To control the situation, the following individuals were detained: 1) Subash Pandari (32), 2) Sagar Dhakal (24), 3) Sandesh Bhandari (20), 4) Dipendra Bhusal (23), and 5) Padam Gairi (29). They were later released to their relatives.

On August 23 at 10:20 PM, approx. 400–500 people gathered at Thakali Chowk (Kanchan-4) for a peaceful candle-lighting ceremony to pay tribute to those who died during the Gen-Z protests in Kathmandu and to wish for the recovery of the injured. A team of 5 from Rudrapur was deployed; the program ended at 10:40 PM.

A curfew order was issued on August 23 from 4:00 PM to 10:00 PM, covering areas from the petrol pump/thread factory bridge in the east to Belwas Chowk in the west, and Vidia Khola in the north to Mangalapur in the south.

A curfew was also issued from 4:00 PM to 10:00 PM on the same day covering 800 meters of the Butwal-Belahiya road section from Rohini Khola in the east to Bethari Bridge in the west, and Buddha Chowk in the north to Pipara in the south.

On August 24 at 11:00 AM, during the prohibitory period, a protest rally led by youth Gaurav Acharya began at Chauraha Chowk (Butwal-10) with approx. 5,000 participants, protesting the social media ban, corruption, and the deaths in Kathmandu. The rally reached the Butwal municipal office and returned via Kalikanagar. At 2:30 PM, the group reached the UML party office in Sukkhanagar (Butwal-8) and engaged in vandalism and arson. A team of 70 led by a DSP was deployed. At 3:00 PM, the group vandalized the Jivraj Ashrit building (Butwal-7). At 3:35 PM, they vandalized and set fire to the house of Provincial Assembly member Bhoj Prasad Shrestha (Butwal-8). At 3:40 PM, they damaged the B.P. Memorial (Butwal-7). At 4:15 PM, they vandalized Tulip Hotel (Tilottama-4). At 4:35 PM, they vandalized, burned, and looted the Inland Revenue Office (Butwal-9), with the crowd increasing to 7,000–8,000. At 4:43 PM, they pelted stones at the police bit and National Investigation Office. At 5:20 PM, they vandalized and burned the house of MP Basudev Ghimire (Tilottama-4). At 5:30 PM, they attacked the CIAA Provincial Office, Area Administration Office, and the Chief Minister's residence. At 5:50 PM, they set fire to the Division Forest Office. At 5:55 PM, they attacked the Province Head's office and residence and the High Court in Butwal. At 6:00 PM, they attacked the Butwal municipal office, Land Revenue Office, and Transport Office. Further damage included Golpark Campus, Bhat-Bhateni Supermarket (looted), the Nepali Congress office, and Amarpath police bit. At 6:30 PM, they attacked the house of Provincial Assembly member Lila Giri (Tilottama-1). During the sequential use of force to disperse the crowds, the police lost 80 lathis, 22 helmets, and 35 shields. They fired 9 SLR blank rounds, 19 tear gas shells, 3 Beretta rounds, and 3 shotgun rubber bullets. Three pairs of handcuffs were lost. The team returned after the area normalized at 9:30 PM.

On August 24 at 11:30 AM, approx. 400 Gen-Z members gathered at the house of Sainamaina Mayor Phanindra Sharma in Jharvera. Teams led by DSP Ganesh Sapkota and an Inspector were deployed. To disperse the growing crowd, police fired 22 tear gas shells and 18 dummy rounds. A police Scorpio (Ba Pr 01-002 Jha 7332) and the Mayor's house windows were damaged. No human casualties occurred.
On August 24 at 11:40 AM, approx. 300 Gen-Z activists locked the Ward No. 12 office in Bakrahar (Tilottama). A team of 12 led by an Inspector arrived; the situation normalized soon after.

On August 24 at 12:00 PM, approx. 600–700 Gen-Z members started a rally from Pharsatikar (Suddhondhan-4), moving from the municipal office to the Area Administration Office and then to the houses of Provincial Assembly member Tulsi Chaudhary and Rural Municipality Chairman Bishnu Prasad Bashyal before dispersing.

On August 24 at 12:00 PM, approx. 400–500 people gathered for a protest at Sainamaina-5. A team of 13 dispersed the crowd. At 12:30 PM, reports came of approx. 250–300 people locking the Ward No. 10 office at Saljhandi Chowk; a team of 6 found no one there upon arrival. Protesters also locked the Ward No. 11 office at 1:10 PM before dispersing.

On August 24 at 12:10 PM, approx. 500 Gen-Z members, including students from private and government schools, marched from Khaireni Chowk to the Devdaha municipal office. A team of 29 was deployed. The group moved to the UML party office, vandalizing it (damaging windows, tables, chairs, and AC), and then to the houses of the Deputy Mayor and Mayor, where they engaged in slogans and arson. They dispersed by 6:30 PM.
On August 24 at 12:30 PM, approx. 200–250 activists locked the Ward No. 8 office at Shantinagar (Tilottama). A team of 12 arrived, but the group had already left.

On August 24 at 12:40 PM, approx. 500–600 people gathered outside the gate of Gautam Buddha International Airport. A team of 120 led by a DSP was deployed. The group grew to 2,000 by 3:25 PM and moved to the District Administration Office. At 3:30 PM, after vandalizing the Nepali Congress office and pelting stones at police, the police fired 3 dummy rounds and 5 tear gas shells. The mob burned tires at Devkota Chowk and moved back to the airport. At 5:00 PM, approx. 1,000 people entered the domestic gate and set fire to 4 government vehicles. The group then split; at 6:00 PM, they burned the house of Siddharthanagar Deputy Mayor Uma Adhikari. At 6:20 PM, they vandalized the District Traffic Police and Government Attorney offices at Goligadh, burning private and police vehicles. At 7:00 PM, they vandalized and looted Maurya Hotel and Bhat-Bhateni Supermarket. Police returned after they dispersed at 7:30 PM.
On August 24 at 3:00 PM, approx. 200–300 activists broke the lock and three window panes at the Ward No. 5 office in Manigram. A team of 12 found no one upon arrival.

On August 24 at 3:30 PM, approx. 300–400 people attacked and burned the house and compound of Provincial Minister for Physical Infrastructure Development, Bhumishwar Dhakal, in Kalauni. At 3:45 PM, approx. 4,500 people vandalized and burned the Nepali Congress office in Kalauni before moving toward Saljhandi.

On August 24 at 4:00 PM, approx. 200–300 people vandalized the UML City Committee office (Tilottama-3). At 4:05 PM, approx. 500–600 people attacked and burned the two houses (one-story and two-story) of former Home Minister Bal Krishna Khand in Tilottama-5. Police fired two tear gas shells. The group dispersed after the arson.
On August 24 at 4:43 PM, approx. 500–600 protesters vandalized the temporary police bit at Butwal Bus Park and took a pair of handcuffs. The police team moved to Butwal Area Police Office for safety.

On August 24 at 4:45 PM, approx. 300–350 activists attacked the house of Tilottama Mayor Ram Krishna Khand, burning household items and breaking all windows. The group dispersed after setting the fire.

On August 24 at 5:10 PM, approx. 100–150 activists attacked and burned the house of Siddharthanagar Mayor Istiyaq Ahmed Khan in Belahiya. At 6:20 PM, approx. 400 protesters set fire to the Bhairahawa Customs Office.

On August 24 at 6:30 PM, the house of Provincial Minister for Youth and Sports, Santosh Pandey, was vandalized and burned. Lost items belonging to his security guards included a motorcycle (Lu 81 Pa 1732), police uniforms, certificates, bedding, a Motorola handset, and a charger.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

Coordination among security agencies during the Gen-Z protests was effective. To control the events, a prohibitory order was issued from 9:30 AM on August 24, 2082, until further notice, banning gatherings of more than five people for protests, sit-ins, or rallies across Rupandehi.

A curfew was issued across Rupandehi from 8:30 PM on August 24, 2082. While there was significant material damage, no human lives were lost. This information is from the incident statement by the then CDO of Rupandehi, Tok Raj Pandey, in Annex 6.
6.5.1 District Administration Office, Banke:

Regarding the government's ban on social media and anti-corruption issues, on August 23 at approximately 9:45 AM, a group of about 200 to 600 people, including Gen-Z youth, gathered at B.P. Chowk in Nepalgunj Sub-metropolitan City, Ward No. 11. They organized a rally, paraded through the city, and staged a sit-in in front of the Nepalgunj Sub-metropolitan City office gate at around 11:55 AM. During the sit-in at approximately 3:00 PM, protesters threw stones and bottles and attempted to force their way into the municipality office. During the police intervention and ensuing scuffle, Binay Nepali (resident of Khajura Rural Municipality-3) and Jay Prakash Bapa (originally from Dailekh, currently residing in Nepalgunj-6) sustained minor injuries and were immediately taken to Bheri Hospital for treatment. Both were reportedly discharged the same day after receiving primary care. Following the municipal sit-in, the rally proceeded to the main gate of the District Administration Office and concluded peacefully after a brief demonstration.

On the same day at approximately 10:25 AM, about 300 individuals, including Gen-Z youth, gathered at Kohalpur Crossroads in Ward No. 11. Following a city parade, they gathered in front of the Kohalpur Municipality gate at 12:25 PM for a protest rally, which concluded peacefully at approximately 2:45 PM. Thus, in the two demonstrations on August 23, a total of two protesters sustained minor injuries and received immediate treatment.

The District Security Committee meeting held after 3:00 PM on August 23 noted the sensitive current situation where demonstrations under the name of 'Gen-Z' were occurring in Kathmandu and various parts of the country. Taking into account the day's protests in Nepalgunj and Kohalpur, the committee decided on microscopic analysis of potential security challenges, effective information gathering, coordination, and maintaining normalcy through patrolling and miking. It was decided that all security agencies would remain on full alert, deploy coordinately, and exchange reliable information.

On the morning of August 24, around 10:00 AM, protesters began vandalizing and committing arson at the CPN (UML) party office. Security personnel stationed there were unable to control the situation. Fearing the fire might spread to nearby houses, various individuals, including the CPN (UML) Banke District Chairman, called the Chief District Officer (CDO) to request fire engines. Although the Mayor was contacted and fire engines were dispatched, they could not reach the site due to obstructions created by protesters in Nepalgunj. Shortly after the arson at the CPN (UML) office, news arrived of arson at the Nepali Congress party office. Subsequently, massive vandalism, arson, and looting occurred at dozens of government buildings and properties, including the Inland Revenue Office, Revenue Investigation Office, Kohalpur Municipality, Land Survey and Land Revenue offices in Kohalpur, Area Administration Office Kohalpur, Nepalgunj Sub-metropolitan City, District Administration Office, District Court, High Court, High Government Attorney's Office, District Post Office, Agriculture Knowledge Center, and the Customs Office. Furniture, computers, documents, and vehicles were destroyed. Statues of B.P. Koirala and Pushpalal at various intersections in Nepalgunj were vandalized, and several police posts, including traffic posts, were demolished. Regarding private property, the CDO received information only about damage to the houses of the Mayor and Deputy Mayor of Kohalpur and some ward chairs; no other private property damage was reported. The violence intensified after 2:00 PM, at which point the situation was felt to be going out of control. From 10:00 AM, a command post consisting of all District Security Committee officials was stationed in the CDO's office. As reports of violence arrived via phone from political leaders, local representatives, and office heads, the CDO repeatedly ordered the security chiefs (specifically the Nepal Police and Armed Police Force) to take immediate actionincluding miking, water cannons, lathi charges, tear gas, and aerial firing (ensuring no human casualties)in accordance with the principle of sequential use of force and the Local Administration Act, 1971. After the vandalism at the Nepalgunj municipal office, as the mob moved toward the District Administration Office, the Nepal Army Lieutenant Colonel suggested 2-3 times that the CDO move to a nearby Army post (approx. 250 meters away) for safety. After receiving similar guidance from the Ministry of Home Affairs, the CDO moved to the 4th Brigade post at 3:30 PM. Within 10-15 minutes of his arrival there, the mob attacked, vandalized, and looted the District Administration Office square. Even while stationed at the Army post for safety, the CDO continued to coordinate via phone to fulfill his duties, handle security demands, and arrange fire engines. Every incident was reported to and coordinated with the Ministry of Home Affairs throughout the day. After 9:00 PM, following reports of increasing theft and looting in Nepalgunj, an emergency security meeting was held at the Army post at 9:30 PM, and joint security patrols were deployed from 10:00 PM.

During the emergency meeting at the Army post at 9:30 PM on August 24, it was decided that due to the extreme situation arising from vandalism, arson, and looting of public and private property, and to ensure the safety of citizens, a prohibitory order (curfew) would be recommended to the CDO under the Local Administration Act, 1971. Based on this recommendation, the CDO issued a prohibitory order for specified areas effective from 10:00 PM on August 24 until further notice. Later, per the recommendation of the meeting on August 26, the prohibitory order was extended with specific time slots for the entire Banke district.

Additionally, the August 24 security committee meeting decided that all security agencies would be deployed jointly to control the chaos. Subsequently, from 10:00 PM that night, joint patrols including the CDO and heads of the Nepal Army, Nepal Police, Armed Police Force, and National Investigation Department were initiated.

Following reports of increased theft in Nepalgunj after 10:00 PM, the security team was deployed. Upon arriving at the Nepalgunj Sub-metropolitan City office based on reports of ongoing theft from the burning building, several individuals were seen fleeing. While patrolling main intersections, the customs chief reported ongoing theft at the Nepalgunj Customs Office; the team immediately proceeded to the Jamunaha border customs area. While at the customs perimeter, the CDO received a call from an unknown number. The caller confirmed the CDO's identity and reported that shooting had occurred at the Juvenile Correction Center (Child Reform Home) and some friends were injured. Between 10:30 PM and 11:00 PM, the CDO questioned the Police and APF Superintendents present with him. After checking their channels, they reported that no shooting had occurred against people, only a few rounds of aerial fire were used. Around midnight, the team, including both SPs, left to inspect the Naubasta Prison and the adjacent Juvenile Correction Center. The CDO noted that his information regarding the events at Naubasta (occurring roughly between 10:15 PM and 11:00 PM) was limited, and detailed reports would follow from the respective offices, including information on escaped inmates.

After the joint patrol ended around 1:30 AM on August 25, the CDO and the district head of the National Investigation Department stayed at the Ranjha Barracks (Major's office) for two nights (the 24th and 25th).

Following Army deployment after 10:00 PM on the 24th, news arrived around 1:00 PM on August 25 that 123 juveniles had escaped from the Jayandu Child Reform Home. The District Security Committee led by the CDO reached the site and used miking to request their return; three juveniles subsequently returned.

Around 3:30 PM on August 26, reports were received that inmates in Block 'B' of the Banke District Prison were attempting to escape after starting a fire. The situation was brought under control by joint forces. During the action, a few rounds of aerial fire were used, and one inmate was injured and taken to the hospital.

Reflecting on the causes, while the August 23 protest in Banke was largely peaceful, the August 24 incident resulted in heavy damage to public and some private property. In my opinion, the potential causes were:

The tragic death of two students from Banke during the August 23 Kathmandu protest fueled anger among local protesters; widespread criticism on social media and mass media regarding the use of force by security personnel in Kathmandu led to hesitation among local security forces to deploy effectively on August 24; reports of attacks on police posts and looting of weapons in Kathmandu negatively impacted the morale of local security personnel; there was a perceived lack of clear direction/guidance from the Ministry of Home Affairs and central security leadership; the news of the attack on the central administrative hub (Singhadurbar) and destruction of vital state organs across the country had a paralyzing effect on district security agenciessimilar to how a brain hemorrhage affects body functions; fear among security personnel that they might face legal action tomorrow for fulfilling their duties today; a lack of adequate resources and equipment; although the CDO has the legal authority to order force under the Local Administration Act, 1971, in practice, security chiefs feel hesitant without explicit orders from their own central headquarters; the long-standing inability of security agencies to remain free from pressure and influence; a deep-seated distrust and anger among citizens toward government agencies due to poor service delivery; a lack of service-oriented behavior among providers; the historical precedent where government property is damaged in every movement without significant consequences for the perpetrators, leading to a lack of fear; a failure to instill a sense of ownership and care for public property among citizens; a protest culture focused solely on rights without a sense of duty or responsibility; a failure of the education system to promote ethics, discipline, and responsible citizenship; and prevalent impunity, weak law enforcement, and a lack of good governance in society.

In the context of Banke, the August 23 protest seemed to involve Gen-Z youth along with some other groups, whereas the August 24 violence appeared to involve individuals with criminal backgrounds and tendencies. Following a public appeal for information by the Banke District Administration and Police, significant evidence including videos, CCTV footage, and photos related to the August 24 violence was collected. Therefore, detailed information regarding the individuals/groups involved can be obtained from the Banke District Police Office. Furthermore, a comprehensive picture of those involved is likely to emerge through the collection of CCTV footage from various government offices and relevant information from affected private individuals.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

In the case of Banke district, on Bhadra 23, no untoward incidents occurred during the demonstrations in Nepalgunj Sub-metropolitan City and Kohalpur Municipality due to effective deployment and coordination of security agencies. To manage the potential impact of the incidents that occurred in Kathmandu on Bhadra 23 and to ensure effective deployment and reliable coordination for Bhadra 24, a District Security Committee meeting was held for nearly three hours from 3:00 PM to 6:00 PM on Bhadra 23, making various decisions and preparations. Additionally, from 10:00 AM on Bhadra 24, a command post was established in the office of the Chief District Officer (CDO) at the District Administration Office, Banke, in an attempt to maintain peace, security, and order throughout the district. Despite these efforts, when incidents of vandalism, arson, and looting by protesters began occurring in various field locations one after another, the security personnel deployed in the field were unable to control the crowd as expected. As soon as information regarding the ongoing vandalism, arson, and looting reached the CDO via telephone from political leaders, local representatives, and office heads, the CDO repeatedly requested and ordered the security chiefs present in his office (specifically the Nepal Police and Armed Police Force chiefs) to take necessary action to control the mobincluding miking, water cannons, lathi charges, tear gas, and aerial firing (ensuring no human casualties) in accordance with the principle of sequential use of force and the Local Administration Act, 1971. However, even as dozens of government offices and hundreds of government vehicles were vandalized, burned, and looted, along with damage to private property, not a single protester was detained or injured, nor were any security personnel in the field seen to be injured. Considering these circumstances, from the CDO's perspective, the deployment and coordination of security agencies in Banke on Bhadra 24 cannot be considered satisfactory or effective as expected. This account by the then CDO of Banke, Dharmaraj Joshi, is contained in the original incident statement in Annex 6.
6.5.2. District Administration Office (Nawalparasi Bardaghat Susta East):

No application was received from any individual or group at this office for permission to hold demonstrations by the Gen-Z group on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082. During the movement, discussions were held with representatives of the protesters who surrounded the District Administration Office, requesting them to keep the protest peaceful. Throughout the protest period, a security command post including all members of the District Security Committee was established at the District Administration Office to issue necessary instructions. For security arrangements on the 24th, 322 personnel from the District Police Office, 200 from the Armed Police Force district units, and 15 from the National Investigation Department were coordinately deployed, during which 7 shells of tear gas were fired across the district. The Nepal Army from the No. 2 Forest and Environment Security Battalion was deployed after 10:00 PM.

The protest and motorcycle rally, said to be organized by the Gen-Z youth generation, involved individuals in the 15-40 age group. At 10:30 AM on the 24th, a group of approximately 2,500-3,000 people first arrived at the District Administration Office location (Kawasoti Municipality-3, Sangam Chowk) with slogans and surrounded it. At that time, all members of the District Security Committee were in a meeting at the command post established within the office.

When some representatives were called into the CDO's office to discuss the protesters' demands and grievances, they demanded that the administration stop service delivery to support the movement; they were sent back after being counseled. Subsequently, that group moved toward the Kawasoti Municipality location and engaged in vandalism and arson of the municipality building. The group, removed from there by the security team, joined with others from Gaidakot, Devchuli, and other areas. This larger group proceeded to various locations in the district, causing damage to 53 structuresincluding ward offices, political party offices, private residences of the Mayor and other leaders, and commercial establishmentsand vandalizing or burning 40 private and government vehicles.

The mob, agitated by the protests, vandalism, and the death of a protester in Kathmandu on Bhadra 23, reacted to these events as they were rapidly disseminated through social media from locations like Kaski and Chitwan, influencing the destructive events here as well. When security personnel stopped a group engaged in arson/vandalism at one location, another group would move to a different location to do the same. It was noted that an informal message from the central leadership of the security agencies not to use excessive force on the 24th gave protesters the psychological strength to engage in destruction. There was a lack of unity in the orders given to district security chiefs. Even after the CDO's orders and committee decisions, the practice of waiting for orders from their own respective departmental headquarters persisted.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

No application was received from any individual or group for permission for the Gen-Z group demonstration on Bhadra 23 and 24. During the movement, discussions were held with representatives of the protesters at the District Administration Office, requesting a peaceful protest. A security command post was established at the office for instructions. On the 24th, 322 police, 200 APF, and 15 NID personnel were deployed, and 7 tear gas shells were fired. The Nepal Army was deployed after 10:00 PM. As the movement turned violent, a curfew was issued at 2:30 PM on the 24th; however, the Nepal Army was not deployed immediately as they stated they had not received orders from their departmental headquarters. Orders and notices under sections 6(a) and 6(b) of the Local Administration Act, 1971, were issued during and after the protests.

To prevent damage, regular contact and coordination were maintained with district political leaders, government offices, educational institutions, and stakeholders. After the events of the 24th, a meeting of office heads was held to normalize the situation and resume services.

Various individuals aged 15 to 40 were found involved. No specific group has been identified so far. Seven individuals involved in the vandalism and arson of the police post and vehicles (owned by Hupsekot Rural Municipality) in Hupsekot Ward No. 1 were arrested and are under investigation.
