The District Police Office is continuing to search for others and collect evidence. This account by the then CDO of Nawalparasi (East), Bhavishwar Pandey, is in Annex 6.
6.6. Karnali Province

6.6.1. District Administration Office, Surkhet: On Bhadra 23, 2082, a general gathering of protesters took place at Ghantaghar Chowk. Approximately 1,500-2,000 Gen-Z youth gathered at Ghantaghar Chowk, Birendranagar-7, held a corner meeting, and marched past the Provincial Assembly and Chief Minister's office through the market area, concluding peacefully. Nepal Police and APF were deployed, and no damage occurred on the 23rd.

On Bhadra 23, the District Security Committee decided that all security agencies should remain on high alert and prepared, based on an assessment of potential activities and security challenges related to recent issues raised by the Gen-Z generation and other contemporary events.

On the morning of Tuesday, Bhadra 24, at 11:00 AM, the District Security Committee analyzed information indicating that protest programs would be held regarding the issues raised by the Gen-Z generation. High alertness was deemed necessary. It was decided to use miking to request the large crowd to remain peaceful and to deploy all security personnel to prevent damage.

On Tuesday afternoon at 3:00 PM, the District Security Committee noted that the anarchic mob in Birendranagar was continuously attempting vandalism, arson, and damage to life and property, including throwing stones and bottles at police. To bring the security situation under control, a decision was made to issue a curfew order under Section 6(a) of the Local Administration Act, 1971, effective from 4:30 PM on Bhadra 24 until further notice, prohibiting movement and gatherings in the specified areas of Birendranagar.

Due to news of damage in the capital and other districts starting from the afternoon of the 24th, the movement in the Surkhet district headquarters also turned violent. That afternoon, party offices of the UML, Nepali Congress, Maoist Center, RPP, and Rastriya Janamorcha in Birendranagar-6 (Hatiyaline) were set on fire, destroying documents, files, and furniture. Police used minimal force to normalize the situation. During the arson, security personnel rescued six protesters from the roof of the Maoist party office. Fire engines were sent to extinguish the fires, but protesters pelted the fire engines with stones, causing damage.

The mob turned violent, ignoring security requests, and vandalized Birendranagar Municipality, the CIAA office, the Chief Minister’s office, the High Government Attorney's office, the Province Head's office and residence, City Hall, the District Coordination Committee office, and various federal and provincial ministries.

After security personnel prevented the mob from vandalizing the Chief Minister's office, the frustrated group moved to the other side and engaged in vandalism, arson, and looting at the Survey Office and the Province Treasury Controller Office, and set fire to vehicles at the Chief Attorney's office.

As the mob became more violent, a curfew was enforced at 4:30 PM on the 24th. Following the curfew, the Provincial Assembly, 12 ward offices in the Birendranagar valley, and the Chief Minister’s residence were vandalized, burned, and looted.

Also after the curfew, the house and vehicles of Nepali Congress Vice President and former Defense Minister Purna Bahadur Khadka, and his brothers Dr. Dambar Khadka and Vishnu Khadka, were burned and looted. Vishnu Khadka’s pharmacy was also burned and vandalized.
Furthermore, the private homes of Chief Minister Yamlal Kandel, Kawasoti Mayor Mohan Maya Dhakal, and Deputy Mayor Nilkantha Khanal were vandalized, burned, and looted.
The angry mob also vandalized and looted private property, including the Shuva Hotel and an NIC ASIA Bank ATM.

As the protesters grew more provocative, the deployed security forces made every effort to protect government, private, and public structures using the principle of minimum force under the Local Administration Act, 1971, and the Essential Services Operation Act, 1958. During crowd control, 26 Nepal Police personnel, 14 APF personnel, and 13 protesters were injured. One individual, Santosh Bohora, remains under treatment for serious injuries, while others have returned home after treatment for minor injuries.

Security action was required to minimize damage as the crowd went out of control. Efforts were made to normalize the situation through requests to stop arson, lathi charges, tear gas, and aerial firing. In this process, the Nepal Police fired 136 shotgun rounds, 122 blank rounds, and 452 tear gas shells, along with 232 blank and 2 live SLR rounds. The APF used 93 tear gas shells and 11 live rounds.

Equipment damage included 30 shields, 25 helmets, 118 lathis, and 7 soft jackets for the Nepal Police, and 13 lathis, 5 body protectors, 32 shields, 3 helmets, and 4 megaphones for the APF. Security forces were deployed with full restraint to avoid any kind of loss to any party, following the principle of minimum force.

A curfew was in effect from 5:00 PM to 10:00 PM on Bhadra 24. Ward offices and private residences were looted and burned during this time. Nepal Police and APF tried to control it. Significant damage occurred because the Nepal Army could not be deployed until 10:00 PM on the 24th after the curfew was imposed. The situation normalized after the Army's deployment at night.

Analysis of the causes and participants suggests that the Gen-Z generation protested with various demands regarding national interest, good governance, and anti-corruption. As a reaction to dissatisfaction with the governance system, an uncontrolled mob including many Gen-Z individuals joined the movement. This was further infiltrated by groups with criminal motives and individuals seeking personal or political revenge, leading to attacks, arson, and looting of private, political, and government property. Participants included Gen-Z groups, unidentified individuals, and people from political parties both outside and inside the then-government acting against each other.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies

Even before the movement began, a command post was established at the District Administration Office, Surkhet, consisting of the Chief District Officer and chiefs of all district security agencies. Security forces were deployed jointly through information exchange, discussion, and coordination to face potential security challenges during the movement.

On the afternoon of August 24, 2082, government vehicles were secured by keeping them within the compounds of the Kalidal Battalion and the Karnali Province Police Office. At 22:00 on August 24, 2082, Nepal Army troops were deployed at various main intersections and checkpoints in the Birendranagar Municipality valley area of Surkhet district, including Mangalgadhi, Ghantaghar Chowk, Itram Chowk, Telecom Chowk, Subbakuna, and Bangesimal. Troops were also stationed to provide security at sensitive and important locations like the District Prison and Surkhet Airport. A total of 565 personnel from various Nepal Army units were deployed: 250 from Shree Kali Bahadur Battalion, 105 from Shree No. 1 Rajdal Battalion, 105 from Shree Binayak Dal Battalion, and 105 from Shree Kalidal Battalion (E). Aside from minor curfew violations, no untoward incidents occurred after 22:00 on the 24th or during the 25th. No Nepal Army personnel were injured. On August 24, 2082, no action was taken by the main army of the Karnali Province government against anyone. Chief Minister Yamlal Kandel was moved to the Kalidal Battalion Kapase Barracks at 13:15 for his safety.

On August 23, 2082, 45 personnel out of a total sanctioned strength of 449 from the Armed Police Force (APF), Nepal, were deployed. No untoward incidents occurred in the district on the 23rd. From 6:00 AM on August 24, 2082, a total of 247 Armed Police personnel were deployed in Surkhet district. During riot management that day, 93 rounds of tear gas and 11 live rounds were used. During the process, 14 APF personnel sustained minor injuries. No action was taken against any individual during the riot control. During the process, 13 lathis (batons), 5 sets of body protector equipment, 32 shields, 3 helmets, and 4 megaphones were completely damaged.

Based on the assessment of the security challenge, additional police personnel were deployed from other security agencies, regional offices, and battalions. Although the district has a sanctioned strength of 543, the available manpower was insufficient for police deployment. Because the Karnali Province Police Training Center and the Nepal Police Special Security Battalion (which should be in Karnali) were located in Nepalgunj, Banke district, the number of security personnel in Surkhet was low. To prevent the movement from becoming more violent and chaotic, the principle of 'minimum force' was adopted by the security forces. During the process, 136 rounds from shotguns, 122 blank rounds from .303 rifles, 452 tear gas shells from gas guns, and 234 rounds of SLR (of which 232 were blank and 2 were live) were fired. This information is contained in the original incident report (Annex 6) submitted by the then Chief District Officer of Surkhet, Jagadishwor Upadhyay.
6.7. Sudurpashchim Province
6.7.1. District Administration Office, Kanchanpur:

On August 23, 2082, regarding the death of a person by security force gunfire during the Gen-Z movement, a student organization held a torch rally and corner meeting around 19:00 in the market located in Bhimdatta Municipality Ward No. 4. At 07:30 AM on August 24, transport entrepreneurs, drivers, and helpers blocked roads to show solidarity. Spontaneously, student leaders and representatives of political parties, representatives of the Chamber of Commerce, local merchants, officials and members of 'Power of Youth', local residents, and Indian laborers/traders gathered at Chamman Chowk and took out a rally. Some individuals went to the Nepali Congress and UML party offices and committed arson and vandalism. Subsequently, they threw stones at former Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak's house in the market, the municipality office gate, and the CIAA office gate, and committed arson and vandalism at the District Administration Office, Shuklaphanta Municipality office, and various ward offices. Vandalism and arson were also carried out at Krishnapur, Punarwas, and Dodhara Chandani municipalities, and vehicles were damaged. After minimal force was used at the District Administration Office, the crowd was controlled to some extent. However, that group proceeded to set fire to former Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak's house in Bedkot Municipality-8, and arson and vandalism occurred at 77 locations including the houses of former Chief Minister Rajendra Rawal, Province Assembly Member Prakash Rawal, Provincial Ministers Hira Sarki, Bahadur Thapa, and Surendra Pal, UML District In-charge Nirudevi Pal, the District Coordination Committee Chief, and mayors/deputy mayors of various municipalities. Causes of the incident: Dissatisfaction among youth regarding the government's social media ban and issues of governance/anti-corruption, combined with uncontrolled social media, appear to have fueled these events.

In identifying and analyzing the group involved, photos and videos of those involved in the vandalism and arson on the 24th are uploaded on social media. Youth and student leaders from UML, Nepali Congress, Maoist Center, RPP, RSP, Biplav-led Maoist, and others were found involved. Additionally, Indian nationals working in the district, transport officials, drivers, helpers, mechanics, hotel entrepreneurs, and hotel staff were seen participating, particularly with the intent of looting property. Deployment of Security Forces: Assessing that the impact of the Kathmandu movement could spread nationwide, a District Security Committee meeting was held under the command of the Chief District Officer to prevent further damage. Nepal Police, APF, and National Investigation Department personnel were deployed. Security personnel were mainly deployed to political party offices, houses of former ministers, municipal offices, the District Administration Office, and other government offices.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies

All security agencies in this district operated in mutual coordination. As security personnel were spread thin across various locations in the district, their presence at specific spots was low, whereas the crowd of protesters remained large and concentrated. Consequently, security forces were unable to prevent damage to private and government offices. After security forces used some tear gas and aerial fire to protect the District Administration Office, the protesters split into various small groups and intensified arson and vandalism targeting leaders, ministers, municipal offices, and the homes of mayors/deputy mayors. The situation turned violent because security forces could not be present at all locations in time. Therefore, to target such future movements, it appears legal provisions should be made so that security deployment follows the direct leadership of the Chief District Officer as per necessity, rather than waiting for orders from higher authorities, with the CDO holding full responsibility for that period. This is based on the incident report submitted by the then Chief District Officer of Kanchanpur, Laxman Dhakal, found in Annex 6.
6.7.2. District Administration, Kailali

Regarding the protests or opposition planned for August 23 in Kailali district, local Gen-Z group leaders like Sarita Joshi and others had informed the District Administration Office and security agencies. The organizers gave a verbal commitment that the protest would be peaceful. Accordingly, the administration provided verbal suggestions and directions to conduct the protest peacefully. The activities of the group and the efforts of the security agencies on the 23rd were reported as follows. Following the tragic incident involving human loss at the Parliament House in Kathmandu on August 23, the District Security Committee and heads of security agencies analyzed the threat, anticipating that the Gen-Z movement could become powerful nationwide on August 24. Considering the possibility of vandalism and arson at provincial ministries, the Provincial Assembly, the District Administration Office, residences of high-ranking political figures, and commercial centers, the security committee analyzed the information and decided to provide security to key offices. APF No. 34 Battalion HQ, Kailali, deployed duties from 08:00 AM on August 24 according to the security plan prepared on August 23.

Continuous coordination was maintained with representatives of various political parties, the District Security Committee, and other security agencies. Information was disseminated to commanders deployed for security through a risk analysis conducted during a meeting at 11:00 AM.

On the morning of August 24, 2082, various individuals gathered from Kailali Multiple Campus, Aishwarya Multiple Campus, and other locations. They took out a rally against corruption and the social media ban, paraded through the market, and held corner meetings in front of the Chief Minister's office gate and the District Administration Office gate. Rallies with banners against corruption and protest programs were also held in other parts of the district. During this time, arson and vandalism occurred at Sudurpashchim Provincial Government ministries, local government buildings, various ward offices, other government offices, party offices, Nepal Police units, the residence of former Prime Minister Sher Bahadur Deuba, the house of former Minister Lekhraj Bhatta, and private residences of mayors and ward members. Private establishments like hotels and commercial sites, along with four-wheel and two-wheel vehicles parked there, were also damaged.

On the same day from 08:05 AM, approximately 9,000 to 10,000 students and Gen-Z supporters took out a protest rally against corruption and the social media ban. Arson and vandalism occurred at provincial ministries, government offices, party offices, police units, and VIP residences. During clashes between protesters and security forces trying to control the violence, at 12:40 PM at the Chief Minister's office in Dhangadhi-4, a 35-member team under the command of APF DSP Durga Datta Bhatta fired 20 tear gas shells and 10 rounds of 7.62mm ball ammunition. An 85-member team under APF DSP Suman Ranabhat fired 17 tear gas shells and 2 rounds of 7.62mm ball ammunition into the air. At the Sudurpashchim Provincial Assembly building, a 35-member team under APF Inspector Narendra Bahadur Bista fired 24 tear gas shells and 6 rounds of 7.62mm ball ammunition into the air. A 35-member team under APF Inspector Kisan Singh Ter fired 4 tear gas shells. At the District Administration Office, a 70-member team under APF SP Surendra Raj Ranjit fired 29 tear gas shells and 7 rounds of 7.62mm ball ammunition into the air. A total of 503 personnel were deployed from APF No. 34 Battalion, No. 7 Baidyanath Brigade, Badimalika Training Center, and No. 43 Reserve Battalion.

Considering the excessive damage to public property, looting, and arson committed by certain groups taking undue advantage of the Gen-Z movement on August 24, the Nepal Army took over security responsibility from 22:00 that night. Joint duties including mobile patrols, foot patrols, and pickets were deployed by all three security agencies at major locations. Although the Gen-Z protesters had damaged provincial ministries, vehicles, and houses of ministers/leaders, and commercial centers like Bhat-Bhateni earlier on the 24th, no further damage to public or private property occurred due to personal grudges from 10 PM on the 24th until the morning of the 25th after army deployment.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies

Anticipating that the impact of the Kathmandu movement could spread nationwide, an emergency meeting of the District Security Committee was held at the District Administration Office under the command of the Chief District Officer to conduct risk assessment and set a security strategy. Per the meeting's decision, Nepal Police, APF, and National Investigation Department were actively deployed according to legal provisions and security procedures. Security personnel were deployed specifically to political party offices, residential areas of former PMs/ministers and local representatives, and municipal offices to maintain surveillance, crowd control, and peace. Upon the CDO's proposal to the committee, prohibitory and curfew orders were also issued. This is based on the incident report submitted by the then Chief District Officer of Kailali, Krishna Prasad Lamsal, found in Annex 6.
Chapter 7: Details Collected During Field Monitoring

Under the leadership of the Chairperson and members of the Commission, field inspections were conducted in districts heavily damaged by the protests of August 23 and 24. Discussions and interactions were held with all stakeholders, including representatives of political parties, government employees, security personnel, and private sector representatives. The views and details provided by individuals from various districts were taken as data for the inquiry and investigation. A summary of these details is presented below.

During the field monitoring by Commission officials, suggestions were collected through inspections of damaged structures and discussions with District Security Committee officials and stakeholders. A synthesis of the collected suggestions is provided at the end of this chapter. The views and suggestions expressed during the district-level discussions are presented district-wise under each province.
7.1. Koshi Province

Field monitoring was conducted in Morang, Jhapa, and Sunsari districts under Koshi Province. The following are the suggestions received during discussions, interactions, and field inspections in those districts.
7.1.1. Jhapa
Opinions received from interactions with stakeholders following the field inspection of damaged structures in Jhapa district on October 27, 2082 (2082/07/11):
Province Assembly Member:

On August 24 at 2:30, a procession came to my house. They threatened to kill me if I didn't resign from the Provincial Assembly. There were 150-200 people. They came again at 10/11 at night and threw stones. Twenty-one people came on 7 motorcycles. They brought fuel (mobile oil) with them; they were boys from outside the village.
Gopal Tamang:

132 individuals involved in criminal activities have been identified. While 17 were named in a police complaint (FIR), they were released on bail. If the country doesn't exist, property has no value. My house was completely burned. They set fire to three rooms within 5 minutes. They broke the remote iron gate in 2 seconds. After I said I must retaliate, I called 100. It was a planned and sponsored incident. Everyone involved in the crime must be brought to justice. Even those who stole gold were released by the police. The location of the stolen iPhones can be seen. Even on-duty police set fire to my house. I have provided CCTV footage and a photo album to the police; let there be action.
Nepalaratna Shivakoti, Son of Former Mayor:

No one has suffered as much loss as I have. Everything is recorded in CCTV footage and photos. About 200 to 300 people arrived at 8:00 AM and burned the vehicles. When I asked to keep them at my neighbors' houses, the neighbors refused. Within 15 minutes, all my money and belongings were looted. My child is still in trauma. That same night, a group returned to set fires again. The next day, they came back to break in and steal. After Bhat-Bhateni was burned, the insurance agents' commission games began.
Bishnu Prasai:

After setting fire in Mechinagar, they asked for my location. About 50 to 60 people arrived with knives and swords while onlookers watched. People from RSP, RPP, and the Maoist party were at the forefront. Despite everyone pleading with joined hands, they refused to listen, used petrol, and set the house on fire along with all the clothing. Some even questioned why we were brought to a place where nothing had happened. We are now helpless. A situation has arisen where we must protect ourselves. The police say they won't register an FIR and will only keep it as a notice.
Prem Giri, Chairperson, CPN-UML:

We question the credibility of this Commission. When we visited the homes of UML and Congress members, we found that the vandalism followed the same model. Skilled destroyers have been used. We have controlled fire; we warn your wild fire to be cautious. We accepted what the security forces said. The police have released those they arrested. We are not just victims but also advocates of democracy. RSP officials led the attacks. We will not remain silent about the damage to our property. All offices of political affiliation have been burned. Damage to private property must be addressed. The state must look into a relief package for this region. Security is the state's responsibility. These criminals are walking around openly. They used druggists and hoodlums to do the work. Let democratic parties not be forced to make undemocratic decisions. YouTubers like Bhagya Neupane claimed 80 kg of gold was seized from the Kankai Mayor's house. Where is that 80 kg of gold? If the government remains restrained, the situation can be handled; otherwise, it will turn into something else. Specific FIRs will be filed; police morale must also be boosted.
Deuman Thebe, Nepali Congress:

I have been experiencing series of protests for 40 to 50 years, but the movement on the 23rd and 24th is unimaginable. I was in Kathmandu. These two days turned the Nepal Police into a pitiful state. There are no uniforms or buildings left; police were attacked with their own weapons. The pattern of vandalism, arson, and looting of both parties is the same here. People are being brought in pickup trucks carrying petrol, Khukuris, spears, and pickaxes after being fed cheap alcohol. After burning a house, they went to another location where two vehicles were kept and burned them too. In Kankai, the houses of my Deputy Mayor, Mayor, and Ward Chairperson were set on fire. First, they break things, then go up to break the water tank and call everyone to joinwhat kind of protest is this? We told the police where the looted goods were, but the police couldn't act. When we searched ourselves, we found the items and brought them back personally. It’s a situation where neither the police nor the administration gets anything done. Will this Commission punish the leadership that instigated looting in the name of Gen-Z? Will the Commission separate truth from lies? People have started socially boycotting those who set the fires, but that is not enough; legal remedy is required. From Kakkadvitta to Damak, the condition of police buildings is dire. Both Nepal Police and APF have been made helpless.
Prasai, Young Entrepreneur:

I am a young entrepreneur. My 80-year-old father and my daughter were taken out before the house was set on fire. It was burned under the label of being a UML 'minion.' If criminals are not punished, we also have resources and youth. There were three groups: one to vandalize with weapons, one to break locks and loot, and one to follow up with fire, carrying petrol in bottles. The state of the police was miserable; it was painful to hear a police officer say, 'I am unsafe.' The police administration has shown restraint. In Birtamod, Congress and UML worked together to build the police office.
Chief, Inland Revenue, Damak:

The office was in a rented house. They came in the afternoon and finished everything; we had gone out at night. First, they took out the files and set them on fire; then another group used petrol brought in bottles to commit arson.
Gopal Budhathoki, Mayor, Mechinagar:

The enraged groups started burning things from 8 AM. They said at the Dhulabari street meeting that they wouldn't vandalize public property, but those who left the Dhulabari intersection kept setting fires in many places. They showed a Khukuri to the municipal firefighters at the BP statue and Bishwa Prakash's house and told them not to extinguish the fire. Professional people are involved. This destruction was carried out with weapons, flammable substances, and tools to break safes and doors. If the state does not control this ruthlessly, no entrepreneur, businessman, or citizen will be safe. Professional drug users and criminals seeking revenge have arrived. There are many motorcycles; the main people used Indian Jeeps. This was a pre-planned act rather than an immediate outburst. People from RSP are directly involved in Mechinagar and are leading the movement. My house is destroyed; I am living in a rented room. My office is also destroyed. We are in a situation where we can't find a place to rent in society; landlords said they wouldn't be safe if we stayed. The police themselves are unsafe; their morale has been weakened. This state of the country does not lead to a good direction.
Pavitra Mahatara, Mayor, Birtamod:

Ward offices No. 7 and 10 in the municipality are destroyed and burned; some documents are completely gone. It's impossible to even work in the municipality. Upon arriving, they first turn off the CCTV cameras, then destroy, break, steal, and loot; knowledgeable people used cylinders to set fires. They came wearing helmets, masks, and glasses. There are 15 municipal guards, but they weren't in a position to defend. On the 23rd, they destroyed Birtamod and Damak municipalities (stone-pelting, vandalism, and looting at 1 PM). At 5 PM on the 23rd, a few guys came to my house and told me to get out immediately; someone came saying they would spray petrol and kill anyone who didn't leave.
Wife of the Mayor, Damak Municipality:

I didn't believe an incident would happen at our house. After the Damak police station was set on fire, they came to our house, vandalized the cooperative on the ground floor, and then committed arson using petrol. No one was there when we needed help. They were known faces. Let the guilty face strict punishment.
Radha Sharma, Deputy Mayor, Bhadrapur:

Nowadays, I feel scared even when someone comes wearing the national flag crosswise. They reached my house around 4:30 PM on the 24th; one known person arrived on a motorcycle. The known person said, 'Hey, lock her up.' They were wearing masks and were drunk. They broke the wall with pickaxes, took out the CCTV cameras, and one person broke the water tank on top. They set fire just as the fire engine was arriving; later they cut the fire engine's pipe, sprayed something, and the fire blazed upit was the height of tyranny. Groups of 20 to 24-year-olds (some up to 28) had been called. My house has become unusable. I haven't insured the house; it is mortgaged to a cooperative.
Khangamaya Parajuli, Deputy Mayor, Arjundhara Municipality:

Out of 11 offices, two are in a condition beyond repair. Arson occurred on the lower floor of the municipality. All vehicles have been destroyed. The loss is estimated to be over 150 million. We have no materials to provide services. We are operating from limited spaces.
Jhapa Rural Municipality:

All ward offices, cars, and motorcycles have been burned. The rural municipality buildings are destroyed; there is no environment to work. Nothing happened on the 23rd. On the 24th, despite being alert after seeing the situation in Kathmandu, they started coming after 10 AM. Protesters broke the municipality gate and then committed arson. When a fire engine was sent, they didn't let it pass. They came wrapped in the national flag, first broke the water tank, looted gold and clothes from cupboards, and then set the fire. They also went and set fire to the Deputy Mayor's place. There was a crowd of 60 to 70 people while others watched. We have videos and police reports. They keep telling us to wait to file an FIR. A looting FIR must be filed within 3 months, but the state hasn't taken action; now we will take action ourselves.
Deputy Mayor, Gauradaha:

We were all watching TV together when the Mayor called around 2:30 PM and told us to go to a safe place immediately. I received a call from a new number telling me to resign and saying protesters were coming to my location. When I asked for an identity, they hung up. First, they broke the water tank and then burned all the rooms. When neighbors tried to extinguish the fire, they were beaten. Everyone is known; 80% are from RSP and the rest are Maoists.
Keshav, Kakkadvitta:

They came to my house and told me to leave and run. I received information after the Kakkadvitta police post was set on fire. Stone pelting occurred while I was still inside. One person on a mic said not to destroy private property. There were 400 people; about half left. They were carrying petrol in their hands. They started stoning around 6 PM; a journalist named Shishir Chaulagain is involved.
Chairperson, Mechinagar Media Foundation:

I have started identifying the people who came to my house; 9 to 10 have been identified. I have filed a specific FIR at the Area Police Office. There are people wearing masks and carrying flags. Why didn't the Home Minister act when private property was being vandalized? Credibility in the Commission is not good. For us who believe in an open economy, this pain and the government's behavior might lead our investment to India or elsewhere. Why are they telling the police to take off their uniforms and run, and to leave their weapons? The state of the police administration is very pathetic. It seems the administration has given a free pass to thieves, looters, thugs, and rascals. People hold deep grudges. Even today, when we are insulted, the state remains silent. Our souls are burning. The police are also victims; the guilty are walking free, which makes the heart burn even more. I have a grievance of feeling like a non-citizen in my own country.
Indu Budhathoki, Regional President, Nepali Congress:

The group that went to set fire in Mechinagar damaged the Nepali Congress regional office and the Girija Foundation. There is more distrust in the individuals than the Commission. There are rumors of an even more powerful protest after Tihar. Who mobilized the people carrying pickaxes and shovels? Social media, and specifically YouTubers, encouraged these acts by spreading them. How can police and citizens be safe if weapons aren't returned? If Singha Durbar wasn't safe, how can there be security in the country? A narrative was built that everyone in political parties is corrupt. There is a state of terror. Political awareness has decreased. They carried the head of Girija Prasad Koirala's statue and shouted slogans against KP Oli. Vandalism occurred at the instigation of Shishir Chaulagain, who worked in the communication sector. A feeling of 'why engage in social service' has increased. Are you considered corrupt just for having a good house or driving a nice car?
President, Nepali Congress, Damak:

People who set out to burn KP Sharma Oli's house on Shanti Marg first attacked the Area Police Office. After the Mayor, they attacked the Deputy Mayor's house. We have the capability to take these criminals out of their homes and take proper revengewe can do itbut we are remaining restrained and will move forward with legal remedies.
Mayor, Bhadrapur:

Nothing happened in Bhadrapur on the 23rd. We never thought arson and looting would happen. After Krishna Sitaula's house was set on fire, they burned mine and the Deputy Mayor's. Then the municipality and later all wards were destroyed. The people are local. It seems some political leaders mobilized them. Leaders from all three parties are involved. People with those tendencies came to cooperate in building the post but weren't included in the reconstruction. The municipality building from 1951 (old) has been destroyed. Even though there is talk of elections now, police morale is very low. Truly, if these criminals are not punished, the country could return to conflict.
Shiva Satakshi:
It is said youth shouldn't go abroad, yet those same youths are given recommendations. All criminals involved in the incident must face legal action.
Rajendra Pokhrel, Kankai Municipality:

The 20 to 25 people who entered the municipality and private homes are mostly of the thieving type. It was difficult to get my 90-year-old sick mother out of my house. They showed very inhumane behavior. There is a large group seeking revenge behind the Gen-Z generation. All 9 ward offices have been damaged. A feeling of 'why should we work' has developed. On the 24th, we thought they wouldn't cause damage anywhere else. Laborers have been incited to come; those who incited them must be punished. 'Routine of Nepal Banda' and Bhagya Neupane incited them to burn my house. Some people planned this the previous day. Police morale is extremely weak. Criminals who used to run from police now see a situation where the police run from criminals. For 4 hours, a man sat on a motorcycle and watched the house burn, leaving only when the job was done. In the evening, when 6 to 7 people came to loot, they couldn't enter because we resisted. We can take revenge but we don't want to break the law; I don't know what will happen if criminals aren't punished. Hope is the greatest thing in life.
Chuddhiman Thapa, Damak:

It’s on CCTV what they did for 1 to 2 hours, saying I voted for KP Oli. They burned two private vehicles. First, they broke the water tank; they were armed. My child was in the house when they set the fire. When I called various agencies, no one came. What country are we living in? My 6-year-old daughter asked, 'Papa, are we going to die?' Neither government nor neighbors; we experienced a time of zero. Why were my house and family sought out and destroyed? I bought those cars with my earnings. I also had hopes from the APF. Everyone says the ones who burned it are Durga Prasai's people. Let this situation never be repeated.
7.1.2. Sunsari
Opinions obtained from the interaction with stakeholders after field inspections of damaged structures in Sunsari district on 2082/08/17:
Chief District Officer, Sunsari:

Currently, we haven't been able to provide security properly, and it’s very hard to ask for damage details; the local people are enraged. Private houses have also been burned. We will take legal action against criminals. A major accident was avoided due to the administration's restraint. We have information that we know everyone who came to burn Itahari Municipality. A minimum consensus must be built within political parties for elections. Parties must also show flexibility and seek consensus within this Constitution. On the 23rd, two people who came to attack the Itahari post died from police bullets. There were 50 students and masked people behind them at the municipality gate. On the 23rd, all the glass was broken. The next day, on the 24th, they burned it.
Jhumka Prison:

Among the 1,796 escaped inmates, more than 1,200 have returned, leaving 600 remaining. Later, there were about 150 people in Itahari. Around 10 AM on the 23rd, there were about 500 to 600 at the intersection, but when going toward and returning from Dharan, about 1,500 to 2,000 gathered; there were others besides Gen-Z.
Kumar Neupane, Armed Police Force (APF) Brigade Commander, Itahari:

Personnel were deployed under the command of an Inspector. In crowd control, there was no command seen from UNITY. Everyone was told to stay safe so that no one suffered loss (Home Ministry and all units). Petrol bombs were also hurled by protesters during the incident. Because we hold public office, our homes and families should not become unsafe. How can we work in a situation where we are accused of corruption or called murderers?
7.1.3. Morang:
Opinions obtained from the interaction with stakeholders after field inspections of damaged structures in Morang district on 2082/08/07:
Bholeshwar Dulal, Senior Vice-President, Chamber of Industries:

There is a deep blow to our self-esteem. The Nepali Rika hotel had been running for 2 years and was damaged. We never thought this would happen in our Nepali society. There are about 300 identified criminals. There were 15 foreign guests in the hotel; we rescued them using a ladder through the window. What must their perception of our country be? We have all the footage. The morale of the police administration has drastically decreased. We are businessmen; who will protect us when all government agencies are in a weak state?
Mayor, Sundar Haraicha:

Private, public, and industrial property has all been destroyed. We need to be convinced whether the Commission's work will ensure justice for victims and punishment for perpetrators. We have heard the Chairperson's statements. Currently, the Prime Minister's statements have also raised many doubts. We are forced to walk in the village in half-pants and shorts. We won't sit idly by. We didn't come to the municipality to loot. There is a state, but we haven't felt it. We have been providing regular service since the 29th while sitting amidst the ashes. The statements of the Home Minister and Prime Minister are objectionable. The government must immediately assist in repairing our offices and purchasing computers. The damage is over 80 million. There is about 2.5 to 3 million in damage to my house alone.
Kari Bahadur Basnet, Ward Chairperson, Ward No. 1:

Even though I applied to the police with evidence that they entered and burned my house at 4:30 PM on the 24th, no action has been taken. We suspect the Commission might just be here to distract us. The guilty are walking openly. People over 60 to 65 years old were signaling while the youth set the fires. Where do we go when the administration doesn't take action? Can you punish those who caused damage and the criminals?
Chairperson, JSP:
Thugs committed all the arson and looting; Bhat-Bhateni was our pride and the criminals burned it all. The individuals responsible must be identified and punished.
Sagar Thapa, Chairperson, CPN-UML:

Damage of over 45 million has occurred at the UML party office. Real Gen-Z saved the Birendra Sabha Gruha. Some are seeking revenge. Those who committed destruction in the name of Gen-Z must be punished. Yesterday, we supported the Gen-Z movement. If the government doesn't show restraint, there will be a counter-reaction and an explosion. Let the Commission work meaningfully.
Bhaktinath Majhi, Acting President, Nepali Congress:

The change in 24 hours was truly unimaginable, even for the protesters. We were active in preventing Morang district from becoming restless. What is the objective of the Commission? How will protesters be punished if the government says not to punish in the name of the Gen-Z movement? The Commission must work impartially. They set fires while asking where the Koirala residence is. If it's just to serve the government's interests, the Commission is useless. We didn't want a confrontation. We facilitated the protest. The Chairperson's statement doesn't show the credibility of this Commission. There is 7 million in damage to the party office and over 20 million to the Koirala residence. Where is the state's responsibility? Resources must be provided immediately to the offices providing services.
Pawan Sarda:

It was calm until the afternoon of the 23rd. After 2 PM, they started burning tires. There were some Gen-Z, but other elements were involved in the looting. We have suffered great physical and mental loss. Business is not running, and we have to pay bank interest. The government must be able to address the voice of the youth. How can we be confident in the future? Let tomorrow not be like this. Those involved in arson and crime must be punished.
Nagesh Koirala, Mayor, Biratnagar:

The Commission's report must clearly explain how 71 people became martyrs in the Gen-Z movement. I have suffered 3.5 to 4 million in personal loss. Outsiders infiltrated in the name of Gen-Z and committed arson and looting. The party office was destroyed; the damage is about 7 million. Even the Metropolitan building is destroyed and there isn't a single resource left. A question mark hangs over the Commission. House belongings were looted and vandalized. If we knew such a situation would arise, we would have organized. If the government doesn't punish the criminals even when I provide names, it will be difficult in the future.
Kishore Pandey, Maoist Center:

The events the Chairperson expressed on social media raised a question mark over the Commission. This situation arose due to the arrogance and orders of parties and employees since 1990. Improvements are needed in the governing structure and all aspects. The movement was mobilized with vested interests. There is no guarantee that trouble won't return tomorrow. Let the social condition be depicted in the report. Planned actions and infiltration must be made clear. The guilty must be brought within the scope of punishment.
Krishna Niraula, RSP:

Who could have predicted that 19 children in school uniforms would die on the 23rd? I have expressed regret regarding the incident. If RSP members or cadres were involved in the crime, they must also be punished. The working style of all parties seems to be the main reason for the current situation. Everyone who vandalized, burned, or looted private homes and property must be brought within the legal scope.
Chandrabir Rai, CPN (Unified Socialist):
Unpleasant and untimely incidents occurred. The issues raised by Gen-Z are valid. Gen-Z must also take responsibility for the following day. Political parties must be extremely sensitive.
Shiv Upreti, Inland Revenue Office:

A group of 200 to 300 people arrived on the 24th. They didn't listen when we said it’s everyone's property and not to vandalize. Three houses were destroyed. Government reports and evaluations are ongoing. Government employees were removed and threatened; employees are discouraged and their morale needs to be boosted.
Chief, District Treasury Control Office (DTCO), Morang:

All our buildings were damaged during the protests on August 23 and 24. The damage assessment committee has prepared a detailed report with a plan, which is currently with the CDO office. Once all reports are in, the Remission Committee will meet. This committee doesn't grant remissions directly but provides recommendations for them.
Chief, Land Revenue Office:

The Land Revenue Office sustained damage. Operations resumed on the 26th. There was significant damage to the records (archive management). All evidence from 1979 to 1983 has been turned to ash. Not a single registration book remains. All documents of the Land Reform Office were also destroyed.

Overall, the peace and security situation in the visited districts was found to be extremely fluid. The morale of security agencies, particularly the police, is very low. Additionally, there is a sentiment that the Commission should serve as a tool to provide a way forward for the country, rather than a weapon for retaliation.
7.2. Madhesh Province
Monitoring was conducted in Bara and Parsa districts under Madhesh Province. The suggestions obtained during discussions, interactions, and field inspections in those districts are as follows.
7.2.1. Parsa
Opinions obtained from the interaction with stakeholders after field inspections of damaged structures in Parsa district on 2082/07/11:
Chief District Officer (CDO) Toya Narayan Subedi:

Permission was initially granted for a normal and peaceful protest for the Gen-Z movement on August 23. Following news of the protests in Kathmandu, the movement escalated here. Damage occurred at the Birgunj Metropolitan City office, ward offices, Inland Revenue Office, Nepal Telecom, High Court, and Transport Management Office; the prison office was also vandalized. Private residences of Pradeep Yadav, Rajkumar Gupta, Ajay Chaurasiya, Lalbabu Raut, and Anil Rungta sustained damage. The events of the 23rd were minor, while the destructive events took place on the 24th.

A curfew was issued on the 24th. We attempted to protect vehicles from all government offices by centralizing them. Some juvenile detainees escaped from the Juvenile Reform Home after breaking the wall. Service delivery resumed on August 29. It was observed that fire engines might be safer if kept within security agency premises. Victims are demanding action against those involved in criminal incidents.
Ram Narayan Kurmi (Vice-President, Nepali Congress Parsa):

Birgunj is the origin of various movements, including the Madhesh movement. In the name of the Gen-Z movement, non-Gen-Z individuals increased criminal activities. We requested security support as police stations were being set on fire, but it wasn't sentperhaps due to low numbersand fire engines didn't arrive on time. In the future, fire engines should be kept with security agencies.
Dr. Bishwamani Sharma (District Chairperson, CPN-UML):

It’s a tragedy when death becomes a recurring guest. These were unexpected events, though some forecasting was possible. Damage in Birgunj only occurred after news of the Kathmandu incidents. There was a scent of personal grudges. Even if UML members were involved, we are ready to face consequences. Action should be taken against anyone according to the law. Why were party offices and leaders' homes targeted? If a government runs based on a show of force, how long can it last? Security personnel reported receiving orders to protect their weapons and flee. If this continues, how can public servants and security personnel serve the country? Therefore, the guilty must be prosecuted according to the law.
Ajay Kumar Chaurasiya (Former Law Minister):

I pay my respects to the deceased and offer condolences to the injured regarding the events following the decision to shut down social media in Nepal. The reprehensible act of setting fire to private and public property will remain on record. This movement attempted to finish off democracy itself; those committing such crimes must be punished by law. I was in Uganda on government business during the incident. Arson and looting were attempted at my home four times. Security forces only managed to extinguish the fire at 3 AM; I thank them for that. This incident was planned, and we will not cover for the guilty. I trust the Commission's report will lead to prosecution in this democratic system.
Rajeshman Singh (Mayor, Birgunj Metropolitan City):

The protest was fueled by grudgesfirst houses were burned, then looted. Is it right to insult our deceased or our mothers on social media in the name of Gen-Z? If the guilty aren't prosecuted, we will be forced to retaliate. The Metropolitan City recovered 21 tractor-loads of looted goods. How much remains unrecovered? The role of journalists was not impartial; they incited the crowd and tried to ruin social harmony. The Metropolitan City is ready to assist the administration in taking necessary steps.
Shashi Kapurnia (JSP Nepal):

On the evening of August 23, political parties themselves protested in the name of Gen-Z. On the 24th, arson and looting occurred in a criminal style. Many commissions have been formed in Nepal, but many reports were never made public. Why are the police refusing to register FIRs? They must register them and proceed with legal action.
Representative, Birgunj Chamber of Commerce and Industry:

The Commission should reach out to industries and businesses. Let the report ensure that businesses can operate properly. The stakeholders in the name of Gen-Z are different; investigate thoroughly and punish the guilty.
Ashok Kumar Gupta (Nepali Congress):

No one has taken responsibility for this movement. Either the state must pay compensation, or the guilty must be identified and made to pay. How active were the security agencies that day? FIRs must be registered and legal proceedings started against those trying to destroy democracy.
Chief Tax Officer (Inland Revenue Office):

Damage details have been submitted to the CDO and higher authorities. There is a need to boost the morale of Nepal Police and security agencies to bring them back to work. Coordination is necessary on this matter.
Chief Administrative Officer (Parsagadhi Municipality):

Video footage of the incidents shows Indian and Bangladeshi nationals working in garages, as well as scrap workers. Let there be an impartial investigation. Local government employees are major targets; please provide necessary security.
Kashiram Wali (Nepal Oil Corporation):
Some people of Indian origin burned tires and protested in front of the Oil Corporation. There seems to be an invisible power behind the name of Gen-Z. Let there be an impartial investigation.
Chief Administrative Officer (Birgunj Metropolitan City):

Damage details have been submitted to all relevant bodies. Many looted items were recovered from scrap dealers. Over 23 tractor-loads of items were recovered from individuals' homes. The guilty are walking free. Police are refusing to register FIRs. Please take note. There are 40 branches in Birgunj Metropolis. We cannot run an office by begging for materials. Permission for new procurement is needed, and work should be done to boost employee morale.
Anish Kumar Gupta (Nepal Telecom):

Six vehicles were burned inside the office. Nepal Telecom, an entity that flows information nationwide, was attacked. Employees' belongings were looted. When will they be returned? Please work to ensure such incidents don't happen again.
7.2.2. Bara
Opinions obtained from the interaction with stakeholders after field inspections of damaged structures in Bara district on 2082/07/11:
Krishnakumar Shrestha (Farmer) (Former Member of the House of Representatives):

Deepest condolences to those martyred on August 23, and let the guilty of the August 24 incidents be punished. I went to Kashi on the 22nd. I requested security agencies not to use force on the 24th. Initially, the protest on the 24th was natural, but later there was infiltration and the Jitpur Municipality building was set on fire, involving even municipality employees.

After Gen-Z postponed the protest, some brokers incited a crowd around 5 PM to burn my house and vehicle. The intention was to close down the industries located there. Police are refusing to register FIRs. A journalist even provided a lighter to burn the housethis doesn't apply to all journalists. Let our cases be registered quickly and the prosecution process begin.
Achyut Mainali (Former Member of the House of Representatives):

I have been active in social service for 14 years. My house was burned using purchased petrol. The chairperson of this Commission should have been a Supreme Court judge. Let the role of the Commission be impartial. Burning 11 out of 13 wards in Nijgadh is politically motivated. When our work became too process-oriented, a protest for good governance arose under the Gen-Z name, but what governance did it achieve?
Jwala Sah (Former Member of the House of Representatives):

Who shot children in the head on August 23? Why hasn't there been action? Why is there a delay in punishing criminals who burned public and private property? Why was K.P. Sharma Oli's passport blocked and his travel outside the valley restricted? Other factions might have caused this when the Congress-UML government was formed. I asked security agencies to send forces when they tried to burn my house, but they didn't listen. The APF didn't help; the APF should be merged into the Civil Police. If the Commission doesn't punish the guilty, we will take action ourselves. Children were killed in the name of Gen-Z; the country is not on the right track. Can you withstand the pressure, Mr. Chairperson? Register cases and punish the guilty immediately.
Amrit Sharma Bajgai:

Others created terror in the name of Gen-Z. A situation has arisen where those who insult others most on social media get highlighted. How will the guilty be punished? Let the Commission act quickly. Police morale has dropped. The August 23 incident is suspiciousdid the police aim for a child's head? Is there a contradiction between the Commission's words and the Home Minister's orders? What is Sudan Gurung's role? Let there be an impartial investigation.
Binod Prasad Sah (Mayor, Kalaiya Sub-Metropolitan City):
The Commission Chairperson must be impartial and not yield to pressure. Order the prosecution of those who looted in a dacoit style on August 24. Let the guilty be punished fairly.
Narendra Sah (Chairperson, District Coordination Committee):

Protesters caused about 3 million in damage while calling me a 70 million corruption suspect; punish the guilty. **Ashok Kumar Jaiswal (Chairperson, Baragadhi Rural Municipality):** On August 25, my house was completely destroyed, leaving not even a light switch. Police refuse to register FIRs; let them be registered and action taken immediately.
Zulfikar Ali (Chairperson, Bishramgadhi Rural Municipality):
They vandalized and burned the officethis was done by party people, not Gen-Z. Punish the guilty immediately.
Rajan Paudel (Mayor, Jitpur Simara Sub-Metropolitan City):

Arson might have been committed with the intent of tax evasion; investigate from that angle too. Tell the PM and Home Minister to create an environment where Nepal Police can work. What was the role of the Home Secretary and Chief Secretary during the incident? They should be investigated too.
Surath Puri (Mayor, Nijgadh Municipality):

Who were the elements that incited Gen-Z into the protest? They must be brought under investigation. Sovereignty was attacked in a democratic system. Why was a wrong narrative built against politicians? Criminalization happened through social media abuse. The current government's role seems suspicious. Security agencies didn't play an effective role. Punish the guilty.
Summary of the field inspection and interaction at Bhimphedi Prison on 2083/07/13:
Subas Paudel (Prison Chief):

There were 1,066 inmates until 2082/05/24. Among them, at 4 AM on the 25th, 440 inmates escaped over the south wall using quilts and mattresses on the barbed wire. To date, 286 have returned, and 154 remain missing. Inmates watched TV and saw that prisoners elsewhere were being allowed to flee. They started protesting and vandalizing, asking why they were being kept locked up. We told them if release was allowed everywhere, we would allow it too, otherwise, escape would lead to further punishment. Additional security was requested at 8 PM and arrived at 9 PM. A double clash ensued from midnight to 4 AM. A prison vehicle was hot-wired and driven at security personnel. During the clash, 2 people were shot below the knee and injured. Escaped inmates aren't even in contact with their families. 27 of the escaped are Indians, and long-term convicts haven't returned. The sentence-serving facilities provided by the Nawalpur court have added more challenges. We will provide all documents to the Chairperson. Let the Commission coordinate further.
Dinesh Sharma (DSP, APF):

The inmates escaped due to an internal protest and vandalism rather than external force. Damage was relatively low because extra security arrived on time. The disaster management team prevented further damage using fire-extinguishing equipment.
Summary of Local Residents' Opinions:

After news of arson and vandalism at Singha Durbar, Parliament, and private homes in Kathmandu spread through TikTok, Facebook, and YouTube, people started burning government offices and leaders' homes in the districts. Local opinion suggests that because social media was kept open on the night of the 23rd, arson and vandalism occurred nationwide outside the capital.
Positive Aspects Observed During Inspection in Parsa District:
Due to effective security coordination and management by the CDO and security agencies, damage was avoided in the following areas:

content.content: No prisoners were allowed to escape.
The Customs Office was saved.
Courts were saved.
No human casualties occurred.

content: At Bhimphedi Prison Office:

Under the initiative of Jailer Subas Paudel, the CDO coordinated the mobilization of Nepal Police, APF, and the Nepal Army from the night of August 24 to the morning of the 25th, preventing major vandalism, arson, and mass escapes. Damage was relatively low due to effective security management.
Suggestions:

content.content: Security forces prevented vandalism, arson, and inmate escapes at Birgunj Prison, one of the largest in the country. The then CDO Toya Narayan Subedi should be rewarded by the government for this.

At Bhimphedi Prison, Jailer Subas Paudel's initiative and the coordination of security forces prevented greater destruction and escapes. Jailer Subas Paudel should be rewarded by the government for his fearless performance.

content: 7.3. Bagmati Province

Monitoring was conducted in Chitwan, Makwanpur, and the three districts of the Kathmandu Valley. Suggestions from those interactions are as follows. A detailed study and analysis of damaged structures in the Valley was conducted, including testimonies from security chiefs and incident reports from staff. Suggestions from Makwanpur and Chitwan are included in this section.
7.3.1. Chitwan
Opinions obtained from the interaction with stakeholders after field inspections of damaged structures in Chitwan district on 2082/07/11:
Chief District Officer (CDO):

I reported for duty in Chitwan on the evening of the 21st. A memorandum was received from Gen-Z youth at 11 AM on the 23rd. We thanked them and asked them to conduct a peaceful sit-in and program. While instructions were given to the police to remain as restrained as possible, force was eventually used. Gas shells, warning shots, and rubber bullets were used in sequence. The use of force did not exceed established principles. Decisions made by the District Security Committee have been sent to the Commission. In events of the same nature, one cannot be declared a criminal while another is declared a martyr. To resume services, we have interacted with stakeholders and the business sector to distribute services through a single-door system. We have information, facts, and evidence of both vandalism and arson, identifying both protesters and those creating terror. Everyone's morale and psychology have dropped, but we have not lost hope. May the Commission and the Government work to restore that hope.
Krishna Bhusal, Chairperson, RSP:

The evidence is scattered and clear. Shots were fired aimed at chests. What action is being taken against those who fired the shots? It is necessary to collect suggestions from our Gen-Z brothers. Why were our government and private offices not dedicated to providing easy facilities and serving citizens? We are all guilty; we should all be handcuffed. We are in a state where one needs a judge from their own party to get justice in court, and the police must belong to the ruling power.
Prakash Pandey, President, Hotel Association:

At this time, we haven't even been able to pay interest or principal. Foreign tourists have canceled their trips. There are no hotel booking entries. The tourism industry is suffering a major blow; we are not in a position to support our staff. Employee morale has decreased, and investment has completely stopped.
Keshav Pokhrel, Samajbadi Party:

Because the reports of 1990 and 2006/07 were not made public, the culprits were not punished. Impunity increased. The most terrifying physical destruction in history has occurred. The Commission must take action against identified suspects. The political parties that led us to this state of human and physical loss are also criminals. Corruption has spread everywhere. No action was taken regarding the corruption in Pokhara Airport.
Hari Prasad Paudel, President, Red Cross:

On the 23rd, the Red Cross did not have ambulances and volunteers on standby. However, on that same day, we deployed them to serve both protesters and security personnel. After 2 or 3 people vandalized an ambulance in front of the District Administration, we stopped the service. Some ambulances and relief materials were also burned.
Suman Shrestha, President, Chitwan Chamber of Commerce and Industry:
We felt we must become alert after the Gen-Z incident. The private sector has suffered huge losses. If the Commission does not take action against the guilty, it will lead to disastrous consequences.
Basanta Parajuli, Federation of Nepali Journalists, Chitwan:

Media workers were threatened, and there was mental stress. Arrests and threatening behavior came from both sides. When journalists cannot write news freely due to self-censorship, the situation can become terrifying.
Shyamlal B.K., Victim:

I am affiliated with the CPN-UML. In Rapti Municipality, the houses of 19 individuals were specifically targeted and vandalized because they belonged to UML members. During the Gen-Z movement, houses were set on fire and looted. The movement on the 23rd was right, but there was infiltration on the 24th. Today, the country has been pushed back 50 years. The country is outside the legal framework. How can a country be built if parties hold grudges and vandalize each other's property?
Sujan Adhikari, Vision Dairy:
I have given the evidence/details of the incidents that happened to me to the police. Let there be action, and may I also receive compensation.
President, Bar Association Chitwan:
Our building was also burned (approx. 3.7 million in damages). Those who have lost their humanity should not be spared. We have audio-visual evidence.
Shiv Sharma Paudel, Land Revenue Officer:
Five vehicles were turned to ash at the Land Revenue office. The state must also provide relief for our personal losses.
Kul Prasad Bhusal, Ward Chairperson:

Three offices have been destroyed. The Chairperson of the Commission expressing his personal views has raised a question mark. Now, a sense of 'I am impartial' must be demonstrated. Evidence of the incidents is public. Let the administration take action against those who went to loot the houses of the Khaireni Mayor and Deputy Mayor. The Commission must gain our trust through its work. Action must be taken against those working to release 17,000 prisoners.
Ramesh Gautam:
Let the Commission also recommend the status and reconstruction work for the destroyed offices.
Chief, Inland Revenue Office:
The 'wind' of destruction blew in from Khaireni. Let the Commission recommend tax exemptions. The private sector must submit applications to us.
Purna Prasad Acharya, Ward Chairperson:

Nepal must be saved; the Constitution and national heritage must be saved. When security agencies were not fully active, representatives, police, and administration had to flee. Cadres of all parties are active in the vandalism. The parties must bring them within the scope of action. Corruption investigation should start from the Ward Chairperson level. A commitment is needed from all security heads to prosecute the criminals.
Laxman Paudel, Ward Chairperson, Kalika Municipality:

We have given footage of those involved in vandalism and arson to the police. The Commission should not hesitate to take action. Anarchic elements have disturbed society; attention must be paid to this as well.
Prabha Baral, Former Mayor, Rapti Municipality:

Various facts have shown that the movement started by our younger generation to run the country according to the law and technology was infiltrated. Let the Commission conduct an investigation that history will respect. The internal planners must also be identified by the Commission.
Narsingh Bhandari, Red Cross:

The Red Cross has less damage compared to others, but one of our ambulances was burned. The office was also burned. Compensation must be provided quickly to public service providers like the Red Cross.
Arjun Sapkota, Chitwan Industry Association:

After the security in Kathmandu failed, we could do nothing when 100 criminal individuals caused destruction in the name of Gen-Z. All security agencies, including the CDO, need to perform self-criticism. We industrialists and businessmen must also learn a lessonwe now need to focus on whether to choose business or politics. I am confident that if all stakeholders improve themselves, an environment for working in the country will be created.
Badri Timilsina, CPN-Maoist Center, Chitwan:

We have seen many commissions; they come to listen, but their findings never come out. I have a slight hope for this Commission. Those guilty in the events of the 23rd must be punished. Regarding the 24th, did the government conduct a prior assessment or not? When the heart of NepalParliament, Singha Durbar, Supreme Courtburned, I have no complaints against the administration in Chitwan. Gen-Z does not commit such crimes.
Ramchandra Adhikari, Maoist District Coordinator:

Investigate the events of the 23rd and 24th separately. The government failed to be a guardian to the people. The Commission must not waver. It should not just fulfill the formality of submitting a report.
Rudra Prasad Dhakal, RPP:

Reports of past commissions never came out. The events of the 24th were a result of the events of the 23rd. The 24th was a volcanic explosion against corruption and for good governance. Protest and crime must be distinguished.
Kamalraj Pathak, UML Chairperson, Chitwan:

The events of the 23rd and 24th have taught many lessons. Citizens, political parties, and all state bodies must be alert. Let there be a proper investigation of the 23rd. The work on the 24th was planned. The planned incidents must also be investigated. This was an attack on state sovereignty. The country must survive first. We must fear that the 'time of death' has arrived. Brains are being used for small, destructive things. Questions have been raised about the Chairperson of the Commissionwill the investigation be neutral? If another movement starts for such reasons, who will be responsible?
7.3.2. Makwanpur
Opinions obtained from interactions with stakeholders after field inspections of damaged structures in Makwanpur district on 2082/07/11:
Secretary, Ministry of Physical Infrastructure, Bagmati Province:

In Bagmati, 5 districts suffered damage while 8 did not. 36 offices were damaged. The Bagmati provincial government estimates damage worth 1.04 billion. Reconstruction is estimated to cost 1.30 billion.
Hidam Lama (Chairperson, Bhimphedi Rural Municipality):

We have submitted the details of the damage to the District Administration Office. The District Police Office has been hesitant to register FIRs against the guilty. Criminals must face legal action as soon as possible. FIRs were not registered according to the Home Minister's instructions. Let Sudan Gurung's role be clarified. The people have high expectations of the Honorable Chairperson; please work accordingly.
Angat Prasad Paudel (Former Honorable Member):

My house has been burned. Why won't the police register the case or FIR? According to the Constitution, the case must be registered as a criminal offense. Criminal cases and FIRs must be registered in the coming days.
Arun Sumargi:

The culprits have started walking around openly. Even when we tried to file a complaint against them, it wasn't registered and no action was taken. The house in my mother's name was burned. Let the process move forward according to the law.
Chir Bahadur Ghalan 'Jwala' (District Secretary, CPN-Maoist):

If the kind of terror that occurred in the name of Gen-Z continues, the country will head toward the state of Uganda. An environment for elections will only be created if a fair investigation is conducted.
Badri Bahadur Paneru (National Examination Board):
A massive movement took place in the name of Gen-Z. Elections cannot be held in an insecure environment. There is currently no condition to conduct elections with high morale.
Hon. Eklal Shrestha (Provincial Member):

The destruction in the name of Gen-Z was different from anything imagined. Efforts were made to save the house, but after three separate attempts to set it on fire, it was eventually burned. How will this loss be compensated? Those named in FIRs must be arrested and prosecuted immediately. This matter must be taken seriously. The state must guarantee the security of every individual. Why was our property not protected?
Meena Kumari Lama (Mayor, Hetauda Sub-Metropolitan City):

Private homes and government offices were burned by people we recognize and see regularly. If this terror is not taken seriously, what will the future hold? A situation like Congo or Rwanda could arise. When sitting with Gen-Z the previous day (August 23), the situation seemed peaceful, and a major movement wasn't expected. However, the next day, August 24, destruction was unleashed in the name of Gen-Z.

Who will run for election in the coming days? There is a fear that if one stands, their house will be burned. What will happen if everyone doesn't feel secure? Criminals are walking freely. We are in a state of fear. Actions must be taken to boost the morale of security agencies, politicians, and social workers.
Dor Mani Paudel (Former Chief Minister, Bagmati Province):

What is the criteria for partial vs. total damage? How do we distinguish them? Everyone's hope and trust lie with the Commission Chairperson. Security may be guaranteed based on the role he plays. In the name of protesters, they looted and carried away whatever they could. Whatever they couldn't carry, they set on fire. The movement on the 23rd was peaceful. On the 24th, destruction was caused in the name of Gen-Z. This was a movement of infiltration and looting. The guilty are walking openly before us, but action has not yet begun. Holding elections is a challenge. The local administration is unable to work with high morale. We are people who believe in democracy. Therefore, we are waiting for things to happen according to the law in a state governed by law. Otherwise, we also had the capacity for retaliation. Why did a situation arise where Sudan Gurung dominates while others become helpless?
Journalist Hari Humagain:
What will you do with the collected and monitored details? What is the basis for reassuring the victims? They must be reassured.
Dhruba Adhikari, Federation of Nepali Journalists, Makwanpur:
Is there confusion between the Commission and the Government? This needs to be cleared. Is the state only focused on the events of the 23rd? What is the Commission's response to these issues?
Chief District Officer (CDO) Skim Shrestha:

The issues raised here have reached the Chairperson. May the facts and impartiality reach the relevant bodies. It appears that the group causing damage in the name of the movement was not Gen-Z. Since the government and the Commission were being formed, there might have been a delay in registering complaints while waiting for orders from above. Instructions have since been received to proceed according to the law regarding criminal charges. Although morale is slightly low, we are firm in our responsibility to provide security. I thank everyone for their role in maintaining peace and order.
7.4. Gandaki Province
Monitoring was conducted in Kaski district under Gandaki Province. The suggestions obtained during the field inspections and interactions are as follows:
7.4.1. Kaski
Opinions from the interaction held with stakeholders after the field inspection of damaged structures in Kaski district on 2082/07/11:
Mayor, Pokhara Metropolitan City:

Hydrogen gas and petrol bombs were used to burn structures. Apil Tripathi of 'Gandaki Chhadke' also incited the group. They set fire to the case branch of District Police Office Kaski; they specifically asked for various sections and burned the one where Rabi Lamichhane's file was kept. Suraj Khadka should be questioned about this; he is a known person from RSP. Those protesting included Maoists, activists, and onlookers. Bhagya Neupane caused provocation. The truths of the events on the 23rd and 24th must be investigated.
Superintendent of Police (SP) Shyambabu Oliya, District Police Kaski:

All of my daughter's jewelry and clothes were destroyed. The internal damage is immeasurable. A taxi driver, who is an ex-convict, was involved in the arson. They brought a vehicle and looted everythingnot even a spoon was left. The District Police Office was abandoned at 6 PM. A girl was later seen wearing the SP's uniform. First, someone comes on a motorcycle to identify the target, then people arrive and throw stones, followed by another group that incites the crowd. Finally, designated people arrive on motorcycles to set fire to the Government Attorney's Office, the Court, and Cooperatives. They are all well-trained.
Former Chief Minister, Gandaki Province:

The Ministry of Home Affairs' statement created confusion and boosted the morale of criminals. People felt they could do anything in the name of protest, leading to looting. Political and violent aspects are different. If the violent elements are not punished today, they will be needed tomorrow. A country cannot be built by chaos. Ethics and social values are high in those over 40 years old but low in the younger generation. Gen-Z doesn't understand ground realities. The current model isn't working. The enemies are within the system. There was more hype than reality; the rulers' language wasn't right. Everyone unhappy with the status quo joined on the 24th. RSP, Maoist, and RPP were involved in the Pokhara protests. Damage under the provincial government is about 2 billion. After Kaski, Tanahun suffered damage to the Government Attorney's and Land Revenue offices. In Tanahun, 44 four-wheelers were burned but can be maintained for use. Protesters would claim to gather in one spot but would meet elsewhere to vandalize. Social media must be regulated. The coalition government has sent a bad message. Radical changes are needed in education. Permanent employees don't work; the system for moving from Section Officer to Under Secretary and Joint Secretary is broken.
Chief District Officer (CDO), Kaski:

Damage occurred across federal, provincial, and local levels. 116 structures (including residences) in the government mechanism were damaged. In the private sector, 27 buildings including houses and hotels were affected. Preliminary estimates show damage of about 2.18 billion to government offices. Some surveys are still pending. Adequate resources haven't arrived from the center. 27 locations, including the District Police Office, were damaged. 773 prisoners/detainees escaped from the prison, of which 516 have returned. 221 remain at large, including foreigners.
Ram Bahadur Paudel, Civil Society Kaski:

We trust that the investigation through the Commission will be documented. Our organization loves the country. We request strict action against those who committed the massacre on the 23rd and the arson, vandalism, and looting on the 24th. We are ready to provide any kind of cooperation.
Gokarna Karki, President, Pokhara Chamber of Commerce and Industry:

The private sector is smiling through the pain. Private sector damage totals 3.52 billion. Without peace and security, the private sector cannot function. Economic flight has already begun. Livelihood and good governance must be maintained. The central government must take the lead in figuring out how to operate government offices. We are encouraging entrepreneurs. We couldn't explain to everyone about paying interest to foreign institutions. The private sector has suffered the most. Arsonists and vandals must be arrested and made to pay compensation. Properties were also burned due to personal grudges.
President, Chamber of Commerce Kaski:
The private sector is the foundation for lenders. Because of this, the private sector may not dare to invest. Strict action should be taken based on evidence and surveillance.
Chief, Insurance Authority:
Regarding compensation, the insurance authority will work on it and provide information.
Salikram Sapkota, Maoist Center:

Our party has suffered losses. Problems are increasing due to impunity in the state. Those taking the law into their own hands must be punished immediately, otherwise, a terrifying situation could arise. Political parties will run this country in the future as well. All sides must work to improve them. Let the investigation be done correctly.
Bimal Adhikari, National Athlete:

We have suffered more psychological damage. The security agencies don't seem to be working actively. No one dares to close taverns after 9 PM. We need to create a feeling of security for our children.
Ramesh Paudel, President, Federation of Nepali Journalists:

The report should come out with facts. The morale of the police administration must be raised. Media and social media are dominating the narrative. News says YouTubers destroyed things in India. Elections aren't possible by boycotting political parties; the Commission must also take responsibility. What happens if there's no election? We must focus on moral education rather than education that prioritizes destruction.
Gauri Bhattarai, Tourism Entrepreneur, Kaski:

The 23rd was unimaginable, and the 24th was terrifying. Tourism is a sector that impacts many areas. Preliminary estimates show losses of 2.5 billion across Bagaincha Resort, Amshuverma, and the house of Rajeev Pahari. There was personal revenge and infiltration. Negative expressions are still coming out. The government is the guardian; special attention must be given to relief packages for highly affected areas.
