There is confusion in businesses; Transport and Land Revenue offices aren't delivering well yet. Why is the license delayed? The government should prioritize pushing back loan installments. Providing 1.5–2 million in credit to small businesses would keep them operational.
Staff, Office of the Chief Minister:
All nine ministries sent damage details to the Commission on 2082/7/2. Work on driving licenses hasn't started yet. Federal laws may hinder the province's work; the center must facilitate.
Taranath Pahari, President, Pokhara Tourism:
Tourism revival work should be done quickly. The federal government should ask ambassadors worldwide to invite people to Nepal. The center must take initiative for a special action plan.
Jitu Gurung, Barpipal Resort:

How is the government looking at the victims when criminals are walking around openly? How can businesses be protected? The strictest possible action must be taken against the criminals. FIRs (First Information Reports) with specific names have been filed, so why are criminals being released after being caught?
Khimlal Shivakoti, National Human Rights Commission:

Multiple human rights violations have occurred. Security has been compromised due to the escape of inmates from prisons. The morale of civil servants has plummeted. The Commission is not currently holding discussions with them. We have sent a fact analysis of the district to the center. Victims must receive justice, and perpetrators must be punished according to the law.
Raman Jung Rana:
The police refuse to accept applications, citing a lack of orders from above. Action must be taken against the criminals.
Rameshwar Aryal, Under Secretary, Office of the Province Head:
The residence is fully damaged; except for a few items, everything is destroyed. The preliminary estimate of the damage is 43.2 million.
Loknath Aryal, Education Office:

Extensive arson occurred. About 52 answer sheets were in the process of being checked. When protesters were told that burning the answer sheets of their own brothers and sisters would cause them trouble, they left. However, records of more than 600 schools and over 3,000 government teachers were destroyed.
Narayan Prasad Acharya, Postal Office:

Post boxes for the general public have been damaged. Initially, regular mail transport between Muglin and Pokhara was difficult. On the night of the 24th, computers and documents were stolen. The rough estimate of damage is 86.5 million. I have filed a police report along with available evidence.
Amrit Subedi, Gali-Gali Khabar:

Taking advantage of the anger on August 23, the arson, looting, and vandalism on the 24th are making society even more terrifying. Everyone involved in burning and breaking property must be identified and punished. Until there is a certainty that criminals will face consequences, impunity will prevail. The destruction on the 24th was a result of extreme despair over widespread corruption and anger at being ruled by the same old people. The attacks were planned, targeting specific houses and properties. Even when naming suspects in complaints, the police are not accepting them.
Chief Administrative Officer, Pokhara Metropolitan City:

Twenty-five ward offices have been completely damaged. The municipal building is fully destroyed. Services are currently being provided from the City Hall (Sabha Griha). Efforts are being made to ease service delivery.
Surya Prasad Bhandari, Entrepreneur, Mini Srijana Market:

If the Truth and Reconciliation Commission had worked effectively, this situation might not have arisen. Criminal acts and political activities must be separated. 33 businesses were operating at 80% capacity. In the evening, petrol and diesel were poured from all sides to start fires. There was a minimum loss of 50 million. News reports suggesting that escaped prisoners were committing crimes (80% of the reports) further incited the crowd. Why are private entrepreneurs always affected in every protest? If the police take action based on evidence found on social media, we will provide full cooperation.
Basu Tripathi, Private Sector Victim:

It appears the events of the 24th were orchestrated to achieve selfish goals by using the 23rd as a pretext. People from three political parties were seen leading the destruction. How will a government that cannot even collect more than 1.1 trillion in revenue manage to operate now? We need to understand not just who came, but who sat together to create the target lists. Since this is a widespread problem and videos/photos have been provided, entrepreneurs fear that filing individual complaints might lead to further misfortune tomorrow. Insurance companies should facilitate the process, and the morale of affected businesses must be raised. May this Commission win the hearts of Nepalis and help purify the economic state.
Representative of Government Employees:

Security agencies must work with adequate resources and high morale. Due to strict rules and laws, government employees are unable to work smoothly. Employees do not feel secure sitting in their chairs to work. For the next two years, values-based training and education should be provided. The government needs to be firm against those spreading rumors via fake news.
Narayan Gurung, RPP:

This situation has arisen because of political parties, corrupt leaders, and cadres. The public is very kind; they are raising money to rebuild police stations and ward offices. The demands of the victims must be heard, and recommendations for action must be made. We all must work to raise the morale of the security forces (Police).
Nita Gurung, Hotel Goodwill Pokhara:
They brought trucks in the evening and stole goods. The loss is about 140 million. We have reported the damage to the ward office.
Subhash Pokharel, Land Revenue Office:

Estimated damage to the administration building is 200 million (100 million for the building and 100 million for computers and equipment). We have started essential services like registration and releasing property blocks. Files dating back to before 1980 have all been destroyed.
Former Minister Yagya Bahadur Thapa:

The Gen-Z movement was innocent and pure, but it was hijacked and became violent through infiltration. The Commission should work without bias or prejudice. Events that never happened in history have occurred in Pokhara. Despite my house being next to a petrol pump and an army barrack, it suffered only minor damage. However, psychological and physical damage is widespread. The Commission has only seen the pictures; please do not make recommendations based solely on the surface. Perform a deep 'surgery' of the situation. Weaknesses have also occurred on the part of political parties, which must be corrected.
DSP Hari Basnet:

When asked to protect the weapons, the Army did not come. Consequently, weapons and files for ongoing cases were loaded into vehicles. Half the weapons were kept at the Armed Police Force's Kalika Battalion and the rest at the Nepal Police Battalion. We had to leave 81 detainees behind as we moved. Seven pistols and eight rounds of ammunition were recovered from two escapees from the Juvenile Correction Home (on the night of the 23rd). Vandalism and arson happened simultaneously in many places. Those involved were mostly intoxicated. This taught us a lesson that small forces cannot contain such chaos.
7.5. Lumbini Province
On-site monitoring was conducted in Banke and Rupandehi districts under Lumbini Province. Suggestions obtained during discussions and site inspections are as follows:
7.5.1. Rupandehi
Narayan Mahat, Maoist, Sainamaina:

My house was also damaged. The protest needs a thorough review. How did the breaking of prohibited zones and the entry into Parliament happen on the same day? Just mentioning elections is not enough. Criminals must be punished. If the police administration cannot protect itself, how can it punish criminals? They should be ostracized from society. I suffered losses worth 8 million. Criminals are known, but why is no action taken? Why were we physically attacked? Our patience is wearing thin; we will retaliate. We have been quiet thinking the administration would act, otherwise, we will rise up ourselves. Let it not reach a point where people have to protect their own lives. Addicts and worthless elements of society, acting on bias, burned the houses of Mayors, Deputy Mayors, and Honorable members. The Martyr Memorial Foundation was destroyed. The government must stand for social justice. What kind of security plan was made that allowed entry into the Parliament building on the first day? There are claims that people were shot in the head from the outside. What exactly happened? If people are still not safe, how will elections take place? Those settling personal scores in the name of Gen-Z must be brought to justice, otherwise, the tendency to commit wrongs will grow. The narrative that anything is permissible in the name of a protest should not be established. Those who caused damage have been identified, but the police ask for specific names. Why is it difficult for the police to work? Political instability is increasing, and young people are becoming more frustrated as the investment climate deteriorates.
Jivraj Ashrit Foundation:

The credibility of the Commission is in question; it is not certain it can maintain neutrality. All events from 5 PM on August 23 through the 24th are criminal acts. Revenge is apparent; there is involvement of those previously linked to crimes and those holding grudges. It must be ensured whether the Home Minister will take action after the report. It seems impunity has been encouraged. Our foundation suffered a loss of about 3.2 million. Most did not have insurance. The police say 'stay safe' and take applications informally, claiming orders from above are not clear.
Basudev Ghimire, Former MP, UML:

Every room in my house has been turned to ash. Anarchic elements, youth who don't listen to their parents, and drug users are the ones who set the fires. we have reached a state of statelessness. Even when providing videos along with specific complaints, the police administration has not acted. The helplessness of the security mechanism is evident. The fire brigade was also stopped; the police stopped them saying the fire truck would be burnt. If this is the state of elected representatives, what must be the plight of the common people? The morale of the Nepal Police has never been this weak. If legal action is not taken in time, impunity will persist for a long time. I have suffered a loss of 32 million.
Parshuram Giri, Ward Chairperson:

Common people have suffered massive physical and mental damage. How do we live now? Where do we go? The Commission should identify and punish the guilty. What can we expect from security agencies that tell us to stay safe on our own? When will a safe environment be created? The Commission must punish those who did wrong. The government must be people-oriented, otherwise, it increases instability and anarchy. Morale-boosting work for security agencies is needed. At 7 PM on the 24th, anarchists burned Lila Giri's house and then looted and burned mine. They took 32 tolas of gold and cash. Thank you to the Commission for creating an environment to share our grief. It seems impossible to survive without providing one's own security. Trust in the security mechanism has declined.
Bishnu Prasad Bashyal, Chairperson, Shuddhodhan Rural Municipality:
I saved my life by running and hiding. Petrol bombs were thrown at my house. I am suffering from extreme mental stress.
Dhruba Kharel, Mayor, Devdaha Municipality:

I am a direct victim. Nothing happened in Devdaha on the 23rd, but on the 24th, a chaotic mob went to the municipality at 2 PM and provoked employees and threw stones. When the mob came to my house, the Nepal Police and Armed Police were nearby but did not come to save it. 20–25 protesters entered the compound and destroyed everything. 5–6 tolas of gold were recovered, but 16 tolas are still missing. They even took earthworm fertilizer, utensils, and rice. They set fire to everything they couldn't take. Property worth 7.5–8 million was destroyed. We know every single person involved. If we hadn't hidden inside, they would have killed us. The police prepared the scene report and took the application but did not register it; criminals are walking openly. Those arrested are being released citing 'orders from above.' We are victims of injustice. All videos are available. Why was such pain given to us for being representatives? If we have committed corruption, let there be an investigation, but currently, it seems the government is protecting criminals rather than the security of its citizens. We have become homeless. We seek justice through the Commission.
Hitraj Panthi, CPN (UML):

The credibility of the Commission is under question. The time has come to save the pride of the Nepali nation. The Gen-Z movement came with pure intentions, but let it not be transformed into destruction; whoever committed a mistake must be punished. CPN (UML) does not protect the guilty. This state of statelessness could invite further destruction. We must review how much our weaknesses affected citizens and service seekers. All parties agree we are in a state of statelessness. Why did the organs of the government appear inactive in such a matter? Party offices at all levels have been vandalized. How did protesters use petrol bombs and chemicals? Personal houses were burned in a planned manner; let there be a proper investigation. Parties have tried to play a responsible role against groups inciting people for their own gain. We will provide all audio and video; let the guilty be punished. The role of the state's security agencies is doubtful. Not all parties shy away from elections, but there is no point in going to elections without a proper environment, including the recovery of looted weapons and ammunition. We should not blame each other but work on nation-building with a spirit of unity and tolerance.
Lila Giri:

We trust you. We believe you won't support wrong things. In the course of work, the desired results may not have been achieved, but changing the country's power in a 'film style' can be mysterious. The state must investigate impartially. Questions have been raised about the government, security agencies, and leaders. All responsible parties should receive appropriate punishment. Party offices and homes have been burned. Arson occurred at business establishments. There has been an effort to discourage and dominate the government machinery. Many government offices have been burned. Action must be taken against the guilty. We will bring the complaints with evidence. The current government cannot say 'do not take action' or 'let them go.' We are all victims. My entire house was burned; no food or property was saved. Complaints must be registered. Let the Commission work impartially. If property earned through wrong means was destroyed, that's fine; otherwise, the state must provide proper compensation.
Raju Gurung, CPN (UML):

There is a question mark over Chairperson Gauri Bahadur Karki. We wonder if this report will just be shelved like previous commissions. Let the guilty be punished after an impartial investigation. On the 24th, militant professional people across the country carried out looting, vandalism, and planned arson. The Home Minister gave orders to release them. This is the situation. It was understood that the fire brigade was stopped by the police, not the protesters. There has been infiltration into security agencies as well; the Commission should investigate. Citizens are in a state of terror. How can elections be held in this condition? People will revolt again if the guilty are not punished. Video footage exists. If the government doesn't act, we will be forced to take action.
Istiyak Ahmed, Mayor, Siddharthanagar:

I want to pay tribute to the Gen-Z martyrs. My house was burned, and I am in a lot of pain. If our community had retaliated when my house was on fire, another incident would have occurred. Three and a half kilograms of gold biscuits are missing. There were 1.5 kg of jewelry and 10.5 million in cash. Nothing was left. We Nepalis are becoming like Somalia.
Honorable Bhojraj Shrestha, Provincial Assembly Member, CPN (UML):

Gen-Z representatives should also have been present today. There are 15 Gen-Z members in my house. Should we make them carry weapons or teach them the teachings of Buddha? I want to ask Gen-Z: Is entering politics a crime? It has been a month and a half, and the criminals haven't been caught. Let the Commission present itself ruthlessly. Who organized the Gen-Z movement? Should the organizers not take responsibility? One must be able to call a good deed good and a wrong deed wrong. Am I a corrupt person? An extremist? Let it be proven. Where did I become a criminal? I suffered a loss of 7.6 million. Is social service and politics a crime? Protest and crime are separate things; we are a business family. I am ready to face punishment according to the law if I have done anything wrong; let our property be investigated. Why do the police administration and the Chief District Officer say 'we can't protect you, how can we protect ourselves?' Our files haven't been registered. My vehicle was damaged, a vehicle associated with an organization, and there was a loss of nearly 7.6 million. I am repairing my house with a loan. We are paying taxes to the state thinking we will get security; where do we go to seek justice now?
Savitri Aryal, Deputy Mayor, Butwal:

Massive damage occurred at the Butwal Municipality office. I couldn't stop my children from joining the Gen-Z protest. On the 24th, we planned to save the Butwal Municipality building at any cost, but the effort wasn't effective. About 70% of the house was destroyed. Neighbors prevented arson, but my house was still destroyed on the evening of the 24th. Doubts have arisen whether we will be able to remain Nepalis tomorrow. The police administration also didn't suggest retaliation. All political parties must come together now. We must get justice, and criminals must be punished.
Dipendra G.C., Dreamland Resort:

Another Dreamland Hotel has been operating in Butwal for 15 years. Vandalism and looting started at 3:30 PM on the 24th. Fires were started inhumanely in hotel rooms while guests were present. The hotel in Manigram suffered more damage. There weren't 100 employees in Manigram. The restaurants, cabins, and reception hall there were all burned. My hotel is the first four-star hotel in Rupandehi. The Nepali flag at the hotel was also burned. A large hall accommodating 1,500 people and the remaining 6 halls were completely destroyed by fire. We were not in a position to retaliate. 60–70 people remained until the end. They said 'the hotel is under our control' and didn't let anyone leave. TVs, furniture, alcohol, and everything else were burned, and many things were looted. Let the investigation commission give us justice.
Bina Rana, Deputy Mayor, Sainamaina:

Despite being told to run, they stayed at home. Some suggested resigning; our Mayor's house was surrounded and set on fire three times. They were saying they would take me around in public with a garland of shoes. Criminals are walking openly. The police say no orders have come from above to arrest them. Let there be action against the guilty. Thank you for giving us the opportunity to express our pain.
RSP:
Gen-Z and criminals were emboldened to protest with the feeling that the police would do nothing and the army wouldn't want to come. The Commission must also search for and investigate video footage.
Navaraj Dhakal, Chairperson, Kanchan Rural Municipality:

All my household items were burned and vandalized. The police have refused to register the complaint. We didn't take the complaint to the police after consulting; all videos are safe with us. May our expectations be met. Let justice be served.

This kind of protest was never seen before. It appeared to be a heartless protest. Protesters went to the municipality at 11:00 AM on the 24th. The Armed Police and Nepal Police knew about it. That place was saved. I want to thank the security personnel. After that, some chaotic individuals were seen riding motorcycles in groups of three, carrying shovels and pickaxes, and running from Ward No. 1 to 10. People were terrified. They returned for the third time, entered rooms, and started fires. My wife (a teacher) is still in a state of shock. We have said: take action against whoever did wrong. The damage assessment was done by the District Assessment Committee. In this incident, trained people with large physiques, such as those from Scouts and Red Cross, were used. They worked with a system where one message reached hundreds. Is it right to commit crimes while wearing the national flag or vandalize while wearing a Red Cross jacket? We can also handle these criminals. People who have been in jail, drug traffickers, users, and those involved in crime were used.
Umakant Chapai, Chairperson, District Coordination Committee:

There is no environment for political parties to go into elections. Why did the Home Minister stop the police from taking action? Why did the police administration tell us 'provide your own security'? Those who incited the arson must be punished. We also have youth and could use them, but we think it would only cause terror. We are responsible. Journalists who broadcasted news of the Mayor's house burning and sent misleading news like 'the former Prime Minister is arriving at Bhairahawa Airport' should also face action. If we cannot punish them now, the coming days will be even more dire.
A) Banke District
Date: 2082/07/11. Opinions obtained from interactions with stakeholders following on-site inspections of damaged structures in Banke district.
Administrative Officer, Nepalgunj Sub-Metropolitan City:

This looting, vandalism, and arson happened after Gen-Z had already left, declaring their protest successful. Gen-Z is not involved in this. We have risen from the ashes and started service delivery for 3–4 days now. People can easily be identified from videos and other evidence of looting. Even when two people were named in a complaint, the police caught and then released them. Civil servants are also terrified. Let the criminals be punished. May the center and province governments quickly provide help for the reconstruction of damaged offices.
Uday K.C., Inland Revenue Office:
Rather than Gen-Z, elements with grudges against the office took advantage of the chaos. Only small groups were involved.
Chief of Customs:

Those who had a grudge against a particular office had those offices destroyed. The influence of Kathmandu was felt in outer districts. People came with tools from motorcycle garages and took goods. We suffered a loss of about 67.5 million. There was more looting than arson. Those involved in crime must be punished. The Building Department must perform reconstruction at a fast pace. The police office has also prepared a report on the damage. They looted goods from the warehouse from morning until evening. Damage occurred to employees' residences and property. The clothes of the Armed Police deployed at customs and their weapons were set on fire. We have video clips; the Commission must punish the guilty without bias, separating 'milk from water'.
Indra Bahadur Karki, High Court, Banke:

The protest on August 24 happened right in front of me while I was at the District Court premises. Arson occurred at the District Court at 3:30 PM. Contact with the police administration could not be made; the court security personnel gave up. All employees were terrified. After burning the District Administration Office, they came to destroy the court. Initially, they went away saying it wasn't the court, but then they returned and burned it. There were more looting groups than Gen-Z. They completely destroyed vehicles. 73 motorcycles and cars were burned, including two new cars belonging to two judges. Five cars were burned. Everything at the High Court was also burned; elements other than Gen-Z were responsible. About 4,000 active case files out of 6,000 (including those under implementation) were burned. Physical structures were completely destroyed. They set fire from the bottom of the records section. Chairs, tables, printers, computers, and ACsnothing is left. Both the District and High Courts are fully destroyed. Damage at the High Court is about 70 million, and at the District Court, it is 92.9 million.
Sabin Chaudhary, Land Revenue Office:

The scene after 2 PM on August 24 showed that there is no law or punishment in this society and anything is permissible. Administration employees told us to save our lives; they stole what could be stolen and then committed arson. Infiltration occurred on the 25th, and some files were also burned. Our office suffered a loss of 59.1 million. We have digitized records, but service seekers whose data entry had errors might face some problems. When data doesn't tally, we'll need to rely on site reports and ward office recommendations. The office has been given a 'Red Sticker.' We are in the process of renting space and expect help from concerned bodies for furniture and infrastructure. Land reform work will take time. This problem arose because two rooms in the tenant section were burned; we might start work next week.
Chief of Survey Office:
In our office, 10% of the documents were burned, and the rest are safe. We will start work after repairs and installing the server.
Binod Ghimire, Agriculture Knowledge Center:
Our office is also in a burnt state. They specifically searched for files to burn. Government employees' motorcycles were burned, but our technical services have already started.
District Postal Office:

They entered our office around 5 PM on the 24th and caused complete damage. The counter and store were completely burned. Service delivery has started. We have resumed mail transport via vehicles. Damage at the Kohalpur Banke District Postal Office is over 8.8 million. Postage stamps and revenue were looted.
Narainapur Rural Municipality:

Arson and looting occurred at the municipality at 9/10 PM after office hours on the 24th by people coming from the Indian side. Records were also destroyed. On the morning of the 25th, the Nepalese Army arrested those coming to burn other ward offices.
Revenue Investigation, Kohalpur:

It is suspected that people who faced action from Revenue Investigation in the past or might face it in the future were involved in the looting and arson. This situation arose after security agencies gave up. At 4 PM, a group of 100–200 people entered the office and first damaged vehicles under investigation and records. Documents that were under investigation or could be investigated were burned. Cars and motorcycles were destroyed during the investigation. If everyone involved is not brought into the legal net, a state of impunity will prevail.
Water Resources and Development Division:

Seven government motorcycles were destroyed. Many people with a criminal mindset, thinking they could steal or take whatever they wanted, were seen. Those individuals were saying they should go loot the customs next.
Juvenile Correction Home, Naubasta, Banke:

Nothing happened throughout the 24th. However, by 6 PM on the 23rd, 25 CCTV cameras had already been vandalized. In this correction home, five people died and two were injured. Out of 176, 78 fled; 35 have made contact, and 43 are still missing, all of whom are girls aged 17 to 25.
Naubasta Prison:
A total of 147 inmates fled, including 25 Indians and the rest Nepalis. 98 are still missing out of a total of 585 in this prison.
Jayantu Juvenile Correction Home:
On the 25th, 115 fled; 49 returned, and 66 are still at large. There's a provision that only police can remain and cannot carry weapons; currently, 10 people are working in civilian clothes.
Nepalgunj Prison:

Due to televisions being turned off on August 23 and 24, the inmates did not receive any information. On the evening of the 24th, outsiders arrived demanding the release of everyone and destroyed two CCTV cameras. They fled after the Armed Police Force fired firecrackers and tear gas. On the evening of the 25th, we also provided counseling. After the internal CCTV cameras were vandalized, the Army and Police used force, resulting in 10 injuries. Although the visitor room was damaged, the prison was not fully destroyed. We have since installed four high-quality CCTV cameras, and we made those responsible for the damage pay for them.
Krishna Shrestha (Former President, Chamber of Commerce and Industry):

Private sector entrepreneurs are living in an atmosphere of terror. How can an environment for earning a living be created? If the looters involved in these incidents are not punished, impunity will increase. Severe action must be taken. Because buildings were destroyed, service seekers are unable to receive services. If such an incident happens again, where will we end up? This incident involved 5% Gen-Z and 95% looters. There is a crowd just waiting for an opportunity to loot by causing incidents. All parties must remain active to prevent such events from recurring.
Mayor Purna Acharya (Kohalpur):

The nature of the damage in all locations is identical. Looters used Gen-Z as a shield to cause destruction; my ancestral property and private house suffered heavy damage. The loss is approximately 9.6 million. Aside from a few parts, the Kohalpur Municipality building was completely destroyed. The Town Development Committee was also fully ruined. In Kohalpur Municipality, approximately 90 million was lost, including 135 types of goods and physical infrastructure. We began reconstruction on the 28th and started providing some services systematically. However, we will be capable of full service delivery within a few days. Can the Commission punish those demonstrating such insolence? Can this government end impunity? It cannot. I want my property to be investigated. Citizens are terrified, and social media has posed a challenge. Those causing rowdiness are walking openly in society. It is objectionable that the entire government is instructing not to take action against such criminals.
High Court Bar Association:

Will the individuals involved in the events of August 23/24 be punished? We have doubts regarding the implementation of the Commission's report. I am also an eyewitness. When trying to contact security chiefs, the phones wouldn't even connect. The Bar Association building has also been destroyed. We have repaired the ground floor of the Bar Association and allowed the District Court to work from there. Some items could still be recovered if the security personnel take initiative. If criminals are not punished, impunity will rise.
INSEC Province Coordinator:

Since there was physical and human loss, an effective investigation must be conducted. It is necessary to find out what causes and weaknesses led to the incident. There were only about 100–150 people causing rowdiness in the district. It is necessary to identify the involved individuals and take legal action. The Commission should wrap up its work as quickly as possible. Nothing happened here on the 23rd; a situation of double-clash developed on the 24th.
Nabin Giri, District Branch President, Federation of Nepali Journalists:

August 23 and 24 should not be viewed in the same light. I doubt the report will be entirely transparent ('separating milk from water'). The Home Minister's statements have increased suspicion. It is easy to protest from outside the government, but difficult to work once in power. Those inciting others through social media must be punished. The presence of the state was not seen during the incident; if it had been, the damage would have been minimal. Why did the security mechanism advise saving vehicles and data the day before?
Sudhanshu Koirala, Nepali Congress:

The Nepali Congress party office was set on fire and vandalized. The damage is estimated at approximately 6.6 million. The actual Gen-Z movement was peaceful, but the country was destroyed due to the infiltration of chaotic groups. In the case of Banke, the administration failed completely. Where should I go with my elderly father and mother? There was a high chance they would be unsafe regardless of where we went. When I asked the SP to keep the leaders' houses safe, the police did come, but the protesters chased them away as soon as they saw them. Statues of B.P. Koirala, Pushpalal, and Ganesh Man Singh were toppled. We do not want a confrontation and could have re-installed them, but we haven't done so yet. This is the sentiment among the citizens of Banke.
Ghananath Khanal, CPN (UML):

Banke has been devastated. Offices are not in a state to function smoothly. Who will take responsibility for the events of the 24th? Given the biased social media status of the Commission's Chairperson two days before the government was formed, will the Commission conduct an impartial investigation? The central government has not paid special attention to the burnt public offices. Why aren't those guilty of the August 24 incidents being punished? Let there be an impartial investigation. Why were measures like passport blocking and travel restrictions outside Kathmandu implemented? It must first be identified who orchestrated the movement on the 24th. There is a need for a commitment to impartial investigation. We will start operating programs from the party office within a few days. We shouldn't have to re-install the statues of major politicians; the government should do it.
Chief of Transport Management Office:

The office was destroyed. We have started vehicle inspections and route permit work and will resume online services in a few days. Vehicle registration records and everything else have been destroyed.
District Coordination Chief, Banke:

The looting started from the Sub-Metropolitan City and spread to the Customs Office, Survey Office, Land Revenue Office, District Court, and High Court. Looting was carried out by others under the name of Gen-Z. When we asked to take action against those who broke the statue of Ganesh Man Singh, the Police and Chief District Officer said they hadn't received orders from above. Civil servants also need to change their working styles significantly. If the police were to conduct a search in a nearby neighborhood now, 50% of the looted goods could be recovered. It is necessary for the police administration to work with activity rather than inactivity; otherwise, impunity will increase.
Points understood during discussions with the District Security Committee:

On August 23, 2082, no untoward incidents occurred; there was only a general demonstration. Assuming that the casualties among protesters in Kathmandu on the 23rd could affect Surkhet, discussions were held in the District Security Committee, and police and armed police deployment was increased early on the 24th. Around 9:00 AM, as the number of protesters gathered at Hatiya Chowk, Neta Chowk, and the main market increased, police were concentrated in the market area. While managing the demonstration in coordination with district Gen-Z representatives, reports of incidents in Kathmandu and across the country arrived around 12:30 PM, causing the number of protesters to swell. First, the political party offices on Hatiya Line were vandalized and set on fire. Afterward, protesters split into various groups and began attacking district government offices and police stations, and forces were deployed accordingly. Around 4:30 PM, after the Nepalese Army took the Chief District Officer to a safe location, the Armed Police Force also withdrew from duty. As the police alone could not control the scattered protesters, the aforementioned damage occurred. Although a curfew was imposed at 3:40 PM, the Nepalese Army did not enter the field, and additional damage could not be prevented as the police ran out of resources, ammunition, and tear gas.
7.6. Karnali Province
Monitoring was conducted in Surkhet district under Karnali Province. The points understood during the process are as follows:
7.5.2. Surkhet:
Date: 2082/07/11. Opinions obtained from interactions with stakeholders following on-site inspections of damaged structures in Surkhet district.
Jagadishwar Upadhyay, CDO Surkhet:

Details of the damage have been categorized based on the value of the affected offices. Damage occurred at Municipalities, Rural Municipalities, and their ward offices. Arson, theft, and looting affected the private property of Mayors, Deputy Mayors, Ward Chairs, and the general public.
Jivraj Budhathoki, Secretary:

The Provincial Assembly building is fully damaged. The administrative building of the Secretariat, committee meeting rooms, and 14 vehicles survived. The library, generator, and the residences of the Speaker and Deputy Speaker were vandalized. No arson occurred. The estimated total damage is 37.7 million.
Dev Kumar Subedi, District Secretary, Nepali Congress Surkhet:

The party's district office was completely destroyed. It is necessary to punish the guilty and guarantee full peace and security. A long-term solution is the need of the hour to prevent incidents from recurring. The total estimated damage is 12.8 million.
Ward Chairperson, Birendranagar Municipality Ward No. 7:
The office was vandalized, and the vandalism was carried out by people known to us.
Bishnu Prasad Sharma, District Chairperson, Rastriya Janamorcha:
The office was vandalized and set on fire. Peace and security can only be restored through a factual investigation and ruthless punishment of the guilty. Total estimated damage is 1.9 million.
Jit Bahadur Rana, CPN (Maoist Centre):

The country's system and Constitution have been attacked. The party office is fully damaged and cannot be reused. Total estimated damage is 11.7 million. While the movement demanded change, action must be taken against the guilty.
Harsha Bikram Khadka, President, Surkhet Chamber of Commerce and Industry:

Arson occurred at the office with a loss of about 500,000–600,000. Why did the destruction happen on August 23 and 24? Serious study is needed to prevent such unimaginable events in the future. The events of the 23rd and 24th must be viewed as two separate aspects. The business environment has been ruined; if an environment of trust cannot be built, it will be impossible to stop the flight of human resources.
Ward Chairperson, Ward No. 9:
The ward office is fully damaged. Daily operations are currently being conducted from the Municipality office.
Khimlal Devkota, Ward No. 11:
Various aspects of the movement need to be viewed through different perspectives. An impartial investigation is necessary to prioritize stability.
Ward Office, Ward No. 6:
The office has suffered total damage. It is necessary to view the movement from a different perspective. Strict legal action must be taken against the guilty.
Ward Office, Ward No. 2:
Two rooms of the office have been completely damaged. Those who caused the damage to the office were individuals known to us.
Ward Office, Ward No. 10:

The new building has been destroyed by fire. Records have been burnt and lost. The assembly hall and the Chairperson’s office were burnt; work is currently being managed from an old building. There is no working environment and no security. Questions have been raised about who the country belongs to when people in government themselves use the language of threats.
Ward Office, Ward No. 1:

Infiltration occurred during the protest, leading to targeted attacks on individuals. Business owners are terrified. The government has not appeared in its role as a guardian. There is government intervention to prevent action against individuals involved in looting. The genuine protest was misused by criminal elements. In a state governed by law, the rule of law must be felt.
Bishnu Khadka, Entrepreneur and Vice-President of NC Surkhet:

May the Commission succeed in giving a new direction to the country by seeking the truth and conducting an impartial investigation, free from political bias or prejudice. There are questions regarding the impartiality of the Commission’s Chairperson; I believe he will prove those allegations wrong through his work. The protest on August 23 was purely a Gen-Z movement, but the destruction on the 24th was planned and sponsored. On the 24th, around 11:30 AM, 30–40 people gathered near my house in Itram. At 12:45 PM, a small group set fire to the CPN (UML) district office. Some people on motorcycles incited the crowd, and a sponsored mob committed arson. My house was set on fire between 1:30 PM and 2:00 PM. This is not a protest but anarchy. If a long-term solution is not found, the situation will become more horrific than today. A state of insecurity and lawlessness has been created. A state of identity-less existence has emerged. This can only be resolved through the rule of law and its correct and impartial application. It is unbecoming of a Home Minister to walk around making statements against political parties. Minimum peace and security can be maintained if legitimate state bodies are left free to perform their duties. Looters are walking around openly issuing threats. The Commission must investigate impartially to establish the rule of law. Estimated losses are around 3.5–4 million (35–40 Lakhs). Compensation for the loss of citizens' property is necessary. Citizens feel like they have no guardian. Chaos has spread in the name of Gen-Z. Rumors have been spread for personal gain. Technology was used to incite the crowd rather than for actual protest.
Ward Chairperson, Ward No. 4, Birendranagar Municipality:
The ward office operated within the Himali Library has been destroyed. Arson also occurred at the Himali Library and the Red Cross sub-branch office.
Kulmani Devkota, Chairperson, CPN (UML) Surkhet:

There was a conspiracy and infiltration in the incident. A sponsored mob took advantage of the movement. There was sponsored, pre-planned, and interference-laden destruction. What is the expertise of the Commission investigating this incident? How can a credible environment be maintained? There have been serious attacks on the organs of the state system and on Nepal’s national integrity. Why is the Nepal Police not being allowed to do their job? Why aren't arrangements for immediate relief being made? People have to live in constant fear while the state itself has become biased. A terrifying state of insecurity and disorder has been created. The country is heading toward crisis; when the state itself becomes partisan, the result is further destruction. It feels as if the state is no longer our own. Properties of six leaders were set on fire. It is impossible to escape this horrific situation through a mindset of revenge. Stability is only possible through co-existence.
Padam Bahadur Shahi, Karnali Province:

The protest on August 23 was justified. The demands were legitimate and not political. However, impunity has flourished as a result of these movements. Entrepreneurs are discouraged, and there is a looming contraction in employment and revenue. Institutionally, the Surkhet Chamber of Commerce and Industry has suffered damage. Six private properties were set on fire. The overall environment has become clouded. Those involved in the events of the 24th are criminals; it wasn't truly a protest. The investment climate has been dampened, and the environment for Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) has been completely destroyed. Business houses feel persecuted and unsafe following the destruction on the 24th. The psychological toll is immense, and the country's confidence has weakened.
Dhruba Shahi, CPN (UML) Karnali Province:

Everyone needs to improve from their respective positions. The current leadership has only provided shelter to this impunity. Even the Home Minister is behaving anarchically. The Commission members are biased; individuals harboring personal grudges have been appointed. Is this intended to create an environment for further violence? Is the government not for everyone? A government must act as a guardian. There is a huge question regarding the Commission's impartiality and the qualifications of its members. In a situation where universal acceptance hasn't been established, the report's outcome feels predetermined. To pull the country out of crisis through credible work, cooperation and co-existence with everyone are essential. Democracy cannot be preserved with a mindset that political parties are insignificant.
Ward Chairperson, Ward No. 12:

The state of peace and security is extremely challenging. Those involved in the arson are auto-rickshaw drivers, and even retired civil servants are involved. There is no trust in the government. Instead of providing relief, the government is inciting people toward anti-system activities. A government that took an oath on the Constitution is focused only on terrifying us by showing guns and mobs. RSP cadres are threatening us today. Are self-respecting Nepalis no longer allowed to live?
Ward No. 8:

Both the ward office and private property have been damaged. Total losses amount to 13.6 million, with 3.4 million being my personal property loss. If security agencies are not allowed to work independently, the country is on the verge of a serious civil war. The security situation is grave, and we face constant threats. The negligence of security agencies on the 24th is a matter for serious evaluation. The government is not serious; may the government directed by the mob become a government for all.
Office of the Chief Attorney:
Two vehicles were burnt, and the office windows were shattered. The total damage is 1,146,000.
Dinesh Bhandari, Survey Office:

The office, including the technical department on the ground floor, has been destroyed by fire. The server is destroyed, making it impossible to deliver services. There is no budget or legal framework for recovery. The fire burned for about three hours. Between the building (16.5 million) and other assets (5.065 million), the total damage is 26,300,000. All those involved in this crime must face strict punishment. The working environment no longer exists.
Commission for the Investigation of Abuse of Authority (CIAA):
There is a loss of approximately 1.3 million, though no arson occurred.
Treasury and Accounts Control Office:
Even this office, which has no direct daily dealings with the public, suffered 17.7 million in damages due to arson.
Office of the Chief Minister:
Looting and vandalism occurred. Estimated damage to the Chief Minister's office and Minister's quarters is approximately 63,000.
Bheri Babai Diversion Project:
Stones were pelted from outside the compound.
Laxmi Prasad Baskota:
Arson occurred at the Municipal office and City Hall, among other locations.
Meghnath Sapkota:
Windows were broken and furniture was vandalized. The damage is around 400,000.
Nilkantha Dhakal, Deputy Mayor, Birendranagar Municipality:

My house was set on fire; even all my clothes were burnt. Society is fragmented and insensitive. The Prime Minister is behaving anarchically and is consumed by revenge. It's becoming hard to even identify as a Nepali. People in government are mobilized for this. All involved should be punished. Everyone must focus on building Nepal's national image. I can no longer walk alone. The peace and security situation is pathetic; the national status is crumbling. The country is in despair.
Mayor Mohanmaya Dhakal:

Birendranagar Municipality serves 591,000 people. While the city was moving toward construction and growth, the destructive events of the 24th dealt a massive blow to this mission, pushing back the pace of development significantly. All ward offices, the main municipality building, records, and resources have been damaged. The brakes have been applied to development. Our contribution toward building a safe city has resulted in betrayal for us and our families. This 'Gen-Z' generation is not and cannot be a barrier to construction; rather, mobilized groups of individuals are involved. Even former judges have worked to inject poison into the movement. Total peace is only possible if this dance of destruction is condemned. They used pickaxes and shovels to vandalize homes. My house was set on fire. Let the committee conduct an impartial investigation. Let the Commission play a role in creating an environment where victims receive justice. Everyone should realize that revenge cannot drive the country forward. Let the state be a guardian. Everyone needs to utilize democracy; the only alternative to democracy is an improved democracy. Let the state be a guardian that listens to everyone and makes rational decisions, rising above the pit of revenge.

31-member BP Bhandari stated: The issues regarding the end of impunity and compensation will be studied with gravity and included in the report. Impartiality will be maintained. The concerns raised by participants will be fully followed in accordance with the Terms of Reference.
7.6. Sudurpashchim Province
Monitoring was conducted in Kailali and Kanchanpur districts under Sudurpashchim Province.
7.6.1. Kanchanpur:
Date: 2082/07/11. Opinions obtained from interactions with stakeholders following on-site inspections of damaged structures in Kanchanpur district:
Surendra Shiladhar, General Public (Tailoring):

My business has suffered immense loss. I have been operating a hosiery industry under the brand name 'New Royal Tailoring.' On the 24th, they burnt and destroyed all my ready-made clothes, raw fabrics, and machines. How will the state provide compensation? It is a major question for a common worker like me on how to work and sustain a livelihood now. This was the Melauli Hosiery Industry, and the loss is approximately 4 million (40 Lakhs).
Government Attorney Office:
Furniture and electrical items have been destroyed by fire. Except for a few, the case files remain safe.
Tilak Prasad Rijal, Punarwas Municipality:
Vehicles, files, and the building have all been burnt; the loss is around 55 million (5.5 Crore). Service delivery is currently being managed from a separate hall.
Bhimdatta Municipality has reported damages, and there have been incidents of daily threats.
Cottage and Small Industries Office:
Two industries suffered damage. The details of which have already been sent.
Mayor of Bedkot Municipality:

The movements of the 23rd and 24th are distinct. The protest on the 23rd was limited to three major demands: control of corruption, lifting the ban on social media, and opposing the expensiveness of the provincial structure. These are issues that political representatives like us have also repeatedly raised. Everyone saw what happened on the 23rd. The Nepalese people are now asking: where were the guns on the 24th when the Parliament building, Supreme Court, Singha Durbar, and even the President's Office were set on fire? I wish to express my respect to all those who were martyred. We must distinguish between criminals and protesters. My house was burnt down. Cadres of RPP, RSP, and Maoists joined together to loot books, jewelry, and belongings from my home while the administration stood by as a mute spectator. My house was set on fire at 6:30 PM. This was not a protest; it was a purely criminal act.
7.6.2. Kailali:
Date: 2082/07/11. Opinions obtained from interactions with stakeholders following on-site inspections of damaged structures in Kailali district:
Various Local Body Officials:

Local body offices, ward offices, and service centers have been looted, vandalized, and set on fire. Protesters who arrived repeatedly since 9:00 AM committed theft, looting, and arson; RSP cadres appeared openly involved in these acts. Specifically, municipalities represented by Congress and UML were targeted. There is no significant damage in municipalities like Janaki Tikapur and Bhajani. Maoist cadres were also involved in the arson. Details of losses from arson and looting have been sent to the local administration and the Ministry of Federal Affairs. Police reports (Muchulka) have been completed. The Urban Development branches of several municipalities have worked on determining the exact extent of damage, and other agencies have begun similar processes. Vandalism occurred in the municipalities due to the movement in Kathmandu on the 23rd. Arson was committed suddenly while employees were still working in the offices, and they barely escaped with their lives.
Trade Union President:

The law of the country exists to take action against employees and evaluate their work; the sentiment that 'all employees are thieves' is incorrect in any sense. Employees living in office quarters have lost everything; even their housing arrangements are gone. State service delivery cannot be improved by making employees feel helpless. Employees are also citizens of this country. No help was received from security agencies even when employees engaged in service delivery were attacked. The fire brigade could not work effectively and was not mobilized. Targeting government offices during a political movement is a wrong practice for everyone. This does not resolve conflict; it only sets a bad precedent. In future movements, private homes could be targeted. Those who led the arson today will not be safe either.
Tek Raj Paneru, Registrar (Srestedar), District Court:

The court is a temple of justice for the helpless. It is not a development office. The court does not even belong to the state; it is the center of people's faith. Burning a court is not a movement; history will judge this poorly. In court, one side always wins and the other loses. Therefore, the anger of a legal defeat cannot be pacified by burning the court. People recognized as cadres of parties that were once in government are involved in the arson. This is treason. The details of the court's damage have been sent through proper channels and presented here as well. Around 3:30 PM, after prisoners escaped from the jail, the court was set on fire. Original case files dating back to 1950 (2007 BS) have been burnt. Computers were stolen. Protests and crimes must be viewed through different perspectives.
Jailer (Prison Chief):

Around 7:00 PM, a large crowd arrived outside the prison and began arson and stone-pelting. Following this, the inmates inside began chanting slogans and became aggressive, saying, 'If Rabi Lamichhane can go out, why can't we?' As a large group was also involved in arson and vandalism at the District Police Office, there was no possibility of additional security personnel arriving. Information that prisoners had escaped from jails in Kathmandu had spread via TV and other media. After Rabi Lamichhane broke out of jail and fled, inmates in other prisons also started escaping or being helped to escape. 667 inmates fled, of whom 409 have returned. The damage is approximately 4.5 million (45 Lakhs).
Transport Management Office:

The online system was not burnt. Arson was committed from outside while we were working in the office. Motorcycles belonging to employees and service seekers were burnt. There is a state of lawlessness. Even now, people are threatening and intimidating in the name of 'Gen-Z.' There is no protection, no security.
Inland Revenue Office:
Arson was committed around 3:45 PM while children were still in the quarters. Service delivery has been affected.
Khadga Joshi, Human Rights Activist:

The movement on the 24th was not a protest; it must be viewed on two major grounds: the limits of state power and the limits of civil rights. Any investigation that ignores these two sides cannot be complete. No commission formed by the state has shown the courage to establish the truth. To give the country the right direction, this commission must be able to provide clear guidelines. These things cannot be understood through a superficial approach. The methodology of study was lacking. Stakeholders are not in a position to speak. The Human Rights Alliance is also working together with the National Human Rights Commission.
Narayan Datta Bhatta, Member of National Assembly:

The country had to suffer unimaginable loss; this in itself is an extremely sad moment. All state agencies proved weak in service delivery in one way or another. My house was also targeted. On the 23rd, we were still in a National Assembly committee meeting discussing good governance. While we were inside the Parliament building, arson was committed from the outside. A spirit of revenge does not benefit the country; we can only take the country forward if everyone joins hands. Today the country is afraid. Singha Durbar, which was rebuilt with grants after the 2015 earthquake, has been burnt. Offices of political parties and commercial establishments have been burnt. This was an act unbecoming of even being called a Nepali. The events of the 23rd and 24th must be viewed from different perspectives. Let there be a proper investigation into the criminal acts. The commission has received an unprecedented opportunity to guide the country; it should utilize it.
7.7. Conclusion of Opinions Expressed During Field Monitoring

The following is the summary of the views and issues raised by various individuals during the observation, interaction, and discussion programs led by the Chairperson and members of the Commission in 13 districts that suffered heavy losses during the protests of August 23 and 24:

content.content: Local residents and stakeholders expressed happiness regarding the presence of the Commission's leadership during the field visits. However, most attendees were worried and angry regarding the physical damage and lack of security during the demonstrations.

Representatives, local residents, and administrative officials in some districts complained that a serious security problem was created due to a lack of expected coordination among security agencies. The complaints specifically pointed to the lack of expected support from the Nepalese Army and, in some places, the Armed Police Force.
Individuals supporting political parties and representatives accused cadres of certain specific parties of being responsible for the incidents.

There were varying opinions regarding the events of August 23 and 24. A common view was that the incident on the 23rd occurred due to weak management by officials in charge and an underestimation of the movement, along with the infiltration of individuals with malicious intent into the peaceful Gen-Z protests, particularly in Kathmandu.

Due to the low morale of security agencies and administration (Chief District Officers) in all districts, there was a demand to boost their morale through robust security management while normalizing daily administration to prevent such incidents from recurring.

In all visited districts, stakeholdersincluding political affiliates, representatives, residents, and Gen-Z youth representativesnoted that during the August 24 protest, known individuals used petrol and chemicals to burn private property, loot, and issue threats due to personal or organizational vendettas. They demanded the Commission initiate action since security agencies have not yet brought these individuals to justice, raising fears of increased criminal activity in the future.
There were complaints that National Investigation Department offices in the districts failed to provide information on time, leading to heavy losses.

Participants expressed that the spread of false and misleading information via social media was a primary cause for the widespread damage to public bodies and private property, especially on August 24.
Participants also demanded that the state evaluate the physical damage and provide compensation.

Stakeholders demanded that the Commission conduct an impartial investigation and take action against the guilty, regardless of who they are. Some participants expressed skepticism regarding the formation of the Commission and its performance.
Overall, there was an expectation that the Commission would analyze the facts/evidence on an objective basis and send recommendations to prevent such incidents in the future.

content: Chapter-8: Correspondence from the Commission and Responses Received from Various Agencies
Details of Responses

The Inquiry Commission was formed by the Government of Nepal, Council of Ministers, exercising the power conferred by Sub-section (2) of Section 3 of the Inquiry Commission Act, 1969. Its purpose is to conduct a factual investigation and submit a report on the human and physical losses incurred during the demonstrations organized by the Gen-Z generation on August 23 and 24, 2082 (Bhadra 23-24, 2025), which raised demands concerning Nepal's national interest, good governance, and anti-corruption. In accordance with the 'Terms of Reference' provided to the Commission, 270 letters were dispatched to various agencies to request details related to human and physical losses, as well as information from security agencies. Out of these, 199 letters specifically requested dataexcluding those related to regular operations, implementation of various decisions, informational notices, and complaints or FIRs. Responses were received for 164 of the requested letters, while 35 letters remain unanswered. The information obtained has been incorporated into this report. The complete details of the correspondence are included in Annex (10).
Chapter-9: Decisions of Government Agencies, Councils, and Committees
9.1. Decisions of the Prime Minister and Council of Ministers

During the anti-corruption movement and protests against social media restrictions led by the Gen-Z generation on 2082/05/23, the Council of Ministers made the following two decisions on the same date:

content.content: To promote and subsequently retire the Technical Joint Secretary of the Ministry of Agriculture.

To transfer Additional Inspector General of Police (AIGP) Mr. Dan Bahadur Karki, currently working at the Nepal Police Headquarters, Human Resource Development Department, to the Kathmandu Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari.

content: 9.2. Decisions of the National Security Council

On Monday, August 23, 2082, the 39th meeting of the National Security Council was held in accordance with Clause (1) of Article 266, Part 28 of the Constitution of Nepal, and the following decisions were made:

content.content: To recommend to the Government of Nepal, Council of Ministers, for policy approval to procure weapons, ammunition, and other military equipment/supplies mentioned in Appendix 'A' required for the Nepalese Army for the fiscal year 082/083, as per the provisions of Sub-section (2) of Section 133 of the Army Act, 2006.

The current security situation of the country was reviewed. It was decided to deploy all security agencies in an effective and coordinated manner to address existing security challenges and maintain peace, security, and public order in the country.

content: The draft of the Eighth Annual Report of the National Security Council was discussed. Furthermore, it was decided to approve the draft with some amendments and submit it to the Right Honorable President in accordance with Clause (3) of Article 266, Part 28 of the Constitution of Nepal.
9.3. Decisions of the Central Security Committee
The Commission requested the decision records of the Central Security Committee meetings held from August 20 to 25, 2082. The letter received from the Ministry of Home Affairs stated the following:

content.content: A meeting of the Central Security Committee was held only on August 22, 2082 (Bhadra 22); however, since no written decisions were recorded from that meeting, only the attendance log was provided.

content: 9.4. Decisions of the District Security Committees
Decisions Received from Various Districts

The Commission requested decisions from District Security Committee meetings held during the Gen-Z demonstrations on August 23 and 24 across all districts. While some districts provided incident reports, others provided formal meeting decisions. The decisions from districts that were relatively more affected are listed and analyzed below.
Koshi Province
District Administration Office, Morang
Decisions of 2082/05/23

content.content: Previous decisions of the District Security Committee and their implementation status were discussed/reviewed; performance was found to be effective.

In view of the peaceful protest programs called by the Gen-Z generation against the ban on social media like Facebook, corruption, and irregularities, all security agencies shall perform effectively through coordinated micro-monitoring to prevent any unwanted/illegal activities and maintain peace and order.

A practical simulation exercise for rescue, relief, and facilitation during disasters shall be conducted at the Singhiya River on October 1, 2082 (Asoj 1) at 8:00 AM, coordinated by all security agencies.
The peace and security situation of the district thus far was analyzed as normal.

content: Decisions of 2082/05/24

content.content: Previous decisions of the District Security Committee and their implementation status were discussed/reviewed; performance was found to be effective.

As various groups and parties have held demonstrations in Biratnagar Metropolitan City and other locations in the district, and since there is a risk of violent activities, riots, or unrest impacting public peace and security, all assemblies, processions, gatherings, and sit-insexcept for the movement of common citizens and vehiclesare prohibited under Section 6(a) of the Local Administration Act, 1971, from 11:00 AM to 10:00 PM on 2082/05/24 in the following locations:

content: Locations:

content.content: Provincial Assembly: East to Nepal Rastra Bank, West to Koshi Hospital Chowk.
Chief Minister's Office: North to Roadshesh Chowk, South to Jatuwa Chowk (near Covid Hospital).
Chief Minister's Residence: North to Roadshesh, South to the road leading to the Ministry of Irrigation.
District Administration Office: North to Sanihat, South to the Telecom turn, West to Golchha Eye Hospital.
Biratnagar Metropolitan City: East to Sanihat Chowk, North to Birateshwar Temple, South to Golchha Chowk.
Provincial Governor's (Pradesh Pramukh) Office: East to the canal, West to Koshi Highway.
Provincial Governor's Residence: North to Madannagar Basic School, South to Managadh Drinking Water, West up to a 300-meter radius around the canal.
A 300-meter radius around the Integrated Check Post (ICP).
The peace and security situation of the district thus far was analyzed as normal.

content: Decisions of 2082/05/25

content.content: Previous decisions of the District Security Committee and their implementation status were discussed/reviewed; performance was found to be effective.

As demonstrations continue in Biratnagar Metropolitan City and other locations, with the potential for violent activities, riots, or unrest impacting public peace, a curfew order is issued for the entire Morang district under Section 6(a) of the Local Administration Act, 1971. This order is effective from 5:00 PM (17:00) on 2082/05/25 until 6:00 AM on 2082/05/26. All assemblies, processions, gatherings, and sit-ins are prohibited, except for the movement of essential vehicles (fire engines, ambulances, hearses, and healthcare workers). Violation of this order will result in action according to prevailing laws.

content: District Administration Office, Dhankuta
Decisions of 2082/05/23

content.content: The overall peace and security situation of the district thus far was analyzed and found to be satisfactory.

Approved various security and action plans for F.Y. 2082/083, including: VVIP security, security for potential programs by the Provincial Renaming Joint Struggle Committee, Holi festival security, general festival security, security for important locations, action plans for controlling improper resource extraction, security for fairs/festivals, and action plans for controlling religious discrimination and untouchability.
Approved the 'QRT/IRT Deployment 2082' plan, including available equipment, to cover all local levels of Dhankuta district.

In view of the currently increasing security challenges in the country, all security agencies shall make their information mechanisms more effective, disseminate factual information promptly, and remain in a state of readiness for immediate deployment as needed to ensure reliable security.

content: Decisions of 2082/05/25

content.content: Analyzing the situation following Gen Z protests in Kathmandu Valley and other locations on 2082/05/23, it is decided to issue a curfew order under Section 6(a) of the Local Administration Act, 1971. This applies to the following locations in Dhankuta Municipality, Dhankuta district, from 5:00 PM (17:00) on August 25, 2082, until 6:00 AM on August 26, 2082, or until further notice.

content: Boundaries:

content.content: North: Kagate
East: Khaniya Bas
West: Industrial Village
South: Chuliban
The overall peace and security situation of the district thus far was analyzed and found to be satisfactory.

content: Decisions of 2082/05/26

content.content: Following the Gen Z protests and the resulting situation, a Prohibitory Order (Nishedhagya) is issued for the entire Dhankuta district under Section 6(3a) of the Local Administration Act, 1971, effective from 11:00 AM on 2082/05/26 until further notice. Assembly, processions, demonstrations, meetings, or picketing are prohibited.

To prevent potential incidents, a curfew order is issued under Section 6(a) of the Local Administration Act, 1971, for the following locations in Dhankuta Municipality from 8:00 PM (20:00) on August 26, 2082, until 4:00 AM on August 27, 2082.

content: Boundaries:

content.content: North: Kagate
East: Khaniya Bas
West: Industrial Village
South: Chuliban
The current peace and security situation of the district was felt to be sensitive.

content: District Administration Office, Udayapur
Decisions of 2082/05/24

content.content: While the movement on 2082/05/23 was peaceful, the Gen Z protests and slogans starting the afternoon of 2082/05/24 have become unruly. Vandalism has occurred in government offices, and attacks, vandalism, and arson are being reported at political party offices and residences of people's representatives. Activities disrupting peace have simultaneously started in the district headquarters, other municipalities, and urban areas. As the situation cannot be controlled solely by local Nepal Police and APF units, it is decided to request immediate assistance from the Nepalese Army (Pashupati Prasad Battalion) to prevent further damage.
Decided to deploy Nepal Police and Armed Police Force across the district under the leadership/coordination of the Pashupati Prasad Battalion.
Decided to issue orders for restricted zones, prohibitory orders, and curfews in the district as necessary.
Decided to bring the situation under control using minimum force during security operations.

content: Decisions of 2082/05/25

content.content: Evaluated the damage from the Gen Z protests on 2082/05/24, noting vandalism of government offices, arson at the municipality and party offices, and attacks on the homes of current and former representatives, resulting in significant loss of property, physical structures, and important documents.
Based on the security assessment, it is deemed necessary to impose a curfew in the following areas. A recommendation is made to the Chief District Officer to issue the curfew order.
District Headquarters, Triyuga Municipality Area:
East: Triyuga Municipality Ward No. 13, Phuljor
West: Triyuga Municipality Ward No. 7, Chuhade
North: Triyuga Municipality Ward No. 10, Chapan Baruwa Bridge
South: Triyuga Municipality Ward No. 6, Jaljale
Katari Municipality Area:
East: Katari Municipality Ward No. 5, Dangdung Khola Bridge
West: Katari Municipality Ward No. 4, Kakur Khola Bridge
North: Katari Municipality Ward No. 4, Maruwa Khola Bridge
South: Katari Municipality Ward No. 3, Taradhari
Chaudandigadhi Municipality Area:
East: Chaudandigadhi Municipality Ward No. 4, Basaha
West: Chaudandigadhi Municipality Ward No. 6, Satdhare
North: Chaudandigadhi Municipality Ward No. 8, Birendra Bazar
South: Chaudandigadhi Municipality Ward No. 6, Madan Bhandari Highway
Decided to appeal to political parties, civil society, intellectuals, journalists, and all stakeholders to help maintain peace and order in the district.
Decided to mobilize District Police and APF Nepal No. 5 Battalion HQ (Triyuga, Udayapur) under the leadership of the Nepalese Army Pashupati Prasad Battalion to maintain security.
Decided to manage security through the District Security Command Post and JOC based on a detailed assessment of the overall security situation.

content: Decisions of 2082/05/26

content.content: Evaluating the security situation following the Gen Z protests on 2082/05/24, it is decided to recommend that the Chief District Officer issue a district-wide Prohibitory Order (Nishedhagya) effective from 6:00 AM to 6:00 PM on 2082/05/26.
To maintain order, it is decided to recommend the re-issuance of the curfew order for the following areas from 7:00 PM tonight (2082/05/26) until 5:00 AM tomorrow morning.

North: Katari Municipality Ward No. 4, Maruwa Khola Bridge
South: Katari Municipality Ward No. 3, Taradhari
