The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) guarantees equality, individual and life liberty, as well as freedom of thought and expression, and the right to peaceful assembly, among many other rights. Similarly, Nepal signed the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR) 1966 and its Optional Protocol on May 14, 1991, and the Second Optional Protocol in March 1998. Thus, the provisions of these covenants and protocols apply obligatorily to Nepal. Section 9 of the Nepal Treaty Act 1990 provides that if treaty provisions conflict with Nepalese law, the Nepalese law shall be ineffective to the extent of the conflict, establishing the primacy of treaties. This covenant plays a vital role in protecting the fundamental rights of citizens of each treaty nation, including freedom of expression and assembly. These sacrosanct values also hold a special place in Nepalese law. Article 17 of the Constitution of Nepal provides for the right to freedom of citizens, protecting the freedom of expression and the freedom to assemble peacefully without arms to manifest such thoughts collectively.

Utilizing these constitutional rights, three separate applications were registered at the District Administration Office, Kathmandu, under the names of Sudan Gurung (from an organization called 'We Nepal'), Ankit Malla, Khemraj Saud, Sabal Gautam, and Purushottam Yadav, requesting to hold a peaceful demonstration in the Maitighar Mandala area on September 8, 2025. Following discussions with the Chief District Officer (CDO) at the District Administration Office, a verbal agreement was reached to demonstrate only in non-prohibited areas and to ensure the demonstration remained peaceful. Accordingly, the District Administration Office, Kathmandu, granted permission. Thus, the plan was to start a peaceful demonstration at Maitighar Mandala on September 8, 2025, and hold a corner meeting at the same location as per the pre-approved schedule.

The demonstration called by the youth on September 8 was targeted against the widespread irregularities in the country. The massive participation of youth in social networks, specific specialized networks, and street movements against poor governance from time to time indicated the youth's anger against existing systemic flaws. Dissatisfaction against these anomalies was also found expressed through comments on social media by ordinary people, articles published in other media/newspapers against corruption, bad governance, and delays in government work, as well as resentment towards the processes of government operation.

Since early September in Nepal, such dissatisfaction was seen pouring out through hashtag campaigns on social networks like TikTok and Reddit, which widely publicized videos and memes accusing the children of politicians of adopting luxurious lifestyles using wealth earned through corruption.

Prior to September 8, Gen-Z movement callers protesting against corruption and social media restrictions had sent posts such as 'Sri Lanka did it, Indonesia did it, Philippines did it, Bangladesh did it, now it's Nepal's turn! It's time for us Gorkhalis to rise. It's time to raise our voice.' AI-generated photos/videos of public structures like the Parliament Building and Singha Durbar being burned or destroyed were also shared on various platforms. Furthermore, news was published in the media that plans for attacks were being made by mapping the houses of top leaders on networks like Netakhor.vercel.app.

There were also various groups on a network named Discord. In one of these Discord groups, views regarding anarchic activities, such as manufacturing Molotov Cocktails, vandalism, looting, and arson, were being shared. One of the organizers, Sudan Gurung, stated before the commission that upon learning of this, they had informed the police on September 7 (Bhadra 22) before the protest and even requested the Cyber Bureau to stop or remove such content.

Social networks have become extremely important and powerful means of communication in today's changing world. This medium has appeared powerful specifically in collectively manifesting the voice of the youth. However, the damage caused through these mediums is equally fast and widespread. There is also a global trend of increasing security threats arising from digital and cyber mediums.
Unawareness of the National Investigation Department

The National Investigation Department had no specific information regarding the messages expressed on these networks. The National Investigation Department appears not to have conducted any analysis of the growing anti-establishment sentiment among the youth. Nor does it appear that any other relevant government agencies conducted any study or research in this regard.

While the three Gen-Z groups that called for the demonstration and received approval from the CDO of Kathmandu stated the protest would be peaceful, it initially started peacefully but began to turn violent after some time. Neither the organizers nor the government security agencies had any inkling that the protest would turn violent in this manner. Police officers assessed that the situation in the capital on September 8 would be peaceful, based on the fact that a National Assembly meeting was scheduled at the Federal Parliament and the President was attending a program at the National City Hall (Rastriya Sabhagriha) that day.

On the evening of September 7 (Bhadra 22), a meeting of the National Security Council was held at the Prime Minister's residence, Baluwatar. During the meeting, heads of all security agencies, including the National Investigation Department and Nepal Police, informed that Gen-Z would demonstrate peacefully on September 8. No information regarding potential violence in the demonstration was provided. According to an official from the National Investigation Department, some intelligence reports did assess that there could be infiltration by some unruly individuals in the peaceful demonstration organized by the youth generation to try and provoke the situation, but there was no analysis that vandalism and arson could occur at the Federal Parliament building.

Instructions were given to remain restrained because youth and students of a specific age group would participate, and to facilitate the peaceful demonstration without using force if possible. According to the then chief of the District Police Range Kathmandu, SSP (now DIG) Bishwo Adhikari, the Inspector General of Police (IGP) Chandra Kuber Khapung had instructed not to use 'heavy deployment' or show too many security personnel since specific age-group youths were involved, but to keep manpower ready in close proximity and use sequential force according to law only if destructive and violent activities like breaking prohibited zones or vandalizing and burning public property occurred.

In the reporting of the National Investigation Department, it was suggested that three thousand to a maximum of five thousand individuals would gather for the demonstration on September 8. All security arrangements were moved forward based on this suggestion, which proved to be insufficient.
Integrated Special Security Plan

Following the announcement of the Gen-Z movement, the Chief District Officer (CDO) of Kathmandu directed the District Security Committee to strengthen security arrangements and coordinate with respective parent agencies for the timely mobilization of additional forces. Based on intelligence from the police administration, intelligence agencies, and district security bodies, the protest was initially perceived as a program to express dissatisfaction regarding social media regulations. Assuming it would remain peaceful in the early stages, the 'Integrated Special Security Plan, 2082' was approved, identifying potential routes, timing, estimated participants, and sensitive areas.

On September 7, 2025 (Bhadra 22, 2082), a meeting of the District Security Committee was held under the chairmanship of the Kathmandu CDO, including high-ranking officials from the Nepal Police, Armed Police Force, Nepal Army, and National Investigation Department. The committee approved the integrated security plan, which included the establishment of command posts, prohibited area cordons, rally escorts, intelligence collection, picketing, and roadblocking. The plan also specified that any manpower shortages would be fulfilled by requesting additional personnel from the relevant security agencies. Consequently, roles were specified under a three-tier security strategy.

content.content: Plan 'A': If the pre-announced program remains peaceful at the designated time and place, security forces will coordinate and collaborate with the organizers to facilitate security and traffic management.

Plan 'B': If there is an attempt to forcibly enter prohibited or sensitive areas, use of sticks or stones, obstruction of government work, vandalism of public or private property, or interference with security personnel, the strategy involves persuasion, informing the mob about legal consequences, and using a graduated application of force to take control.

Plan 'C': In case of fatal attacks on security personnel, armed clashes, forced entry into vital installations, arson, or a situation where casualties are imminent and control requires a curfew, force will be used per the Local Administration Act, 1971. This includes declaring a curfew and requesting Nepal Army mobilization upon a further decision by the District Security Committee.

content: Reflecting on the strategies prepared by government agencies, the meetings held on the evening of September 7 and the morning of September 8 (Bhadra 23) analyzed the peace and security situation in Kathmandu as 'satisfactory' at that time.
Peaceful Demonstration Turns into a Violent Mob

The protest, which began around 9:00 AM on September 8 and was expected to be peaceful, remained normal until 10:15 AM. However, from 11:00 AM onwards, the situation progressively deteriorated. By the time the protesters reached Bijuli Bazar, the gathering had transformed into a massive, unmanageable crowd.

Since it was anticipated that the crowd would only arrive from the Bijuli Bazar side, police barricades were placed only on the main road in front of the Everest Hotel; no barricades were placed on the service lanes. When protesters suddenly entered from all directions through these unguarded routes, the security management became extremely complex.

There was no visible effort to contain the crowd before it merged at Bijuli Bazar. After the failure of Plan 'A', Plan 'B' should have been implemented immediately, but this transition did not occur in real-time. Specifically, as the crowd size increased, the security presence failed to increase proportionately.

By 11:14 AM, when students and individuals from various locations reached the Everest Hotel in New Baneshwor, the protest had taken the form of a massive, agitated mob shouting provocative slogans. Administrative officials have admitted that the security forces present did not have sufficient strength to hold back such a large crowd at that location.
Breaching the Barricades and Entering Prohibited Zones

The crowd behind the front lines at the Everest Hotel began pushing forward while chanting slogans, causing the front-line protesters to crash into and break the police barricades. Protesters coming from the rear (the Bijuli Bazar side) also contributed to toppling the structures. Once the barricades were breached, the police were forced to retreat while deploying water cannons and tear gas, as the protesters pushed forward toward the Federal Parliament building.

Two major weaknesses were noted in the security response near the Everest Hotel. First, the security presence was weak; the deployment should have been increased based on situational assessment. Second, the barricades were placed only on the main road and were of a lightweight, outdated nature. Being only a single line of defense, they were easily bypassed via the service lanes, leaving the police caught in the middle of the crowda significant tactical failure.
Situation Around the Parliament Complex

Around 11:45 AM, as the massive, agitated mob pushed toward the Federal Parliament area while throwing stones, police used water cannons to control the situation. The available water cannon had the capacity to use two jets simultaneously, but doing so exhausts the water in just 3 minutes. CCTV footage shows the water cannon was used twice, but once empty, refilling was not immediately possible. Since the police had only one functional water cannon at the site, it became ineffective after two uses. Although the Armed Police Force had another water cannon at Baneshwor Chowk, it was moved to the South Gate without ever being deployed.

The ineffective use of water cannons further incited the young protesters instead of dispersing them. They began using stones, bricks, and concrete blocks found at the site to attack security forces. Some protesters used slingshots, axes, and other domestic tools, while others shouted incitements to 'capture' the Parliament building. Some even focused their attacks directly on the water cannon vehicle.

Following the failure of the water cannons, security personnel appeared somewhat panicked and began using less-lethal weapons such as batons, tear gas, and rubber bullets. The tear gas proved ineffective as protesters picked up the canisters with wet towels and threw them back at the security forces. As protesters became increasingly aggressive, both Nepal Police and Armed Police Force personnel inside and outside the complex felt they were losing control of the situation.

During this sensitive time, if field commanders had attempted to engage in dialogue with the youth instead of relying solely on force, the situation might have normalized. However, caught between the fear of being blamed for a mob breach and the escalating aggression of the protesters, security forces continued using batons, gas, and rubber bullets. There was a noticeable lack of a 'defusing mechanism' to de-escalate the anger of the young protesters.

As the peaceful demonstration turned into a riot, some organizers, including Sudan Gurung, President of the 'We Nepal' organization, reportedly announced the withdrawal of the movement after failing to calm the crowd. It was evident that the mob had moved far beyond the control of the original protest organizers.
Incitement by Motorcyclists and Escalation of the Mob (September 8)

At approximately 12:00 PM, around 100 motorcyclists arriving from Chabahil, Gaushala, and the Dhobikhola Corridor converged on the Baneshwor area. Some participants wore black T-shirts with 'TOB' (Tibetan Original Blood/The Original Brothers) markings. They actively incited the crowd by revving engines to create loud backfire noises and shouting provocative slogans. This led to a significant escalation, with the mob attacking security forces with stones, iron rods, and slingshots while attempting to breach the West Gate of the Federal Parliament.
The Declaration and Execution of Curfew Orders

The Chief District Officer (CDO) of Kathmandu issued the first curfew order for the Baneshwor area effective from 12:30 PM to 10:00 PM. Subsequent orders covered prohibited zones like Shital Niwas, Baluwatar, and Singha Durbar. The CDO justified these as a 'last resort' under Section 6(A) of the Local Administration Act, 1971. However, communication was a critical failure; police used small handheld megaphones that were drowned out by the massive crowd. Many young protesters, unfamiliar with the concept of a curfew, remained in the area unaware of the legal implications or the impending use of lethal force.
Breach of the Federal Parliament and Use of Force

Protesters successfully breached the Federal Parliament's perimeter, breaking walls in over 400 locations and scaling the South Gate. Arson attacks were launched against security posts and administrative rooms. Radio logs from a female police officer recorded distress calls stating, 'They are going to kill us,' as the mob closed in. Approximately 500 protesters managed to enter the complex before being repelled. Security leadership defended the use of lethal force, citing the 'Doctrine of Necessity' to prevent the total destruction of the Parliament building.
Legal Analysis and Findings of Excessive Force
The Commission's investigation identified several critical legal and procedural violations during the September 8 incident:
1. Premature Use of Force: CCTV footage from Civil Hospital shows injured victims arriving at 12:07 PM23 minutes before the curfew was legally in effect.

2. Indiscriminate Firing: Evidence of live rounds was found in high-rise offices (Hartford Education Center) and local cafes (Himalayan Java, Arabica Café). The shooting was not targeted at the mob's legs but was widespread, hitting individuals inside buildings.

3. Violation of the 'Below the Knee' Rule: Post-mortem reports confirm that most of the 17 immediate fatalities were caused by gunshot wounds to the upper body (chest and head), violating Section 6(1)(B) of the Local Administration Act.

4. Ballistic Accountability: There is a complete lack of records regarding weapon assignments and ammunition expenditure. Both the Nepal Police and the Armed Police Force (APF) failed to provide ballistic audit logs, citing the destruction of records during the subsequent day's arson.
Ammunition Expenditure Summary (September 8)
Reports indicate the following rounds were fired by security forces in the Kathmandu Valley on the first day of the uprising:

* Lethal (Metal) Bullets: 52 Rounds
* Rubber Bullets: 417 Rounds
* Blank Rounds: 117 Rounds
* Tear Gas Shells: 1,017 Rounds (Many were found to be expired and ineffective)
Failure to Preserve Evidence in the Baneshwor Incident (September 8)

During the clashes on September 8 (Bhadra 23), security forces utilized lethal weapons. Standard operational procedure requires the immediate securing of ballistic evidence, such as spent shell casings. However, no such record was maintained. Several factors contributed to this failure: the high-tension environment that persisted until evening, the prioritization of 'situation control' over forensic collection, and the potential contamination of the site by journalists, protesters, and pedestrians.

The Commission notes a troubling lack of accountability. Weapons used that day were not sealed for forensic examination, and no detailed inventory exists regarding which officers checked out specific arms or the exact number of rounds fired. When questioned, the Inspectors General of both the Nepal Police and the Armed Police Force (APF) were unable to provide these specifics. This absence of evidence effectively hinders independent investigations by the judiciary, media, and human rights organizations, raising suspicions that the failure to preserve evidence may have been intentional to avoid self-incrimination.
The TOB Group (Tibetan Original Blood / The Original Brothers)

The TOB group gained visibility in mid-2024 through social media platforms like Instagram and TikTok. Originally standing for 'Tibetan Original Blood,' the term served as a cultural tag for youth expressing pride in their Tibetan heritage and identity. Over time, the group rebranded as 'The Original Brothers,' evolving primarily into a motorcycle/biker community comprising diverse ethnicities.

While the group was labeled by some as a separatist or foreign-funded violent entity, recent police investigations have clarified that TOB lacks a political or separatist agenda. However, the Commission found that during the September 8 protests, TOB members on motorcycles actively incited and agitated the crowd near Chabahil and Bijulibazar. This incitement played a role in escalating the violence, and such actions cannot be dismissed as mere cultural expression.
The Gen-Z Movement and Youth Council Involvement

The Gen-Z movement is characterized as a group of urban, educated youth focused on issues of governance and anti-corruption. Similarly, the 'Youth Council,' an NGO supported by foreign funding, promotes leadership, accountability, and civic engagement. While the goals of the two groups overlap, the Commission found no evidence that the Youth Council participated in the protests as an organization. Instead, some members joined the demonstrations in an individual capacity.

Despite public suspicion and Nepal's sensitive geopolitical position, no concrete evidence was found to prove that the Gen-Z movement was directed or funded by foreign powers. However, the lack of transparency in NGO funding and weak government regulation continues to fuel such suspicions.
Escalation of Violence on September 9 (Bhadra 24)

While the September 8 protests were targeted at corruption, the events of September 9 were fueled by public outrage over the 29 deaths (17 immediate, 12 subsequent) caused by police firing. Social media played a dual role: it spread awareness of the 'state repression,' but also served as a tool for criminal elements to coordinate looting, arson, and jailbreaks.

A significant 'Security Vacuum' was created when police units were ordered to prioritize personal safety and the security of their arms over defending their posts if overwhelmed. This led to many units abandoning their stations. In the chaos, 39 police units in the Kathmandu Valley alone were looted or burned. Tragically, three police officerstwo in Maharajgunj and one in Koteshworwere brutally lynched by mobs.

The decision to withdraw, while intended to minimize human casualties, backfired by emboldening criminal psychology. Mobs felt they had achieved 'victory' over the state, leading to targeted attacks on the Federal Parliament, Singha Durbar, and private commercial interests. The failure to implement a Unified Command or a prioritized Asset Protection List allowed the crisis to spiral into a nationwide breakdown of law and order until the eventual military intervention.
Participants in the September 9 (Bhadra 24) Attacks

The investigation reveals that the nature of the protests shifted dramatically between September 8 and 9. While the initial protests on September 8 in Kathmandu were largely peaceful demonstrations against corruption and poor governance, the situation escalated into extreme violence following police firing and high casualties. By September 9, the movement was infiltrated by criminal elements.

Analysis of CCTV footage and witness interviews identified a diverse range of participants involved in looting, arson, and attacks on security personnel. These included street vendors, scrap collectors, garage and workshop workers, drivers and helpers from the transport sector, construction workers from outside the valley, and residents of informal settlements (sukumbasi). Notably, individuals who escaped from prisons and police custody during the chaos were also active participants. While some 'Gen Z' youth were present, many were swept up in the mob mentality without a clear objective, leading to the destruction of both private and public property.

The attacks on Vital Installations such as Singha Durbar, the Federal Parliament, the Supreme Court, and Shital Niwas (President's Office) showed signs of coordinated sabotage. Attackers followed a specific pattern: first destroying CCTV cameras, then disabling water tanks and fire extinguishers, smashing IT/Computer centers to destroy records, and finally using gas cylinders or Petrol Bombs (Molotov Cocktails) to set buildings ablaze. In some instances, looters were trapped inside burning commercial buildings like Bhat-Bhateni and perished.
Status of Nakkhu Jail and Other Prisons

On September 9, prisons became primary targets. At Nakkhu Jail, a massive crowd gathered demanding the release of Rabi Lamichhane, Chairman of the Rastriya Swatantra Party, who was in judicial custody. To ensure his safety from the mob, prison administration moved him to the onsite mental hospital. After Lamichhane addressed the crowd and expressed responsibility for the staff's safety, the Jailer reportedly signed a release letter under duress. Following his departure, the mob stormed the prison, leading to a chain reaction of jailbreaks across the country.

According to prison administration, a total of 12,440 inmates escaped nationwide on that day. As of January 2026, 8,294 have returned or been re-arrested, but 4,146 remains at large, posing a significant ongoing security threat to society.
Role of the Nepal Army on September 8 and 9

The role of the Nepal Army during these two days has faced intense scrutiny. On September 8, despite the District Security Committee requesting Army assistance as per Section 6(2) of the Local Administration Act, 1971, the deployment was delayed. A unit from the Narasingha Dal at Singha Durbar was reportedly blocked by protesters sitting under their truck tires, and the troops did not enter the Parliament compound until 3:00 PM, by which time significant casualties had already occurred.

On September 9, the Army's role was described as passive during attacks on the nation's most sensitive landmarks. At Shital Niwas, although the Army is responsible for the President's security, officers failed to effectively resist protesters who breached the eastern gate, leading to the arson of the historic archaeological building and its documents. Similarly, at Singha Durbar and the Prime Minister's Residence (Baluwatar), CCTV footage shows troops standing by or withdrawing as mobs looted and burned buildings. Army officials defended these actions by citing the principle of 'Life over Property', arguing that using lethal force would have resulted in a massacre.

Despite these failures in protecting infrastructure, the Army was instrumental in high-risk rescues, including using helicopters to extract police officers trapped on the roof of the Maharajgunj Circle. It is widely acknowledged that the full mobilization of the Nepal Army after 10:00 PM on September 9following the Prime Minister's resignationsaved the country from total collapse and prevented further loss of life on September 10.
Former Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli

In Nepal's executive system, the Prime Minister serves as the pivotal figure and chief executive. As such, he bears ultimate responsibility for the actions and failures of agencies under his direct supervision. Under his tenure, the National Investigation Department (NID) was moved from the Ministry of Home Affairs to the Office of the Prime Minister and Council of Ministers to ensure direct oversight. However, the Commission found that the PM's office failed to empower the NID with the necessary cyber-intelligence tools and analytical capabilities. This led to the disastrously inaccurate intelligence report on September 8 (Bhadra 23), which predicted only 3,000–5,000 protesters when the actual turnout was significantly higher.

The Commission notes that following the violence on September 8, the National Security Council (NSC) meeting chaired by the PM resulted only in informal verbal discussions rather than a decisive, written plan of action for the following day. This lack of gravity contributed to the unchecked chaos of September 9 (Bhadra 24). Furthermore, the decision to lift social media restrictions on the evening of the 8th was made via a verbal order from the Communications Minister without a formal risk assessment. This move, intended to appease youth demands, ignored the high probability of further mobilization and violence.

Critically, despite receiving real-time reports of the four-hour conflict in Baneshwor on September 8, the Prime Minister did not intervene to coordinate a 'Cease Fire' or order the 'Demonstrative Deployment' of the Nepal Army in coordination with Army Headquarters. Such high-level intervention could have de-escalated the tension and significantly reduced the death toll.
Former Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak

The Home Minister is responsible for maintaining national peace and ensuring the effectiveness of security agencies. While Minister Lekhak made some attempts at legal reforms, no concrete results were achieved. The Commission attributes the tragedy to a lack of training in crowd control, the absence of modern non-lethal equipment, and the unnecessary use of lethal force. As the political head of the ministry, Lekhak is held accountable for these systemic failures.

The investigation highlighted that during the peak of the clashes on September 8, the Home Minister remained occupied in a State Affairs Committee meeting, suggesting a lack of urgency regarding the developing crisis. He failed to issue a 'Cease Fire' order or coordinate effectively between the Nepal Police and the Armed Police Force (APF), despite both agencies admitting to a massive breakdown in operational tactics. Like the Prime Minister, he failed to leverage the 'Demonstrative Effect' of the Army to normalize the situation, which could have prevented many casualties.
Former Home Secretary Gokarna Mani Duwadi

As the administrative head of the Home Ministry, the Secretary is tasked with policy formulation and solving coordination issues between the Nepal Police and APF. Secretary Duwadi was informed by the Chief District Officer (CDO) of the escalating situation in Baneshwor but failed to take active measures to normalize the crisis. Instead of mobilizing additional support or advising the Home Minister on immediate risk mitigation (Harm Minimize), he remained a passive observer during the four-hour conflict, effectively distancing himself from his professional duties.
Former Chief District Officer (CDO) of Kathmandu, Chhabiraj Rijal

Under the Local Administration Act, 1971, the CDO has the authority to declare a curfew, but his responsibility does not end there. CDO Rijal failed to adequately monitor the situation post-declaration. He did not ensure a sufficient security presence at the Everest Hotel barricade and failed to manage non-lethal resources like water cannons and tear gas effectively. Even as the area turned into a 'battlefield' for four hours, he took no initiative to coordinate a 'Cease Fire' or deploy reinforcements strategically. His failure to issue effective instructions to protect both public property and human lives resulted in 17 immediate deaths and a significant loss of public trust in the administration.
Armed Police Force (APF) Chief Raju Aryal

As the head of the Armed Police Force (APF) Nepal, IGP Raju Aryal is responsible for providing modern training and integrating new technologies into the force. During the September 8 (Bhadra 23) incident, APF personnel were initially deployed to contain protesters. Although APF officials claimed only 'warning shots' were fired using SLR and INSAS rifles, evidence collected by the Commission confirms that the use of force was both excessive and indiscriminate.

A critical failure noted is the absence of a clear Rules of Engagement (ROE). Without an ROE, field commanders face ambiguity, leading to confusion among personnel and a breakdown in the chain of command. An effective ROE establishes standards for necessity, proportionality, and the 'last resort' principle, which are vital for reducing casualties and protecting the organization from human rights allegations. IGP Aryal failed to issue clear directives to implement such a system, contributing to the disproportionate violence.

Furthermore, coordination was severely lacking. CCTV footage and testimony from Nepal Police commanders reveal that the APF failed to provide full support at the Everest Hotel barricade. On September 9 (Bhadra 24), this lack of support became a nationwide trend; in a representative incident at the New Baneshwor Police Circle bridge, APF personnel reportedly stood by and watched as protesters attacked Nepal Police officers. IGP Aryal’s failure to provide clear instructions and effective coordination led to widespread looting and arson across the country.

The Commission also finds IGP Aryal's conduct irresponsible regarding accountability. He failed to provide individual records of arms and ammunition used by his personnel despite repeated requests, hindering the identification of those who fired lethal shots. Additionally, the APF refused to provide 'Voice Logs' of radio communications, likely to avoid self-incrimination. The loss of APF weaponry to protesters on September 9 also highlights a failure to evaluate risks or neutralize sensitive materials before they were looted.
Former National Investigation Department (NID) Chief Hutraj Thapa

The NID is the backbone of national security, tasked with identifying threats and providing accurate intelligence to state agencies. However, on September 8, the NID estimated only 3,000 to 5,000 protesters, a figure that was vastly lower than the actual turnout. This massive gap between intelligence and reality represents a significant professional failure.

Chief Thapa attempted to excuse this by citing a lack of budget for cyber technology and legal hurdles in tracking digital dissent. However, the Commission finds that the department failed to even monitor open-source platforms like TikTok, X (Twitter), and YouTube, which were being used extensively by the 'Gen Z' movement. This shows a dereliction of his professional duty.

Perhaps most critically, when the government abruptly lifted the social media ban on the night of September 8 without consulting security agencies, Chief Thapa remained silent. He failed to exercise leadership by warning the government of the imminent security risks this posed. This silence allowed the situation to escalate into the nationwide violence and destruction seen on September 9. The NID, under his leadership, failed to accurately assess the environment, leading to a catastrophe for the nation's people and property.
Former Inspector General of Police (IGP) Chandra Kuber Khapung

As the highest-ranking officer in the Nepal Police command structure, the IGP bears the ultimate responsibility for organizational effectiveness and public trust. The investigation confirmed through official timelines that on September 8 (Bhadra 23) at 12:40 PM, IGP Khapung issued a directive over the police communication set stating: 'Curfew is already in effect; no need to wait for further orders, use necessary force.' This signal effectively authorized field officers to escalate to lethal force, contradicting his statement to the Commission that he had no role in the process once a curfew is declared.

The high casualty count (17 dead on the day, 12 succumbing later) highlights a failure to learn from past incidents where non-combatants and bystanders were hit. IGP Khapung failed to take the initiative to implement a policy where only the most skilled marksmen are permitted to carry firearms during protests. Furthermore, there was a gross lack of investment in modern non-lethal equipment such as pepper spray, sticky foam, or Tasers. The absence of these tools forced personnel to rely on lethal weapons prematurely.

Despite having access to intelligence via Discord and other social media, the IGP failed to mobilize his information network to monitor the 'Gen Z' movement effectively. His attempt to shift blame onto the District Security Committee for planning failures is seen as an evasion of responsibility. On September 9 (Bhadra 24), his 'blanket order' to release detainees and focus on 'tactical withdrawal' severely damaged police morale, leading to the abandonment of posts and the looting of state weaponry. He must take responsibility for the atmosphere of surrender that resulted in 3 police deaths and widespread arson.
Former AIG and Current IGP Dan Bahadur Karki

On the morning of September 9, Dan Bahadur Karki took charge of the Kathmandu Valley Police Office (Ranipokhari). While he was active in high-level security meetings at Baluwatar and Army Headquarters, his strategic decisions are under scrutiny. Under the direction of the then-IGP, Karki relayed the 'Victor Control' order to release detainees and secure weapons as stations were being overrun.

The Commission notes that while the situation was dire, the 'blanket' nature of this order caused many officers to abandon their posts prematurely. A more professional approach would have been to issue orders for 'Tactical Withdrawal' only as a last resort when ammunition was exhausted and reinforcements were impossible. His failure to conduct an internal investigation into officers who abandoned their duty or to prosecute those who let weapons fall into the hands of rioters under Section 36 of the Police Act, 1955, indicates a lack of accountability.
AIG Siddhi Bikram Shah

As the Chief of Operations and the ultimate commander of the Special Task Force (STF), AIG Shah's claim that his role was limited because the IGP was giving direct orders to the field is viewed as unprofessional. He failed to advise the IGP against breaking the chain of command. Furthermore, as the head of the department responsible for the STF, he failed to ensure they had clear Rules of Engagement (ROE) and scenario-based training. This lack of guidance contributed to the four-hour-long indiscriminate firing. However, the Commission recommends he be rewarded for his leadership in successfully defending the Police Headquarters at Naxal from attackers on September 9.
APF AIG Narayan Datta Paudel

AIG Paudel, as the Operation Chief of the Armed Police Force, claimed his forces only fired 19 warning shots in the air. This is factually inconsistent with autopsy reports showing numerous protesters killed by gunshot wounds. He failed to play a role in 'Damage Control' during the peak of the violence and did not issue any 'Cease Fire' instructions. Like his counterparts, his failure to implement ROE and ensure coordination with Nepal Police on September 9 led to significant state losses.
DIG Om Bahadur Rana and DIG Bishwa Adhikari

DIG Rana (then Acting Chief of Ranipokhari) was heard by the CDO ordering units to 'open fire to take control as necessary' after the curfew. He made no effort to monitor the casualties or stop the lethal force during the four-hour conflict.

DIG Bishwa Adhikari (then SSP of Kathmandu District) is held responsible for the weak initial deployment at the Everest Hotel barricade. His strategy of 'low visibility' backfired when the crowd outnumbered the police. He failed to clear the tall buildings in the Baneshwor area for security and admitted to not implementing a Layered Defense system. His claim that he only learned of the deaths at 2:30 PM reveals a massive failure in his communication and intelligence channels.
Armed Police Force (APF) Deputy Inspector General Suresh Kumar Shrestha

As the Valley Chief of the APF, DIG Shrestha failed to play a significant role in damage control during the four-hour clash on September 8 (Bhadra 23). A more serious failure occurred on September 9, when weapons and ammunition were looted from the Min Bhawan Security Base. The Commission questions why highly sensitive materials were not secured or, if evacuation was impossible, neutralized/destroyed to prevent them from falling into criminal hands. Furthermore, DIG Shrestha failed to conduct an internal investigation into the command failures that led to this loss of state weaponry.

The Commission emphasizes that for an armed organization, having clear Rules of Engagement (ROE) and scenario-based training is essential. Without an ROE, field commanders face ambiguity, leading to disproportionate force, breakdown in the chain of command, and human rights violations. The lack of initiative to implement such standards led to the indiscriminate use of force on September 8.
National Investigation Department (NID) Officials: Krishna Prasad Khanal and Riben Kumar Gachhedar

Both the Valley Division Chief (Khanal) and the Kathmandu District Chief (Gachhedar) failed in their fundamental duty of intelligence gathering. They were unaware of how Gen Z protesters used digital platforms to coordinate. Despite their claims of being 'diligent,' their failure to provide accurate situational assessments meant other security agencies could not formulate effective strategies, leading to high casualties.
SSP Deep Shamsher J.B.R. (Nepal Police)

SSP J.B.R. served as the commander inside the Federal Parliament premises. His claim that police only used non-lethal weapons and fired in self-defense is contradicted by autopsy reports and victim testimony. He failed to implement a layered defense system or coordinate the 400+ personnel under his command. His inability to order a 'Cease Fire' during the four-hour shooting outside the gates demonstrates a lack of professional leadership.
SP Rishiram Kandel (STF) and SP Jivan K.C. (APF)

SP Kandel (Special Task Force) denied using live fire, a claim refuted by hospital records. He also failed to ensure that STF communication sets were compatible with local police units. SP Jivan K.C., the overall APF commander for Kathmandu, failed to coordinate with Nepal Police. His claim that his forces only conducted 'warning shots' is logically inconsistent with the high number of gunshot casualties found in the field.
Analysis of Liability and Recommendations for Prosecution

The Commission finds that the 'indiscriminate force' used resulted in irreparable human loss. To prevent future impunity, those responsiblefrom the Prime Minister to field commandersmust face legal consequences. Under Article 16 of the Constitution, the state has a duty to protect life; instead, state actions on September 8 and 9 led to its destruction.

Despite the shooting lasting four hours and hospitals being overwhelmed, no senior officialincluding the PM, Home Minister, or Home Secretaryissued orders to stop the fire. This indifference by the 'guardians of the people' is characterized as Criminal Negligence. Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak’s statement that he 'discussed' the matter with the PM and Army Chief is insufficient, as no concrete steps were taken to restore peace.

Regarding the defense of 'necessity' (protecting state buildings or police lives), the Commission notes that almost all victims were shot outside the boundary walls on public sidewalks. No protesters who breached the initial line were shot; force was applied to those already outside or even watching from nearby buildings (e.g., the shop owner in Sunari and a tailor in Baneshwor). The 'self-defense' argument is therefore invalid.

The Commission also highlights a massive failure on September 9. By lifting the social media ban on the night of the 8th without a security assessment, Communication Minister Prithvi Subba Gurung and Chief Secretary Eknarayan Aryal allowed violence to spread nationwide. The government's 'haphazard' decision-making turned a local protest into a national disaster involving arson and looting.

Finally, the Commission notes that the Prime Minister's attempt to shift all blame onto 'local administration' is unacceptable. The policy of suppression and the subsequent negligence in managing the aftermath directly caused the national catastrophe.
National Legal Provisions and Arrangements

Section 6 of the National Penal Code, 2017 (2074 BS) establishes a general principle of criminal justice: an act performed in accordance with the law or deemed excusable by law shall not be considered an offense. A critical question arises whether security personnel and government officials involved in the September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082) incident can claim full immunity under this provision.

Section 24 provides for the Right to Private Defense, granting every individual the right to protect their own or another's life and property from unlawful harm. However, this right is contingent upon a 'reasonable belief' that immediate action is necessary to prevent such harm.

Conversely, Section 25 restricts this right, stating that the right to private defense is not available in various circumstances, specifically prohibiting the use of force exceeding what is 'reasonably necessary' under Section 25(3).

While Section 26(1) generally prohibits taking a life during private defense, Section 26(2)(f) provides an exception for security personnel. It states that if a security officer, acting under the orders of a competent authority to protect individuals or public/government property, must immediately retaliate against an attack, a resulting death shall not be considered a crime. This aligns with the Essential Goods Protection Act, 1952, which suggests a graduated responseaiming below the knee to incapacitate. Firing with the intent to kill is strictly a last resort.

In the case of the protester deaths outside the Parliament premises on September 8, the evidence fails to confirm that the government side acted within the lawful bounds of the Right to Private Defense as defined in Section 26(2).

The Commission notes that the right to life provided by Article 16 of the Constitution and the freedom of peaceful assembly under Article 17 were effectively suspended by the government's actions on September 8. Despite public allegations regarding the shooting of minors, the then-Prime Minister and Home Minister continue to deny issuing such orders.

Legally, the Local Administration Act, 1971 (2028 BS) grants only the Chief District Officer (CDO) the authority to order firing. However, the Prime Minister and Home Minister maintain direct control over the Ministry of Home Affairs and security agencies. Despite Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak reportedly informing the Prime Minister once the shooting began at 12:15 PM, no effort was made by the executive leadership to stop the four-hour-long gunfire, even as hospitals like Civil, Trauma Center, and Bir were overwhelmed with victims.

The investigation revealed that at 12:40 PM on September 8, then-IGP Chandra Kuber Khapung issued an order via the communication set to 'use necessary force' without waiting for formal curfew orders. Following this, the Kathmandu District Police Chief and Acting Chief of Valley Police DIG Om Bahadur Rana also ordered force. While the IGP claimed in his statement that he had no legal role in ordering force, his direct commands over the radio preceded the escalation to lethal fire.

On September 9, the situation worsened as police offices were attacked and weapons looted. The leadership's orders to 'remain safe' and use 'only tear gas'including instructions to release detaineesresulted in a breakdown of morale and state authority, leaving the country in a state of virtual lawlessness.

As the head of state and the ultimate recipient of intelligence via the National Investigation Department (NID), the Prime Minister is presumed to have been aware of the 17 deaths and thousands of injuries. The failure to intervene despite this knowledge points toward significant executive negligence.
International Human Rights Law Context

content.content: The incident violated Articles 19 (Freedom of Expression) and 20(1) (Right to Peaceful Assembly) of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), 1948.
It violated Articles 6 (Right to Life), 19 (Freedom of Expression), and 21 (Right to Peaceful Assembly) of the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR), 1966.

content: Furthermore, the state failed to uphold the Directive Principles under Article 51 of the Constitution of Nepal, particularly regarding the protection of human rights and the maintenance of the rule of law. According to UN Human Rights Committee General Comment No. 31, Nepal has failed to meet the following established obligations:
General Comment No. 31 [80]
This comment outlines the nature of the general legal obligations imposed on States Parties to the Covenant, adopted on March 29, 2004. Key requirements include:

content.content: Preventing violations committed by security forces.
Conducting prompt and independent investigations into killings.
Initiating prosecution or disciplinary action against those found guilty.
Providing effective remedies to the families of victims.

content: Since the head of the government at the time of the incident (the Prime Minister) resigned and left office, that specific administration was unable to conduct the investigation. The subsequent government began prosecutions for the September 9 (Bhadra 24) events, and this Inquiry Commission was immediately formed. It is critical to note that the legal obligations mentioned above apply even if the Prime Minister, Home Minister, or other high-ranking officials did not directly order the use of force or the shooting.

The investigation reveals that security forces directly violated these provisions. While the government provided free medical treatment and reached financial assistance agreements with victims' families and the 'Gen Z' protesters (including a 10-point agreement and the formation of a Gen Z Council), there are discrepancies in administrative records. Although it was claimed that the K.P. Oli cabinet decided on September 8 to form a judicial inquiry commission, no such decision was reflected in the official minutes of that day's meeting.

Public skepticism remains high regarding the government's investigation style. This Commission officially informed the government on October 7, 2025, that criminal investigations and prosecutions should not wait for the Commission's final report. However, the release of suspects on bail without proper prosecution has been viewed as a mockery of the rule of law and indicates gross government negligence.

When executive heads fail to prevent foreseeable harm, control subordinates, or ensure accountability, they violate their fundamental duty to protect citizens. Human Rights Watch commented on the September 8 incident, stating that Nepal police used 'excessive, unlawful, and lethal force,' resulting in at least 19 deaths and hundreds of injuries during protests against corruption and a social media ban.
Failures of the State During the September 8 Incident at Parliament Premises:

content.content: The majority of protesters were unarmed.
Lethal weapons and metal bullets were used indiscriminately.
Effective warnings were not given before the use of weapons, and non-lethal measures (water cannons, batons, barricades, warning shots, or dialogue) were not sufficiently or correctly applied.
There was a complete collapse of human rights accountability across the police, armed police, army, administrative, and executive leadership.
The Special Task Force (STF) of the Nepal Police failed to follow established 'Rules of Engagement' required for armed personnel.

content: Questions of Accountability for September 8 (Bhadra 23):

While the protest began peacefully, it escalated at the south gate of the Parliament. However, data from Civil Hospital shows the first gunshot victim arrived at 12:07 PM, and others followed by 12:15 PMbefore a curfew was even declared. By early afternoon, news of deaths and hundreds of injuries had already reached the media.

Despite this, no state organ (District Security Committee, Central Security Committee, or National Security Council) took steps during the day to stop the firing or prevent further human loss. There was an apparent lack of consultation between the CDO, Home Secretary, IGPs, NID Chief, Home Minister, and Prime Minister regarding damage control. This collective indifference makes both the political and administrative leadership fully responsible for the casualties on that day.

Responsible leaders like the Prime Minister and Home Minister cannot claim a lack of 'direct command' as a defense. Under constitutional and international human rights standards, leadership is held responsible because the outcomes were foreseeable and the government failed to control its armed forces.

Under the mandate provided to this Commission, it has been observed that the events of September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), involving the 'Gen Z' youth protest, resulted in human rights violations alongside the tragic loss of life. It is the core duty of this Commission to uncover the truth regarding these incidents and the subsequent human casualties.

Human rights are the essential rights required for a human being to live with dignity. These are considered inherent, birthright, and inalienable. They encompass civil, political, economic, social, and cultural dimensions. The Human Rights Commission Act, 1997 (2053 BS) defines these as 'rights relating to life, liberty, equality, and dignity of the individual guaranteed by the Constitution and other prevailing laws, as well as rights embodied in international treaties to which Nepal is a party.' Regardless of whether they are called natural, fundamental, or constitutional rights, their focal point remains human value and the right to life.

The Constitution of Nepal provides a robust framework for these rights. Under Part 3 (Articles 16 to 45), 31 fundamental rights are guaranteed, with Article 46 providing for constitutional remedies if these rights are violated. Key protections include: Article 17 (Right to Freedom, including peaceful assembly without arms), Article 18 (Right to Equality), Article 20 (Rights relating to Justice), Article 27 (Right to Information), and Article 16 (Right to live with Dignity).

Furthermore, the National Human Rights Commission Act (2012), the Civil Rights Act (1955), and various child and prison acts support these protections. Nepal is also a party to 18 international human rights treaties. Under Section 9 of the Nepal Treaty Act, 1990, treaty provisions prevail if they conflict with national laws, effectively making international human rights standards part of Nepal's domestic legal landscape.

There is no dispute that the 'Gen Z' generation organized the protest on September 8, 2025, against corruption and the social media ban, exercising their fundamental right to peaceful assembly under Article 17(2)(b) of the Constitution.

Preliminary evaluation of reports from the National Human Rights Commission suggests that state forces committed excesses against mostly unarmed protesters. Security forces used widespread gunfire and baton charges, resulting in significant casualties.
Casualty and Incident Summary

A total of 37 protesters died in the Kathmandu Valley alone. On September 8, 17 individuals were killed outside the Parliament premises, and 2 others were killed outside the capital. Twelve others who were injured that day succumbed to their wounds later. In total, 1,084 protesters were injured by gunfire or other means. On the side of the security forces, 1,618 police personnel were injured, and 3 were brutally killed by protesters.

Security forces expended 7,873 rounds of ammunition, including 1,081 rounds from SLR semi-automatic weapons. Autopsy reports confirm deaths were caused by fatal shots to the head, chest, and abdomen. Victims included teenagers from the Gen Z generation. Analysis of audio-visual materials indicates that extreme suppression occurred under the guise of enforcing curfews and prohibited zones.

The Commission notes that accountability for state actions rests with the highest level of policy-makers and executive leadership. While former PM K.P. Sharma Oli and former Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak claimed they did not order the shooting and were unaware of it, CDO Chhabiraj Rijal testified that the Home Minister had instructed him to use 'force in a sequence' to control the situation.
Legal Framework for Accountability: Recklessness and Negligence

The Commission finds that the actions of certain high-level officials attract charges under the National Penal Code, 2017 (2074 BS). Specifically, their behavior is categorized as 'Reckless' (Section 181) and 'Negligent' (Section 182).

Recklessness (लापरबाही) is defined as acting with total disregard for the dangerous consequences or risks to the life and safety of others. Judicial precedents, such as *Duckers v. Lynch*, describe it as an indifference to the results of one's actions.

Negligence (हेलचेक्र्याई) refers to the failure to exercise the care that a reasonably prudent person would show in similar circumstances. Per *Jacob Mathew v. State of Punjab*, three components are required: 1) A legal duty of care, 2) A breach of that duty, and 3) Consequential damage or death.

Based on the gravity of their involvement and the failure to perform their official duties during the four-hour shooting outside the Parliament, the Commission recommends legal action and prosecution against the following individuals:

content.content: Then-Prime Minister Mr. K.P. Sharma Oli
Then-Home Minister Mr. Ramesh Lekhak
Then-Inspector General of Police Mr. Chandra Kuber Khapung

content: The responsibility lies with the authorities to ensure that the use of ultimate force (firing metal bullets) in anti-government or any protests is adopted only as a last resort.

There was a deliberate violation of duty. Evidence collected by the Commission shows that despite hours of live firing, no efforts were made to stop it, reflecting a failure to adhere to the principles of Proportionality and Necessity. No 'Corrective Orders' were issued to halt the firing of metal bullets. Theoretically, it is unacceptable for high-ranking officials to evade responsibility by claiming they were unaware of the 17 deaths outside the Parliament premises on September 8. Even in peaceful processions organized under the constitutional guarantee of safety and the right to unarmed assembly, excessive force was deployed.

Nepal operates under a parliamentary system, which is a prime ministerial system. It was a significant failure on the part of the Prime Minister, as the guardian of all citizens, to not intervene and prevent further human loss while shooting continued for nearly four hours outside the Parliament, resulting in several deaths and hundreds of injuries.

During the inquiry, then-Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli did not answer all questions related to the incident. He was uncooperative, stating that the questions were more suited for SEE (secondary level) students or field-level police officers.

Then-Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak and Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli failed to take initiatives to prevent high casualties on the afternoon of September 8. Although a meeting was called at 2:00 PM on that day, the National Security Council meeting only took place after 10:00 PM, by which time significant loss of life had already occurred at 1:00 PM, and nearby hospitals were filled with hundreds of gunshot victims.

Based on the analysis, while there may not have been a prior intent to commit murder, the negligence and failure of the security forces to stop firing after the initial casualties led to the deaths of several individuals, including teenagers.
Therefore, the leadership is held responsible for the deaths of these individuals due to recklessness and negligence during the September 8 incident.

On September 8, unlike the events of September 9, there were no instances of protesters attacking police, snatching weapons, or setting the Parliament building on fire. Protesters in public areas had thrown bricks and stones and set fire to a room near the south gate, but they had not advanced to burn the building. Security forces should have adopted alternative procedures. No steps were taken to maintain peace through other means once the firing and casualties began.

As a result, 17 people died that day, and 12 others injured at the site died later. The events of September 8 triggered the violence of September 9, leading to a total of 76 deaths. The burning of the country, looting, and high death toll were direct consequences of the government's failure to stop the firing on the 8th and its failure to take preventive security measures for the 9th. The reckless and negligent behavior of the Prime Minister and other officials in not stopping the gunfire led to excessive human loss.

Regarding the deaths caused by police fire outside the Baneshwor Parliament premises on September 8, Section 181(1) of the Penal Code, 2017 (2074 BS) prohibits killing anyone through a reckless act. Section 181(2) stipulates that such acts, if not covered under Sections 177 (intent), 178 (knowledge of probable death), or 179 (provocation), constitute a crime. The three mentioned high-ranking officials showed 'Culpable Negligence' under Section 181 and 'Negligence' under Section 182 by allowing the shooting to continue for four hours.

Consequently, it has been decided to recommend that the Government of Nepal investigate and prosecute former Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli, former Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak, and former IGP Chandra Kuber Khapung under Section 181(1) and Section 182 of the Penal Code, 2017.
Recommendation for Action Against Home Secretary and Three Others under Section 182

The roles, lapses, and negligence of political, administrative, and security heads regarding the September 8 Parliament premises incident have been discussed. The following four officials must be held accountable for their specific roles and responsibilities.

content.content: Then-Home Secretary Mr. Gokarna Mani Duwadi
Inspector General of Armed Police Force Mr. Raju Aryal
Then-Chief of National Investigation Department Mr. Hutraj Thapa
Then-Chief District Officer of Kathmandu Mr. Chhabilal Rijal

content: These officials are found to have been involved in the negligence that led to the deaths. They remained indifferent to using their administrative authority to stop the four-hour clash and indirectly supported the use of force. They failed to take any initiative to restore peace even after the first deaths occurred.

The investigation shows that the Executive Head, the Home Minister, and these officials failed to exercise the necessary caution, thereby consenting to and assisting in the loss of life through negligence. This falls under the crime of 'Negligence' as defined by Section 182 of the Penal Code, 2017.

Therefore, the Commission recommends the Government of Nepal to investigate and prosecute Mr. Gokarna Mani Duwadi, Mr. Raju Aryal, Mr. Hutraj Thapa, and Mr. Chhabilal Rijal under Section 182(1) of the Penal Code, 2017, for the reckless and negligent acts that resulted in the deaths of several people, including teenagers.
Regarding the current IGP Mr. Dan Bahadur Karki (then AIG at the Kathmandu Valley Police Office during the September 9 incident):

On September 8, he was an AIG at the Human Resource Development Department. Following that day's events, the Cabinet transferred him to the vacant AIG post at the Valley Police Office. He assumed duty at 9:00 AM on September 9. He was then called to the Ministry of Home Affairs and spent the day with the IGP and other security heads, eventually moving to the Army Headquarters.

When the movement turned violent and police circles were attacked, he issued orders via his 'Victor Control' set to prepare lists of detainees and release them if necessary to ensure the safety of police personnel, weapons, and vehicles, and instructed using no force other than tear gas. This was not a 'blanket' order. He also facilitated the helicopter rescue of police officers in Maharajgunj after three personnel were brutally killed. Since he acted under the direct instructions of IGP Chandra Kuber Khapung while stationed at Army HQ, he cannot be held solely responsible for these orders. However, he is cited for failing to ensure that weaponry was properly secured or decommissioned before falling into the hands of criminals.
For these lapses, the Commission recommends the Government of Nepal to take departmental action against IGP Mr. Dan Bahadur Karki under Rule 109 of the Police Rules, 2014 (2071 BS).
Recommendations for Action: Nepal Police, Armed Police Force, and NID

The following officials of the Nepal Police are recommended for disciplinary action under Section 9(4) of the Police Act, 1955 and Rule 109 of the Police Rules, 2014 for their failure to perform their duties on September 8 and 9:

content.content: AIG Mr. Siddhi Bikram Shah, Operations Head, Police HQ
DIG Mr. Om Bahadur Rana, Then-Acting Chief of Valley Police
DIG Mr. Bishwa Adhikari, Then-Chief of Kathmandu District Police
SSP Mr. Deep Shamsher J.B.R., Valley Police Office
SP Mr. Rishiiram Kandel, Special Task Force, Parliament House

content: Recommendations for Action: Armed Police Force

content.content: Then-Operation Commander AIG Mr. Narayan Datta Paudel, APF HQ
DIG Mr. Suresh Kumar Shrestha, Valley Commander, APF
SP Mr. Jiban KC, Disaster Rescue, Sinamangal

content: Action against these APF officers is recommended under Rule 112 of the Armed Police Force Rules, 2015 (2072 BS).
Recognition for Protecting Weapons and Stations

The Commission recommends that the Ministry of Home Affairs identify and reward those police officers and civilians who risked their lives to defend police headquarters, circles (including Naxal, Halchowk, and Bhadrakali), and weaponry from violent attackers on September 9.
National Investigation Department (NID)

It has been observed that the National Investigation Department failed to provide accurate and timely intelligence regarding the events of September 8 and 9, 2025 (Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082), as discussed in the previous analysis. Therefore, the following officials are recommended for disciplinary action:

content.content: Mr. Krishna Prasad Khanal, Co-Director, Valley Campaign Division
Mr. Riben Kumar Gachhadar, Co-Investigation Director and Kathmandu District Chief

content: The Commission recommends that the Government of Nepal initiate proceedings against these individuals in accordance with Rule 10.2 of the Nepal Special Service Rules, 1987 (2044 BS), including the 19th amendment, and the Nepal Special Service Act, 1985 (2042 BS).
Recommendations for Action Against Army Personnel and the TOB Group Regarding Security Lapses at Vital Installations
The four vital locations where the Nepal Army was deployed for security:

content.content: Presidential Palace (Shital Niwas): Security team led by Brigadier General Mr. Manoj Baidawar.
Prime Minister’s Residence (Baluwatar): Security team led by Lieutenant Colonel Mr. Diwakar Khadka.
Singha Durbar Secretariat: Security team led by Lieutenant Colonel Mr. Ganesh Khadka (also a member of the District Security Committee).
Parliament House/International Convention Center (Baneswor): Security team led by Major Mr. Santosh Dhungel.

content: The failure to protect these vital installations attracts the provisions of Section 105 of the Army Act, 2006 (2063 BS) against the respective commanders. Section 105 pertains to departmental punishments. According to Section 2(g)(1) of the Act, these officers qualify as 'Commanding Officers' as they were the heads of their respective units/sub-units at these locations.

The Commission recommends the Government of Nepal to proceed with legal and departmental actions against these four commanders under Section 105(1)(a)(7) of the Army Act, 2006, for failing to secure the nation's vital installations on September 9, 2025.
Recommendation for Action Against the TOB Group

During the September 8 protests, members of the TOB Group arrived on motorcycles from the Chabahil/Om Hospital area via the Dhobi Khola corridor. They were seen inciting the Gen Z protesters and the larger crowd near Bijulibazar and New Baneshwor, leading to an escalation of violence. The Commission recommends a criminal investigation and action under Section 35 of the Criminal Code against the guilty members of this group.
Investigation into the Shooting at Parliament Premises and Recovery of Funds at the Residence of Former Congress President Sher Bahadur Deuba:

content.content: A detailed legal investigation must be conducted regarding the large sums of money (burned or unburned) found during the attack on the residence of former PM Sher Bahadur Deuba and others on September 9.

The violence of September 9 cannot be justified merely as public anger against the previous day's state suppression. The Commission has analyzed CCTV footage, audio-visual materials, and CDR data from Telecom providers. The Government must conduct a deeper investigation to identify political leaders and activists who participated in looting and arson under the guise of the Gen Z protests.

content: Accountability for Firearms and Looted Weaponry:

During testimony, the Inspectors General of both the Nepal Police and Armed Police Force failed to provide specific data on the number of rounds fired or the specific officers involved in the September 8 shooting. They also failed to collect, seal, or send shell casings for forensic testing. This lack of accountability has made it difficult for the Commission to pinpoint exactly which officers were responsible for the deaths of protesters at the Parliament premises.

Furthermore, a detailed investigation is required into the looting of firearms, communication sets, and vehicles from various police circles, sectors, and security bases (including Ranipokhari, Kalimati, and others) on September 9. The Commission recommends the Government of Nepal investigate any actions taken outside the scope of the law by the security forces during these two days.
Recommendation for Rewarding Officers Who Protected Police Stations and Weaponry
The Commission recommends the Government of Nepal to reward the following officers for their bravery in successfully defending their stations and preventing the looting of arms during the attacks:

content.content: Mr. Hari Bahadur Basnet, DSP, District Police Office Kaski
Mr. Madhu Thapa, DSP, Kalimati Police Circle, Kathmandu
Mr. Sunil Shah, DSP, Maligaun Police Circle, Kathmandu
Ms. Chinimaya Tamang, DSP, National Police Academy
Mr. Krishna Kumar Chand, DSP, Janasewa Police Circle (For preventing the burning of the Trauma Center on the night of September 8)
Mr. Nawaraj Dhungana, DSP, Thimi Police Circle

content: Rewarding the Following Two Citizens for Saving Police Personnel:
1. Shivaram Banda, Owner of Hotel Swornim & Guest House, Koteshwor.

On September 9, 2025 (Bhadra 24, 2082), during the protests where the Koteshwor Police Circle was vandalized and set on fire, police personnel were brutally attacked with bricks, stones, and sharp weapons. While fleeing for their lives in a semi-clothed state, the officers reached Hotel Swornim & Guest House. Shivaram Banda (49), son of Tikaram Banda, originally from Ramechhap and currently residing in Koteshwor, allowed them entry through his rooftop. He hid them in various rooms, locked the main and terrace doors, provided them with food, and purchased new clothes for them. Since his actions saved the lives of the police personnel, it is recommended that he be rewarded.
2. Gita Kumari Sah, Owner of a Watch Shop, Koteshwor.

On September 9, 2025 (Bhadra 24, 2082), protesters surrounded Police Head Constable Chij Kumar Krimal near Koteshwor and attempted to kill him using sharp weapons, stones, and sticks. Gita Kumari Sah (48), daughter of Bhola Sah, originally from Saptari and currently operating a watch shop at Koteshwor Chowk, witnessed the attack and rushed to save him. Protesters threw a flammable liquid, likely petrol, at the officer and Gita Kumari. The liquid caught fire on her body, causing burn injuries. Despite the risk to her own life, she successfully saved the Head Constable. It is recommended that she be rewarded for her bravery.
Recommendation Regarding Ms. Ekta Shah, Injured Gen Z Protester (Card No. 165):

On September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), while protesting in front of the South Gate of the Parliament Secretariat in Baneshwor, she sustained a gunshot wound to her left knee. Despite her injury, she sat on an ambulance stretcher to take the MBBS entrance exam and passed with 57.5%. Given her status as an injured protester, a woman, a member of the Madhesi community, and her physical disability resulting from the injury, this Commission decided on January 29, 2026 (2082/10/15) to request the Government of Nepal to facilitate her admission under a scholarship for the current academic year. It is understood that the government is currently processing this admission.
Recommendation for Rewarding Outstanding Commission Staff:
The following staff members, who performed exceptionally while deployed to this Inquiry Commission, are recommended to the Government of Nepal for honors, medals, and cash prizes:
Joint Secretary Mr. Prakash Poudel (Ministry of Home Affairs); Under Secretary Mr. Kamal Pandey (MoHA); Computer Operator Gyanu Thapa (MoHA).
Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP) Mr. Bishnu Prasad Soti (Police Headquarters); DSP Mr. Mukesh Kumar Tiwari (PHQ); Police Inspector Mr. Gajendra Rawal (PHQ).

Police Head Constable Mr. Jaykishan Prasad Kurmi (PHQ); Assistant Head Constable Mr. Nawaraj Nepali (PHQ); Assistant Head Constable Mr. Hari Prasad Joshi (PHQ). (The last three are recommended for a one-level honorary promotion).
Chapter 14: Commission's Suggestions/Recommendations for Governance Reform

Decades of governance experience and research indicate that the primary reason development results do not match resource allocation is weak governance or 'misgovernance.' This was further validated by the recent Gen Z uprising. A massive transformation in the current governance system is essential. Political parties often fail to fulfill promises, the administration lacks accountability, and transparency is missing in the private and non-governmental sectors. Corruption is rising; Transparency International’s February 2025 report ranked Nepal 107th out of 180 countries with a score of 34/100. Currently, 20.27% of the population lives below the poverty line, with the highest rate in Sudurpashchim (34.16%). Youth unemployment has hit 20.36%. To prevent a recurrence of the events of September 2025, the following recommendations must be implemented.
14.1. Political Parties and the Electoral System:

Nepal has transitioned from the Rana oligarchy through the Shah monarchy to a Federal Democratic Republic following the 2006 (2062/63) People’s Movement and the 2015 Constitution. However, the proportional representation system makes it nearly impossible for a single party to gain a full majority, leading to mandatory political coalitions. These coalitions often focus more on power-sharing and post-allocation than on development or public will.

In the 35 years since the restoration of democracy in 1990 (2046 BS), Nepal has seen 30 Prime Ministers, none of whom completed a full five-year term. This instability culminated in the Gen Z revolt on September 8, 2025, leading to the collapse of the Congress-UML coalition and the dissolution of Parliament. General elections are now scheduled for March 6, 2026 (Falgun 21, 2082).

During this 35-year period, Girija Prasad Koirala and Sher Bahadur Deuba each served as PM five times, KP Oli four times, and Pushpa Kamal Dahal 'Prachanda' three times. Other leaders like Krishna Prasad Bhattarai, Surya Bahadur Thapa, and Lokendra Bahadur Chand served twice. Most recently, the government has been led by individuals including Sushila Karki. The root cause of the Gen Z movement lies in the refusal of these leaders to improve internal party democracy and governance. To address public anger, reforms in the electoral system and internal party governance are now a matter of urgency.
Problems Observed in the Current Political Mechanism:

content.content: Leadership Syndicate: The same individuals repeatedly hold top positions in both political parties and the government.
Declining Moral Standards: A significant erosion of the ethical foundation among political party members.
Politics of Retribution: A growing trend of using political power for personal or partisan vengeance.
Weak Internal Democracy: Party election systems are neither robust nor transparently democratic.
Resistance to Succession: Failure to accept the natural transfer of leadership to the younger generation.
Extravagant Lifestyles: Ostentatious living standards of political leaders that are disconnected from the public reality.
Unnatural Networking: Opaque alliances between political leaders and powerful interest groups.
Traditional Poverty Politics: Continuing the culture of exploiting poverty for political gain rather than solving it.
Climate of Fear: The use of threats and intimidation by political leadership.
Institutional Interference: Political parties encroaching upon every organ of state administration.
Lack of Financial Transparency: Failure to disclose internal party expenses and election spending.
Asset Disclosure Issues: Politicians failing to submit their property details within the required timeframe.
Increasing State Burden: Leaders adding financial burdens to the state treasury without transparency.
Procurement Corruption: The government becoming mired in large-scale contracts and procurement scandals under the guise of 'policy decisions'.
Constitutional Gaps: The removal of 'improper conduct' from the jurisdiction of the Commission for the Investigation of Abuse of Authority (CIAA) through constitutional changes.

Proportional Representation Flaws: The current system prevents the formation of a stable majority government; constitutional amendments are needed to ensure a directly elected majority in the House of Representatives.

content: Problems in the Electoral System:

content.content: Expensive Elections: Adoption of high-cost electoral methods where candidates spend far beyond reasonable limits, often admitted by the candidates themselves after the polls.
Opaque Management: Lack of transparency in the overall conduct and management of the electoral process.
Ineffective Code of Conduct: The election code of conduct remains limited to formalities and lacks real-world enforcement.
Persistent Malpractices: Continuation of booth capturing and other fraudulent activities during elections.
Visionless Manifestos: Manifestos are issued based on vague policies but fail to instill practical hope or provide a roadmap for the citizens.
Influence of 'Power and Money': Due to stagnant social consciousness, candidates who use money, threats, and manipulation (Saam, Daam, Danda, Bhed) are still the most likely to win.
Lack of Term Limits: Absence of restrictions on how many times a single individual can contest elections.
Delayed Modernization: Failure to effectively implement E-voting systems across all polling stations.
Exclusion of Diaspora: Continued inability to ensure voting rights for the Nepali diaspora living abroad.

content: Recommendations for Solution:

Develop a refined political system and promote political morality and integrity. Political parties must mandatorily prepare a governance framework and educate everyone from leadership to members. Implementation should be monitored periodically to make governance effective. These aspects should be integrated into party operation policies. Only when political leadership excels in integrity can they possess the moral authority to influence state agencies positively.
Establish legal provisions for internal party elections within fixed intervals and set minimum educational qualifications for candidates contesting specific positions.
Create a training mechanism to educate party members and leaders on making the government effective, understanding governance systems, and identifying areas for reform.

Provide at least two months of training for elected representatives regarding government working procedures, constitutional and legal provisions, and accountability. This can be conducted through universities or professional institutions. Legal arrangements should ensure that responsibility is handed over and oaths are taken only after the completion of such studies.
